This drift is a separate, often more critical parameter than $V_{IO}$ itself in precision applications.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:---------------------|:------|
| 1 | $V_{IO} = \dfrac{V_{O,\,off}}{A_d}$ | Definition: input offset referred to input | V |
| 2 | $V_{O,\,off} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{IO}$ | Output offset in closed-loop gain $A_{CL}$ config | V |
| 3 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | Thermal voltage @ 300 K = **25.85 mV** | V |
| 4 | $V_{IO} = V_T \left( \dfrac{\Delta I_S}{I_S} + \dfrac{\Delta R_C}{R_C} \right)$ | BJT diff-pair $V_{IO}$ (mismatch model) | V |
| 5 | $V_{IO,\,\beta} \approx V_T \cdot \dfrac{\Delta \beta}{\beta_1 \beta_2}$ | $\beta$-mismatch contribution (small) | V |
| 6 | $A_d = g_m R_C = \dfrac{I_{EE}}{2 V_T} R_C$ | Differential gain of input stage | V/V |
| 7 | $V_{IO,\,MOS} \approx \Delta V_{TH} + \dfrac{V_{OV}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta (W/L)}{W/L}$ | CMOS op-amp input offset | V |
| 8 | $TCV_{IO} = \dfrac{dV_{IO}}{dT}$ | Drift of offset with temperature | $\mu V/^{\circ}C$ |
| 9 | $V_{O,\,drift} = A_{CL} \cdot TCV_{IO} \cdot \Delta T$ | Output drift over temperature swing $\Delta T$ | V |
| 10 | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right)$ | (Side note) Limited partly by mismatches that also create $V_{IO}$ | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on notation:** In KTU board exam answer scripts, both $V_{IO}$ and $V_{OS}$ are accepted. The unit is **Volts (V)**, but always state the order of magnitude explicitly (mV, $\mu$V, nV) in your final numerical answer to gain easy valuation marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chopper-stabilized amplifiers** use AC modulation/demodulation to *cancel* the DC $V_{IO}$ to sub-$\mu$V levels (e.g., chopper op-amps like LTC2050, AD855x).
- **Auto-zero amplifiers** (e.g., AD8628, ICL7650) sample and store the offset on a capacitor and subtract it in real-time.
- **Laser trimming** of thin-film resistors on the silicon die reduces $\Delta R_C / R_C$ to < 0.1%, yielding $V_{IO}$ in the tens of $\mu$V range.
- **Nulling/Trim Pins** on op-amps (e.g., $\mu$A741, LM358) allow the user to inject a small correction current that effectively *adds* an opposite $V_{IO}$ to cancel the intrinsic offset.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Input Offset Voltage of a BJT Differential Pair

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Derive a closed-form expression for $V_{IO}$ of an emitter-coupled BJT differential pair, starting from first principles. The result will be:
> $$V_{IO} = V_T \left( \frac{\Delta I_S}{I_S} + \frac{\Delta R_C}{R_C} \right)$$

### Step 1 — Circuit Setup

Consider the emitter-coupled pair shown below in conceptual form. Q1 and Q2 share an ideal tail current source $I_{EE}$. The collector resistors are $R_{C1}$ and $R_{C2}$, and the saturation currents are $I_{S1}$ and $I_{S2}$.

Let us define mismatch variables for the two halves of the otherwise "matched" pair:

$$I_{S1} = I_S - \frac{\Delta I_S}{2}, \qquad I_{S2} = I_S + \frac{\Delta I_S}{2}$$

$$R_{C1} = R_C - \frac{\Delta R_C}{2}, \qquad R_{C2} = R_C + \frac{\Delta R_C}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Bias Point When Both Inputs Are Grounded

When $V_{in}^+ = V_{in}^- = 0$, the two base-emitter voltages are nominally equal: $V_{BE1} = V_{BE2} = V_{BE}$. Using the Ebers–Moll forward-active relation:

$$I_{C1} = I_{S1} \exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right), \qquad I_{C2} = I_{S2} \exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right)$$

Apply KCL at the common emitter node, ignoring base currents (a standard simplification valid for large $\beta$):

$$I_{C1} + I_{C2} = I_{EE}$$

Substituting the expressions above:

$$\exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right) \left( I_{S1} + I_{S2} \right) = I_{EE}$$

Using the symmetry definition $I_{S1} + I_{S2} = 2 I_S$:

$$\exp\!\left( \frac{V_{BE}}{V_T} \right) = \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Each Collector Current

Substitute the result of Step 2 back into the $I_C$ expressions:

$$I_{C1} = I_{S1} \cdot \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S} = \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S} \left( I_S - \frac{\Delta I_S}{2} \right)$$

$$I_{C2} = I_{S2} \cdot \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S} = \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S} \left( I_S + \frac{\Delta I_S}{2} \right)$$

### Step 4 — Form the Output Offset Voltage

The differential output voltage is:

$$\Delta V_O = V_{C2} - V_{C1} = R_{C2} I_{C2} - R_{C1} I_{C1}$$

Substitute the matched forms for $R_{C1}$, $R_{C2}$, $I_{C1}$, $I_{C2}$:

$$\Delta V_O = \left( R_C + \frac{\Delta R_C}{2} \right) \cdot \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S} \left( I_S + \frac{\Delta I_S}{2} \right) - \left( R_C - \frac{\Delta R_C}{2} \right) \cdot \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S} \left( I_S - \frac{\Delta I_S}{2} \right)$$

Factor out the common prefactor $\dfrac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S}$:

$$\Delta V_O = \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S} \Big[ \left( R_C + \frac{\Delta R_C}{2} \right)\!\left( I_S + \frac{\Delta I_S}{2} \right) - \left( R_C - \frac{\Delta R_C}{2} \right)\!\left( I_S - \frac{\Delta I_S}{2} \right) \Big]$$

### Step 5 — Expand the Bracket (the "Match-Mismatch" Identity)

Use the algebraic identity $(R_C + a)(I_S + b) - (R_C - a)(I_S - b) = 2 R_C b + 2 a I_S$, with $a = \dfrac{\Delta R_C}{2}$ and $b = \dfrac{\Delta I_S}{2}$:

$$2 R_C b + 2 a I_S = 2 R_C \cdot \frac{\Delta I_S}{2} + 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta R_C}{2} \cdot I_S = R_C \, \Delta I_S + I_S \, \Delta R_C$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta V_O = \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S} \left( R_C \, \Delta I_S + I_S \, \Delta R_C \right)$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Differential Gain of the Stage

The differential-mode transconductance of the pair is $g_m = \dfrac{I_{C,avg}}{V_T} = \dfrac{I_{EE}/2}{V_T}$, and the differential gain is:

$$A_d = g_m \cdot R_C = \frac{I_{EE} \, R_C}{2 V_T}$$

### Step 7 — Refer the Output Offset Back to the Input

By definition, the input-referred offset voltage is:

$$V_{IO} = \frac{\Delta V_O}{A_d} = \frac{ \dfrac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S} \left( R_C \, \Delta I_S + I_S \, \Delta R_C \right) }{ \dfrac{I_{EE} \, R_C}{2 V_T} }$$

Cancel the common factor $\dfrac{I_{EE}}{2}$ from numerator and denominator:

$$V_{IO} = \frac{V_T}{I_S \, R_C} \left( R_C \, \Delta I_S + I_S \, \Delta R_C \right)$$

Distribute and simplify:

$$V_{IO} = V_T \left( \frac{\Delta I_S}{I_S} + \frac{\Delta R_C}{R_C} \right) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the final result:**
> - The offset is **directly proportional** to the thermal voltage $V_T$ (≈ 25.85 mV at room temperature).
> - It scales **linearly** with the *fractional mismatch* in $I_S$ and in $R_C$.
> - Even a 1% mismatch in either quantity gives $V_{IO} \approx 0.26$ mV — comparable to a real discrete op-amp's offset spec.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Scenarios

| Case | $\Delta I_S / I_S$ | $\Delta R_C / R_C$ | $V_{IO}$ (mV) | Comment |
|:----:|:------------------:|:------------------:|:-------------:|:--------|
| Ideal | 0% | 0% | 0.000 | Textbook "perfect" pair |
| Matched IC | 0.5% | 0.5% | 0.258 | Typical precision op-amp (post-trim) |
| Discrete BJTs | 5% | 2% | 1.808 | Real lab-built pair on breadboard |
| Poor (worst) | 10% | 5% | 3.880 | Edge-of-spec discretes |

**Worked Numerical Example (Case 3 above):** $V_T = 25.85$ mV, mismatches 5% and 2%:

$$V_{IO} = 25.85 \times (0.05 + 0.02) = 25.85 \times 0.07 = 1.8095\ \text{mV} \approx 1.81\ \text{mV}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Origin & Cancellation of $V_{IO}$

The diagram below shows the functional flow inside an op-amp input stage: where mismatches are introduced, how they propagate to the output, and the standard cancellation strategies used in precision design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef ideal fill:#dff5e1,stroke:#1a7f37,color:#0a3d1f,stroke-width:1.5px;
    classDef mismatch fill:#fde2e1,stroke:#b42318,color:#5a0d0d,stroke-width:1.5px;
    classDef path fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#1e1b4b,stroke-width:1.5px;
    classDef cancel fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#92580a,color:#4a2c00,stroke-width:1.5px;

    IN1[IN PLUS]:::ideal
    IN2[IN MINUS]:::ideal
    IB1[INPUT PAIR Q1 BJT]:::mismatch
    IB2[INPUT PAIR Q2 BJT]:::mismatch
    ISRC[IDEAL TAIL CURRENT IEE]:::ideal
    RC1[COLLECTOR RESISTOR RC1]:::mismatch
    RC2[COLLECTOR RESISTOR RC2]:::mismatch
    SUM[VBE AND IC NODES]:::path
    VOFF[DC OUTPUT OFFSET VO OFF]:::mismatch
    GAIN[DIFFERENTIAL GAIN AD]:::path
    VREF[INPUT OFFSET VOLTAGE VIO]:::mismatch
    TRIM[TRIM NETWORK NULL PINS]:::cancel
    AZ[CHOPPER OR AUTOZERO BLOCK]:::cancel
    OUT[CLEAN OUTPUT VO]:::ideal

    IN1 --> IB1
    IN2 --> IB2
    ISRC --> IB1
    ISRC --> IB2
    IB1 --> SUM
    IB2 --> SUM
    RC1 --> SUM
    RC2 --> SUM
    SUM --> VOFF
    VOFF --> GAIN
    GAIN --> VREF
    VREF --> TRIM
    VREF --> AZ
    TRIM --> OUT
    AZ --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Schematic — BJT Differential Pair with Offset Sources

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef bjt fill:#ffe7d6,stroke:#9a3412,color:#431407,stroke-width:1.5px;
    classDef res fill:#fde2e1,stroke:#b42318,color:#5a0d0d,stroke-width:1.5px;
    classDef src fill:#dff5e1,stroke:#1a7f37,color:#0a3d1f,stroke-width:1.5px;

    VCC[(VCC SUPPLY)]:::src
    RC1A((RC1 PLUS DELTA RC BY 2)):::res
    Q1A((BJT Q1 IS1 MINUS DELTA IS BY 2)):::bjt
    Q2A((BJT Q2 IS2 PLUS DELTA IS BY 2)):::bjt
    RC2A((RC2 PLUS DELTA RC BY 2)):::res
    IEEA[(TAIL CURRENT IEE)]:::src
    VINP[VIN PLUS]:::src
    VINM[VIN MINUS]:::src
    VIOA[(VIO APPLIED HERE TO NULL OUTPUT)]:::src
    VOUTA[DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT]:::src

    VCC --- RC1A
    RC1A --- Q1A
    VCC --- RC2A
    RC2A --- Q2A
    Q1A --- IEEA
    Q2A --- IEEA
    VINP --- Q1A
    VINM --- Q2A
    VIOA --- VINP
    VIOA --- VINM
    Q1A --- VOUTA
    Q2A --- VOUTA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** The orange blocks (Q1, Q2) are the *mismatched* BJT pair; the red blocks (RC1, RC2) are the *mismatched* load resistors. The mismatch in these components is the *physical origin* of $V_{IO}$. The arrow entering the differential input is the *compensation* $V_{IO}$ that is applied externally to drive the output back to its ideal reference.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Writing $V_{IO}$ in mV when the value is in $\mu$V (or vice-versa) costs the unit mark. Always quote the *order of magnitude* explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{IO}$ with output offset $V_{O,\,off}$:** $V_{IO}$ is *input-referred*; $V_{O,\,off}$ is at the *output*. They differ by the factor of (differential) gain.
> 3. **Forgetting $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K:** When numerical problems do not state $V_T$, students must write the assumed temperature (usually 300 K) and compute $V_T$ explicitly for full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the small-signal approximation:** The mismatch formula $V_{IO} = V_T (\Delta I_S/I_S + \Delta R_C/R_C)$ is valid for *small* mismatches (≤ 10%). Writing it for "any" mismatch loses a conceptual mark.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps in derivation problems:** KTU valuation keys award *step marks* for each logical stage of a derivation. Skipping from $I_{C1} = I_{S1}\exp(V_{BE}/V_T)$ directly to the final $V_{IO}$ equation will cost at least 4–5 marks on a 14-mark question.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model]**
*Define the term "Input Offset Voltage" of an operational amplifier. State the typical range of $V_{IO}$ for general-purpose and precision op-amps.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Definition (2 marks):** Input Offset Voltage ($V_{IO}$) is the small differential DC voltage that must be applied between the inverting and non-inverting input terminals of an op-amp in order to nullify (drive to zero) the DC output voltage when no input signal is present. It arises from internal mismatches in the input transistor pair and load resistors of the input differential stage.

**Typical Range (1 mark):**
- **General-purpose op-amps** (e.g., $\mu$A741, LM358): $V_{IO} \approx$ **1 to 10 mV**.
- **Precision op-amps** (e.g., OP-07, AD8628, chopper-stabilized): $V_{IO} \approx$ **1 to 100 $\mu$V**.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]**
*List any **three** practical sources/causes of input offset voltage in a BJT differential pair input stage of an op-amp.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Mismatch in saturation current ($I_S$)** of the two input BJTs, caused by differences in emitter area, base doping, or device geometry.
2. **Mismatch in collector load resistors ($R_C$)**, caused by lithographic/doping variations in integrated thin-film or diffused resistors.
3. **Mismatch in current gain ($\beta$)** of the input transistors, leading to unequal base currents and asymmetric voltage drops across any source resistance.

*(Optional 4th answer for extra credit: Thermal gradient across the die, mechanical stress, or aging effects.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both sub-parts are compulsory. Two alternative questions are provided; only **one** is to be answered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the input offset voltage of a BJT emitter-coupled differential pair with a tail current $I_{EE}$, taking into account mismatches in the saturation currents $I_S$ and collector resistors $R_C$ of the two halves of the pair. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BJT differential pair uses transistors with $V_T = 25.85$ mV. The saturation currents of the two BJTs differ by 3%, and the collector load resistors differ by 2%. Compute the input offset voltage and the corresponding output offset voltage if the differential gain of the stage is $A_d = 80$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- BJTs operate in forward-active region.
- Tail current source is ideal ($I_{EE}$ constant, infinite output impedance).
- Base currents are negligible (large $\beta$).
- Mismatches are small (≤ 10%) so that higher-order cross-products are ignored.

**Derivation Steps (6 marks):**

*Step 1:* Define mismatches: $I_{S1} = I_S - \Delta I_S/2$, $I_{S2} = I_S + \Delta I_S/2$; $R_{C1} = R_C - \Delta R_C/2$, $R_{C2} = R_C + \Delta R_C/2$.

*Step 2:* With both inputs grounded, $V_{BE1} = V_{BE2} = V_{BE}$. KCL at the emitter node:

$$I_{C1} + I_{C2} = I_{EE} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right) = \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S}$$

*Step 3:* Compute each collector current:

$$I_{C1} = \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S}\left(I_S - \frac{\Delta I_S}{2}\right), \qquad I_{C2} = \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S}\left(I_S + \frac{\Delta I_S}{2}\right)$$

*Step 4:* Form output offset $\Delta V_O = R_{C2} I_{C2} - R_{C1} I_{C1}$ and expand using the identity $(R_C + a)(I_S + b) - (R_C - a)(I_S - b) = 2 R_C b + 2 a I_S$:

$$\Delta V_O = \frac{I_{EE}}{2 I_S} \left( R_C \Delta I_S + I_S \Delta R_C \right)$$

*Step 5:* Differential gain is $A_d = g_m R_C = \dfrac{I_{EE} R_C}{2 V_T}$.

*Step 6:* Refer to input:

$$V_{IO} = \frac{\Delta V_O}{A_d} = V_T \left( \frac{\Delta I_S}{I_S} + \frac{\Delta R_C}{R_C} \right) \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $V_T = 25.85$ mV, $\Delta I_S / I_S = 3\% = 0.03$, $\Delta R_C / R_C = 2\% = 0.02$, $A_d = 80$.

**Input offset voltage (3 marks):**

$$V_{IO} = 25.85 \times (0.03 + 0.02) = 25.85 \times 0.05 = 1.2925\ \text{mV}$$

**Output offset voltage (3 marks):**

$$V_{O,\,off} = A_d \cdot V_{IO} = 80 \times 1.2925\ \text{mV} = 103.4\ \text{mV}$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** $\boxed{V_{IO} \approx 1.29\ \text{mV}, \quad V_{O,\,off} \approx 103.4\ \text{mV}}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat functional diagram the internal mechanism by which input offset voltage is generated in a CMOS op-amp input stage. Compare it qualitatively with the BJT case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A precision instrumentation amplifier built using a generic op-amp with $V_{IO} = 250\ \mu V$ and $TCV_{IO} = 0.5\ \mu V/^{\circ}C$ is operated in a closed-loop gain of $A_{CL} = 200$. The ambient temperature varies from $+25^{\circ}C$ to $+75^{\circ}C$ over a day. Calculate (i) the DC output offset at $25^{\circ}C$, and (ii) the worst-case additional output drift over the $50^{\circ}C$ temperature swing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**CMOS Mechanism (5 marks):** In a CMOS differential pair, the two input transistors (M1, M2) are matched MOSFETs. The drain currents are:

$$I_{D1} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left( \frac{W}{L} \right)_1 (V_{GS} - V_{TH1})^2, \quad I_{D2} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \left( \frac{W}{L} \right)_2 (V_{GS} - V_{TH2})^2$$

When both gates are grounded, $V_{GS}$ is nominally the same for both. However, mismatches in (i) threshold voltage $V_{TH1} \neq V_{TH2}$ (channel doping variation) and (ii) transconductance parameter $K = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)$ (geometry/mobility variation) cause unequal drain currents. With a tail current $I_{SS}$ fixed, the $I_{D1} \neq I_{D2}$ condition produces a differential output offset that, when referred back to the input, gives the CMOS $V_{IO}$:

$$V_{IO,\,MOS} \approx \Delta V_{TH} + \frac{V_{OV}}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta K}{K}$$

where $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ is the overdrive voltage.

**Qualitative Comparison with BJT (2 marks):**

| Aspect | BJT | CMOS |
|:------|:----|:-----|
| Dominant mismatch | $\Delta I_S$ (saturation current) | $\Delta V_{TH}$ (threshold voltage) |
| Secondary mismatch | $\Delta R_C$ | $\Delta (W/L)$, $\Delta \mu$ |
| Typical $V_{IO}$ magnitude | $\sim$V_T × (mismatch %); tens of $\mu$V–few mV | $\sim\Delta V_{TH} + $ overdrive term; few mV–tens of mV |
| Drift | Lower drift (well-controlled $V_{BE}$) | Higher drift (process-sensitive $V_{TH}$) |
| Matching quality | Better (BJTs match well in IC) | Poorer (especially in sub-$\mu$m CMOS) |

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $V_{IO} = 250\ \mu V$, $TCV_{IO} = 0.5\ \mu V/^{\circ}C$, $A_{CL} = 200$, $\Delta T = 75 - 25 = 50^{\circ}C$.

**(i) DC output offset at $25^{\circ}C$ (3 marks):**

$$V_{O,\,off}(25^{\circ}C) = A_{CL} \cdot V_{IO} = 200 \times 250\ \mu V = 50{,}000\ \mu V = 50\ \text{mV}$$

**(ii) Worst-case additional output drift over $\Delta T = 50^{\circ}C$ (3 marks):**

$$\Delta V_{IO} = TCV_{IO} \cdot \Delta T = 0.5\ \mu V/^{\circ}C \times 50^{\circ}C = 25\ \mu V$$

$$\Delta V_{O,\,drift} = A_{CL} \cdot \Delta V_{IO} = 200 \times 25\ \mu V = 5000\ \mu V = 5\ \text{mV}$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):**
- (i) $V_{O,\,off}(25^{\circ}C) = \mathbf{50\ mV}$
- (ii) Additional output drift = $\mathbf{5\ mV}$ (worst case)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU exam prep):**

- ✅ **Definition:** $V_{IO}$ is the *input-referred* DC voltage that nulls the output offset. *Not* the output offset itself.
- ✅ **Thermal Voltage:** Always remember $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$ at $T = 300\ K$. This is the magic number behind most offset formulas.
- ✅ **Master Formula (BJT pair):** $V_{IO} = V_T \left( \dfrac{\Delta I_S}{I_S} + \dfrac{\Delta R_C}{R_C} \right)$ — memorize it, derive it, love it.
- ✅ **Master Formula (CMOS pair):** $V_{IO} \approx \Delta V_{TH} + \dfrac{V_{OV}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta K}{K}$.
- ✅ **Conversion:** $V_{O,\,off} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{IO}$ (closed-loop) and $V_{O,\,off} = A_d \cdot V_{IO}$ (open-loop, no feedback).
- ✅ **Drift Equation:** $V_{O,\,drift} = A_{CL} \cdot TCV_{IO} \cdot \Delta T$.
- ✅ **Order of Magnitude:**
  - General-purpose op-amp: $V_{IO} \sim$ **mV**.
  - Precision/chopper op-amp: $V_{IO} \sim$ **$\mu$V** or sub-$\mu$V.
- ✅ **Mitigation Techniques:** (i) External nulling via trim pins, (ii) Chopper stabilization, (iii) Auto-zero architecture, (iv) Laser-trimmed on-chip resistors, (v) Choosing precision-grade op-amps in critical paths.
- ✅ **Common Mistake Trap:** $V_{IO}$ is a *small-signal linear* parameter — do not apply it to large differential inputs; the formula breaks down outside the small-mismatch regime.
- ✅ **Examiner Expectation:** In 14-mark derivations, always *draw the equivalent circuit*, *state assumptions*, and *show every algebraic step*. Do **not** jump from the KCL equation directly to the final $V_{IO}$ expression.
- ✅ **Temperature Rule of Thumb:** $V_{IO}$ *increases* (in absolute value) at temperature extremes. Both $V_{BE}$ and $V_{TH}$ vary with $T$, so circuit-level compensation (e.g., using a temperature-stable bias network) is a frequent design question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Drift
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Drift

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Drift** is defined as the gradual, undesirable change in the output or operating parameters of a network component (such as a transmitter, receiver, repeater, oscillator, or transmission line characteristic) caused by variations in ambient or internal temperature. It is quantified as a **temperature coefficient** (expressed in units of the parameter per °C, e.g., dBm/°C, ppm/°C, or V/°C).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** Computer Networks syllabus (Module 1: Foundations & Physical Layer), Thermal Drift is a critical physical-layer impairment that affects the **reliability, signal integrity, and synchronization** of data communication systems. It is a deterministic, slowly varying disturbance that degrades the long-term stability of analog and digital hardware used in networking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 1 emphasizes Transmission Media, Signal Encoding, and Physical Layer Devices. Thermal Drift falls under the broader umbrella of *channel impairments* and *hardware non-idealities* that every network engineer must understand.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are stretching a **rubber band** to measure a fixed distance.

- On a **cool morning**, the rubber is stiff, and you get one reading.
- On a **hot afternoon**, the rubber becomes soft and stretchy, giving you a different (wrong) reading — even though the actual distance hasn't changed.

The rubber band hasn't "broken" or "failed" — it has simply **drifted** from its calibrated state due to temperature. The same phenomenon occurs inside network equipment:

- A **copper cable's resistance** increases when hot, attenuating the signal more.
- A **laser diode's output power** in a fiber optic transmitter shifts with temperature.
- A **quartz crystal oscillator** in a network card's clock circuit changes frequency slightly with temperature, causing synchronization errors.

The "rubber band" of networking is every temperature-sensitive component, and the "morning vs. afternoon" is the operating environment's thermal variation.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **bold** constants and metrics are used throughout thermal drift analysis:

- **Temperature Coefficient (TC / α)**: Rate of change of a parameter per unit temperature change (e.g., **0.4 dB/km/°C** for optical fiber attenuation).
- **Reference Temperature (T₀)**: The standard reference, typically **+25 °C (298.15 K)**.
- **Parts Per Million (ppm/°C)**: Standard for oscillator frequency stability (e.g., **±50 ppm/°C** for commercial crystals).
- **Thermal Noise Floor (kT)**: Boltzmann's constant × absolute temperature, fundamental to **Johnson-Nyquist noise** ≈ **4.14 × 10⁻²¹ J at 300 K**.
- **dBm**: Decibel-milliwatts, the standard unit for measuring optical/RF power drift in network links.

---

## 1.4 Types of Thermal Drift in Networking

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exam purposes, classify drift into the following four categories:

| # | Type | Affected Component | Typical Magnitude |
|---|------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Attenuation Drift** | Copper cables, optical fibers | 0.1–0.5 dB/km/°C |
| 2 | **Power Drift** | Laser diodes, LEDs, photodetectors | 0.3–1.0 dBm/°C |
| 3 | **Frequency Drift** | Clock oscillators, PLL circuits | ±10 to ±100 ppm/°C |
| 4 | **Impedance Drift** | PCB traces, connectors, terminations | 1–5 %/°C |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Thermal Drift Occur? — The Physics

At the **atomic level**, temperature represents the average kinetic energy of particles in a material. As temperature rises:

1. **Lattice vibrations (phonons)** in conductors increase → **scattering of electrons** increases → **resistance rises** (in metallic conductors).
2. In **semiconductors** (used in every network IC, laser, photodiode), the **band gap energy** effectively shrinks → carrier concentration changes → output current, gain, and threshold voltage drift.
3. In **optical fibers**, the **glass refractive index** is temperature-dependent → changes the modal propagation characteristics → affects attenuation and dispersion.
4. In **quartz oscillators**, the **elastic modulus of the crystal** changes with T → resonant frequency shifts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" for KTU:** Thermal drift is unavoidable because it stems from fundamental solid-state physics. The engineering goal is not to eliminate it, but to **characterize, compensate, and budget for it** during network design.

---

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — The Drift Mechanism

### Step 1: Heat Generation
Network equipment dissipates power as heat. P = I²R in conductors, P = V·I in active devices.

### Step 2: Temperature Rise
Heat accumulates in the device until thermal equilibrium: **T_junction = T_ambient + (θ_JA × P_dissipated)**, where θ_JA is the junction-to-ambient thermal resistance.

### Step 3: Parameter Shift
Critical parameters shift linearly (or quadratically) with temperature: **X(T) = X(T₀) × [1 + α(T − T₀)]**.

### Step 4: Signal Degradation
Drifted parameters cause **increased BER (Bit Error Rate)**, **jitter**, **wander**, **power loss**, and ultimately **link failure** if not compensated.

### Step 5: Compensation Mechanisms
Modern networking gear uses:
- **Thermoelectric Coolers (TECs)** in DWDM transceivers
- **Automatic Gain Control (AGC)** in receivers
- **Temperature-compensated crystal oscillators (TCXOs)**
- **Adaptive equalization** in high-speed SerDes

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the complete, exam-ready mathematical toolkit for Thermal Drift problems.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Units |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Linear Drift Model** | $X(T) = X_0 \, [1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$ | Generic parameter drift, α in /°C |
| 2 | **Copper Resistance vs. T** | $R(T) = R_{20} \, [1 + \alpha_{Cu} (T - 20)]$ | α_Cu ≈ **0.00393 /°C** |
| 3 | **Attenuation vs. T (Copper)** | $A(T) = A_{20} \, [1 + k_a (T - 20)]$ | k_a ≈ **0.002 /°C** (0.2 %/°C) |
| 4 | **Attenuation vs. T (Fiber)** | $A(T) = A_{25} + k_{fiber} (T - 25)$ | k_fiber ≈ **0.1–0.5 dB/km/°C** |
| 5 | **Frequency Drift (Oscillator)** | $f(T) = f_0 + \Delta f = f_0 [1 + \alpha_f (T - T_0)]$ | α_f in **ppm/°C** |
| 6 | **Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist)** | $N = k_B T B$ | k_B = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**, B in Hz |
| 7 | **Noise Power in dBm** | $N_{dBm} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(B_{Hz})$ | At T = 300 K (room temperature) |
| 8 | **Junction Temperature** | $T_j = T_a + \theta_{JA} P_D$ | θ_JA in **°C/W** |
| 9 | **BER Drift Approximation** | $BER \propto e^{-SNR_{eff}/2}$ | Effective SNR drops as drift increases |
| 10 | **Link Power Budget with Drift** | $P_{margin} = P_{tx} - P_{rx(min)} - A_{total} - Drift_{worst}$ | All in **dBm** |
| 11 | **Time Interval Error (Wander)** | $TIE = \int \frac{\Delta f(t)}{f_0} dt$ | Wander accumulates over time |
| 12 | **Wander Generation (MTIE)** | $MTIE(\tau) = \frac{\alpha_f \cdot \Delta T \cdot \tau}{f_0}$ | τ = observation window |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - α_Cu is **positive** → resistance always increases with T.
> - For **optical fibers**, attenuation *increases* with T in the 850 nm window but has a complex λ-dependence at 1310/1550 nm.
> - **Reference temperatures:** T₀ = 20 °C (copper cabling standards) or 25 °C (electronics/optical).
> - **Worst-case design** uses ΔT = T_max − T_min over the equipment's operating range (e.g., 0 °C to 70 °C for commercial gear).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermal drift analysis is **mission-critical** in the following KTU-relevant scenarios:

1. **Long-Haul Optical Networks (DWDM):** A 0.3 dB power drift per °C over 80 km spans can accumulate to several dB — potentially exceeding the link budget.
2. **5G/6G Base Stations:** Outdoor units experience −40 °C to +85 °C, demanding **wide-temperature-range** oscillators.
3. **Data Center Cooling:** Temperature gradients across racks cause *spatially non-uniform* drift, creating intermittent packet loss.
4. **Industrial IoT & Automotive Ethernet:** Harsh environments (engines, factories) push components to extreme thermal limits.
5. **Synchronous Ethernet (SyncE) & PTP:** Frequency drift directly translates to **time synchronization error**, critical for financial trading and power-grid networks.
6. **Submarine Cables:** Repeaters along trans-oceanic cables must compensate drift over 25-year lifetimes with minimal maintenance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Copper Cable Resistance Drift

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A twisted-pair copper cable has a resistance of **R₂₀ = 100 Ω** at **T₀ = 20 °C**. The temperature rises to **T = 60 °C**. Find the new resistance, given the copper temperature coefficient **α_Cu = 0.00393 /°C**.

### Solution

**Step 1: Identify the governing equation.**
The linear resistance-temperature model for metallic conductors is given by:

$$R(T) = R_{20} \, [1 + \alpha_{Cu} (T - 20)]$$

**Step 2: Substitute the known values.**
R₂₀ = 100 Ω, T = 60 °C, α_Cu = 0.00393 /°C.

$$R(60) = 100 \, [1 + 0.00393 \times (60 - 20)]$$

**Step 3: Compute the temperature difference.**

$$\Delta T = 60 - 20 = 40 \, ^\circ C$$

**Step 4: Multiply the coefficient by ΔT.**

$$0.00393 \times 40 = 0.1572$$

**Step 5: Add 1 to obtain the multiplicative factor.**

$$1 + 0.1572 = 1.1572$$

**Step 6: Multiply by the reference resistance.**

$$R(60) = 100 \times 1.1572 = 115.72 \, \Omega$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{R(60^\circ C) = 115.72 \, \Omega \quad \text{(a 15.72\% increase)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Link Power Budget with Thermal Drift

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** An optical link has a transmitter power **P_tx = +3 dBm**, receiver sensitivity **P_rx(min) = −18 dBm**, total passive attenuation **A_total = 12 dB**, and a worst-case thermal drift of **Δ_drift = 4 dB** over its operating range. Determine the **link power margin**.

### Solution

**Step 1: Write the power margin equation.**

$$P_{margin} = P_{tx} - P_{rx(min)} - A_{total} - \Delta_{drift}$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values (all in dB/dBm).**

$$P_{margin} = (+3) - (-18) - 12 - 4$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the bracket term.**

$$(+3) - (-18) = 3 + 18 = 21 \, dB$$

**Step 4: Subtract attenuation.**

$$21 - 12 = 9 \, dB$$

**Step 5: Subtract the thermal drift penalty.**

$$9 - 4 = 5 \, dB$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{P_{margin} = 5 \, dB \quad \text{(positive margin = link is viable)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A **negative** margin would mean the link is **unreliable** under worst-case temperature — students often forget to subtract the drift term and incorrectly declare the link "viable."

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Oscillator Frequency Drift → Wander Error

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A network interface card uses a quartz oscillator with nominal frequency **f₀ = 125 MHz** (common for gigabit Ethernet). The temperature coefficient is **α_f = ±50 ppm/°C**, and the operating temperature varies by **ΔT = 30 °C**. Find the **maximum frequency deviation** and the **time error accumulated over 1 hour**.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute the fractional frequency deviation.**

$$\frac{\Delta f}{f_0} = \alpha_f \times \Delta T = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 30 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2: Compute the absolute frequency deviation in Hz.**

$$\Delta f = f_0 \times \frac{\Delta f}{f_0} = 125 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 187{,}500 \, Hz$$

**Step 3: Compute the time error per clock cycle.**

$$\Delta t_{cycle} = \frac{\Delta f}{f_0^2} = \frac{187{,}500}{(125 \times 10^6)^2} = 1.2 \times 10^{-14} \, s$$

**Step 4: Compute the number of clock cycles in 1 hour.**

$$N = f_0 \times t = 125 \times 10^6 \times 3600 = 4.5 \times 10^{11} \, \text{cycles}$$

**Step 5: Compute the accumulated time error.**

$$\Delta T_{wander} = N \times \Delta t_{cycle} = 4.5 \times 10^{11} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-14} = 5.4 \times 10^{-3} \, s$$

$$\Delta T_{wander} = 5.4 \, \text{ms per hour}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\Delta f_{max} = 187.5 \, \text{kHz} \quad ; \quad \text{Wander} = 5.4 \, \text{ms/hour}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write "187.5 kHz" but forget the units. Always include **Hz, dB, ppm, °C** explicitly to earn full marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Drift vs. Networking Subsystem

| Subsystem | Dominant Drift Parameter | Mathematical Form | Mitigation Strategy |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Twisted-Pair Copper** | DC resistance, insertion loss | $R(T) = R_{20}[1 + 0.00393 \Delta T]$ | Thicker gauge, derated cable spec |
| **Coaxial Cable** | Attenuation | $A(T) = A_{20}(1 + 0.002 \Delta T)$ | Active equalization |
| **Optical Fiber (SMF)** | Attenuation, dispersion | $A(T) = A_{25} + 0.2 \cdot \Delta T$ | TEC, AGC in EDFA |
| **Laser Transmitter** | Output power, threshold current | $P_{opt}(T) = P_0 - k_T \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermoelectric cooler (TEC) |
| **Photodetector** | Responsivity, dark current | $R(T) = R_0(1 + 0.1\% /^\circ C \cdot \Delta T)$ | Temperature-stabilized housing |
| **Clock Oscillator** | Frequency | $f(T) = f_0(1 \pm \alpha_f \Delta T)$ | TCXO, OCXO, GPS disciplining |
| **RF Front-End (Wireless)** | Gain, noise figure | $NF(T) = NF_0 + 0.01 \, \Delta T$ dB | Calibrated AGC loops |
| **PCB Traces** | Characteristic impedance | $Z_0(T) = Z_0(25) \sqrt{L(T)/C(T)}$ | Matched dielectric materials |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Thermal Drift Mechanism in a Network Link

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how thermal drift propagates from environmental conditions through the network hardware to the user-visible performance metric (BER / packet loss).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ambient Temperature Variation] --> B[Equipment Internal Heating]
    B --> C{Temperature-Sensitive Subsystem}
    C --> D1[Copper Cable: Resistance R]
    C --> D2[Optical Fiber: Attenuation A]
    C --> D3[Laser: Optical Power P]
    C --> D4[Crystal Oscillator: Frequency f]
    C --> D5[RF Amplifier: Gain G]
    D1 --> E[Shifted Electrical Parameters]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Signal Degradation at Receiver]
    F --> G[Increased BER and Jitter]
    G --> H[Compensation Controller]
    H --> I1[TEC Cooler Activated]
    H --> I2[AGC Adjustment]
    H --> I3[TCXO Recalibration]
    H --> I4[Adaptive Equalizer]
    I1 --> J[Restored Link Performance]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    J --> K[Stable Network Operation]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Drift Compensation Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Parameter X0] --> B[Compare with Current X T]
    B --> C{Drift Exceeds Threshold?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Activate Compensation]
    C -- No --> E[Hold Steady State]
    D --> F[Update Calibration]
    F --> B
    E --> B
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Power Budget Visualization with Drift

```mermaid
graph LR
    Ptx[Transmitter +3 dBm] -- A_passive --> Mid1[After Attenuation 12 dB]
    Mid1 -- A_drift --> Mid2[After Thermal Drift 4 dB]
    Mid2 -- Loss --> Prx[Receiver Sensitivity -18 dBm]
    Ptx -. Power Margin 5 dB .-> Prx
```

## 4.4 Subsystem Interaction Map — Drift Sources & Countermeasures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Source[Drift Sources]
        S1[Thermal Expansion]
        S2[Carrier Mobility Change]
        S3[Refractive Index Shift]
        S4[Elastic Modulus Change]
    end
    subgraph Effect[Hardware Effects]
        E1[Resistance]
        E2[Gain Drift]
        E3[Attenuation]
        E4[Frequency Error]
    end
    subgraph Network[Network-Level Impact]
        N1[Higher BER]
        N2[Link Failure]
        N3[Sync Loss]
        N4[Packet Drops]
    end
    S1 --> E1 --> N1
    S1 --> E3 --> N2
    S2 --> E2 --> N1
    S3 --> E3 --> N2
    S4 --> E4 --> N3
    S4 --> E4 --> N4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** For complex physical drawings (e.g., laser diode cross-sections, oscillator tank circuits), the Mermaid block diagrams above provide a **functional architecture view** that maps the cause-and-effect chain — this is fully compliant with KTU 2024 Scheme module-level assessment expectations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Thermal Drift in the context of computer network hardware. Mention two physical-layer components it affects most. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Thermal drift is the **gradual, temperature-induced change** in a network component's operating parameter away from its calibrated value. It is a deterministic impairment caused by the temperature dependence of material properties (resistance, refractive index, band-gap, elastic modulus).

The two most affected physical-layer components are:
1. **Optical transmitters (laser diodes / LEDs)** — output power drops with rising T.
2. **Quartz crystal oscillators** — clock frequency shifts, causing wander/sync errors.

*(Acceptable: copper cables, photodetectors, RF amplifiers.)*

---

### Q2. State any three standard reference temperatures used in network equipment thermal analysis. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **T₀ = 20 °C** — used for **copper cabling standards** (TIA/EIA, ISO/IEC).
2. **T₀ = 25 °C** — used for **electronic and optical components** (semiconductor datasheets).
3. **T₀ = 0 °C** — used as the **lower bound** of the commercial operating range.
4. *(Bonus)* T₀ = 27 °C is occasionally used in **Indian telecom specifications** (TEC).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part B Module-1 questions carry **internal choice**. Both options are provided below.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat block diagrams the **mechanism of thermal drift** in an optical fiber communication link. How does it affect the **link power budget** and **bit error rate**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-mode optical fiber link has the following specifications:
- Transmitter power: **P_tx = +5 dBm**
- Receiver sensitivity: **P_rx(min) = −22 dBm**
- Fiber attenuation at 25 °C: **0.35 dB/km**
- Total link length: **L = 50 km**
- Worst-case operating temperature: **65 °C** (reference 25 °C)
- Temperature coefficient of attenuation: **k = 0.002 /°C**

Calculate:
1. The attenuation at 65 °C.
2. The total passive loss.
3. The link power margin (ignoring drift and connector losses).
4. Comment on link viability.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Definition (2 marks):**
Thermal drift in optical links refers to the **temperature-induced change** in fiber attenuation, laser output power, and detector responsivity. The dominant contributor in SMF is the **fiber attenuation drift** with temperature.

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
1. **Temperature rise** changes the **refractive index profile** of the silica glass.
2. This alters the **scattering losses (Rayleigh + micro-bending)**.
3. The result is a **linear increase in attenuation** with temperature: $A(T) = A_0 + k(T - T_0)$.
4. Additionally, **laser diode threshold current** rises with T, reducing output power.
5. **Photodetector dark current** also rises, raising the noise floor.

**Impact on Power Budget and BER (2 marks):**
- Higher attenuation → **lower received power** → lower SNR.
- Lower SNR → exponentially higher **BER** ($BER \approx e^{-SNR/2}$ for coherent, $BER \approx \frac{1}{2} \text{erfc}(\sqrt{SNR})$ for OOK).
- Eventually, BER exceeds the **FEC threshold (10⁻³)** or **HD-FEC (10⁻⁵)**, and the link fails.

*(Insert the block diagram from Section 4.1 here — earn 1 extra mark for schematic.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Step 1: Temperature difference.**

$$\Delta T = 65 - 25 = 40 \, ^\circ C$$

**Step 2: Attenuation at 65 °C.**

$$A(65) = A_{25} \times [1 + k \cdot \Delta T] = 0.35 \times [1 + 0.002 \times 40]$$

$$A(65) = 0.35 \times [1 + 0.08] = 0.35 \times 1.08 = 0.378 \, \text{dB/km}$$

**Step 3: Total passive loss over 50 km.**

$$A_{total} = A(65) \times L = 0.378 \times 50 = 18.9 \, \text{dB}$$

**Step 4: Link power margin.**

$$P_{margin} = P_{tx} - P_{rx(min)} - A_{total} = (+5) - (-22) - 18.9$$

$$P_{margin} = 5 + 22 - 18.9 = 26.9 - 18.9 = 8.0 \, \text{dB}$$

**Step 5: Viability comment.**

$$\boxed{P_{margin} = +8.0 \, \text{dB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Link is viable with positive margin.}}$$

The link has an **8 dB margin** to accommodate connector losses, splice losses (typically 0.1–0.3 dB each), aging, and additional drift over the equipment's lifetime.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss **thermal drift in clock oscillators** used in network interface cards. Derive the relationship between **frequency drift, temperature variation, and accumulated time error (wander)**. Why is this critical for **synchronous Ethernet and PTP** networks?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A network card oscillator has:
- Nominal frequency: **f₀ = 156.25 MHz** (10 GbE reference clock).
- Temperature coefficient: **α_f = ±30 ppm/°C**.
- Operating temperature swing: **ΔT = 50 °C**.

Find:
1. The maximum absolute frequency deviation.
2. The fractional frequency error.
3. The accumulated time error over **6 hours**.
4. State whether this is acceptable for **G.8273.2 (PTP Class C)** applications requiring wander < **150 ns/s**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**Discussion (3 marks):**
- Clock oscillators (typically **AT-cut quartz crystals**) in NICs generate the bit-timing reference.
- Their resonant frequency depends on the **elastic, piezoelectric, and dimensional properties** of the crystal — all of which are **temperature-sensitive**.
- Commercial XO: ±50 ppm/°C; **TCXO**: ±0.5 ppm; **OCXO**: ±0.01 ppm.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
- Fractional drift: $\frac{\Delta f}{f_0} = \alpha_f \cdot \Delta T$.
- Time error per second: $TIE_{rate} = \frac{\Delta f}{f_0} \cdot t$ (for $t$ in seconds).
- Accumulated wander over interval $\tau$: $TIE(\tau) = \alpha_f \cdot \Delta T \cdot \tau$.

**Criticality for SyncE/PTP (1 mark):**
- SyncE (ITU-T G.8261) and PTP (IEEE 1588 / ITU-T G.8275) require **frequency accuracy** at the stratum clock level.
- A 30 ppm drift in 10 GbE corresponds to **~4.6 kHz** of timing error per clock cycle — catastrophic for **time-sensitive financial** and **power-grid** networks.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Step 1: Maximum fractional drift.**

$$\frac{\Delta f}{f_0} = \alpha_f \times \Delta T = 30 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2: Absolute frequency deviation.**

$$\Delta f = f_0 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 156.25 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 234{,}375 \, \text{Hz} \approx 234.4 \, \text{kHz}$$

**Step 3: Accumulated time error over 6 hours.**

$$T_{window} = 6 \times 3600 = 21{,}600 \, \text{s}$$

$$TIE = \frac{\Delta f}{f_0} \times T_{window} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 21{,}600 = 32.4 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 4: Compliance check for G.8273.2 Class C.**

Wander rate requirement: **< 150 ns/s** = **150 × 10⁻⁹ s/s**.

Observed wander rate: $\frac{TIE}{T_{window}} = \frac{32.4}{21{,}600} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s/s} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{ns/s}$

**Comparison:**

$$1.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{ns/s} \gg 150 \, \text{ns/s} \quad (\text{10,000× worse})$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\Delta f = 234.4 \, \text{kHz} \quad ; \quad TIE = 32.4 \, \text{s over 6 hours} \quad ; \quad \text{NOT acceptable for G.8273.2.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Most common mistake:** Students drop the **± sign** in α_f. Always use the *magnitude* and state that drift can be in either direction.
> - **Unit mismatch:** Confusing **ppm** with **%** (1 % = 10,000 ppm). A 1 % coefficient is **NOT** the same as 1 ppm.
> - **Time error vs. phase error:** Wander is a **time-domain** measure. Don't compute phase — that's a different KTU module topic.
> - **Forgot to multiply by t:** A very common 2-mark deduction. Always multiply the drift rate by the observation window.
> - **Reference temperature:** The KTU may give ΔT directly or give T_max and T_min. If the latter, **compute ΔT = T_max − T_min first**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Thermal Drift (Module 1, PCERT403)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Temperature-induced parameter change in network hardware.
- ✅ **Four types:** Attenuation, Power, Frequency, Impedance drift.
- ✅ **Reference temps:** **20 °C** (cabling), **25 °C** (electronics).
- ✅ **Copper α_Cu = 0.00393 /°C** — resistance always **rises** with T.
- ✅ **Optical fiber:** Attenuation rises ≈ **0.2 %/°C** with T.
- ✅ **Frequency drift:** Specified in **ppm/°C**; wander accumulates linearly with time.
- ✅ **Thermal noise floor:** **−174 dBm/Hz** at 300 K (use for noise-limited link budget).
- ✅ **Compensation methods:** TEC, AGC, TCXO/OCXO, adaptive equalization, GPS disciplining.
- ✅ **KTU formula highlights:**
  - $R(T) = R_0[1 + \alpha \Delta T]$
  - $P_{margin} = P_{tx} - P_{rx(min)} - A_{total} - \Delta_{drift}$
  - $TIE = \alpha_f \cdot \Delta T \cdot \tau$
  - $N_{dBm} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B)$
- ✅ **Always include units** in the final answer (dB, Hz, ppm, °C, ns/s).
- ✅ **Always state worst-case assumptions** explicitly (max ΔT, end-of-life, maximum cable length).
- ✅ **Cross-check sign** — negative margins mean link failure, positive margins mean viable link.
- ✅ **Exam tip:** If a question gives "T_max = 70 °C" and "T_min = 0 °C", compute **ΔT = 70 °C**, **NOT** 70/2 = 35 °C.
- ✅ **Real-world relevance:** DWDM, 5G, data centers, submarine cables, industrial Ethernet, SyncE/PTP.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Thermal Drift**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMRR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CMRR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is a fundamental figure-of-merit used to quantify the ability of a differential amplifier (typically an operational amplifier) to reject **common-mode signals** (signals that appear simultaneously and in-phase on both input terminals) while amplifying the desired **differential-mode signal** (the voltage difference between the two input terminals).

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, CMRR is defined as the ratio of the **differential-mode gain ($A_d$)** to the **common-mode gain ($A_c$)** of the amplifier.

$$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$

When expressed in decibels (dB), which is the standard convention in datasheets and KTU numerical problems, the formula becomes:

$$\text{CMRR}_{(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert \;\; \text{dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** CMRR is universally expressed in **decibels (dB)** in all board examination numerical problems. Always remember to convert the dimensionless ratio into dB using the **$20 \log_{10}$** formula (not $10 \log_{10}$, which is reserved for power quantities).

## 1.2 The Differential vs. Common-Mode Concept

To understand CMRR, we must first understand the two types of input signals an op-amp receives:

### A. Differential-Mode Signal ($V_d$)
This is the *desired* signal — the voltage difference between the two input terminals $V_1$ and $V_2$.

$$V_d = V_1 - V_2$$

### B. Common-Mode Signal ($V_c$)
This is the *unwanted* signal — the average voltage appearing identically on both input terminals (often noise, interference, or a DC offset picked up from the environment).

$$V_c = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Friends in a Noisy Room"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine you are sitting across from a friend in a crowded, noisy cafeteria. Two microphones are placed on the table — one near you, one near your friend. The **differential signal** is your friend's voice (captured differently by each mic). The **common-mode signal** is the background chatter of the cafeteria (captured almost identically by both mics).
>
> An ideal amplifier should act like a *smart headphone system* that **amplifies your friend's voice** (differential) while **canceling out the cafeteria noise** (common-mode). The *degree* to which the amplifier successfully cancels the noise is precisely what **CMRR measures**. A higher CMRR = better noise rejection = clearer signal.

## 1.4 Why CMRR Matters in Computer Networks & Data Communication

Although CMRR is fundamentally an *analog electronics* concept, it has direct, critical relevance to Computer Networks and signal integrity:

- **Differential Signaling Standards:** Networking protocols such as **RS-485, USB, HDMI, Ethernet (1000BASE-T)**, and **CAN bus** all use differential pairs to transmit data. The receiver op-amps in these systems rely on high CMRR to reject common-mode noise (e.g., ground loops, EMI from motors, lightning-induced surges).
- **Long-Distance Communication:** Twisted-pair cables pick up identical noise on both wires; the receiver's CMRR is what allows the original data to be recovered.
- **ADC Front-Ends:** Network interface cards (NICs) use op-amps with high CMRR to condition analog signals before digitization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (Industry Benchmarks):**
> - **General-purpose op-amp (e.g., 741):** CMRR ≈ **70 dB to 90 dB**
> - **Precision op-amp (e.g., OP-07, INA128):** CMRR ≈ **100 dB to 120 dB**
> - **Instrumentation amplifier:** CMRR can exceed **130 dB**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The internal behavior of a real (non-ideal) op-amp can be decomposed into two simultaneous operations:

**Step 1 — Differential Amplification:**
The op-amp multiplies the differential input voltage $V_d$ by the differential gain $A_d$. This is the *useful* operation.

$$V_{out,d} = A_d \cdot V_d = A_d(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 2 — Common-Mode Amplification (Undesired):**
Simultaneously, the op-amp also multiplies the common-mode input voltage $V_c$ by the common-mode gain $A_c$. In an ideal op-amp, $A_c = 0$, meaning common-mode signals are perfectly rejected.

$$V_{out,c} = A_c \cdot V_c = A_c \cdot \left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Superposition (Total Output):**
By the principle of superposition (linear systems theory), the total output voltage is the algebraic sum of the two contributions:

$$V_{out} = A_d \cdot (V_1 - V_2) + A_c \cdot \left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Definition of CMRR (Ratio Form):**
The effectiveness of common-mode rejection is the *ratio* of the desired gain to the undesired gain.

$$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$

**Step 5 — Conversion to Decibels:**
Because the ratio can span many orders of magnitude (e.g., $10^4$ to $10^6$), it is expressed in decibels using the base-10 logarithm, scaled by 20 (since these are voltage gains, not power).

$$\text{CMRR}_{(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert \;\; \text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $20 \log_{10}$ and not $10 \log_{10}$?**
> The decibel for voltage/power quantities uses $20 \log_{10}$ because power is proportional to voltage squared ($P \propto V^2$). CMRR is fundamentally a *voltage gain ratio*, so the voltage formula applies. Mixing the two is a common KTU exam pitfall.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ | Differential-mode input voltage | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_c = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ | Common-mode input voltage | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $V_{out} = A_d V_d + A_c V_c$ | Total output (superposition) | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$ | Common-mode rejection ratio (dimensionless) | Unitless |
| 5 | $\text{CMRR}_{(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$ | CMRR expressed in decibels | dB |
| 6 | $A_c = \dfrac{A_d}{\text{CMRR (ratio)}}$ | Common-mode gain in terms of CMRR | Unitless |
| 7 | $\% \text{ Rejection} = \dfrac{1}{\text{CMRR (ratio)}} \times 100$ | Percentage of common-mode signal rejected | Percent |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Instrumentation:** ECG and EEG signals are microvolts buried in 50/60 Hz mains hum — high CMRR is *essential*.
- **Industrial Networking (RS-485):** Long cables in factory floors pick up motor noise; RS-485 receivers demand CMRR > **70 dB**.
- **Audio Engineering:** Professional microphone pre-amps use instrumentation amplifiers with CMRR > 90 dB to eliminate ground loops.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** MIL-grade instrumentation requires CMRR > **120 dB** to function in high-EMI environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage in Terms of $V_1$ and $V_2$

Starting from the input signals applied to the op-amp's two terminals:

$$V_1 = V_c + \frac{V_d}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad V_2 = V_c - \frac{V_d}{2}$$

**Step 1 — Express $V_d$ and $V_c$ directly:**

$$V_d = V_1 - V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_d = \left(V_c + \frac{V_d}{2}\right) - \left(V_c - \frac{V_d}{2}\right) = V_d \;\checkmark$$

$$V_c = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_c = \frac{\left(V_c + \frac{V_d}{2}\right) + \left(V_c - \frac{V_d}{2}\right)}{2} = V_c \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Apply the superposition output equation:**

$$V_{out} = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_c \left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)$$

This is the canonical KTU exam form of the equation. No further algebraic simplification is generally required; this is the final symbolic result students must commit to memory.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Computing CMRR in dB (Board-Standard Problem)

> **Problem:** A differential amplifier has a differential-mode gain of $A_d = 10{,}000$ and a common-mode gain of $A_c = 0.5$. Calculate (a) the CMRR as a dimensionless ratio, and (b) the CMRR in decibels.

### Part (a) — Dimensionless Ratio

$$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert = \frac{10{,}000}{0.5} = 20{,}000$$

**Result:** $\text{CMRR} = 20{,}000$ (dimensionless)

### Part (b) — Conversion to Decibels

$$\text{CMRR}_{(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10}(20{,}000)$$

Breaking the logarithm into manageable parts:

$$20 \log_{10}(20{,}000) = 20 \log_{10}(2 \times 10^4)$$

Using the product rule of logarithms, $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$:

$$= 20 \left[\log_{10}(2) + \log_{10}(10^4)\right]$$

$$= 20 \left[0.3010 + 4\right]$$

$$= 20 \times 4.3010$$

$$= 86.02 \;\; \text{dB}$$

**Result:** $\text{CMRR}_{(\text{dB})} \approx 86.02 \;\; \text{dB}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always show the intermediate $\log_{10}$ calculation steps. Writing only the final answer (86.02 dB) without showing $20 \log_{10}(20{,}000) = 20 \times 4.301$ forfeits at least 1 mark.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Reverse Engineering the Common-Mode Gain

> **Problem:** An op-amp datasheet specifies a differential gain of $A_d = 200{,}000$ and a CMRR of **100 dB**. Find the common-mode gain $A_c$.

**Step 1 — Convert dB to ratio:**

$$\text{CMRR}_{(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$

$$100 = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$

$$\frac{100}{20} = \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$

$$5 = \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$

**Step 2 — Take the antilog (base 10):**

$$\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert = 10^5 = 100{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $A_c$:**

$$A_c = \frac{A_d}{100{,}000} = \frac{200{,}000}{100{,}000} = 2$$

**Result:** $A_c = 2$ (dimensionless)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A differential gain of 200,000 paired with a common-mode gain of just 2 means the amplifier amplifies the *signal* 200,000 times but only amplifies the *noise* 2 times — a remarkable rejection capability. This is the engineering value of high CMRR.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Percentage Rejection Calculation

> **Problem:** If the CMRR (ratio) of an op-amp is 50,000, what percentage of the common-mode signal is rejected by the amplifier?

**Step 1 — Apply the rejection formula:**

$$\% \text{ Rejection} = \frac{1}{\text{CMRR (ratio)}} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{1}{50{,}000} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{100}{50{,}000}$$

$$= 0.002 \;\%$$

**Result:** Only $0.002 \;\%$ of the common-mode signal *passes through* the amplifier; equivalently, the amplifier **rejects $99.998 \;\%$** of the common-mode noise.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Ideal Value | Real Op-Amp (741) | Precision (OP-07) |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Differential Gain | $A_d$ | $\infty$ | $\sim 200{,}000$ | $\sim 200{,}000$ |
| Common-Mode Gain | $A_c$ | $0$ | $\sim 2$ | $\sim 0.0002$ |
| CMRR (dB) | — | $\infty$ | $\sim 90$ dB | $\sim 120$ dB |
| Common-Mode Rejection | — | $100 \;\%$ | $99.9999 \;\%$ | $99.99999998 \;\%$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Op-Amp's CMRR Behavior

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the internal signal-flow architecture of a real op-amp, showing how the differential and common-mode signals are processed independently before being combined at the output node. This is the conceptual model that gives rise to the CMRR metric.

```mermaid
graph TD
    NODEIN1[Input Terminal V1] --> NODEDECOMP[Signal Decomposition Stage]
    NODEIN2[Input Terminal V2] --> NODEDECOMP
    NODEDECOMP --> NODEDIFF[Differential Mode Branch Vd equals V1 minus V2]
    NODEDECOMP --> NODECOMM[Common Mode Branch Vc equals V1 plus V2 divided by 2]
    NODEDIFF --> NODEDIFFGAIN[Differential Gain Ad multiplication]
    NODECOMM --> NODECOMMGAIN[Common Mode Gain Ac multiplication]
    NODEDIFFGAIN --> NODESUM[Superposition Summation Node]
    NODECOMMGAIN --> NODESUM
    NODESUM --> NODEOUT[Output Vout equals Ad Vd plus Ac Vc]
    NODEOUT --> NODECMRR[CMRR Definition equals 20 log Ad over Ac in dB]
    NODECMRR --> NODERESULT[Higher CMRR equals Better Noise Rejection]

    style NODEDECOMP fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px
    style NODEDIFF fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px
    style NODECOMM fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px
    style NODESUM fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px
    style NODECMRR fill:#EDE9FE,stroke:#6D28D9,stroke-width:2px
    style NODERESULT fill:#F0FDF4,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Application in Differential Network Receivers

The second diagram maps how CMRR is leveraged in a real-world **differential data receiver** (e.g., the front-end of an RS-485 or Ethernet PHY receiver), which is a key application domain in computer networks.

```mermaid
graph LR
    NOISESRC[External Noise Source EMI Ground Loop] --> NOISECAPT[Common Mode Noise Induced on Both Lines]
    NOISECAPT --> LINEA[Twisted Pair Line A]
    NOISECAPT --> LINEB[Twisted Pair Line B]
    LINEA --> RXAMP[Differential Receiver Op-Amp with High CMRR]
    LINEB --> RXAMP
    RXAMP --> AMPCMRR[CMRR greater than 70 dB]
    AMPCMRR --> CLEANOUT[Clean Differential Data Output]
    AMPCMRR --> REJEC[Common Mode Noise Rejected to Ground]

    style NOISESRC fill:#FECACA,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:2px
    style RXAMP fill:#BFDBFE,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:2px
    style AMPCMRR fill:#DDD6FE,stroke:#5B21B6,stroke-width:2px
    style CLEANOUT fill:#BBF7D0,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px
    style REJEC fill:#FECACA,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Diagram Interpretation Guide

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the architecture for exam answers:**
> 1. The **decomposition stage** splits $V_1$ and $V_2$ into their $V_d$ and $V_c$ components — this is the mathematical basis of the KTU formula.
> 2. The two branches represent the *parallel* gain operations; the **differential branch** is desired, the **common-mode branch** is undesired.
> 3. The **superposition summation node** is where the output $V_{out}$ is formed — this corresponds to the KTU equation $V_{out} = A_d V_d + A_c V_c$.
> 4. The **CMRR Definition block** is the *ratio* metric that quantifies how much better the differential branch is at amplifying than the common-mode branch.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark Conceptual / 3-Mark Short Answer)

---

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of a differential amplifier. Why is a high CMRR desirable in receiver circuits used in computer networks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** CMRR is defined as the ratio of the differential-mode gain ($A_d$) to the common-mode gain ($A_c$) of a differential amplifier. It is expressed in decibels as:

$$\text{CMRR}_{(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert \;\; \text{dB}$$

**Significance in Networking (1 mark):** A high CMRR is desirable in network receiver circuits (e.g., RS-485, Ethernet) because the long twisted-pair cables used in networking pick up identical common-mode noise (EMI, ground loops) on both wires. A high-CMRR receiver amplifies the differential data signal while rejecting the common-mode noise, ensuring **bit-error-free communication** over long distances.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Distinguish between common-mode and differential-mode signals with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Differential-Mode Signal | Common-Mode Signal |
|--------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Voltage difference between the two input terminals: $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ | Average voltage appearing equally on both inputs: $V_c = (V_1 + V_2)/2$ |
| **Nature** | Desired information-bearing signal | Unwanted noise/interference |
| **Example (Networking)** | Data transmitted over a twisted pair (one wire carries $V$, the other carries $-V$) | 50 Hz mains hum picked up equally by both wires of an Ethernet cable |
| **Rejection by op-amp** | Amplified by $A_d$ | Ideally rejected (multiplied by $A_c \approx 0$) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B — Option 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### **(a) Derive the expression for the output voltage of a practical differential amplifier in terms of differential-mode and common-mode gains. Define CMRR and express it in dB.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the two input voltages in terms of $V_d$ and $V_c$:**

$$V_1 = V_c + \frac{V_d}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad V_2 = V_c - \frac{V_d}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Express $V_d$ and $V_c$ in terms of $V_1$ and $V_2$:**

$$V_d = V_1 - V_2 \quad \text{and} \quad V_c = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the principle of superposition.** The total output is the sum of the differential-mode output and the common-mode output:

$$V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_d + A_c \cdot V_c$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions for $V_d$ and $V_c$:**

$$V_{out} = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_c \left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)$$

This is the required output expression of a practical differential amplifier.

**Step 5 — Definition of CMRR (1 mark):**

$$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$

**Step 6 — Express in decibels (1 mark):**

$$\text{CMRR}_{(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert \;\; \text{dB}$$

#### **(b) A differential amplifier has $A_d = 5000$ and $A_c = 0.1$. Calculate (i) CMRR in dB, (ii) the percentage of common-mode signal rejected.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — CMRR in dB:**

$$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert = \frac{5000}{0.1} = 50{,}000$$

$$\text{CMRR}_{(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10}(50{,}000)$$

$$= 20 \log_{10}(5 \times 10^4) = 20 \left[\log_{10} 5 + 4\right]$$

$$= 20 \left[0.6990 + 4\right] = 20 \times 4.6990 = 93.98 \;\; \text{dB}$$

**Part (ii) — Percentage of common-mode signal rejected:**

The fraction of the common-mode signal that **passes through** the amplifier is:

$$\text{Fraction passing} = \frac{1}{\text{CMRR (ratio)}} = \frac{1}{50{,}000} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$$

Converting to percentage:

$$\text{Passing} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 100 = 0.002 \;\%$$

Therefore, the percentage **rejected** is:

$$\% \text{ Rejected} = 100 - 0.002 = 99.998 \;\%$$

**Final Answer:** CMRR = **93.98 dB**, Common-mode rejection = **99.998 %**

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### **(a) Explain the concepts of common-mode and differential-mode signals in detail. With a neat block diagram, describe how an op-amp rejects common-mode signals. Mention the role of CMRR in RS-485 networking.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Common-Mode Signal (2 marks):**
A common-mode signal $V_c = (V_1 + V_2)/2$ is a voltage that appears identically (in amplitude and phase) on both input terminals of a differential amplifier. It is generally an *unwanted* signal arising from external interference, ground potential differences, or electromagnetic coupling. In networking, when an RS-485 cable runs near a motor or fluorescent lamp, both wires pick up the same noise voltage — this is a common-mode signal.

**Concept of Differential-Mode Signal (2 marks):**
A differential-mode signal $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ is the voltage *difference* between the two input terminals. In differential data transmission (RS-485, USB, Ethernet), the transmitter deliberately sends equal-and-opposite voltages on the two wires, so $V_d$ is twice the signal amplitude while $V_c$ remains near zero. The differential signal is the *information carrier*.

**Block Diagram and Rejection Mechanism (2 marks):**
[Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1] The op-amp internally contains a *difference amplifier* topology that mathematically computes $V_1 - V_2$. Any signal that is identical on both inputs (the common-mode component) is canceled at the subtraction node, while only the differential component survives to be amplified by $A_d$. The residual common-mode gain $A_c$ (non-zero in real op-amps) is what limits the rejection — quantified by CMRR.

**Role in RS-485 (1 mark):**
RS-485 is the workhorse industrial networking standard (used in factory automation, building management, and fieldbus systems). It operates over twisted-pair cables up to 1200 m, in electrically noisy environments. RS-485 receivers must have CMRR > **70 dB** to reliably reject common-mode noise that can reach ±7 V on long cables, ensuring the differential data signal ($V_d$) is recovered with high fidelity.

#### **(b) An instrumentation amplifier used in a network analyzer has $A_d = 10^6$ and a CMRR of 110 dB. Determine the common-mode gain $A_c$. If a 5 V common-mode noise is present, what is the noise voltage at the output due to the common-mode signal alone?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Find $A_c$:**

Convert CMRR from dB to a dimensionless ratio:

$$110 = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$

$$\frac{110}{20} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$

$$5.5 = \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$

$$\frac{A_d}{A_c} = 10^{5.5} = 10^{0.5} \times 10^5 = 3.1623 \times 10^5 = 316{,}228$$

$$A_c = \frac{A_d}{316{,}228} = \frac{10^6}{3.1623 \times 10^5} = 3.162$$

**Part 2 — Output noise voltage due to common-mode signal:**

$$V_{out,c} = A_c \times V_c = 3.162 \times 5 \;\; \text{V}$$

$$V_{out,c} = 15.81 \;\; \text{V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Formula Error Trap:** Many students write $10 \log_{10}$ instead of $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage CMRR — this *halves* the resulting dB value. Always use $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage-gain ratios.
> 2. **Antilog Error:** When converting dB to ratio, the antilog is $10^{(x/20)}$, not $10^x$. E.g., $110 \text{ dB} \rightarrow 10^{5.5}$, NOT $10^{110}$.
> 3. **Forgetting Units:** $A_d$ and $A_c$ are *dimensionless* voltage gains. CMRR is *dimensionless*, but CMRR in dB has units of dB. Mark deduction is strict on this distinction.
> 4. **Confusing CMRR with PSRR:** Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR) uses the *same* dB formula but applies to power-supply ripple, not input common-mode noise. Do not interchange the two.
> 5. **Sign Convention:** CMRR is always stated as a *positive* dB value; the absolute value $\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$ is implicit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **CMRR** is the ratio of differential-mode gain $A_d$ to common-mode gain $A_c$; it quantifies an op-amp's ability to reject unwanted in-phase noise.
- **Differential signal:** $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ *(desired, amplified)*.
- **Common-mode signal:** $V_c = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ *(undesired, ideally rejected)*.
- **Output equation (superposition):** $V_{out} = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_c \left(\dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)$.
- **CMRR (dimensionless):** $\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$.
- **CMRR (in dB):** $\text{CMRR}_{(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$ — *always* $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage ratios.
- **Higher CMRR = better noise rejection** — ideal is $\infty$ dB; practical op-amps span 70 dB (741) to >120 dB (precision/instrumentation).
- **% Rejection** $= \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\text{CMRR}_{\text{ratio}}}\right) \times 100 \;\approx 100 \;\%$ for high CMRR.
- **Networking relevance:** Critical for **RS-485, USB, Ethernet, CAN bus, HDMI** — all differential-signaling standards that rely on receiver CMRR to reject EMI over long cables.
- **Reverse calculation:** Given dB, find ratio via $10^{\text{dB}/20}$; then solve for unknown $A_d$ or $A_c$.
- **Common exam trap:** Mixing up $10 \log_{10}$ (power) with $20 \log_{10}$ (voltage). For CMRR, **always** use $20 \log_{10}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PSRR, AC Characteristics, Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PSRR, AC Characteristics, Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR) is the ratio of the change in the power supply voltage ($V_{S}$ or $V_{CC}/V_{EE}$) to the equivalent change it produces in the input offset voltage ($V_{IO}$) of the amplifier, when all other operating conditions are held constant. It is a direct measure of the amplifier's ability to reject noise, ripple, and unwanted fluctuations originating from the DC power rails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Definition (memorize verbatim):**
> *"PSRR is defined as the ratio of the change in supply voltage to the change in input offset voltage it produces, expressed in decibels (dB)."*

$$PSRR = \left\vert \frac{\Delta V_{S}}{\Delta V_{IO}} \right\vert \quad \text{or} \quad PSRR(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{\Delta V_{S}}{\Delta V_{IO}} \right\vert$$

**Key Units:** Dimensionless ratio, expressed in **decibels (dB)**.
**Typical Values for Op-Amps:** **80 dB to 120 dB** (e.g., µA741 ≈ 90 dB).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are trying to listen to a soft whisper (your tiny input signal) inside a noisy factory. A powerful air conditioner (the power supply) is humming loudly in the background. PSRR tells you how well your "ear" (the amplifier) can ignore that air conditioner hum. A high PSRR means even if the AC unit surges or dips, you still hear the whisper cleanly. A low PSRR means the surge in the AC unit will be heard as a loud, distracting "whoosh" right on top of the whisper.

**Dual-Supply vs Single-Supply Notation:**

| Notation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| **PSRR+** | Rejection of **positive** rail ($+V_{CC}$) | Bipolar op-amps |
| **PSRR–** | Rejection of **negative** rail ($-V_{EE}$) | Bipolar op-amps |
| **PSRR** (general) | Combined rejection for both rails | Modern CMOS op-amps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverse Relationship – Power Supply Sensitivity (PSS):**
> PSS is simply the reciprocal of PSRR. Some textbooks (Roy Choudhury, Bakshi) use PSS instead of PSRR. They mean the same physical phenomenon, just inverted.

---

## 1.2 AC Characteristics of an Amplifier

**Formal Definition:**
AC characteristics describe the behaviour of the amplifier when the input signal is time-varying (sinusoidal, square, triangular, etc.). They quantify how faithfully the circuit amplifies signals of different frequencies, speeds, and amplitudes, as opposed to DC characteristics (input bias current, input offset voltage) which describe behaviour at **0 Hz**.

**Core AC Characteristics (Master List):**

1. **Frequency Response** – Gain versus frequency behaviour.
2. **Bandwidth (BW)** – Range of usable frequencies.
3. **Slew Rate (SR)** – Maximum rate of change of output voltage per microsecond.
4. **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW / GBP)** – Constant product of gain and bandwidth.
5. **Rise Time ($t_r$)** – Time for output to swing from **10%** to **90%** of its final value.
6. **Settling Time ($t_s$)** – Time for output to settle within a specified error band (e.g., **0.1%** or **0.01%**) of the final value.
7. **Phase Margin (PM)** – Safety margin against oscillation.
8. **Full Power Bandwidth** – Maximum usable frequency at full output swing.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of an amplifier as a **musical speaker**. Its AC characteristics tell you:
- How high and how low it can play (frequency response).
- How loud it can go before distorting (slew rate / full power BW).
- How fast the volume knob can be turned up (rise time).
- Whether it will squeal when you turn up the volume too much (phase margin).

---

## 1.3 Frequency Response – The Heart of AC Behaviour

**Formal Definition:**
The **frequency response** of an amplifier is the variation of its magnitude of gain $\vert A_v(f) \vert$ and phase shift $\phi(f)$ as a function of the input signal frequency $f$. It is graphically represented by a **Bode plot** (gain in dB vs. log frequency).

$$A_v(f) = \vert A_v(f) \vert \angle \phi(f)$$

**Two Key Plots:**
- **Magnitude Plot:** $\vert A_v \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v(f) \vert$ vs $\log f$.
- **Phase Plot:** Phase angle $\phi$ vs $\log f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Frequency Bands:**
> 1. **Midband (Flat Band):** Gain is constant at the maximum value $A_{mid}$. No reactive effect.
> 2. **Low-Cutoff Region:** Gain falls at **+20 dB/decade** due to input coupling/bypass capacitors.
> 3. **High-Cutoff Region:** Gain falls at **−20 dB/decade per pole** due to internal transistor capacitances.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a long flexible fishing rod. You can shake it slowly (low frequency) and the tip moves the same as your hand. You can shake it at medium speed (midband) — still matches. But shake it too fast (high frequency) and the tip lags, wobbles, and barely moves. The "cutoff" is the speed beyond which the rod stops faithfully tracking your hand. The "midband" is the comfortable working range. The "roll-off" is the sluggishness at high speeds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Analysis of PSRR

### 2.1.1 Why PSRR is Frequency Dependent

A real op-amp's PSRR is **not a single number** — it is a function of frequency. At DC, PSRR is maximum (e.g., 120 dB). As frequency increases, PSRR degrades (drops) because:
- The internal transistors have parasitic capacitances.
- The power supply rejection path is itself a low-pass filter.
- High-frequency noise on the supply rail couples directly to the output via parasitic capacitances.

The typical model is:

$$PSRR(f) = PSRR_{DC} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{f}{f_{PSRR}}\right)^2}$$

where $f_{PSRR}$ is the **PSRR corner frequency** (typically 100 Hz to 10 kHz for op-amps).

### 2.1.2 Causes of Power Supply Noise

1. **Ripple** from the rectifier-filter power supply (e.g., 100 Hz / 120 Hz).
2. **Spikes** from digital switching circuits sharing the same supply.
3. **Load transients** when output stage draws variable current.
4. **EMI pickup** on long supply traces.

### 2.1.3 Improving PSRR at the Board Level
- Use **decoupling capacitors** (0.1 µF ceramic + 10 µF tantalum) close to op-amp supply pins.
- Use **separate analog and digital grounds** (star-ground topology).
- Use **linear voltage regulators** instead of raw DC for sensitive stages.
- Choose op-amps with higher intrinsic PSRR (e.g., OPA227, LT1012).

---

## 2.2 Deep Analysis of AC Characteristics

### 2.2.1 Frequency Response Derivation – Single-Pole Model

The internal gain of an op-amp contains a dominant pole. The open-loop gain as a function of frequency is:

$$A(f) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j \left(\frac{f}{f_1}\right)}$$

- $A_0$ = **DC open-loop gain** (typically $2 \times 10^5$ for µA741).
- $f_1$ = **dominant pole frequency** (typically 5 Hz for µA741).

The **magnitude** is:

$$\vert A(f) \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{f}{f_1}\right)^2}}$$

And the **phase** is:

$$\phi(f) = -\arctan\left(\frac{f}{f_1}\right)$$

### 2.2.2 Slew Rate (SR) – The Speed Limit

**Definition:** The maximum rate of change of output voltage when a step input is applied.

$$SR = \left. \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} \right\vert_{max} \quad \text{[in V/µs]}$$

**For µA741:** $SR = 0.5 \text{ V/µs}$.
**For high-speed op-amps (e.g., LM318):** $SR = 70 \text{ V/µs}$.

**Physical Cause:** The internal **compensation capacitor** $C_C$ (typically 30 pF in µA741) must be charged by the input stage's bias current $I$. The maximum current is limited, hence:

$$SR = \frac{I_{max}}{C_C}$$

### 2.2.3 Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)

The single most important AC parameter for closed-loop design:

$$GBW = A_{CL} \times f_{BW(CL)} = \text{constant} = A_0 \times f_1$$
