This is why op-amp data sheets quote "GBW = 1 MHz" — at any closed-loop gain, the achievable bandwidth can be calculated as:

$$f_{BW(CL)} = \frac{GBW}{A_{CL}}$$

### 2.2.4 Rise Time and Bandwidth Relationship

For a single-pole (RC) system:

$$t_r = \frac{0.35}{f_H} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad f_H = \frac{0.35}{t_r}$$

This is the famous "0.35 rule" – comes from the 10% to 90% step response of an RC low-pass filter.

### 2.2.5 Full-Power Bandwidth

The maximum frequency at which the op-amp can deliver its **rated output swing** $V_{om}$ without slew-rate limiting:

$$f_{FP} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{om}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Trap:** If the input frequency is higher than $f_{FP}$, the output becomes a **triangle wave** instead of a sine wave — even though the amplitude is below the supply rails! This is called **slew-rate induced distortion**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Value (µA741) |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | PSRR (DC) | $\vert \Delta V_S / \Delta V_{IO} \vert$ in dB | dB | **90 dB** |
| 2 | PSRR(f) | $PSRR_{DC} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (f/f_{PSRR})^2}$ | dB | — |
| 3 | Open-loop gain (DC) | $A_0$ | V/V | $2 \times 10^5$ |
| 4 | Dominant pole freq. | $f_1$ | Hz | **5 Hz** |
| 5 | Gain at frequency f | $A(f) = A_0 / \sqrt{1 + (f/f_1)^2}$ | V/V | — |
| 6 | GBW | $A_0 \times f_1 = A_{CL} \times f_{BW}$ | Hz | **1 MHz** |
| 7 | Closed-loop BW | $f_{BW} = GBW / A_{CL}$ | Hz | depends on $A_{CL}$ |
| 8 | Slew Rate | $SR = dV_{out}/dt \vert_{max}$ | V/µs | **0.5 V/µs** |
| 9 | Full-Power BW | $f_{FP} = SR / (2\pi V_{om})$ | Hz | depends on $V_{om}$ |
| 10 | Rise Time | $t_r = 0.35 / f_H$ | s | depends on $f_H$ |
| 11 | Phase at $f_1$ | $\phi = -45°$ | degrees | — |
| 12 | Phase at $10 f_1$ | $\phi \approx -84°$ | degrees | — |
| 13 | Roll-off slope | $\pm 20 \text{ dB/decade}$ per pole | dB/dec | — |
| 14 | Phase margin | $PM = 180° + \phi(f_{unity})$ | degrees | typically **45° to 60°** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule:** Notice that all ratios above use `\vert` (LaTeX absolute-value bars), **NOT** the markdown pipe `|`. This is mandatory to avoid breaking the table.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of PSRR / AC Characteristics |
|-------|------------------------------------------|
| **Audio amplifiers** | PSRR determines hum rejection (50/60 Hz) |
| **Instrumentation** | PSRR is critical for sensor signal conditioning |
| **ADC front-ends** | GBW must exceed sampling rate to avoid aliasing |
| **Active filters** | GBW sets the upper limit of filter design |
| **Oscilloscope probes** | SR determines accuracy of fast edges |
| **Communication receivers** | PSRR affects adjacent-channel rejection |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PSRR as a Function of Frequency

**Step 1 – Start with the DC definition:**
$$PSRR_{DC} = \left\vert \frac{\Delta V_S}{\Delta V_{IO}} \right\vert$$

**Step 2 – Recognize that PSRR degrades with frequency due to the power-supply rejection path behaving as a low-pass filter with corner $f_{PSRR}$:**

$$\frac{PSRR(f)}{PSRR_{DC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{PSRR})^2}}$$

**Step 3 – Solve for PSRR(f):**

$$\boxed{PSRR(f) = \frac{PSRR_{DC}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{PSRR})^2}}}$$

**Step 4 – Express in decibels:**

$$PSRR_{dB}(f) = 20 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{PSRR_{DC}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{PSRR})^2}} \right]$$

**Step 5 – At $f = f_{PSRR}$:**

$$PSRR(f_{PSRR}) = \frac{PSRR_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \cdot PSRR_{DC}$$

**In decibels:** $-3 \text{ dB}$ drop from DC value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW) Invariance

**Step 1 – Midband gain equation:**

$$A(f) = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_1)^2}}$$

**Step 2 – Define the unity-gain frequency $f_T$ (where gain = 1):**

$$1 = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f_T/f_1)^2}}$$

**Step 3 – Since $A_0 \gg 1$ and $f_T \gg f_1$, the "1" inside the square root is negligible:**

$$1 \approx \frac{A_0}{f_T/f_1}$$

**Step 4 – Rearrange:**

$$f_T = A_0 \cdot f_1$$

**Step 5 – Conclude that the GBW is a constant equal to the unity-gain frequency:**

$$\boxed{GBW = A_0 \cdot f_1 = f_T}$$

**Step 6 – Generalize to any closed-loop gain $A_{CL}$:**

$$A_{CL} \cdot f_{BW(CL)} = GBW = f_T$$

Therefore:

$$f_{BW(CL)} = \frac{GBW}{A_{CL}} = \frac{1 \text{ MHz}}{A_{CL}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Slew Rate and Full-Power Bandwidth

**Step 1 – Assume output is $v_{out}(t) = V_{om} \sin(2\pi f t)$.**

**Step 2 – Maximum rate of change occurs at zero crossings (when $t = 0$):**

$$\left. \frac{dv_{out}}{dt} \right\vert_{max} = 2\pi f \cdot V_{om}$$

**Step 3 – By definition, this maximum must equal the slew rate:**

$$SR = 2\pi f \cdot V_{om}$$

**Step 4 – Solve for the maximum usable frequency (full-power bandwidth):**

$$\boxed{f_{FP} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{om}}}$$

**Step 5 – Numerical example for µA741 with $V_{om} = 10$ V:**

$$f_{FP} = \frac{0.5 \text{ V/µs}}{2\pi \times 10 \text{ V}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{62.83} \approx 7.96 \text{ kHz}$$

**Conclusion:** Even though µA741's GBW is 1 MHz, its full-power bandwidth is only ~8 kHz at 10 V output — a **frequently tested trap** in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Rise Time from Frequency Response

**Step 1 – For a single-pole system, the transfer function is:**

$$H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + s/\omega_H}$$

where $\omega_H = 2\pi f_H$.

**Step 2 – Step response in time domain:**

$$v_{out}(t) = V_{final} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), \quad \tau = 1/\omega_H$$

**Step 3 – Find time to reach 10%:**

$$0.1 = 1 - e^{-t_1/\tau} \Rightarrow t_1 = -\tau \ln(0.9) = 0.1054 \tau$$

**Step 4 – Find time to reach 90%:**

$$0.9 = 1 - e^{-t_2/\tau} \Rightarrow t_2 = -\tau \ln(0.1) = 2.3026 \tau$$

**Step 5 – Rise time:**

$$t_r = t_2 - t_1 = 2.1972 \tau$$

**Step 6 – Substitute $\tau = 1/(2\pi f_H)$:**

$$t_r = \frac{2.1972}{2\pi f_H} = \frac{0.3499}{f_H}$$

$$\boxed{t_r \approx \frac{0.35}{f_H}}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example – KTU Style

**Problem:** An op-amp has $A_0 = 2 \times 10^5$, $f_1 = 5$ Hz, $SR = 0.5$ V/µs. Used in a non-inverting amplifier with $A_{CL} = 100$.

**Step A – Compute GBW:**

$$GBW = A_0 \times f_1 = 2 \times 10^5 \times 5 = 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step B – Closed-loop bandwidth:**

$$f_{BW} = \frac{GBW}{A_{CL}} = \frac{10^6}{100} = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step C – If output swing required is $V_{om} = 5$ V peak, compute $f_{FP}$:**

$$f_{FP} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{om}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 5} = 15.92 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step D – Determine usable bandwidth:**
Since $f_{FP} = 15.92 \text{ kHz} > f_{BW} = 10 \text{ kHz}$, the **closed-loop bandwidth (10 kHz) is the limiting factor**, not slew rate.

**Step E – Compute rise time for a step input:**

$$t_r = \frac{0.35}{f_{BW}} = \frac{0.35}{10{,}000} = 35 \text{ µs}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix – Identifying Question Type from Given Data

| Given in Question | What to Compute | Formula to Use |
|-------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| $A_0$, $f_1$ | GBW | $A_0 \cdot f_1$ |
| $A_{CL}$, GBW | Closed-loop BW | $GBW / A_{CL}$ |
| $V_{om}$, $f$ (input) | Required SR | $2\pi f V_{om}$ |
| $SR$, $V_{om}$ | $f_{FP}$ | $SR / (2\pi V_{om})$ |
| $f_H$ | Rise time | $0.35 / f_H$ |
| $\Delta V_S$, $\Delta V_{IO}$ | PSRR (dB) | $20 \log \vert \Delta V_S / \Delta V_{IO} \vert$ |
| $A_0$, $f$ | Gain at frequency f | $A_0 / \sqrt{1 + (f/f_1)^2}$ |
| $t_r$ | Bandwidth | $0.35 / t_r$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Block Diagram of PSRR Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["DC Power Supply"] -- Vplus --> node2["Op-Amp Under Test"]
    node1 -- Vminus --> node2
    node2 -- Vout --> node3["Oscilloscope or DMM"]
    node4["Varying DC Source Vary"] -- inject ripple --> node1
    node5["Compute Ratio dVs over dVio"] -.measure.-> node2
```

> The **DC Power Supply** feeds the op-amp, the **Varying DC Source** deliberately perturbs the supply, and the **Measuring Instrument** captures the resulting change in input-referred offset. The PSRR is the inverse ratio of these two changes.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Bode Plot – Gain vs Frequency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Frequency f in Hz"] --> B{"Compare f with f1 = 5 Hz"}
    B -- "f less than f1" --> C["Flat Region: Gain = A0 = 2x10^5 = 106 dB"]
    B -- "f greater than f1" --> D["Roll-off Region: -20 dB per decade"]
    D --> E{"Compare f with fT = 1 MHz"}
    E -- "f less than fT" --> F["Gain decreasing: Av = fT divided by f"]
    E -- "f greater than fT" --> G["Gain less than 1: Op-amp useless above fT"]
    C --> H["Bode Magnitude Plot"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

> **Reading the plot:** From DC to **5 Hz**, the gain is flat at 106 dB. Above 5 Hz, the gain rolls off at **−20 dB/decade**. It crosses 0 dB at **1 MHz** (the unity-gain bandwidth $f_T$).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: AC Characteristics Inter-Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Slew Rate SR"] --> B["Determines Full-Power BW"]
    B --> C["Slew-Rate Limited Region above fFP"]
    D["Gain-Bandwidth Product GBW"] --> E["Determines Small-Signal BW"]
    E --> F["Small-Signal Limited Region above fBW"]
    G["Frequency Response Av of f"] --> H["Three regions: Low-cutoff, Midband, High-cutoff"]
    H --> I["Midband: Gain flat and maximum"]
    H --> J["Cutoffs: Gain drops at 20 dB per decade"]
    K["Rise Time tr"] --> L["Inverse of High-Cutoff Frequency fH"]
    L --> E
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Step Response with Slew-Rate Limiting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Small Step Input: delta Vin small"] --> B["Exponential RC Rise: tr = 0.35 over fH"]
    C["Large Step Input: delta Vin large"] --> D["Linear Slew-Rate Region: SR constant"]
    B --> E["Combined Output: First linear slope, then exponential settle"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Settling Time ts: Time to stay within 0.1 percent of final value"]
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Frequency Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Open-Loop Response"] --> A1["A0 = 2x10^5 at DC"]
    A1 --> A2["Drops -20 dB per decade after f1 = 5 Hz"]
    A2 --> A3["Unity gain at fT = 1 MHz"]
    B["Closed-Loop Response ACL = 100"] --> B1["Gain 100 = 40 dB flat up to fBW = 10 kHz"]
    B1 --> B2["Drops -20 dB per decade after fBW"]
    B2 --> B3["Meets open-loop curve at fT"]
    A3 --> C["GBW Product remains constant at 1 MHz"]
    B3 --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway from Diagram 5:** As closed-loop gain $A_{CL}$ **decreases** (more feedback), bandwidth $f_{BW}$ **increases** — but their product is **always** the GBW.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR) of an op-amp. What does a high PSRR value indicate?**

**Model Answer:**
PSRR is defined as the ratio of the change in the power supply voltage to the change in the input offset voltage it produces, expressed in decibels:

$$PSRR = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{\Delta V_S}{\Delta V_{IO}} \right\vert \text{ dB}$$

A **high PSRR value** (e.g., 100 dB) indicates that the op-amp is highly immune to fluctuations, noise, and ripple present on its power supply rails — only a tiny fraction of the supply variation leaks into the output as an effective input offset change.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for practical significance.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define slew rate of an op-amp. State its typical value for IC 741 and mention one factor on which it depends.**

**Model Answer:**
Slew rate is the maximum rate of change of output voltage with time, when a step input of large amplitude is applied:

$$SR = \left. \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} \right\vert_{max} \quad \text{[V/µs]}$$

For IC µA741, $SR \approx 0.5 \text{ V/µs}$. It depends primarily on the **internal compensation capacitor** $C_C$ and the **maximum current** the input stage can source/sink:

$$SR = \frac{I_{max}}{C_C}$$

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark value, 1 mark dependency.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

### Question A
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the frequency response of an op-amp. Define dominant pole, unity-gain bandwidth, and gain-bandwidth product. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp has an open-loop gain of $2 \times 10^5$ and a dominant pole at 5 Hz. Calculate:
   (i) Gain-bandwidth product.
   (ii) Closed-loop bandwidth for a non-inverting amplifier with gain of 50.
   (iii) Rise time for a small step input. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) – Frequency Response Sketch and Definitions:**

The open-loop gain of an op-amp rolls off with frequency due to internal capacitances. The Bode magnitude plot has three regions:

1. **Low-frequency flat region** (DC to $f_1$): Gain = $A_0$ (DC open-loop gain).
2. **Roll-off region** (above $f_1$): Gain falls at **−20 dB/decade**.
3. **Unity-gain crossover** at $f_T$ where gain = 1.

```
Gain (dB)
   |
106|- - - - - - - - - ____
   |                  /
 80|                 /
   |                /
 40|               /  (slope -20 dB/dec)
   |              /
  0|_____________/_____________________
   0   5Hz   10kHz         1MHz      f (log)
                   f1            fT
```

**Definitions:**
- **Dominant Pole ($f_1$):** The lowest frequency pole that determines the bandwidth of the op-amp. All higher poles are at much higher frequencies.
- **Unity-Gain Bandwidth ($f_T$):** The frequency at which the open-loop gain magnitude drops to 1 (0 dB).
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW):** The product of the DC gain $A_0$ and dominant pole $f_1$, numerically equal to $f_T$ for a single-pole op-amp. It is a constant for a given op-amp.

**Part (b) – Numerical Computations:**

**Given:** $A_0 = 2 \times 10^5$, $f_1 = 5$ Hz, $A_{CL} = 50$.

**(i) Gain-Bandwidth Product:**

$$GBW = A_0 \times f_1 = 2 \times 10^5 \times 5 = 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) Closed-loop bandwidth:**

$$f_{BW} = \frac{GBW}{A_{CL}} = \frac{10^6}{50} = 20{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 20 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Rise time for small step input:**

$$t_r = \frac{0.35}{f_{BW}} = \frac{0.35}{20{,}000} = 17.5 \text{ µs}$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Question B
**(a)** Define PSRR. Why does PSRR degrade at higher frequencies? Explain with a suitable frequency-dependent expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 741 op-amp has $SR = 0.5$ V/µs and is connected as a unity-gain buffer. Calculate:
   (i) Full-power bandwidth for an output swing of 10 V peak.
   (ii) Maximum input sine-wave frequency that can be reproduced at 5 V peak without slew-rate limiting.
   (iii) Comment on what happens if the input frequency exceeds the value obtained in (ii). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) – PSRR Definition and Frequency Dependence:**

PSRR is the ratio of the change in supply voltage to the change in input offset voltage, in dB:

$$PSRR = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{\Delta V_S}{\Delta V_{IO}} \right\vert \text{ dB}$$

**Why PSRR degrades at high frequencies:**
The internal power-supply rejection path contains parasitic capacitances and transistor transit-time effects, which act as a low-pass filter. High-frequency noise on the supply rail couples directly to the signal path via these parasitic capacitances (Miller effect), bypassing the rejection mechanism.

The frequency-dependent expression is:

$$PSRR(f) = \frac{PSRR_{DC}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{PSRR})^2}}$$

where $f_{PSRR}$ is the corner frequency of the supply-rejection path (typically 100 Hz to 10 kHz). At $f \gg f_{PSRR}$, the PSRR drops at **−20 dB/decade**.

**Part (b) – Slew Rate and Full-Power Bandwidth:**

**Given:** $SR = 0.5$ V/µs $= 0.5 \times 10^6$ V/s.

**(i) Full-power bandwidth for $V_{om} = 10$ V peak:**

$$f_{FP} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{om}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10} = 7957.7 \text{ Hz} \approx 7.96 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Maximum frequency for $V_{om} = 5$ V peak without slew-rate limiting:**

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{om}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 5} = 15{,}915.5 \text{ Hz} \approx 15.92 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Comment on exceeding the limit:**

If the input frequency exceeds $f_{FP}$, the op-amp can no longer change its output fast enough to follow the input. The output becomes **slew-rate limited** and turns into a **triangle wave** instead of a sine wave — even though the peak amplitude is still within the supply rails. This is known as **slew-rate induced distortion** or **non-linear distortion**, and it is a hard limit imposed by the internal compensation capacitor of the op-amp.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where students LOSE MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PSRR with CMRR** – They look similar but PSRR deals with **supply** variations, CMRR with **common-mode** input variations. (Lose 1–2 marks if confused.)
> 2. **Forgetting the `20 log` factor** – PSRR is always in dB. Writing the raw ratio without unit conversion loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Using `|A|` as the open-loop gain at high frequencies** – The formula $\vert A(f) \vert = A_0 / \sqrt{1 + (f/f_1)^2}$ must be used, not just $A_0$.
> 4. **Mixing up small-signal BW and full-power BW** – $f_{BW} = GBW/A_{CL}$ is the **small-signal** limit. $f_{FP} = SR/(2\pi V_{om})$ is the **large-signal** limit. The lower of the two is the actual usable bandwidth.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers** – Always write Hz, kHz, V/µs, dB explicitly.
> 6. **Not mentioning "dominant pole" in the Bode plot explanation** – Examiners expect this phrase specifically.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **PSRR** = ratio of supply change to equivalent input offset change, in **dB**.
- **Higher PSRR ⇒ better supply noise rejection.**
- **PSRR drops with frequency** at **−20 dB/decade** after $f_{PSRR}$.
- **AC characteristics** describe time-varying (sinusoidal) behaviour, distinct from DC characteristics.
- **Frequency response** is plotted as **Bode plot** (gain in dB vs log frequency).
- **Dominant pole** $f_1$ = the lowest pole; sets the open-loop bandwidth.
- **Gain at any frequency:** $A(f) = A_0 / \sqrt{1 + (f/f_1)^2}$.
- **Phase at $f_1$** = **−45°**; at $10 f_1$ ≈ **−84.3°**.
- **GBW** = $A_0 \times f_1$ = $f_T$ (unity-gain bandwidth) = **constant**.
- **Closed-loop BW:** $f_{BW} = GBW / A_{CL}$.
- **Slew Rate** $SR$ = max $dV_{out}/dt$ in **V/µs**.
- **Full-Power BW** $f_{FP} = SR / (2\pi V_{om})$ — distinguish from small-signal BW.
- **Rise time** $t_r = 0.35 / f_H$ — for single-pole system only.
- **Roll-off slope** = **−20 dB/decade per pole** (single-pole = −20, two-pole = −40).
- **Slew-rate limiting** converts sine wave → triangle wave at high frequencies.
- **µA741 typical values (memorize):** $A_0 = 2\times10^5$, $f_1 = 5$ Hz, $SR = 0.5$ V/µs, $GBW = 1$ MHz, $PSRR \approx 90$ dB.
- **The 0.35 rule:** $t_r \cdot f_H = 0.35$ — frequently tested in KTU papers.
- **PSRR vs CMRR:** PSRR ⇒ supply; CMRR ⇒ common-mode signal.
- **PSS = 1/PSRR** (Power Supply Sensitivity — alternate name in some textbooks).
- **Phase margin** typically **45° to 60°** for stable operation; < 45° ⇒ oscillations.
- **Closed-loop gain trade-off:** Higher gain ⇒ Lower bandwidth, and vice-versa.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slew Rate, Basic Applications: Inverting Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Slew Rate, Basic Applications: Inverting Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: Slew Rate & Basic Applications — Inverting Amplifier

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Slew Rate (SR)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Slew Rate is defined as the maximum rate of change of the output voltage of an operational amplifier with respect to time, when a large-amplitude step signal is applied to its input. It is a measure of how fast the op-amp output can swing and is expressed in **volts per microsecond (V/μs)**.

$$\text{SR} = \left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{\max}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Slew Rate is the maximum rate at which the output of an op-amp can change. If the input demands a faster change, the op-amp cannot keep up and produces a **distorted (triangularised) output**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Elevator Problem:**
Imagine you enter a skyscraper and press the button for the 50th floor. The elevator can only move so fast (say, 5 floors per second). If you wanted to reach the 50th floor in 1 second, the elevator simply cannot do it — that is its **slew rate limit**. Similarly, an op-amp has a maximum speed at which its output voltage can swing. If the input signal demands faster changes, the output "lags behind" and gets distorted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Origin:** Slew rate arises because the op-amp has internal **capacitances** (Miller compensation capacitor ~30 pF) that must be charged/discharged by limited **bias currents**. There is only so much current available to charge the capacitor in a given time.

---

### 1.2 Inverting Amplifier

**Formal Definition:**
The **Inverting Amplifier** is the most widely used op-amp configuration where the input signal is applied to the **inverting terminal (−)** through resistor $R_1$, while the **non-inverting terminal (+)** is grounded. The feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output back to the inverting input. The output is **180° out of phase** with the input.

$$V_o = -\left(\frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$$

**Conceptual Analogy — The Seesaw:**
Think of the inverting input as the left end of a seesaw. The op-amp "tries its best" to keep the inverting input voltage equal to the non-inverting input (which is at ground = 0 V). This creates a phenomenon called **virtual ground**. Whatever voltage you put at the input ($V_{in}$), the op-amp inverts it and scales it by $R_f/R_1$ at the output. The negative sign simply means the output is upside-down (flipped) compared to the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The Inverting Amplifier has the following signature properties:
> - Output is **inverted** (180° phase shift)
> - Voltage gain $A_v$ is **negative**
> - Input impedance equals $R_1$ (low to moderate)
> - **Virtual ground** at the inverting input
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Slew Rate — Operational Breakdown

**Why does Slew Rate exist?**

An op-amp internally contains a **differential amplifier stage** followed by a **gain stage** with a **compensation capacitor** $C_c$. The output stage has a **limited bias current** $I_{bias}$ available to charge this capacitor. The fundamental relationship is:

$$\text{SR} = \frac{I_{bias}}{C_c}$$

This is the root cause — the output cannot change faster than the bias current can charge the compensation capacitor.

**How Slew Rate Distorts Signals:**

| Input Signal Type | Effect of Slew Rate |
|---|---|
| Low-frequency sine wave (small amplitude) | No visible distortion; output tracks input |
| High-frequency sine wave (large amplitude) | Output becomes **triangular** (slopes limited) |
| Square wave (high frequency) | Output becomes **trapezoidal** with linear ramps |
| Step input | Output rises linearly with slope = SR |

**Mathematical Condition for Distortion-Free Sine Output:**

For an undistorted output $V_o(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$, the maximum rate of change is:

$$\left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{\max} = 2\pi f V_p$$

For no slewing distortion:

$$2\pi f V_p \leq \text{SR}$$

**Full-Power Bandwidth (FPBW):**

The maximum frequency at which the op-amp can produce a peak output of $V_p$ without slewing:

$$f_p = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a problem gives SR and a maximum amplitude $V_p$, you must compute $f_p$ — this is a guaranteed 2-mark question every cycle.

---

### 2.2 Inverting Amplifier — Operational Breakdown

**Working Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning):**

- The non-inverting terminal (+) is connected directly to ground → $V^+ = 0$
- Due to **virtual short** between the two input terminals, $V^- = V^+ = 0$ V (this is the **virtual ground**)
- Since no current flows into the ideal op-amp's input terminals, the entire current from $V_{in}$ through $R_1$ must flow through $R_f$
- Applying KCL at the inverting node:
  - Current through $R_1$: $I_1 = (V_{in} - 0)/R_1$
  - Current through $R_f$: $I_f = (0 - V_o)/R_f$
  - $I_1 = I_f$ (KCL)
- Solving yields: $V_o = -(R_f/R_1) V_{in}$

**Why the Output is Inverted:**
When $V_{in}$ goes positive, the inverting input tends to go positive. The op-amp forces its output to swing **negative** to keep $V^- = 0$. Hence, positive input → negative output. The gain factor $-R_f/R_1$ sets how much the output is amplified.

**Key Characteristics of the Inverting Amplifier:**

| Parameter | Formula/Value |
|---|:---:|
| Closed-loop voltage gain | $A_v = -R_f / R_1$ |
| Input impedance | $R_{in} = R_1$ |
| Output impedance | $R_{out} \approx 0$ (ideal) |
| Phase shift | $180°$ |
| Virtual ground potential | $V^- = 0$ V |
| Current through feedback | $I_f = V_{in} / R_1$ |
| Bandwidth | $BW = \text{Gain-Bandwidth Product} / \vert A_v \vert$ |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Slew Rate | $\text{SR} = \left.\dfrac{dV_o}{dt}\right\|_{\max}$ | V/μs |
| 2 | Slew Rate (physical) | $\text{SR} = I_{bias} / C_c$ | V/s |
| 3 | Sine wave slew requirement | $\left.\dfrac{dV_o}{dt}\right\|_{\max} = 2\pi f V_p$ | V/s |
| 4 | Full-Power Bandwidth | $f_p = \text{SR} / (2\pi V_p)$ | Hz |
| 5 | Maximum undistorted freq. for given $V_p$ | $f_{\max} = \text{SR} / (2\pi V_p)$ | Hz |
| 6 | Inverting Amplifier Gain | $A_v = -R_f / R_1$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Inverting Amp Input Impedance | $R_{in} = R_1$ | Ω |
| 8 | Output Voltage | $V_o = -\left(R_f / R_1\right) V_{in}$ | V |
| 9 | Bandwidth of Inverting Amp | $BW = f_T / \vert A_v \vert$ | Hz |
| 10 | Rise time limitation | $t_r \geq \Delta V / \text{SR}$ | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** The inverting amplifier is the **backbone** of:
> - Audio mixers and summing amplifiers
> - Active filters (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass)
> - Signal inversion and level shifting
> - Analog computation circuits
>
> Slew Rate is the **critical specification** for high-frequency op-amps used in video amplifiers, data converters, and RF circuits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Voltage Gain of the Inverting Amplifier

**Given:**
- Op-amp is ideal (infinite input impedance, zero output impedance, infinite open-loop gain)
- $V^+ = 0$ (non-inverting terminal grounded)
- Input $V_{in}$ applied through $R_1$ to the inverting terminal
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ between output and inverting terminal

**Step 1 — Establish the virtual short principle:**
Because the op-amp is in a negative feedback configuration and has infinite open-loop gain, the differential input voltage must be zero for a finite output. Therefore:

$$V^+ = V^-$$

Since $V^+ = 0$ (grounded):

$$V^- = 0 \quad \text{(this is the virtual ground)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (Node A):**
The sum of currents entering the node equals the sum of currents leaving the node. Since no current enters the ideal op-amp's inverting terminal, all current flowing through $R_1$ must flow through $R_f$.

$$I_1 = I_f$$

**Step 3 — Express each current using Ohm's Law:**
Current through $R_1$ from $V_{in}$ to virtual ground:

$$I_1 = \frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

Current through $R_f$ from virtual ground to output:

$$I_f = \frac{V^- - V_o}{R_f} = \frac{0 - V_o}{R_f} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $V_o$:**

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = -V_o \cdot R_1$$

Solving for $V_o$:

$$V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_{in}$$

**Step 5 — Extract the gain:**

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

The negative sign indicates **phase inversion of 180°**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Slew Rate of an Op-Amp from Internal Parameters

**Given:**
- Compensation capacitor $C_c$ at the high-gain stage
- Bias current $I_{bias}$ available to charge $C_c$

**Step 1 — Voltage across the capacitor:**
The output stage must charge $C_c$. The voltage developed across $C_c$ is the op-amp's output voltage $V_o$.

$$V_o(t) = \frac{1}{C_c} \int I_{bias} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to time:**

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = \frac{I_{bias}}{C_c}$$

**Step 3 — This differential is constant (because $I_{bias}$ is constant), so the maximum rate of change of output is:**

$$\boxed{\text{SR} = \left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{\max} = \frac{I_{bias}}{C_c}}$$

**Units Check:**
- $I_{bias}$ is in Amperes (A)
- $C_c$ is in Farads (F) = A·s/V
- Ratio: A / (A·s/V) = **V/s**

Often quoted in V/μs for practical op-amps (typical 741 op-amp: SR ≈ 0.5 V/μs).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Slew-Rate-Limited Frequency for Sine Output

**Given:**
- Desired output: $V_o(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$ where $\omega = 2\pi f$
- Op-amp has Slew Rate SR

**Step 1 — Compute $dV_o/dt$:**

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = V_p \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t) = 2\pi f \cdot V_p \cdot \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2 — Identify maximum value:**
The cosine function has a maximum value of **1**. Therefore, the maximum rate of change of output is:

$$\left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{\max} = 2\pi f V_p$$

**Step 3 — For no slewing distortion, this must be ≤ SR:**

$$2\pi f V_p \leq \text{SR}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum frequency:**

$$\boxed{f_{\max} = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_p}}$$

This is the **Full-Power Bandwidth** of the op-amp.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** An op-amp has Slew Rate = 0.5 V/μs. Find (i) the maximum frequency at which an undistorted sine wave of peak amplitude 5 V can be produced, and (ii) the output voltage when a step input of 4 V is applied, 10 μs after the step.

**Part (i) — Maximum Frequency:**

$$f_{\max} = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_p} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{V/s}}{2\pi \times 5 \, \text{V}}$$

$$f_{\max} = \frac{500000}{31.4159} \approx 15915 \, \text{Hz} \approx 15.9 \, \text{kHz}$$

**Part (ii) — Output after 10 μs:**
The op-amp output cannot change faster than 0.5 V/μs. In 10 μs, the maximum change is:

$$\Delta V_o = \text{SR} \times \Delta t = 0.5 \, \text{V/μs} \times 10 \, \text{μs} = 5 \, \text{V}$$

For an inverting amplifier with gain $A_v = -10$ and step input of 4 V:
Ideal output = $-10 \times 4 = -40$ V. But slew-rate-limited output after 10 μs is only 5 V below starting level, so:

$$V_o(10\,\mu s) = 0 - 5 = -5 \, \text{V}$$

(Assuming output started at 0 V and is slewing toward −40 V.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Slew Rate Distortion Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[INPUT SIGNAL] --> nodeB[DIFFERENTIAL AMPLIFIER STAGE]
    nodeB --> nodeC[HIGH GAIN STAGE WITH COMPENSATION CAPACITOR CC]
    nodeC --> nodeD[BIAS CURRENT IBIAS CHARGES CC]
    nodeD --> nodeE[OUTPUT STAGE]
    nodeE --> nodeF[OUTPUT VOLTAGE SLEWING AT MAX SR]
    nodeG[CC AND IBIAS LIMITATION] -.-> nodeD
    nodeH[IF INPUT DEMANDS FASTER CHANGE] -.-> nodeI[DISTORTED TRIANGULAR OUTPUT]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Inverting Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTSTAGE[INPUT SIGNAL PATH]
        nodeA[VIN SOURCE] --> nodeB[RESISTOR R1]
        nodeB --> nodeC[INVERTING INPUT NODE]
    end
    subgraph OPAMP[OPERATIONAL AMPLIFIER CORE]
        nodeC --> nodeD[DIFF AMP STAGE]
        nodeD --> nodeE[GAIN STAGE WITH SLEW LIMIT]
        nodeE --> nodeF[OUTPUT BUFFER]
    end
    subgraph FEEDBACK[FEEDBACK NETWORK]
        nodeF --> nodeG[OUTPUT TERMINAL VO]
        nodeG --> nodeH[RESISTOR RF]
        nodeH --> nodeC
    end
    subgraph REFERENCE[REFERENCE PATH]
        nodeI[GROUND 0V] --> nodeJ[NON INVERTING INPUT PLUS]
        nodeJ --> nodeD
    end
    nodeK[VIRTUAL GROUND AT NODE C] -.-> nodeC
    nodeL[AV = MINUS RF BY R1] -.-> nodeG
```

### 4.3 Slew Rate Distortion — Block-Level Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[INPUT SINE WAVE HIGH FREQUENCY] --> nodeB[IDEAL EXPECTED SINE OUTPUT]
    nodeA --> nodeC[OP AMP WITH SR LIMIT]
    nodeC --> nodeD[SLEW LIMITED OUTPUT TRIANGULAR LIKE]
    nodeE[SR CHECK 2PI F V LESS THAN EQUAL TO SR] -.-> nodeD
    nodeF[IF VIOLATED OUTPUT DISTORTED] -.-> nodeD
```

### 4.4 Inverting Amplifier — Signal Inversion Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[VIN POSITIVE 1V] --> nodeB[VIRTUAL GROUND AT INVERTING INPUT]
    nodeB --> nodeC[OP AMP INVERTS AND AMPLIFIES]
    nodeC --> nodeD[OUTPUT VO NEGATIVE SCALED]
    nodeE[GAIN MAGNITUDE RF BY R1] -.-> nodeD
    nodeF[PHASE SHIFT 180 DEGREES] -.-> nodeD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a functional stage. The dotted arrows indicate **constraints or governing relationships**. The inverting amplifier feedback loop creates a closed system where the inverting input is held at virtual ground.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define Slew Rate of an operational amplifier. Mention its unit.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Slew Rate is defined as the maximum rate of change of output voltage of an op-amp with respect to time when a large-amplitude step signal is applied at the input.

$$\text{SR} = \left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{\max}$$

**Unit:** Volts per microsecond (V/μs) — *1 Mark*
**Definition with formula** — *2 Marks*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is an inverting amplifier? Write its voltage gain expression.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An inverting amplifier is an op-amp configuration in which the input signal is applied to the **inverting terminal (−)** through a resistor $R_1$, the **non-inverting terminal (+)** is grounded, and a feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected between the output and the inverting input.

The closed-loop voltage gain is:

$$A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Circuit description** — *2 Marks*, **Gain expression** — *1 Mark*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Define Slew Rate. Explain its causes and effects on the output of an op-amp. Derive the expression for Full-Power Bandwidth. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For an inverting amplifier with $R_1 = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$ having an input voltage $V_{in} = 0.4\,\sin(2000\pi t)$ V, calculate (i) Closed-loop voltage gain (ii) Output voltage (iii) Input impedance of the amplifier. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(A)(a):

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Slew Rate is the maximum rate of change of output voltage of an op-amp with respect to time. It is caused by the limited bias current available to charge the internal compensation capacitor.

$$\text{SR} = \frac{I_{bias}}{C_c}$$

**Causes (2 Marks):**
- Internal **compensation capacitor** $C_c$ in the high-gain stage
- Limited **bias current** $I_{bias}$ available to charge/discharge $C_c$
- Output stage current limitations

**Effects on Output (1.5 Marks):**
- Sinusoidal outputs at high frequencies become **triangular**
- Square wave outputs become **trapezoidal**
- Step response becomes a **linear ramp** instead of an ideal step

**Derivation of Full-Power Bandwidth (1.5 Marks):**
For $V_o(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$:
Maximum $\dfrac{dV_o}{dt} = 2\pi f V_p$
For distortion-free output: $2\pi f V_p \leq \text{SR}$

$$\boxed{f_p = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_p}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(A)(b):

**Given:** $R_1 = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{in} = 0.4 \sin(2000\pi t)$ V

**(i) Closed-loop voltage gain (2.5 Marks):**

$$A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} = -\frac{50\,\text{k}\Omega}{2\,\text{k}\Omega} = -25$$

**(ii) Output voltage (2.5 Marks):**

$$V_o = A_v \cdot V_{in} = -25 \times 0.4 \sin(2000\pi t) = -10 \sin(2000\pi t)\,\text{V}$$

**(iii) Input impedance (2 Marks):**

For an inverting amplifier:

$$Z_{in} = R_1 = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$$

---

**Q3(B). [Alternative Question — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an inverting amplifier using an op-amp. Derive its voltage gain. Mention its input impedance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp has a Slew Rate of 2 V/μs. Determine (i) the maximum frequency at which an undistorted sine wave of peak amplitude 6 V can be amplified, and (ii) the rise time if a step input of 0 to 10 V is applied. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(B)(a):

**Circuit diagram description (2 Marks):**
Op-amp with $V_{in}$ fed through $R_1$ to the inverting terminal; non-inverting terminal grounded; $R_f$ feedback from output to inverting input.

**Virtual ground concept (1 Mark):**
Since $V^+ = 0$ and $V^- = V^+$ (virtual short), $V^- = 0$ — this is **virtual ground**.

**KCL application & derivation (3 Marks):**
At inverting node:
$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{0 - V_o}{R_f}$$

Solving:
$$A_v = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Input impedance (1 Mark):**
$$Z_{in} = R_1$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q3(B)(b):

**Given:** SR = 2 V/μs = $2 \times 10^6$ V/s, $V_p = 6$ V

**(i) Maximum frequency (3.5 Marks):**

$$f_{\max} = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_p} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 6} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{37.699}$$

$$f_{\max} \approx 53{,}051 \, \text{Hz} \approx 53.05 \, \text{kHz}$$

**(ii) Rise time (3.5 Marks):**

For a step input of $\Delta V = 10$ V:

$$t_r \geq \frac{\Delta V}{\text{SR}} = \frac{10 \, \text{V}}{2 \, \text{V/μs}} = 5 \, \mu\text{s}$$

The op-amp will take **at least 5 μs** to slew from 0 V to 10 V.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $A_v = -R_f/R_1$. Always mention the **phase inversion (180°)** explicitly.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** in slew rate problems: 1 V/μs = $10^6$ V/s. Mismatching units causes wrong answers.
> 3. **Confusing SR with bandwidth** — they are different. SR is in V/μs; Bandwidth is in Hz.
> 4. **Not stating "virtual ground"** in the inverting amplifier derivation — this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss if skipped.
> 5. **Forgetting the condition** $2\pi f V_p \leq \text{SR}$ when solving slew rate problems.
> 6. **Input impedance of inverting amplifier** is $R_1$, NOT the op-amp's own input impedance — common confusion!

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Recall Bullet Bank (Rapid Revision):**

- **Slew Rate (SR):** Maximum $dV_o/dt$ of op-amp output; unit V/μs; caused by limited $I_{bias}$ charging $C_c$.
- **SR Formula:** $\text{SR} = I_{bias} / C_c$ (internal) and $\text{SR} = 2\pi f V_p$ (signal-related maximum).
- **Full-Power Bandwidth:** $f_p = \text{SR} / (2\pi V_p)$.
- **Slew rate effect:** Sine wave becomes triangular; square wave becomes trapezoidal.
- **Inverting Amplifier:** Input at inverting terminal via $R_1$; non-inverting terminal grounded; $R_f$ as feedback.
- **Gain:** $A_v = -R_f / R_1$ (negative sign = 180° phase inversion).
- **Virtual Ground:** $V^- = 0$ V (because $V^+ = 0$ and virtual short holds).
- **Input Impedance (Inverting Amp):** $R_{in} = R_1$.
- **Output Voltage:** $V_o = -(R_f/R_1) V_{in}$.
- **Bandwidth of Inverting Amp:** $BW = \text{GBP} / \vert A_v \vert$.
- **Typical 741 Op-amp SR:** 0.5 V/μs.
- **Distortion-free condition:** $2\pi f V_p \leq \text{SR}$.
- **Rise time with slew limit:** $t_r \geq \Delta V / \text{SR}$.
- **Why negative sign?** Positive input → inverting terminal goes positive → op-amp output swings negative to maintain virtual ground → output is inverted.
- **Two-resistor design** makes gain easily adjustable by varying $R_f / R_1$ ratio.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Inverting Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Non-Inverting Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Non-Inverting Amplifier** is a specific closed-loop operational amplifier (op-amp) configuration in which the input signal $V_{in}$ is applied directly to the **non-inverting (+) terminal** of the op-amp, while the output is fed back to the **inverting (-) terminal** through a resistive voltage-divider network comprising a feedback resistor $R_f$ and a ground-connected resistor $R_i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The non-inverting amplifier is a foundational building block in linear analog signal conditioning. It belongs to the family of *negative-feedback topologies* and is widely tested under the "Linear Applications of Op-Amps" cluster.

The defining electrical property of this topology is that the **output voltage is in phase** (0° phase shift) with the applied input voltage, and the magnitude of the output is a scaled replica of the input scaled by a factor strictly greater than or equal to unity ($\geq 1$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

Imagine standing in front of a **public address (PA) system microphone**:

- Your voice (the *input signal*) enters the microphone.
- The amplifier (the *op-amp*) makes the sound louder at the loudspeaker.
- The loudspeaker output is a **bigger version of the exact same waveform** — it is louder, but the *up and down* of the waveform is preserved (no flipping, no inversion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Non-Inverting" name comes from the fact that the output waveform is NOT flipped (NOT inverted) with respect to the input.** A positive-going input produces a positive-going output. This is the key distinction from the *inverting amplifier* configuration.

In the PA system, the volume knob controls how much louder the system makes your voice — analogous to the **feedback resistors $R_f$ and $R_i$** that control the *gain* in our amplifier.

### 1.3 Key Physical Parameters and Boundary Conditions

- **Ideal Op-Amp Assumptions:** $A_{OL} \to \infty$ (open-loop gain), $Z_{in} \to \infty$, $Z_{out} \to 0$.
- **Feedback Type:** Negative (shunt-shunt at the inverting node).
- **Closed-Loop Gain Range:** $A_v \geq 1$ (always positive, never less than unity).
- **Standard Supply Voltages:** Typically $\pm 15\,V$ for general-purpose op-amps such as the **$\mu A741$**.
- **Virtual Short Concept:** $V_{+} = V_{-}$ (in closed loop).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and "How"

**Why does the input get applied to the (+) terminal?**
Because the op-amp's internal differential pair amplifies the difference $(V_{+} - V_{-})$. By feeding the signal into $V_{+}$, any positive change in input causes a positive change in output, producing a *non-inverted* waveform.

**How is the gain controlled?**
The op-amp, through its extremely high open-loop gain ($A_{OL} \approx 10^5$ to $10^6$), continuously adjusts its output to enforce the **virtual short principle**:

$$V_{-} \approx V_{+} = V_{in}$$

The output is forced to whatever value is necessary to maintain this virtual equality, and that value is mathematically set by the external resistor ratio $R_f / R_i$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow (Operational Reasoning)

1. **Input Application:** $V_{in}$ is connected to the non-inverting (+) terminal.
2. **Virtual Short Activation:** Through negative feedback, the op-amp enforces $V_{-} = V_{+}$.
3. **Feedback Network Sensing:** The resistor pair $(R_f, R_i)$ acts as a voltage divider from $V_{out}$ to ground, sampling a fraction $\beta$ of $V_{out}$ back to $V_{-}$.
4. **Voltage at Inverting Node:** By superposition/KVL, $V_{-} = V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}$.
5. **Equating Both Nodes:** $V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}$.
6. **Solving for Gain:** $\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU board examination on this topic. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Closed-Loop Voltage Gain | $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | Dimensionless (V/V) | $\geq 1$ |
| 2 | Feedback Fraction ($\beta$-network) | $\beta = \dfrac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}$ | Dimensionless | $0 < \beta \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Loop Gain | $T = A_{OL} \cdot \beta$ | Dimensionless | $T \gg 1$ |
| 4 | Ideal Input Impedance | $Z_{in,ideal} = \infty$ | $\Omega$ | Practically $> 10^{12}\,\Omega$ |
| 5 | Ideal Output Impedance | $Z_{out,ideal} = 0$ | $\Omega$ | Practically $< 1\,\Omega$ |
| 6 | Closed-Loop Input Impedance | $Z_{in,CL} = Z_{in,OL} \cdot (1 + A_{OL}\beta)$ | $\Omega$ | Very high (GΩ range) |
| 7 | Closed-Loop Output Impedance | $Z_{out,CL} = \dfrac{Z_{out,OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$ | $\Omega$ | Very low (mΩ range) |
| 8 | Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW) | $GBW = A_{CL} \cdot f_{-3dB} = \text{constant}$ | Hz | Set by op-amp IC |
| 9 | Output Voltage Swing | $V_{out,min} < V_{out} < V_{out,max}$ | Volts | Limited by $V_{CC}, V_{EE}$ |
| 10 | Slew Rate Constraint | $SR \geq 2\pi f_{max} V_{out,peak}$ | V/µs | Avoids slew-rate distortion |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sensor Signal Conditioning:** Used to buffer low-amplitude signals from high-impedance sources such as piezoelectric transducers, pH electrodes, and biomedical sensors (ECG, EEG).
- **Active Filters:** Forms the first stage of Sallen-Key low-pass and high-pass filters.
- **Audio Pre-amplifiers:** The first stage of hi-fi audio chains where signal integrity and high input impedance are critical.
- **Voltage Follower (Unity-Gain Buffer):** A special case where $R_f = 0$ and $R_i = \infty$, yielding $A_{CL} = 1$ — used for impedance matching between cascaded stages.
- **Instrumentation Front-End:** Pre-amplifier stage feeding into instrumentation amplifiers in data acquisition systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Closed-Loop Voltage Gain

**Given Circuit:** A non-inverting amplifier with input $V_{in}$ at the (+) terminal, feedback resistor $R_f$ between $V_{out}$ and $V_{-}$, and a resistor $R_i$ between $V_{-}$ and ground.

**Assumptions (Ideal Op-Amp Model):**
- Open-loop gain $A_{OL} \to \infty$
- Input current to both terminals $I_{+} = I_{-} = 0$
- Output impedance $Z_{out} = 0$

**Step 1 — Apply the virtual short principle.**
Because the op-amp operates in a closed negative-feedback loop, the differential input is driven to zero in the limit:

$$V_{+} - V_{-} = \frac{V_{out}}{A_{OL}} \approx 0 \quad \text{(as } A_{OL} \to \infty\text{)}$$

Therefore:

$$V_{-} = V_{+}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the voltage at the non-inverting node.**
The input $V_{in}$ is directly applied to the (+) terminal. Since no current flows into the ideal op-amp input, there is no IR drop:

$$V_{+} = V_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the inverting node voltage using the virtual short.**

$$V_{-} = V_{+} = V_{in}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the voltage-divider relation at the inverting node.**
The two resistors $R_f$ (between $V_{out}$ and $V_{-}$) and $R_i$ (between $V_{-}$ and ground) form a simple voltage divider. The voltage at $V_{-}$ is the fraction of $V_{out}$ appearing across $R_i$:

$$V_{-} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for $V_{-}$.**

$$V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}$$

**Step 6 — Solve algebraically for the ratio $\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$.**

$$\frac{V_{in}}{V_{out}} = \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}$$

Invert both sides:

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_i + R_f}{R_i}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the expression.**

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:** A non-inverting amplifier is constructed with $R_f = 10\,k\Omega$ and $R_i = 2\,k\Omega$. If $V_{in} = 0.5\,V$ (DC), determine:
(a) The closed-loop gain
(b) The output voltage
(c) The feedback fraction $\beta$

**Solution — Part (a): Closed-Loop Gain**

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$$

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{10\,k\Omega}{2\,k\Omega}$$

$$A_{CL} = 1 + 5 = 6 \text{ V/V}$$

**Solution — Part (b): Output Voltage**

$$V_{out} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{in}$$

$$V_{out} = 6 \times 0.5\,V = 3\,V$$

**Solution — Part (c): Feedback Fraction $\beta$**

$$\beta = \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_f} = \frac{2\,k\Omega}{2\,k\Omega + 10\,k\Omega} = \frac{2}{12} = 0.1667$$

**Verification using loop-gain identity:** $A_{CL} = \dfrac{1}{\beta} = \dfrac{1}{0.1667} = 6.0$ ✓

### 3.3 Derivation of Closed-Loop Input Impedance

For a non-inverting amplifier, the input is applied to the (+) terminal. The closed-loop input impedance is "boosted" by the loop gain:

$$Z_{in,CL} = Z_{in,OL} \cdot (1 + A_{OL}\beta)$$

**Derivation outline:**
- Differential voltage at op-amp input: $V_{diff} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$
- Input current: $I_{in} = \dfrac{V_{diff}}{Z_{in,OL}}$
- Therefore: $Z_{in,CL} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{I_{in}} = Z_{in,OL}(1 + A_{OL}\beta)$

**Numerical example (typical $\mu A741$):** $Z_{in,OL} \approx 2\,M\Omega$, $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, $\beta = 0.1$:

$$Z_{in,CL} = 2 \times 10^6 \times (1 + 2 \times 10^5 \times 0.1) = 2 \times 10^6 \times 20001 \approx 4 \times 10^{10}\,\Omega$$

This extremely high input impedance is the **single most important advantage** of the non-inverting topology.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram represents the **block-level signal flow architecture** of the non-inverting amplifier, mapping the input/output/feedback topology. (Op-amp pin-level schematics are not rendered; this functional view is what KTU examiners expect in design problems.)

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcNode[Vin Source] --> inpNode[Non-Inverting Input V+]
    inpNode --> diffAmpNode[Differential Gain Stage Aol]
    diffAmpNode --> outStageNode[Output Buffer Zout approx 0]
    outStageNode --> voutNode[Vout Node]
    voutNode --> fbResistorNode[Feedback Resistor Rf]
    fbResistorNode --> invNode[Inverting Input V-]
    invNode --> gndResistorNode[Ground Resistor Ri]
    gndResistorNode --> gndNode[Ground 0V]
    invNode -.Virtual Short.-> inpNode
```

### 4.2 Signal Conditioning Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    sensorNode[High Impedance Sensor e.g. ECG Electrode]
    sensorNode --> bufferStage[Non-Inverting Buffer Stage Acl equals 1]
    bufferStage --> gainStage1[Non-Inverting Gain Stage 1 Rf1 over Ri1]
    gainStage1 --> gainStage2[Non-Inverting Gain Stage 2 Rf2 over Ri2]
    gainStage2 --> filterNode[Active Low Pass Filter Sallen Key]
    filterNode --> adcNode[ADC Input Channel]
    adcNode --> mcuNode[Digital Signal Processing MCU]
```

### 4.3 Architecture Legend & Interpretation

| Block ID | Functional Role | Engineering Purpose |
|----------|----------------|---------------------|
| `sensorNode` | Physical Transducer Source | Provides weak, high-impedance analog signal |
| `bufferStage` | Unity-Gain Impedance Transformer | Prevents source loading (Zin → ∞) |
| `gainStage1` | First Amplification Block | Boosts signal amplitude using $1 + R_{f1}/R_{i1}$ |
| `gainStage2` | Second Amplification Block | Provides additional gain in cascade |
| `filterNode` | Anti-Aliasing Sallen-Key Filter | Restricts bandwidth below Nyquist rate |
| `adcNode` | Analog-to-Digital Converter | Digitizes the conditioned signal |
| `mcuNode` | Microcontroller / DSP | Executes digital processing algorithms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Note:** The non-inverting topology is preferred as the **first stage** in any cascaded signal chain precisely because its near-infinite input impedance prevents *source loading*, preserving signal fidelity from the sensor to the processing circuitry.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a non-inverting amplifier. Write its closed-loop voltage gain expression.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A non-inverting amplifier is an op-amp configuration in which the input signal is applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal and the output is fed back to the inverting (-) terminal through a resistor divider network $(R_f, R_i)$.

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$$

The output is **in phase** with the input and the gain is always $\geq 1$. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for phase/gain range statement)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
**State any three advantages of the non-inverting amplifier over the inverting amplifier.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Very high input impedance** — does not load the source. (1 mark)
2. **Output is in phase** with the input (no 180° phase reversal). (1 mark)
3. **Gain is always $\geq 1$** and can be precisely controlled by the resistor ratio $R_f/R_i$ alone. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, S3 B.Tech ECE]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the closed-loop voltage gain of a non-inverting amplifier using an ideal op-amp. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a non-inverting amplifier with $R_f = 47\,k\Omega$ and $R_i = 4.7\,k\Omega$, calculate the closed-loop gain, the feedback factor $\beta$, and the output voltage for an input of $V_{in} = 150\,mV$ (sinusoidal, peak value). Comment on the phase relationship between input and output. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Apply the virtual short and zero input current ideal op-amp assumptions:

$$V_{+} = V_{in} \quad \text{(since no current flows into the (+) terminal)}$$

By virtual short:

$$V_{-} = V_{+} = V_{in}$$

Apply voltage divider at the inverting node:

$$V_{-} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}$$

Equating and solving:

$$V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}$$

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$$

**Mark Split:** Virtual short (2), Voltage divider (2), Algebraic manipulation (2), Final expression (1).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Closed-loop gain:**

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 1 + \frac{47\,k\Omega}{4.7\,k\Omega} = 1 + 10 = 11 \text{ V/V}$$

**Step 2 — Feedback factor $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_f} = \frac{4.7}{4.7 + 47} = \frac{4.7}{51.7} = 0.0909$$

**Step 3 — Output voltage (peak value):**

$$V_{out,peak} = A_{CL} \times V_{in,peak} = 11 \times 150\,mV = 1.65\,V$$

**Step 4 — Phase relationship:**
The output is **in phase (0° phase shift)** with the input — a positive half-cycle at the input produces a positive half-cycle at the output, with no inversion. (1 mark)

**Mark Split:** Gain (2), Beta (2), Output voltage (2), Phase comment (1).

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a non-inverting amplifier as a **voltage follower (unity-gain buffer)**. Derive the gain and state two applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp has an open-loop gain $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$ and open-loop input impedance $Z_{in,OL} = 2\,M\Omega$. It is used in a non-inverting configuration with $\beta = 0.05$. Calculate the closed-loop input impedance and the closed-loop output impedance if $Z_{out,OL} = 75\,\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **voltage follower** is the special case of the non-inverting amplifier where $R_f = 0\,\Omega$ (a direct wire) and $R_i = \infty\,\Omega$ (open circuit). Substituting:

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} = 1$$

The output follows the input exactly: $V_{out} = V_{in}$.

**Operating principle:** Any small deviation in $V_{out}$ away from $V_{in}$ creates a differential input $(V_{+} - V_{-})$ that the op-amp amplifies by $A_{OL} \approx 10^5$ to correct the error, effectively making $V_{out} = V_{in}$.

**Applications (any two):**
1. **Impedance matching / buffering** between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load.
2. **Isolation stage** in cascaded filters to prevent inter-stage loading.
3. **Sample-and-hold** output buffering in data acquisition systems.

**Mark Split:** Circuit (2), Derivation (2), Two applications (3).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Closed-loop input impedance:**

$$Z_{in,CL} = Z_{in,OL} \cdot (1 + A_{OL} \cdot \beta)$$

$$Z_{in,CL} = 2 \times 10^6 \times (1 + 2 \times 10^5 \times 0.05)$$

$$Z_{in,CL} = 2 \times 10^6 \times (1 + 10000) = 2 \times 10^6 \times 10001$$

$$\boxed{Z_{in,CL} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{10}\,\Omega = 20\,G\Omega}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop output impedance:**

$$Z_{out,CL} = \frac{Z_{out,OL}}{1 + A_{OL} \cdot \beta}$$

$$Z_{out,CL} = \frac{75}{1 + 10000} = \frac{75}{10001}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{out,CL} \approx 7.5\,m\Omega}$$

**Interpretation:** The non-inverting topology simultaneously provides *extremely high* input impedance (ideal for source loading prevention) and *extremely low* output impedance (ideal for driving low-impedance loads). (1 mark for interpretation)

**Mark Split:** Input Z formula (2), Numerical evaluation (2), Output Z formula (1.5), Numerical evaluation (1.5).

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in the gain formula** — Writing $A_{CL} = R_f/R_i$ instead of $1 + R_f/R_i$ is the **#1 most common error**. Always remember the unity baseline.
> 2. **Confusing inverting and non-inverting terminals** — Input *always* goes to (+) in this topology. Reversing the terminals converts the circuit into an inverting amplifier, changing both the gain expression and the phase.
> 3. **Not stating the phase relationship** — KTU examiners explicitly test whether you note that the output is **in phase** (0°, not 180°).
> 4. **Missing units and boundary conditions** in numerical problems — Always specify whether gain is in V/V or dB, and verify $A_{CL} \geq 1$.
> 5. **Omitting the virtual short assumption** in derivations — You must explicitly state $V_{+} = V_{-}$ and $I_{+} = I_{-} = 0$ as starting assumptions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Core Gain Formula:** $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ — never less than 1.
- **Input goes to (+)**; feedback goes to (-) through $R_f$ and $R_i$.
- **Virtual Short:** $V_{+} = V_{-}$ is the foundational analysis principle.
- **Zero input current:** $I_{+} = I_{-} = 0$ for ideal op-amps.
- **Phase:** Output is **in phase** with input (0° shift, non-inverted).
- **Input Impedance:** $Z_{in,CL} = Z_{in,OL}(1 + A_{OL}\beta)$ — boosted to GΩ range.
- **Output Impedance:** $Z_{out,CL} = \dfrac{Z_{out,OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$ — reduced to mΩ range.
- **Voltage Follower Special Case:** $R_f = 0$, $R_i = \infty \Rightarrow A_{CL} = 1$, used as a buffer.
- **Bandwidth Trade-off:** Higher gain ⇒ lower closed-loop bandwidth (GBW = constant).
- **Slew Rate Limitation:** Output cannot change faster than the op-amp's specified SR (V/µs).
- **Common ICs:** $\mu A741$, TL081, LM358, OPA227 — all support non-inverting topology.
- **Feedback Factor:** $\beta = \dfrac{R_i}{R_i + R_f}$ — useful for analyzing loop gain and stability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Operational Amplifiers: Differential Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of Operational Amplifiers: Differential Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differential Amplifier (Subtractor):** A differential amplifier is an analog circuit that amplifies the **algebraic difference** between two input voltages while **rejecting any voltage common to both inputs**. It is formally characterized by a **differential gain ($A_d$)** and a **common-mode gain ($A_c$)**, with the figure of merit being the **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**.

In the KTU 2024 OBE framework, this is a **high-weightage analytical topic** that integrates:
- Superposition theorem
- Virtual short / virtual open concepts
- Negative feedback analysis
- Practical non-idealities (finite open-loop gain $A$, finite input resistance $R_{id}$)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Balanced Weighing Scale"

Imagine two children sitting on opposite ends of a seesaw (weighing scale):
- **The seesaw (amplifier)** only tilts based on the **weight difference** between the two children.
- If **both children are equally heavy** (common-mode signal), the scale stays **perfectly horizontal** → output = 0.
- If one child is heavier (differential signal), the scale tilts → output is proportional to the **difference**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering mapping:** ECG (Electrocardiogram) machines in hospitals use differential amplifiers to extract the tiny millivolt heart signal riding on top of large 50/60 Hz mains noise. The **differential** heart signal is amplified; the **common-mode** noise is rejected. This is why hospitals use shielded, twisted-pair patient cables.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Gain | $A_d$ | Gain for $(V_1 - V_2)$ | 1 to 1000 |
| Common-Mode Gain | $A_c$ | Gain for average of inputs | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| **CMRR** | $\rho$ | $\vert A_d / A_c \vert$ | **60 dB – 120 dB** |
| Input Impedance | $R_{in}$ | Resistance seen by source | $10^6\ \Omega$ – $10^{12}\ \Omega$ |

The CMRR is conventionally expressed in **decibels (dB)**, and **board questions almost always** require this conversion.

$$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20\ \log_{10}\left(\frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_c \vert}\right)$$

## 1.4 Mathematical Representation of Any Differential Amplifier

The general output equation of *any* linear differential amplifier is:

$$V_o = A_d \cdot V_d + A_c \cdot V_c$$

where the **differential-mode** and **common-mode** input voltages are defined as:

$$V_d = V_1 - V_2 \qquad \text{and} \qquad V_c = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** When $A_c = 0$ (ideal matching), the output depends *only* on the difference. This is the **hallmark** of an ideal subtractor. Marks are awarded for stating this explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology — The Single Op-Amp Subtractor

The classical op-amp based differential amplifier (often called the **"subtractor"**) is built from a single op-amp with four precision resistors and two voltage sources $V_1$ and $V_2$. The non-inverting input receives $V_2$ through a divider ($R_1', R_2'$); the inverting input receives $V_1$ through $R_1$, with feedback resistor $R_2$.

### Operating Principles (Step-by-Step Logic)

- **Step 1 — Virtual Open:** No current flows into the op-amp's $V_+$ or $V_-$ terminals ($I_{in} \approx 0$).
- **Step 2 — Virtual Short:** With negative feedback active, $V_- = V_+$ (both inputs are at the *same* potential).
- **Step 3 — KCL at Inverting Node:** The current entering through $R_1$ from $V_1$ must equal the current leaving through $R_2$ toward $V_o$.
- **Step 4 — KCL at Non-Inverting Node:** $V_2$ is divided by $R_1', R_2'$, producing $V_+ = V_2 \cdot R_2'/(R_1' + R_2')$.
- **Step 5 — Superposition Combine:** Sum the contributions of $V_1$ and $V_2$ independently and add them to obtain $V_o$.

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Operating Regimes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 module questions frequently ask students to derive $V_o$ under one of three regimes. **You must know all three** to handle "derive with finite $A$" type questions.

| Regime | Op-Amp Assumption | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Subtractor** | $A = \infty,\ R_{id} = \infty$ | Quick textbook problems |
| **Practical (Finite $A$)** | $A$ finite, $R_{id} = \infty$ | Design verification |
| **Real-World (Finite $A$ & $R_{id}$)** | Both finite, source $R_s$ present | Industry-grade analysis |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Memorize This Block)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ | Differential-mode input | V |
| 2 | $V_c = (V_1 + V_2)/2$ | Common-mode input | V |
| 3 | $V_o = A_d V_d + A_c V_c$ | General output | V |
| 4 | $A_d = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$ | Diff. gain (1-op-amp, matched R) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $A_c = 0$ (ideal) | Common-mode gain | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_c \vert}\right)$ | Rejection metric | dB |
| 7 | $V_o = \dfrac{A \cdot R_2}{A \cdot R_1 + R_1 + R_2}\,(V_2 - V_1)$ | With finite $A$ | V |
| 8 | $A_d^{\text{inst}} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | 3-op-amp instrumentation amp | dimensionless |
| 9 | $A_c^{\text{inst}} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Instrumentation amp (mismatch) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_o^{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ | Output for matched R | V |

> [!NOTE]
> In formula rows above, $\Delta R$ denotes the *maximum* resistor mismatch (e.g., due to 1% tolerance). The pipe symbol $\vert$ in the table is rendered as $\vert A_d \vert$ using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax — **this is mandatory per KTU 2024 digital submission norms**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Instrumentation:** ECG, EEG, EMG front-ends (extract micro-volt biosignals).
- **Audio Engineering:** Balanced audio line drivers (XLR) — reject ground-loop hum.
- **Industrial Sensors:** Strain gauge bridges, thermocouple amplifiers.
- **Data Acquisition:** ADC driver stages rejecting common-mode ground noise.
- **Motor Control:** Current sensing in 3-phase motor drives using shunt resistors.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Common-Mode Rejection

The op-amp's output depends only on $(V_+ - V_-)$. If both inputs shift by the **same** amount (common-mode), their *difference* remains unchanged, so the output is theoretically invariant. In practice, **resistor mismatch** creates a small residual output, quantified by $A_c \ne 0$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Note:** Marks are awarded for **every** algebraic transition. Skipping a step (e.g., going directly from KCL to the final $V_o$ equation) typically costs **3 out of 7** marks. Each derivation below is therefore expanded to a publication-quality level.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ideal Subtractor (Infinite $A$, Infinite $R_{id}$)

**Circuit:** Single op-amp with $R_1, R_2$ on inverting branch and $R_1', R_2'$ on non-inverting branch.

**Step 1 — Compute $V_+$ using voltage divider:**

Since no current enters the $V_+$ terminal, $R_1'$ and $R_2'$ form a simple unloaded voltage divider between $V_2$ and ground.

$$V_+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{R_1' + R_2'}$$

**Step 2 — Apply virtual short:**

Negative feedback forces the inverting terminal to track the non-inverting terminal.

$$V_- = V_+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{R_1' + R_2'}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at inverting node (superposition / direct):**

The current flowing *into* the node from $V_1$ through $R_1$ equals the current flowing *out* through $R_2$ to $V_o$ (since the op-amp draws zero input current).

$$\frac{V_1 - V_-}{R_1} = \frac{V_- - V_o}{R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for $V_o$:**

$$V_1 R_2 - V_- R_2 = V_- R_1 - V_o R_1$$

$$V_o R_1 = V_- R_1 + V_- R_2 - V_1 R_2 = V_-(R_1 + R_2) - V_1 R_2$$

$$V_o = V_- \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1} - V_1 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V_-$ from Step 2:**

$$V_o = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{R_1' + R_2'} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1} - V_1 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the matching condition $R_1 = R_1'$ and $R_2 = R_2'$:**

The factor $\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}$ and $\frac{R_2'}{R_1' + R_2'} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$. Their product simplifies beautifully.

$$\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}$$

**Step 7 — Final compact result:**

$$\boxed{\,V_o = \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,(V_2 - V_1)\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — With Finite Open-Loop Gain $A$

This is the **most common KTU 14-mark question variant**. We now drop the $A = \infty$ assumption.

**Step 1 — Op-amp output equation (non-ideal):**

$$V_o = A\,(V_+ - V_-)$$

**Step 2 — $V_+$ from voltage divider (unchanged, $R_{id} = \infty$ assumed):**

$$V_+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{R_1' + R_2'}$$

**Step 3 — $V_-$ from KCL (unchanged structure):**

The KCL equation at the inverting node is:

$$\frac{V_1 - V_-}{R_1} = \frac{V_- - V_o}{R_2}$$

Solving for $V_-$:

$$V_-(R_1 + R_2) = V_1 R_2 + V_o R_1$$

$$V_- = \frac{V_1 R_2 + V_o R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_+$ and $V_-$ into the op-amp equation:**

$$V_o = A \left[\, V_2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{R_1' + R_2'} - \frac{V_1 R_2 + V_o R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,\right]$$

**Step 5 — Group the $V_o$ terms on the LHS:**

$$V_o + A \cdot \frac{V_o R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = A V_2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{R_1' + R_2'} - A V_1 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_o \left[\, 1 + \frac{A R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\,\right] = A \cdot \frac{R_2 V_2 - R_2' V_1 + (R_2' - R_2) V_2 \text{ terms}}{\ldots}$$

**Step 6 — Apply matched-resistor assumption $R_1 = R_1'$ and $R_2 = R_2'$:**

This collapses the expression dramatically. After substitution:

$$V_o \cdot \frac{A R_1 + R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{A R_2}{R_1 + R_2}\,(V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 7 — Final result for finite $A$:**

$$\boxed{\,V_o = \frac{A\,R_2}{A\,R_1 + R_1 + R_2}\,(V_2 - V_1)\,}$$

**Step 8 — Limiting case check (sanity verification):**

As $A \to \infty$, the term $A R_1$ dominates the denominator, yielding $V_o \to (R_2/R_1)(V_2 - V_1)$ ✓. This matches the ideal subtractor result from §3.1.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — General $V_o$ in Terms of $V_d$ and $V_c$

**Step 1 — Express inputs in terms of common-mode and differential-mode components:**

$$V_1 = V_c + \frac{V_d}{2} \qquad V_2 = V_c - \frac{V_d}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the ideal subtractor result $V_o = (R_2/R_1)(V_2 - V_1)$:**

$$V_o = \frac{R_2}{R_1}\left[\left(V_c - \frac{V_d}{2}\right) - \left(V_c + \frac{V_d}{2}\right)\right] = \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,(-V_d)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the gains:**

$$\boxed{\,A_d = \frac{R_2}{R_1},\qquad A_c = 0\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> This shows that the **single op-amp subtractor is ideal in CMRR** *if* and *only if* the four resistors are *perfectly matched*. KTU problems often present a **mismatched** resistor scenario and ask for the *residual* $A_c$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Instrumentation Amplifier Gain (3 Op-Amp Topology)

The instrumentation amplifier uses **two input buffer stages** and **one difference stage** — a KTU favourite for 14-mark questions.

**Stage 1 — Input buffers ($A_1, A_2$):** Two non-inverting amplifiers with a gain-setting resistor $R_g$ between their inverting inputs.

**Step 1 — Output of $A_1$:**

The voltage across $R_g$ equals the differential input $V_d = V_1 - V_2$ (since both non-inverting inputs see $V_1$ and $V_2$ directly). The current through $R_g$ is $V_d / R_g$, and this same current flows through both feedback resistors $R_1$ (one in each buffer).

$$V_{o1} = V_1 + \frac{V_d}{R_g} \cdot R_1 = V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2)$$

$$V_{o2} = V_2 - \frac{V_d}{R_g} \cdot R_1 = V_2 - \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 2 — Differential input to Stage 2:**

$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 3 — Stage 2 is a standard unity-gain-matched subtractor** (with $R_3 = R_3'$ and $R_2 = R_2'$):

$$V_o = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o1} - V_{o2})$$

**Step 4 — Combined gain:**

$$\boxed{\,A_d^{\text{inst}} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical insight:** Gain is tunable via **a single resistor $R_g$** without destroying the symmetric input impedance — making this the *de facto* standard for sensor signal conditioning.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 digital submission guidelines, Mermaid diagrams are validated against the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** and **Label Formatting Restriction**. All node IDs below are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; labels are clean uppercase text without embedded markdown formatting.

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Single Op-Amp Differential Amplifier (Subtractor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[V1 SOURCE] --> res1a[Rs1]
    res1a --> nodeA1[R1]
    nodeA1 --> invNode[INVERTING INPUT]
    src2[V2 SOURCE] --> res2a[Rs2]
    res2a --> nodeB1[R1 PRIME]
    nodeB1 --> nonInvNode[NON INVERTING INPUT]
    gnd[GND] --> nodeB2[R2 PRIME]
    nodeB2 --> nonInvNode
    invNode --> opampBlock[OP AMP BODY GAIN A]
    nonInvNode --> opampBlock
    opampBlock --> outNode[Vo OUTPUT TERMINAL]
    nodeA2[R2 FEEDBACK] --> invNode
    outNode --> nodeA2
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Signal Decomposition & Recomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw1[V1 RAW INPUT] --> decom[COMMON DIFFERENTIAL DECOMPOSER]
    raw2[V2 RAW INPUT] --> decom
    decom --> vcmode[VC COMMON MODE SIGNAL]
    decom --> vdmode[VD DIFFERENTIAL MODE SIGNAL]
    vcmode --> acGain[AC GAIN BLOCK]
    vdmode --> adGain[AD GAIN BLOCK]
    acGain --> summer[SUMMING JUNCTION]
    adGain --> summer
    summer --> voOut[Vo OUTPUT VOLTAGE]
    cmrrBlock[CMRR QUALITY METRIC] -.evaluates.-> acGain
    cmrrBlock -.evaluates.-> adGain
```

---

## 4.3 Architecture — 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[V1 SENSOR INPUT] --> buf1[OP AMP 1 INPUT BUFFER]
    in2[V2 SENSOR INPUT] --> buf2[OP AMP 2 INPUT BUFFER]
    rg[GAIN SETTING RESISTOR Rg] -.between inverting inputs.-> buf1
    rg -.between inverting inputs.-> buf2
    rfb1[FEEDBACK RESISTOR R1] --> buf1
    rfb2[FEEDBACK RESISTOR R1] --> buf2
    buf1 --> diff1[DIFFERENCE STAGE INVERTING INPUT]
    buf2 --> diff2[DIFFERENCE STAGE NON INVERTING INPUT]
    diff1 --> diffOp[OP AMP 3 DIFFERENCE STAGE]
    diff2 --> diffOp
    diffOp --> outFinal[Vo BRIDGEOUTPUT]
    rdiv1[R2 PRIME TO GND] --> diff2
    rdiv2[R2 FEEDBACK] --> diff1
    outFinal --> rdiv2
```

---

## 4.4 Mode Separation Tree — How $V_1, V_2$ Decompose

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inA[V1 INPUT] --> modeSep[MODE SEPARATION TRANSFORM]
    inB[V2 INPUT] --> modeSep
    modeSep --> cmBranch[COMMON MODE BRANCH VC]
    modeSep --> dmBranch[DIFFERENTIAL MODE BRANCH VD]
    cmBranch --> acProc[PROCESSED BY AC GAIN]
    dmBranch --> adProc[PROCESSED BY AD GAIN]
    acProc --> finalSum[SUMMED]
    adProc --> finalSum
    finalSum --> finalOut[FINAL Vo OUTPUT]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: 3-mark short answers in Part A; 14-mark analytical questions in Part B with **internal choice (a/b)**. Marks are awarded strictly as per **KTU Bloom's Taxonomy mapping** to Course Outcomes.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`
**Define Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR). Why is it expressed in decibels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** CMRR is the ratio of the differential-mode gain $A_d$ to the common-mode gain $A_c$ of a differential amplifier. It quantifies the amplifier's ability to reject signals common to both inputs.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $\text{CMRR} = \vert A_d / A_c \vert$ (dimensionless) or $\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_d / A_c \vert)$.
- **Decibel rationale (0.5 marks):** CMRR is a *very large* ratio (often $10^3$ to $10^6$); expressing it in dB (typically 60–120 dB) provides a compact, human-friendly scale.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`
**List any two advantages of the instrumentation amplifier over the basic differential amplifier.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1.5 each):**
1. **Infinitely high / extremely high input impedance** at both inputs (set by op-amp gate), allowing direct connection to high-impedance sources like thermocouples and strain gauges without loading effects.
2. **Gain is variable via a single resistor** $R_g$, while maintaining perfect input symmetry — this is impossible in a basic subtractor where changing $R_2$ unbalances CMRR.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Analytical Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the output voltage expression for an op-amp based differential amplifier having finite open-loop gain $A$. Show that the result reduces to the ideal case as $A \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a differential amplifier with $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_1' = 10.1\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2' = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$, compute the differential gain $A_d$ and the common-mode gain $A_c$ in dB. Hence calculate the CMRR. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B-A

**Part (a) — Full Derivation (7 marks):**

**(1 mark) — Circuit diagram:** Single op-amp with $V_1$ connected to the inverting input through $R_1$, feedback resistor $R_2$ from output to inverting input, $V_2$ connected to non-inverting input through $R_1'$, and $R_2'$ from non-inverting input to ground.

**(1.5 marks) — Apply virtual open and superposition principles:** Since $I_{in} = 0$ at both op-amp inputs, the voltage at the non-inverting terminal is determined purely by the $R_1', R_2'$ divider:

$$V_+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{R_1' + R_2'}$$

**(1.5 marks) — KCL at inverting node:**

$$\frac{V_1 - V_-}{R_1} = \frac{V_- - V_o}{R_2} \implies V_- = \frac{V_1 R_2 + V_o R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**(2 marks) — Finite open-loop gain substitution:** With $V_o = A(V_+ - V_-)$, substituting and applying $R_1 = R_1'$, $R_2 = R_2'$:

$$V_o = \frac{A \cdot R_2}{A \cdot R_1 + R_1 + R_2}\,(V_2 - V_1)$$

**(1 mark) — Limit check:** As $A \to \infty$, the $A R_1$ term dominates the denominator, and we recover:

$$V_o \to \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,(V_2 - V_1) \quad \text{(ideal subtractor result)}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(2 marks) — Differential gain $A_d$:** For matched resistors, $A_d = R_2 / R_1 = 100\text{k} / 10\text{k} = 10$.

**(2 marks) — Common-mode gain $A_c$:** Use the *general* expression for the 1-op-amp diff amp with mismatch. The full transfer function (from KTU reference) is:
