<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
*Define a voltage follower. Mention any two of its salient features.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A voltage follower is an op-amp circuit in which the output is connected directly to the inverting input, producing a closed-loop voltage gain of **unity (1)**.

**Two salient features:**
1. It provides **very high input impedance** and **very low output impedance**, making it an excellent **impedance buffer**.
2. It exhibits **100% negative feedback** ($\beta = 1$), giving excellent linearity, wide bandwidth equal to the op-amp's gain-bandwidth product, and zero phase inversion between input and output.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
*Why is a voltage follower called a buffer amplifier? List one application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A voltage follower is called a **buffer amplifier** because it "buffers" or **isolates** a high-impedance source from a low-impedance load, preventing the load from drawing excessive current and disturbing the source signal. The voltage at the load equals the source voltage, but the op-amp's output stage supplies the required load current — effectively **transferring the signal without attenuation**.

**One application:** It is widely used between a **high-impedance sensor** (e.g., pH probe or piezoelectric transducer) and a **low-impedance amplifier stage** to prevent signal loss due to loading effects.

---

## 📝 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the closed-loop voltage gain of a non-inverting amplifier. Show that when $R_f = 0$ and $R_1 = \infty$, the circuit reduces to a **voltage follower**, and state its voltage gain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a voltage follower using an op-amp with open-loop gain $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$ and open-loop input impedance $Z_{in,OL} = 2\ \text{M}\Omega$, calculate the **closed-loop input impedance** and the **closed-loop output impedance** (assume $Z_{out,OL} = 75\ \Omega$). Comment on the significance of the results. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Derivation of Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain (7 marks):**

Consider a non-inverting amplifier with input $V_{in}$ at the non-inverting terminal ($V^+$), feedback resistor $R_f$ between output and inverting input ($V^-$), and resistor $R_1$ from $V^-$ to ground.

**Step 1:** Apply the virtual short rule:

$$V^+ = V^- = V_{in}$$

**Step 2:** Apply KCL at the inverting node (no current enters the op-amp input):

$$
\frac{V^- - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $V^- = V_{in}$ and rearrange:

$$
\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}
$$

**Step 4:** Cross-multiply:

$$
V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 (V_{out} - V_{in})
$$

**Step 5:** Expand and solve for $V_{out}/V_{in}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} R_f &= R_1 V_{out} - R_1 V_{in} \\[6pt]
R_1 V_{out} &= V_{in} R_f + R_1 V_{in} \\[6pt]
R_1 V_{out} &= V_{in} (R_f + R_1) \\[6pt]
\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} &= \frac{R_f + R_1}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Voltage Follower Condition:**

Setting $R_f = 0$ (short between output and $V^-$) and $R_1 = \infty$ (open from $V^-$ to ground):

$$
A_v = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} = 1
$$

**Conclusion:** The voltage follower has a closed-loop voltage gain of exactly **1**, meaning $V_{out} = V_{in}$. ✓

---

**(b) Closed-Loop Impedance Calculations (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Closed-Loop Input Impedance**

The general formula for closed-loop input impedance of a non-inverting amplifier:

$$
Z_{in,CL} = Z_{in,OL} \cdot (1 + A_{OL} \beta)
$$

For a voltage follower, the feedback factor is $\beta = 1$ (since 100% of the output is fed back). Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{in,CL} &= 2 \times 10^6 \cdot (1 + 2 \times 10^5 \cdot 1) \\[6pt]
          &= 2 \times 10^6 \cdot 200001 \\[6pt]
          &= 4.00002 \times 10^{11}\ \Omega \\[6pt]
          &\approx 400\ \text{G}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Closed-Loop Output Impedance**

The general formula:

$$
Z_{out,CL} = \frac{Z_{out,OL}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta}
$$

Substituting $\beta = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{out,CL} &= \frac{75}{1 + 2 \times 10^5} \\[6pt]
           &= \frac{75}{200001} \\[6pt]
           &\approx 3.75 \times 10^{-4}\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Significance (1 mark for proper discussion)**

- The closed-loop input impedance is **astronomically large** (≈400 GΩ), meaning the voltage follower draws **virtually no current** from the source. It does not "load" the source.
- The closed-loop output impedance is **negligibly small** (≈0.375 mΩ), meaning the op-amp behaves as a **near-ideal voltage source**, capable of driving heavy or varying loads without the output voltage drooping.
- Together, these properties make the voltage follower an **ideal impedance transformer** (high-Z to low-Z), which is its principal engineering value.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the operation of a **voltage follower** using an op-amp. State and justify the values of feedback and input resistors used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1 kHz, 2 V peak sine wave is applied to a voltage follower built around an op-amp having slew rate $SR = 0.5\ \text{V/µs}$. Determine the **maximum operating frequency** at which the output can faithfully reproduce the input without slew-rate induced distortion. Comment on whether 1 kHz operation is safe. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Circuit and Operation (7 marks):**

**Diagram description (text-rendered for answer script):**

> Draw an op-amp triangle. Mark the non-inverting input (+) on the left, inverting input (−) below it, and output on the right. Connect $V_{in}$ to the (+) input. Connect the output directly via a **plain wire (no resistor)** to the (−) input. Show the (−) input is **not** connected to ground via any resistor (i.e., $R_1$ is absent / open). Show ±V_CC power supply pins.

**Resistor values:**
- **Feedback resistor $R_f = 0\ \Omega$** (a short circuit / plain wire): Justified because the inverting input must "see" the full output voltage to enforce $V^- = V_{out}$.
- **Input resistor $R_1 = \infty$** (open circuit / no resistor): Justified because any finite $R_1$ would form a voltage divider with $R_f$ and yield a gain greater than 1, defeating the purpose of unity-gain operation.

**Operation:** The non-inverting input receives $V_{in}$, so $V^+ = V_{in}$. The output is wired to $V^-$, so $V^- = V_{out}$. The op-amp's internal open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ drives the differential input $(V^+ - V^-)$ to zero, enforcing $V_{out} = V_{in}$. The output stage can source/sink large currents, while the input draws none — hence the **buffering action**.

---

**(b) Slew Rate Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Slew Rate Limit Formula**

The maximum frequency at which a sine wave of peak amplitude $V_p$ can be reproduced without slew-rate distortion is:

$$
f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute Given Values**

Given: $SR = 0.5\ \text{V/µs} = 0.5 \times 10^6\ \text{V/s}$, $V_p = 2\ \text{V}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{max} &= \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \cdot 2} \\[6pt]
        &= \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{12.566} \\[6pt]
        &\approx 39{,}789\ \text{Hz} \\[6pt]
        &\approx 39.8\ \text{kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Comparison with 1 kHz Operating Frequency**

The applied frequency is $f = 1\ \text{kHz} = 1000\ \text{Hz}$, which is far below $f_{max} \approx 39.8\ \text{kHz}$.

**Conclusion:** The operating frequency of 1 kHz is **well within the slew-rate limit**. The op-amp can comfortably reproduce the 2 V peak / 1 kHz sine wave **without slew-rate induced distortion**. The output will faithfully follow the input.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the direct wire** between the output and inverting input. Examiners specifically look for this connection — without it, your circuit is just an open-loop op-amp (worth 0 marks for the diagram).
> 2. **Confusing the inverting and non-inverting terminals.** A voltage follower MUST have input at the **non-inverting (+)** terminal. If you draw it at the inverting terminal, you have built an inverting amplifier with infinite gain (unstable) — full marks lost.
> 3. **Writing $A_v = 0$** instead of $A_v = 1$. Some students mistakenly think "follower = 0 gain." This is wrong; it has *unity* gain.
> 4. **Omitting the slew-rate comment** in numerical problems. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for "commenting on practical implications." Always add a concluding sentence.
> 5. **Ignoring the offset voltage effect.** In a 7-mark calculation, mentioning the role of $V_{OS}$ and noise gain ($= 1$ for voltage follower) shows depth and earns extra marks.
> 6. **Writing $R_f = R_1 = 0$**. The correct answer is $R_f = 0$ AND $R_1 = \infty$ (open), not both zero. Many students mistakenly think both resistors must be shorted.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **voltage follower** is a unity-gain, non-inverting op-amp buffer with $A_v = 1$, $V_{out} = V_{in}$, and **zero phase shift**.
- ✅ It is constructed by connecting the output **directly** to the inverting input: $R_f = 0\ \Omega$ and $R_1 = \infty\ \Omega$.
- ✅ It provides **impedance transformation**: very high $Z_{in}$ (≈$A_{OL} \cdot Z_{in,OL}$) and very low $Z_{out}$ ($= Z_{out,OL} / (1 + A_{OL})$).
- ✅ The **feedback factor** $\beta = 1$ (100% negative feedback), giving the maximum linearity, the widest bandwidth (= GBW), and the most stable operation.
- ✅ The **voltage gain is 1**, but the **current and power gains are very large** — this is the heart of its "buffering" function.
- ✅ Primary applications: **impedance buffering**, **sensor signal isolation**, **cascading filter stages**, **ADC/DAC drivers**, and **active filter inter-stage coupling**.
- ✅ The **maximum undistorted sine wave frequency** is limited by slew rate: $f_{max} = SR / (2\pi V_p)$.
- ✅ Input offset voltage $V_{OS}$ appears directly at the output (noise gain = 1) — choose precision op-amps for low-offset applications.
- ✅ Compared to a voltage divider (passive buffer), the voltage follower provides **active, lossless, load-independent buffering** with no signal attenuation.
- ✅ Always remember the two golden rules of op-amp analysis: **virtual short** ($V^+ = V^-$) and **zero input current** ($I^+ = I^- = 0$).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When asked "compare voltage follower with emitter follower," state clearly that both are unity-gain buffers, but the op-amp follower offers **DC coupling, zero $V_{BE}$ drop, and adjustable DC level** (with $V_{OS}$ trim) — a feature the BJT emitter follower cannot match.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: V-I Converter (Grounded Load Type and Floating Load Type)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** V-I Converter (Grounded Load Type and Floating Load Type) # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Voltage-to-Current Converter (V-I Converter):** An active linear analog signal-conditioning circuit built around an operational amplifier (and optionally a power transistor) that converts a proportional input voltage signal **Vin** into a proportional output current **IL** such that the magnitude of **IL** depends ONLY on **Vin** and the input/sense resistor, and is **completely independent of the load impedance RL**. The unit of conversion is **Transconductance Gain (Gm = IL / Vin)**, measured in **Siemens (S)** or **mhos (℧)**. Because the output is a current (not a voltage), this topology is also known as a **Transconductance Amplifier (TCA)** or **Voltage-Controlled Current Source (VCCS)**. > [!NOTE] > **Classification Criterion — "Grounded" vs "Floating" Load:** > The classification depends entirely on **how the load resistor RL is connected to the rest of the circuit:** > - **Floating Load:** *Neither* terminal of RL is tied to ground (0 V). RL sits **inside the feedback loop**, typically between the op-amp output and the inverting input node. > - **Grounded Load:** *One* terminal of RL is hard-connected to a supply rail (**VCC** or **VEE**) acting as the circuit reference. The op-amp (often combined with a BJT/MOSFET) drives the *other* terminal. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hydraulic Valve" Intuition Imagine a precision water-control valve: - The **input voltage (Vin)** is the *rotation angle* of the valve handle (your control command). - The **input/sense resistor (R)** is the *fixed orifice size* that sets the maximum flow per unit rotation. - The **load (RL)** is the *downstream pipeline* — its length, narrowness, or blockage. - The **load current (IL)** is the *water flow rate* measured at the outlet. In an **ideal V-I converter**, no matter how clogged or wide the downstream pipeline (any RL), the flow rate stays locked to "handle-angle × orifice-size" — i.e., **IL = Vin / R**, perfectly independent of RL. In a **floating load** design, the valve and the flow-meter sit *inside* the same feedback loop — the meter corrects the handle the instant flow deviates. Simple, but the pipe can swing in voltage (it's "floating"). In a **grounded load** design, one end of the pipe is bolted to a fixed water-tower (supply rail); the op-amp + transistor acts as an *active current pump* that pushes exactly the right flow regardless of how high the tower's pressure changes. This is more practical for real-world actuators (motors, LEDs, solenoids) whose negative terminal must return to the supply rail. --- ## 1.3 KTU Syllabus Highlights > [!NOTE] > **Module-2 High-Priority Concepts (must know for KTU ESE):** > 1. Block diagram and topology of the **floating-load V-I converter** (op-amp + load in feedback). > 2. Block diagram and topology of the **grounded-load V-I converter** (op-amp + BJT, or op-amp + MOSFET). > 3. **Derivation of IL** for both types — a guaranteed 7-mark derivation question. > 4. Identification of which terminal of RL is at ground. > 5. Advantages and disadvantages of each topology. # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Floating-Load V-I Converter — Operational Logic This is the **simplest, most fundamental** V-I topology and almost always appears first in KTU derivations. ### Circuit Topology | Element | Connection | |---|---| | Input resistor **R** | Between source **Vin** and the inverting node (−) | | Non-inverting node (+) | Hard-grounded (0 V) | | Feedback element = **Load RL** | Between op-amp output and the inverting node (−) | > The load itself is the **feedback impedance** — that is why neither terminal sits at ground. The op-amp is a generic 741-class op-amp with high open-loop gain A, supplied from ±VCC = ±15 V. ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic - **Step 1 — Virtual Short:** Because of the heavy negative feedback (load RL closes the loop), the op-amp drives its output so that the differential input **Vd = V+ − V− ≈ 0**. With V+ grounded, **V− = 0 V** (virtual ground). - **Step 2 — Define load current direction:** By convention, define **IL as the current flowing from the op-amp output, through the load RL, into the inverting node**. The direction will be consistent with the standard textbook derivation. - **Step 3 — Current through the input resistor R:** The left side of R is at Vin; the right side is at virtual ground (0 V). Therefore **IR = (Vin − 0) / R = Vin / R**. - **Step 4 — KCL at the inverting node:** Because the op-amp's input bias current is ≈ 0 (FET/BJT-input op-amp), the same current IR must flow through RL. Hence **IL = IR = Vin / R**. - **Step 5 — Load-independence check:** RL does NOT appear in the final expression. **IL is set purely by Vin and R**, satisfying the definition of a true V-I converter. > [!TIP] > **Why does this work?** The op-amp is essentially *commanding* the voltage across RL to be whatever is needed so that the current equals Vin/R. If RL increases, the op-amp output voltage Vo rises proportionally — but IL stays locked. The load is **inside the loop**, so any RL variation is automatically corrected. --- ## 2.2 Grounded-Load V-I Converter — Operational Logic A **pure op-amp** cannot produce a true constant-current drive into a load that is hard-grounded, because the load lies *outside* the feedback path. The classical KTU-approved solution therefore uses a **two-active-device combination: an op-amp driving a power BJT (or MOSFET)** in a **voltage-to-current-with-grounded-load** arrangement. ### Circuit Topology | Element | Connection | |---|---| | Source **Vin** | Directly to the **non-inverting (+) input** of the op-amp | | Sense/emitter resistor **R** | Between the **emitter (E)** of the NPN BJT and **ground** | | **Inverting (−) input** | Wired to the **emitter (E)** node | | Op-amp output **Vo** | Drives the **base (B)** of the NPN BJT | | **Load RL** | Between **+VCC** (positive supply rail) and the **collector (C)** of the BJT | ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic - **Step 1 — Virtual short again:** With feedback through the emitter node, **V− = V+ = Vin**, so the **emitter sits exactly at Vin volts**. - **Step 2 — Current through R:** The emitter resistor R connects V− = Vin to ground (0 V). Hence the emitter current is **IE = (Vin − 0) / R = Vin / R**. - **Step 3 — Transistor action:** For a BJT with large current gain β (typically 100–500 for BC547/2N2222), **IC ≈ IE**. The base current IB is negligible compared to IC. - **Step 4 — Load current:** The entire collector current flows through the load (which is the only path from VCC into the collector), so **IL = IC ≈ IE = Vin / R**. - **Step 5 — Load-independence:** Once again, RL has vanished from the expression. The BJT *absorbs* any change in VCC − VCE (caused by changing RL) by adjusting VBE, but IL remains **Vin / R**. > [!TIP] > **The transistor's role:** It acts as a *current buffer* — the op-amp only has to supply the tiny base current IB = IL / β, while the BJT handles the heavy load current IL. This makes the circuit practical for driving **real-world loads** like motors, relays, LEDs, and solenoids whose negative terminal must return to VCC or ground. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Topology | Key Equation | Direction Convention | Load Independence? | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Floating-load V-I converter | $I_L = -\dfrac{V_{in}}{R}$ | IL flows from inverting node → through RL → to op-amp output | ✓ Yes, RL absent | | 2 | Grounded-load V-I converter (Op-amp + NPN BJT) | $I_L = +\dfrac{V_{in}}{R}$ | IL flows from +VCC → through RL → into collector → out emitter → through R → to ground | ✓ Yes, RL absent | | 3 | Transconductance Gain | $G_m = \dfrac{I_L}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | Units: Siemens (S) or A/V | — | | 4 | Output impedance of ideal VCCS | $Z_{out} \rightarrow \infty$ | Thevenin/Norton equivalent | Theoretically infinite | | 5 | Op-amp output saturation limit | $\vert V_o \vert \leq V_{sat} \approx \pm 13\text{ V}$ (for ±15 V supply) | Constraints the maximum IL | Limits $I_{L,max} = V_{sat}/R$ | | 6 | Practical IL range (grounded load) | $I_{L,min} = 0, \quad I_{L,max} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_L} \text{ (BJT cutoff to saturation)}$ | Limits set by transistor | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign convention warning:** Many KTU papers present the floating-load result as $I_L = -V_{in}/R$. The minus sign simply means the **reference direction of IL was chosen opposite to the actual electron-flow direction**. The magnitude $\vert I_L \vert = V_{in}/R$ is what matters. Always quote the magnitude and mention the assumed direction explicitly to grab full marks. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility V-I converters are the workhorse of **current-mode analog signal processing**: - **Constant-current battery chargers** — Li-ion and lead-acid chargers require a precise IL profile regardless of battery internal resistance or state-of-charge. - **LED driver circuits** — high-brightness LEDs must be driven with constant current (not constant voltage) to prevent thermal runaway and ensure uniform brightness. - **Strain-gauge and RTD signal conditioning** — sensor resistance varies with temperature/strain, but a constant current through them produces a voltage proportional to the measured quantity. - **Process-control 4–20 mA current loops** — industrial sensors transmit analog measurements as a current (not voltage) so the signal is immune to long-cable resistance. - **Electroplating and electrolysis** — uniform metal deposition requires IL independent of electrolyte resistance. - **Deflection yokes in CRT displays** and **solenoid/relay drivers** in automotive ECUs. # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Floating-Load V-I Converter ### Circuit Restatement - Input voltage **Vin** connected through resistor **R** to the **inverting input (−)** of an ideal op-amp. - **Non-inverting input (+)** connected directly to **ground (0 V)**. - **Load resistor RL** placed in the **feedback path** — one terminal at op-amp output, the other at the inverting node. - We take the load current **IL** to flow from the **inverting node (−) through RL into the op-amp output node**. ### Derivation Steps **Step 1 — Apply the ideal-op-amp virtual-short principle.** For an op-amp with very large open-loop gain AOL and negative feedback through RL, the differential input voltage is forced to zero: $$ V_d = V_+ - V_- \approx 0 $$ Because the non-inverting input is grounded, $V_+ = 0$. Therefore: $$ V_- = V_+ = 0 \quad \text{(virtual ground)} $$ > **Text-row explanation:** The op-amp adjusts its output VO so that the inverting input sits at exactly 0 V, matching the grounded non-inverting input. This is the foundational property of negative feedback. **Step 2 — Compute the current through the input resistor R.** The left terminal of R is at potential $V_{in}$, and the right terminal is at the virtual ground (0 V). Using Ohm's law with the assumed current direction (from Vin → through R → into the inverting node): $$ I_R = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_-}{R} = \dfrac{V_{in} - 0}{R} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R} $$ > **Text-row explanation:** The current IR is the *command* current set by the input voltage and the input resistor. **Step 3 — Apply KCL at the inverting node.** At the inverting node, three currents meet: 1. $I_R$ flowing **into** the node from the input resistor (magnitude Vin/R). 2. $I_L$ flowing **into** the node from the load resistor RL (this is our assumed direction). 3. The op-amp's input bias current $I_B$, which is ≈ 0 for an ideal op-amp (and very small — typically nA — for real FET-input op-amps). KCL (sum of currents entering = sum of currents leaving): $$ I_R + I_L = I_B \approx 0 $$ Therefore: $$ I_L = -I_R = -\dfrac{V_{in}}{R} $$ > **Text-row explanation:** The negative sign indicates that the *actual* current direction in RL is opposite to our assumed reference direction. The load current physically flows from the op-amp output, through RL, into the inverting node, then out through R back to the Vin source. **Step 4 — Verify load-independence by deriving the op-amp output voltage.** For completeness, we find the required output voltage Vo: $$ V_o = V_- + I_L \cdot R_L = 0 + \left(-\dfrac{V_{in}}{R}\right) \cdot R_L = -\dfrac{V_{in} \cdot R_L}{R} $$ > **Text-row explanation:** As RL changes, the op-amp adjusts VO linearly so that the *same* IL is maintained. For example, if RL doubles, VO doubles — but IL is unchanged. This is the essence of load-independence. **Final boxed result (floating-load):** $$ \boxed{\,I_L = -\dfrac{V_{in}}{R}\,} $$ with magnitude $\vert I_L \vert = V_{in}/R$, **independent of $R_L$**. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Grounded-Load V-I Converter (Op-amp + NPN BJT) ### Circuit Restatement - Source voltage **Vin** applied directly to the **non-inverting (+) input** of the op-amp. - **Inverting (−) input** connected to the **emitter (E) node** of an NPN BJT. - **Emitter resistor R** between emitter (E) and **ground**. - Op-amp output **VO** connected to the **base (B)** of the BJT. - **Load RL** between **+VCC** and the **collector (C)** of the BJT. - Transistor parameters: large current gain β, so $I_C \approx I_E$ and $I_B = I_C / \beta$ is negligible. ### Derivation Steps **Step 1 — Identify the feedback path and apply the virtual-short property.** The feedback loop runs from op-amp output → base → emitter → through R to ground, with the emitter node tied back to the inverting input. With negative feedback active: $$ V_- = V_+ = V_{in} $$ > **Text-row explanation:** The op-amp is configured so that its output VO drives the BJT base until the *emitter voltage* exactly equals Vin. The emitter sits in the feedback path and is forced to "track" the non-inverting input. **Step 2 — Compute the emitter current IE.** The emitter resistor R has one terminal at the emitter node (potential V− = Vin) and the other at ground (0 V). By Ohm's law, with current IE flowing from emitter → through R → to ground: $$ I_E = \dfrac{V_- - 0}{R} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R} $$ > **Text-row explanation:** This is the *sense* current — it is the current that the circuit *forces* to equal Vin/R by virtue of the feedback loop. The emitter resistor is sometimes called the "sense resistor" for this reason. **Step 3 — Relate IE to IC using the BJT equation.** For a BJT in the active region, the fundamental current relationships are: $$ I_E = I_C + I_B $$ $$ I_C = \beta \cdot I_B $$ Eliminating IB: $$ I_E = I_C + \dfrac{I_C}{\beta} = I_C \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right) = I_C \cdot \dfrac{\beta + 1}{\beta} $$ Solving for IC: $$ I_C = I_E \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{\beta + 1} \approx I_E \quad \text{(for } \beta \gg 1\text{)} $$ > **Text-row explanation:** For a typical small-signal BJT with β ≈ 100–500, the ratio β/(β+1) is between 0.99 and 0.998 — essentially unity. We can therefore approximate $I_C \approx I_E$ with negligible error (under 1%). **Step 4 — Equate load current IL with collector current IC.** By Kirchhoff's Current Law at the collector node, the only current path into the collector is through the load RL from the +VCC supply (the base draws its tiny IB current from the op-amp output, not from the collector). Therefore: $$ I_L = I_C \approx I_E = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R} $$ > **Text-row explanation:** The load current IL equals the collector current IC because RL is the only branch feeding the collector. The BJT acts as a *current buffer* — the op-amp only supplies the small base current, but the BJT handles the heavy load current at the collector. **Step 5 — Verify load-independence by computing VCE.** The collector-to-emitter voltage adjusts automatically based on RL: $$ V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_L \cdot R_L $$ For the BJT to stay in the active region (and the formula to remain valid), we require: $$ V_{CE} \geq V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2 \text{ V} $$ > **Text-row explanation:** If RL becomes so large that $I_L \cdot R_L$ approaches VCC, VCE drops below VCE(sat) and the BJT enters saturation. Beyond that, IL can no longer be maintained constant — this defines the maximum permissible RL. **Final boxed result (grounded-load):** $$ \boxed{\,I_L = +\dfrac{V_{in}}{R}\,} $$ with $\vert I_L \vert = V_{in}/R$, **independent of $R_L$**, valid as long as the BJT remains in the active region. --- ## 3.3 Side-by-Side Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Floating-Load V-I Converter | Grounded-Load V-I Converter | |---|---|---| | Active devices used | 1 op-amp only | 1 op-amp + 1 BJT (or MOSFET) | | Position of RL | Inside the feedback loop | Outside the op-amp loop, in the collector circuit | | Grounding of RL | Neither terminal grounded | One terminal (collector side, via +VCC) acts as reference; load's *other* end at collector | | Driving capability | Low (limited by op-amp output current ≈ 20–25 mA for 741) | High (limited by BJT power dissipation, easily 100 mA – several amps) | | Sign of IL | $I_L = -V_{in}/R$ (depending on assumed reference) | $I_L = +V_{in}/R$ | | Output voltage swing | $V_o = -V_{in} R_L / R$ (can exceed supply rails if RL large) | $V_{CE}$ adjusts between VCE(sat) and (VCC − small drop) | | Typical application | Lab signal generation, instrumentation | Motor/LED/solenoid drivers, battery chargers | | Polarity of output current | Bidirectional (depending on sign of Vin) | **Unidirectional** (NPN → sinks current from load; PNP → sources) | | Sensitivity to load changes | Perfect (RL cancels in equation) | Perfect in active region; breaks down near saturation | # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The op-amp-based V-I converter is a physical analog circuit that cannot be rendered literally in a text-based Mermaid diagram. Per the protocol, the diagrams below use **block-level functional-architecture** notation that maps signal flow and node interconnections, mirroring the schematic in KTU textbooks. ## 4.1 Floating-Load V-I Converter — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeVin["Vin Source"] -- R --> nodeInv["Inverting Node (-)
Virtual Ground 0V"] nodeGND["Ground (0V)"] --> nodeNonInv["Non-inverting Node (+)
Tied to Ground"] nodeInv -- RL Load --> nodeVout["Op-amp Output Node Vo"] nodeVout --> opampBody["Internal Op-amp
741-class"] opampBody -- Feedback Vo --> nodeInv nodeInv --> currentSense["Current IR = Vin/R
flowing into node"] nodeVout --> currentReturn["Current IL returning
via RL into node"] classDef nodeStyle fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gndStyle fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class nodeVin,nodeInv,nodeVout,opampBody,currentSense,currentReturn nodeStyle class nodeGND,nodeNonInv gndStyle ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The blue path shows the **signal flow** (Vin → R → inverting node → through RL → op-amp output → back through op-amp internal gain → output node). - The red path shows the **ground reference** anchored at the non-inverting input, which forces V− = 0 V. - Notice that **RL sits inside the feedback loop**, which is why the load current IL is forced to equal the input current IR — independent of RL's value. ## 4.2 Grounded-Load V-I Converter (Op-amp + BJT) — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeVin["Vin Source"] --> nodeNonInv["Non-inverting Node (+)
Reference Voltage"] nodeNonInv -- Virtual Short --> nodeInv["Inverting Node (-)
Forced Equal to Vin"] nodeInv --> emitterNode["Emitter Node of NPN BJT"] emitterNode -- R Sense Resistor --> nodeGND["Ground (0V)"] emitterNode --> bjtActive["NPN BJT Active Region
IE = Vin/R, IC approx IE"] nodeOpOut["Op-amp Output Vo"] --> bjtBase["Base of BJT"] nodeOpOut --> opampStage["Internal Op-amp
Drives Base"] opampStage -- Negative Feedback --> nodeInv nodeVCC["+VCC Supply Rail"] -- Load RL --> collectorNode["Collector of BJT"] collectorNode --> bjtActive bjtActive -- IL = IC flows through RL --> nodeVCC classDef sigStyle fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gndStyle fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pwrStyle fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef devStyle fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#FBBC04,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class nodeVin,nodeNonInv,nodeInv,emitterNode,nodeOpOut,opampStage,collectorNode sigStyle class nodeGND gndStyle class nodeVCC pwrStyle class bjtActive,bjtBase,RL_Text,devStyle ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Yellow** elements are the active semiconductor devices (op-amp + BJT). - **Green** path is the +VCC supply feeding the load. - **Blue** path is the small-signal control loop: Vin sets the reference, op-amp drives the BJT base, and the emitter node (tied back to the inverting input) closes the negative feedback. - **Red** is the ground reference — note that **only R's lower end is grounded**; the load's *upper* terminal is at +VCC and *lower* terminal is at the collector (not at ground). This is why we call it a *grounded load* in the KTU sense: the load returns to a fixed rail rather than floating. ## 4.3 Signal-Flow Comparison — Which Circuit for Which Application? ```mermaid flowchart LR decisionStart["Need a V-I Converter?"] --> q1{"Load must return
to ground rail?"} q1 -- No --> floating["Use FLOATING-LOAD circuit
Op-amp only, load in feedback
IL = -Vin slash R"] q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Output current
more than 25 mA?"} q2 -- No --> groundedSimple["Use GROUNDED-LOAD circuit
Op-amp + BJT or MOSFET
IL = +Vin slash R"] q2 -- Yes --> groundedPower["Use GROUNDED-LOAD circuit
with power BJT or Darlington
Add heatsink if Pdiss greater than 0.5W"] classDef startStyle fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#FBBC04,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decisionStyle fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resultStyle fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class decisionStart startStyle class q1,q2 decisionStyle class floating,groundedSimple,groundedPower resultStyle ``` # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign convention trap:** Many students blindly write $I_L = V_{in}/R$ for the floating-load circuit and lose 1–2 marks. The textbook answer is $I_L = -V_{in}/R$ (negative sign). State the **assumed reference direction** of $I_L$ explicitly before writing the equation — examiners reward this discipline. > 2. **Forgetting the BJT in the grounded-load derivation:** A common error is to derive a *grounded-load* V-I converter using an op-amp *alone* and then forget to include the BJT collector-emitter equation $I_C \approx I_E$. Always mention the role of the **transconductance buffer (BJT)** explicitly. > 3. **Drawing the load between output and ground in a floating-load circuit:** This is a circuit-drawing mistake that costs full marks even if the derivation is correct. The load must be **between output and inverting node** in the floating configuration. > 4. **Forgetting the virtual-short assumption:** Always start your derivation with "$V_- = V_+ = 0$ due to virtual short" — this single line shows the examiner you understand the *Why*, not just the *What*. --- ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **"Differentiate between floating-load and grounded-load voltage-to-current converters with one example application each."** **Model Answer (≈ 90 words):** > A **floating-load V-I converter** has its load resistor RL placed *inside the feedback loop* between the op-amp output and the inverting input, so neither terminal of RL is grounded. It uses a single op-amp and gives $I_L = -V_{in}/R$. It is used in **laboratory instrumentation and signal generators**. > > A **grounded-load V-I converter** has one terminal of RL tied to the supply rail (+VCC) and uses an op-amp *plus* a BJT/MOSFET current buffer. The output is $I_L = V_{in}/R$. It is used in **LED drivers, motor-speed controllers, and constant-current battery chargers**. ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **"Why is the load current IL in an ideal V-I converter independent of the load resistance RL? Explain using the floating-load topology."** **Model Answer (≈ 70 words):** > In the floating-load V-I converter, the load RL is placed **inside the negative feedback loop** of the op-amp. The op-amp continuously adjusts its output voltage $V_o$ so that the inverting node remains at **virtual ground (0 V)**. As a result, the current through the input resistor R is fixed at $I_R = V_{in}/R$. By KCL at the inverting node (no current enters the op-amp), $I_L = I_R = V_{in}/R$, which contains **no RL term**. Hence IL depends only on Vin and R, making it perfectly independent of RL. --- ## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. ### ⭐ Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **"With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the load current IL in a **floating-load voltage-to-current converter**. State the advantages and limitations of this topology."** **Model Answer Outline:** #### (a) Circuit Diagram and Explanation (7 Marks) - Draw the op-amp with Vin connected through R to the (−) input, (+) input grounded, and RL between the output and the (−) input. *(2 marks for neat diagram with labels)* - Identify the feedback path: **load RL is the feedback element**. *(1 mark)* - State the virtual-short property and define the assumed current direction. *(2 marks)* - Explain why neither terminal of RL is grounded (hence "floating"). *(2 marks)* #### (b) Derivation of IL and Discussion (7 Marks) - Apply virtual short: $V_- = V_+ = 0$ *(1 mark)* - Compute current through R: $I_R = (V_{in} - 0)/R = V_{in}/R$ *(1 mark)* - Apply KCL at inverting node: $I_L = -I_R = -V_{in}/R$ *(2 marks)* - Show $V_o = -V_{in} R_L / R$ and explain that $V_o$ adjusts automatically as $R_L$ varies *(1 mark)* - Advantages: (i) Simple single-op-amp design, (ii) Perfect load-independence, (iii) Bidirectional current *(1 mark)* - Limitations: (i) Cannot drive heavy loads (limited by op-amp output current ~25 mA), (ii) Output voltage $V_o$ can exceed supply rails for large $R_L$, causing saturation *(1 mark)* **Final boxed answer:** $\boxed{I_L = -V_{in}/R}$ --- ### ⭐ Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **"Explain the operation of a **grounded-load voltage-to-current converter** using an op-amp and an NPN BJT. Derive the expression for the load current and discuss why this topology is preferred for driving real-world loads like DC motors."** **Model Answer Outline:** #### (a) Circuit Diagram and Operational Principle (7 Marks) - Draw the circuit: Vin → (+) input; R between emitter and ground; load RL between +VCC and collector; op-amp output → base. *(2 marks for diagram)* - Explain the feedback path: emitter node tied back to (−) input through the inverting terminal. *(1 mark)* - Explain the role of the BJT as a **current buffer**: op-amp only supplies base current IB = IL/β, while BJT handles heavy load current. *(2 marks)* - Explain why the load is "grounded" — one end at +VCC supply rail, which acts as the circuit reference. *(2 marks)* #### (b) Derivation of IL and Discussion (7 Marks) - Apply virtual short: $V_- = V_+ = V_{in}$, so the emitter sits at $V_{in}$ *(1 mark)* - Compute emitter current: $I_E = (V_{in} - 0)/R = V_{in}/R$ *(1 mark)* - Apply BJT relation: $I_C = \beta I_B = \beta I_E / (\beta+1) \approx I_E$ for large β *(2 marks)* - Conclude: $I_L = I_C = V_{in}/R$ *(1 mark)* - Discuss active-region constraint: $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_L R_L \geq V_{CE(sat)}$ *(1 mark)* - Justify preference for DC motor drive: high current capability, easy heatsinking, unidirectional control, simple PWM-compatible interface *(1 mark)* **Final boxed answer:** $\boxed{I_L = +V_{in}/R}$ --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bullet-Form Rapid Summary:** ### Floating-Load V-I Converter - **Topology:** Single op-amp; RL between output and (−) input; (+) grounded; Vin through R to (−). - **Result:** $I_L = -V_{in}/R$ (sign depends on assumed current direction). - **Why it works:** RL is *inside* the feedback loop → op-amp adjusts $V_o$ to keep $I_L$ constant. - **Use case:** Instrumentation, lab signal generation. - **Drawback:** Limited to ~25 mA; output can saturate for large RL. ### Grounded-Load V-I Converter - **Topology:** Op-amp + NPN BJT (or MOSFET); RL between +VCC and collector; R between emitter and ground. - **Result:** $I_L = +V_{in}/R$. - **Why it works:** Emitter is *inside* the feedback loop → forces $I_E = V_{in}/R$ → BJT buffers it to RL. - **Use case:** LED drivers, motor speed control, battery chargers, solenoids. - **Active-region constraint:** $V_{CC} - I_L R_L \geq V_{CE(sat)}$. - **Polarity:** NPN BJT sinks current from load (load's high side at +VCC); swap to PNP for sourcing. ### Common Pitfalls to Avoid - ❌ Forgetting the negative sign in floating-load equation. - ❌ Drawing RL between output and ground in the floating-load circuit. - ❌ Using only an op-amp (no BJT) and claiming a true grounded-load constant-current drive — impossible in practice. - ❌ Confusing "grounded load" with "load's lower terminal at 0 V" — in KTU convention, "grounded" means *load returns to a fixed supply rail*, not necessarily 0 V. - ❌ Writing $I_L = V_{in}/R_L$ — this is the Ohm's-law error and guarantees zero marks. ### Must-Memorize Equation Pair $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Floating load:} \quad & I_L = -\dfrac{V_{in}}{R} \\ \text{Grounded load:} \quad & I_L = +\dfrac{V_{in}}{R} \end{aligned} $$ Both yield **load-independent constant current** — this is the *defining feature* of any V-I converter and the most-tested concept in KTU Module-2.

topics.title: I-V Converter, Instrumentation Amplifier (3 Op-Amp), Op-Amp Integrator, Op-Amp Differentiator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** I-V Converter, Instrumentation Amplifier (3 Op-Amp), Op-Amp Integrator, Op-Amp Differentiator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Current-to-Voltage Converter (I-V Converter / Transimpedance Amplifier)

### Formal Definition
A **Current-to-Voltage Converter (I-V Converter)**, also known as a **Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA)**, is an operational amplifier circuit that accepts an input **current signal** $I_{in}$ and converts it into a proportional **output voltage** $V_{out}$ using a feedback resistor $R_f$. The ideal transfer relationship is:

$$V_{out} = -I_{in} \, R_f$$

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a water flow meter that converts the rate of water flow (current, in amperes) into a pressure reading on a gauge (voltage, in volts). The I-V converter does exactly this for electrical signals. A photodiode (current source) connected to it produces a voltage proportional to the light falling on it — this is the heart of every light-meter, CT scanner, and optical communication receiver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The I-V converter is fundamental to **sensor interfacing** (photodiodes, phototransistors, electrochemical sensors) where the source naturally delivers current rather than voltage. Its near-zero input impedance (virtual ground) prevents the current source from being loaded.

---

## 1.2 Three Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)

### Formal Definition
The **3 Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier** is a high-precision, differential-input, single-ended-output amplifier built from **three internally-matched op-amps** and **seven precision resistors**. It provides extremely high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**, high input impedance on both inputs, and a gain that is adjustable via a **single external resistor** $R_g$.

The ideal closed-form transfer equation is:

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

If the difference-amplifier stage is unity-gain matched ($R_2 = R_3$), this simplifies to:

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

### Intuitive Analogy
Picture a sensitive weighing scale that must measure the **difference** between two nearly equal forces, while completely ignoring any background vibration (common-mode noise). The INA acts like a "smart scale" — two buffer stages first sense the small difference and amplify it, while a final stage rejects all common disturbances. This is why **ECG machines**, **strain-gauge bridges**, and **industrial process controls** all use INAs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Property:** Because $R_g$ appears only in the **first stage** (the differential gain stage) and not in the **difference amplifier**, the CMRR of the INA is **independent of the gain setting**. This is the unique advantage of the 3 op-amp topology over a simple difference amplifier.

---

## 1.3 Op-Amp Integrator (Miller Integrator)

### Formal Definition
An **Op-Amp Integrator** is a circuit that performs the **time-domain mathematical integration** of an input signal $V_{in}(t)$. It is realized by replacing the feedback resistor of an inverting amplifier with a **capacitor** $C$, while a resistor $R$ sets the input current.

The governing time-domain equation is:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{R C} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau + V_{out}(0)$$

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of an integrator as a **water tank with an electronically controlled inlet and outlet**. The input voltage controls how fast water flows in (via $R$), and the capacitor stores the accumulated charge. The output voltage is the *history* of all the input — past values are "added up" over time. In frequency domain, an integrator acts like a **low-pass filter** with a $-20 \, \text{dB/decade}$ roll-off, ideal for converting square waves into triangle waves.

> [!WARNING]
> **Drift Problem (KTU Favorite Question):** Under ideal DC conditions ($V_{in} = 0$), any tiny op-amp input offset current integrates indefinitely, causing the output to **saturate** to the supply rail. This is mitigated by the **Practical Integrator** (lossy integrator), which adds a large feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C$.

---

## 1.4 Op-Amp Differentiator (Miller Differentiator)

### Formal Definition
An **Op-Amp Differentiator** is a circuit that produces an output proportional to the **time derivative** of the input signal. It uses a **capacitor** $C$ at the input and a **resistor** $R$ in the feedback path.

The governing time-domain equation is:

$$V_{out}(t) = -R C \, \frac{d V_{in}(t)}{d t}$$

In the frequency domain (for $V_{in} = V_m \sin \omega t$):

$$V_{out} = -RC \, \omega V_m \cos(\omega t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert V_{out} \vert = R C \, \omega \, V_m$$

### Intuitive Analogy
A differentiator is like the **speedometer of a car**, not the odometer. The odometer (integrator) tells you the *total distance* traveled; the speedometer (differentiator) tells you the *rate of change* of position — your *velocity*. Sharper changes in input → larger output. In the frequency domain, it behaves like a **high-pass filter** with $+20 \, \text{dB/decade}$ gain growth, which unfortunately also amplifies high-frequency noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The ideal differentiator has **unbounded gain at high frequencies**, making it highly susceptible to noise and prone to oscillation. The **Practical Differentiator** adds a small input resistor $R_1$ in series with $C$ to limit high-frequency gain and stabilize the circuit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumed Ideal Op-Amp Properties (Used in All Derivations)

| # | Property | Mathematical Consequence |
|---|----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Infinite open-loop gain ($A \to \infty$) | $V^+ \approx V^-$ (Virtual Short) |
| 2 | Infinite input impedance ($Z_{in} \to \infty$) | Input current into op-amp terminals = **0** |
| 3 | Zero output impedance ($Z_{out} = 0$) | Output voltage independent of load |
| 4 | Infinite CMRR | Only differential component is amplified |
| 5 | Infinite bandwidth | Frequency-independent gain |
| 6 | Zero input offset voltage and bias current | Output is purely a function of input signal |

## 2.2 Block-by-Block Operational Analysis

### A. I-V Converter — Step-by-Step Logic
- **Step 1:** Input current source $I_{in}$ is connected to the **inverting terminal**; the non-inverting terminal is tied to ground.
- **Step 2:** Because of the **virtual short**, the inverting terminal is also at $0 \, \text{V}$ (virtual ground). This forces the **entire current** $I_{in}$ to flow through $R_f$ — none is consumed by the op-amp.
- **Step 3:** Apply **KCL at the inverting node**:
$$I_{in} = I_f = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f}$$
- **Step 4:** Rearrange to obtain $V_{out}$.
- **Why It Works:** Maintaining the inverting input at $0 \, \text{V}$ regardless of $I_{in}$ is the key. This means the source sees **zero input impedance**, which is exactly what a high-impedance current source requires to operate in its linear region.

### B. 3 Op-Amp INA — Three-Stage Logic
- **Stage 1 (Buffer + Differential Gain):** Op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$ form a **symmetric differential amplifier** with gain controlled by $R_g$. Both inputs see **infinite impedance**.
- **Stage 2 (Common-Mode Tracking):** The voltages at the inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$ follow $V_1$ and $V_2$ respectively (virtual short). Common-mode signals (i.e., $V_1 = V_2 = V_{cm}$) produce **zero** current through $R_g$, hence zero differential output. This is the **CMRR generation mechanism**.
- **Stage 3 (Difference Amplifier):** Op-amp $A_3$ with $R_2, R_3$ converts the differential signal $(V_{A1} - V_{A2})$ into a single-ended output. The matched resistor ratio $\frac{R_3}{R_2}$ provides the difference-amplifier CMRR.

### C. Op-Amp Integrator — Logic
- **Step 1:** Input voltage $V_{in}$ drives current $I_{in} = V_{in}/R$ into the inverting node.
- **Step 2:** This current must flow into capacitor $C$ (since none flows into the op-amp).
- **Step 3:** The capacitor relationship $I_C = C \, \frac{dV_C}{dt}$ applied across $C$ (which spans from the inverting node at $0 \, \text{V}$ to $V_{out}$) yields a **time-domain integration** relationship.
- **Step 4:** The negative sign arises because increasing $V_{in}$ *decreases* $V_{out}$ (inverting action).

### D. Op-Amp Differentiator — Logic
- **Step 1:** Input voltage $V_{in}$ drives a current through $C$ that is proportional to its **rate of change**: $I_{in} = C \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$.
- **Step 2:** This same current flows through feedback resistor $R$.
- **Step 3:** The voltage drop across $R$ (with sign) is the output. Since the input current depends on the **derivative** of $V_{in}$, the output becomes the **derivative** of $V_{in}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety:** In all tables below, the absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. Do not use the vertical pipe `\vert` symbol directly in table cells.

| Circuit | Output Equation | Transfer Function $H(s) = V_{out}(s)/V_{in}(s)$ | Time Constant / Pole |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I-V Converter** | $V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f$ | Not applicable (input is current) | Bandwidth set by GBW of op-amp |
| **3 Op-Amp INA** | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$ | $A_v = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\right)$ | Adjustable via $R_g$ |
| **Ideal Integrator** | $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC} \displaystyle\int V_{in}(t)\, dt$ | $H(s) = -\dfrac{1}{sRC}$ | Pole at **origin** (DC) |
| **Practical Integrator** | $V_{out}(t) \approx -\dfrac{1}{RC} \displaystyle\int V_{in}\, dt$ for $f \gg f_a$ | $H(s) = -\dfrac{R_f/R}{(1 + sR_f C)(1 + sR_f'/R_f)}$ | Pole at $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$ |
| **Ideal Differentiator** | $V_{out}(t) = -RC \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | $H(s) = -sRC$ | Zero at **origin** |
| **Practical Differentiator** | $V_{out} \approx -RC \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ for $f \ll f_b$ | $H(s) = -\dfrac{sRC}{(1 + sR_1 C)(1 + sRC_f)}$ | Pole at $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C}$ |

### Key Frequency-Domain Magnitudes

| Input Signal | Integrator $\vert V_{out} \vert$ | Differentiator $\vert V_{out} \vert$ |
|---|---|---|
| DC $V_m$ | Linearly grows until saturation | **0** (derivative of constant is 0) |
| Sine $V_m \sin \omega t$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\omega RC}$ | $\omega RC \, V_m$ |
| Square Wave $V_m$ | **Triangle Wave** with peak $\dfrac{V_m \, T}{4RC}$ | **Spikes** of amplitude $\dfrac{2 V_m RC}{T}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **I-V Converter:** Photodiode amplifiers in fiber-optic receivers, pH sensor readouts, smoke detectors, particle-physics instrumentation.
- **Instrumentation Amplifier:** ECG / EEG / EMG biopotential amplifiers, weigh scales using strain-gauge bridges, thermocouple amplifiers in process industries.
- **Integrator:** Analog computers, ramp generators, ADC integrators (dual-slope), active filters (low-pass), function generators (square → triangle conversion).
- **Differentiator:** Edge detectors in digital circuits, FM demodulators, PID controller D-term, rate-of-change alarms in industrial safety systems.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of I-V Converter Output

We start with the inverting op-amp topology: input current source $I_{in}$ connected to the inverting terminal, non-inverting terminal grounded, feedback resistor $R_f$ between the inverting terminal and the output.

**Step 1 — Apply the Virtual Short Concept**

Because of the infinite open-loop gain of the op-amp, the voltage difference between its two input terminals is zero. Since the non-inverting input is at ground, the inverting input is also at $0 \, \text{V}$. This is called the **virtual ground**:

$$V^- = V^+ = 0 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node**

The currents entering the node must sum to zero. The currents are: input current $I_{in}$ (entering) and feedback current $I_f$ (leaving through $R_f$ toward $V_{out}$):

$$I_{in} = I_f$$

**Step 3 — Express the Feedback Current in Terms of $V_{out}$**

The feedback resistor connects the inverting node (at $0 \, \text{V}$) to the output (at $V_{out}$). By Ohm's law:

$$I_f = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and Solve**

$$I_{in} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Multiplying both sides by $-R_f$:

$$V_{out} = -I_{in} \, R_f$$

This is the **I-V transfer equation** of the Transimpedance Amplifier.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the 3 Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

The circuit consists of two input buffer op-amps ($A_1, A_2$) with gain-setting resistor $R_g$ between their inverting inputs, followed by a unity-gain difference amplifier ($A_3$) with resistors $R_2, R_2', R_3, R_3'$.

**Step 1 — Input Voltages at the Inverting Terminals**

By the virtual short principle, the inverting input of $A_1$ is forced to $V_1$, and the inverting input of $A_2$ is forced to $V_2$:

$$V^-_{A1} = V_1 \quad \text{and} \quad V^-_{A2} = V_2$$

**Step 2 — Voltage Across the Gain Resistor $R_g$**

Since $R_g$ is connected between these two inverting terminals:

$$V_{R_g} = V_1 - V_2$$

**Step 3 — Current Through $R_g$**

Using Ohm's law, the current flowing from $A_1$'s inverting node through $R_g$ to $A_2$'s inverting node is:

$$I_g = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}$$

**Step 4 — Same Current Flows Through Both $R_1$ Resistors**

Because the op-amp input currents are zero (infinite input impedance), the entire current $I_g$ must continue through the feedback resistor $R_1$ of $A_1$ and then through the feedback resistor $R_1$ of $A_2$. The current is the same throughout this series path:

$$I_{R_1} = I_g = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Output of $A_1$**

The output of $A_1$ equals the voltage at its inverting input plus the voltage drop across its feedback $R_1$ (in the direction of current flow):

$$V_{A1} = V_1 + I_g \, R_1 = V_1 + R_1 \left(\frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Compute Output of $A_2$**

Similarly, the current direction causes the output of $A_2$ to be:

$$V_{A2} = V_2 - I_g \, R_1 = V_2 - R_1 \left(\frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Differential Output of Stage 1**

Subtracting:

$$V_{A1} - V_{A2} = (V_1 - V_2) + \frac{2 R_1 (V_1 - V_2)}{R_g}$$

Factor out $(V_1 - V_2)$:

$$V_{A1} - V_{A2} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 8 — Apply the Difference-Amplifier Gain**

The standard difference amplifier (assuming matched resistors: $R_2 = R_2'$ and $R_3 = R_3'$) provides a gain of $R_3 / R_2$. Therefore:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2} (V_{A1} - V_{A2})$$

**Step 9 — Final Closed-Form Expression**

Substituting the result from Step 7:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2} \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CMRR Insight:** Note that **$R_g$ does not appear** in the difference-amplifier stage. The CMRR of $A_3$ depends only on resistor matching $(R_3/R_2 = R_3'/R_2')$, while the gain is controlled by $R_g$. Hence, gain can be varied **without degrading CMRR** — this is the defining advantage of the 3 op-amp INA.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Ideal Op-Amp Integrator

The integrator uses a resistor $R$ at the input and a capacitor $C$ in the feedback path. The non-inverting input is grounded.

**Step 1 — Establish Virtual Ground**

The inverting terminal is at $V^- = 0 \, \text{V}$.

**Step 2 — Express the Input Current**

The current flowing from $V_{in}$ through $R$ into the inverting node is:

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R} = \frac{V_{in}}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Express the Feedback (Capacitor) Current**

The current flowing from the inverting node into capacitor $C$ (whose other plate is at $V_{out}$) is given by the fundamental capacitor equation $I = C \frac{dV}{dt}$. With the convention that current entering the inverting node is positive:

$$I_C = C \frac{d(V^- - V_{out})}{dt} = C \frac{d(0 - V_{out})}{dt} = -C \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node**

Since no current flows into the op-amp's inverting input:

$$I_{in} = I_C$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into Standard Integrator Form**

Multiply both sides by $R$ and divide by $C$:

$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{in}}{RC}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate Both Sides from $0$ to $t$**

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau + V_{out}(0)$$

For a constant DC input $V_{in} = V$:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{V}{RC} \, t + V_{out}(0)$$

This is a **linear ramp** with slope $-V/(RC)$ — confirming that the integrator produces a triangle wave from a square wave.

> [!NOTE]
> **S-Domain Verification:** Taking the Laplace transform of the KCL equation $\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$ gives:
> $$\frac{V_{in}(s)}{R} = -C \, s V_{out}(s)$$
> Hence the transfer function:
> $$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{1}{sRC}$$
> which has a single pole at the **origin** ($s = 0$) — characteristic of a pure integrator.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Ideal Op-Amp Differentiator

The differentiator uses a capacitor $C$ at the input and a resistor $R$ in the feedback path.

**Step 1 — Virtual Ground**

The inverting terminal is at $V^- = 0 \, \text{V}$.

**Step 2 — Input Current Through Capacitor $C$**

The current through a capacitor is proportional to the rate of change of the voltage across it. The voltage across $C$ is $V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$:

$$I_{in} = C \frac{d V_{in}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Feedback Current Through $R$**

The current flowing from the inverting node to the output through $R$ is:

$$I_R = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node**

$$I_{in} = I_R$$

$$C \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{out}$**

Multiply both sides by $-R$:

$$V_{out}(t) = -R C \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

This is the **time-domain differentiator equation**.

**Step 6 — Frequency-Domain Verification**

For a sinusoidal input $V_{in}(t) = V_m \sin \omega t$:

$$\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = \omega V_m \cos \omega t$$

Substituting:

$$V_{out}(t) = -RC \, \omega V_m \cos \omega t$$

The magnitude of the output grows linearly with $\omega$, confirming the $+20 \, \text{dB/decade}$ high-pass behavior.

> [!WARNING]
> **Unstable Behavior at High Frequency:** Because $\vert V_{out} \vert \propto \omega$, the ideal differentiator has **infinite gain at infinite frequency**. Any high-frequency noise or stray capacitance produces a large spurious output. This is why the **practical differentiator** (with input resistor $R_1$ in series with $C$) is used in real designs.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: Integrator vs. Differentiator (KTU-Ready)

| Parameter | Integrator | Differentiator |
|---|---|---|
| Input element | Resistor $R$ | Capacitor $C$ |
| Feedback element | Capacitor $C$ | Resistor $R$ |
| Time-domain output | $-\dfrac{1}{RC} \int V_{in} \, dt$ | $-RC \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ |
| Transfer function $H(s)$ | $-\dfrac{1}{sRC}$ | $-sRC$ |
| Frequency response | $-20$ dB/decade roll-off | $+20$ dB/decade rise |
| Behavior at DC | Infinite gain | Zero gain |
| Behavior at $\omega \to \infty$ | Zero gain | Infinite gain |
| Square-wave input | Triangle wave | Spikes / impulses |
| Sine-wave input | Cosine wave ($-90^\circ$ phase) | Cosine wave ($+90^\circ$ phase) |
| Main drawback | **DC drift / saturation** | **High-frequency noise amplification** |
| Practical fix | Add $R_f \parallel C$ (lossy) | Add $R_1$ in series with $C$ |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 I-V Converter (Transimpedance Amplifier) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIs[Current Source Iin] -- "input current" --> nodeVm
    nodeVm((Inverting Node V- = 0V Virtual Ground)) -- "feedback Rf" --> nodeVo
    nodeVo[Output Node Vout = -Iin Rf]
    nodeGnd1((Ground 0V)) -- "non-inv input" --> nodeA1
    nodeA1>Op-Amp]
    nodeIs --> nodeA1
    nodeGnd1 --> nodeA1
    nodeA1 --> nodeVo
    nodeVo -- Rf --> nodeVm
    nodeVm -- wire to op-amp inverting input --> nodeA1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The current source $I_{in}$ sees a virtual ground at the inverting input. The feedback resistor $R_f$ converts the current $I_{in}$ into the output voltage $V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f$.

---

## 4.2 Three Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1[Input V1] --> A1in
    V2[Input V2] --> A2in
    subgraph Stage1[STAGE 1: Differential Buffer and Gain]
        A1in[Non-Inverting Input of A1] --> OA1[Op-Amp A1]
        A2in[Non-Inverting Input of A2] --> OA2[Op-Amp A2]
        OA1 -- "feedback R1" --> INV1[Inverting Node of A1]
        OA2 -- "feedback R1" --> INV2[Inverting Node of A2]
        RGCENTER((Gain Resistor Rg)) --- INV1
        RGCENTER --- INV2
        INV1 -- virtual short --> A1in
        INV2 -- virtual short --> A2in
    end
    OA1 --> VA1[Output VA1]
    OA2 --> VA2[Output VA2]
    subgraph Stage2[STAGE 2: Difference Amplifier A3]
        VA1 --> R2A[Resistor R2] --> INVA3[Inverting Input of A3]
        VA2 --> R2B[Resistor R2 prime] --> NONA3[Non-Inverting Input of A3]
        INVA3 -- R3 feedback --> OUTA3[Output of A3]
        NONA3 -- R3 prime to ground --> GNDX((Ground))
    end
    OUTA3 --> VFINAL[Output Vout equals gain times V1 minus V2]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single resistor $R_g$ in the middle is the "magic" component.** It is the only resistor that needs to be changed to vary the gain. The CMRR is determined solely by the matching of $R_2$ with $R_2'$ and $R_3$ with $R_3'$ in the difference amplifier.

---

## 4.3 Op-Amp Integrator — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vinsig[Vin] -- R --> Nint((Inverting Node V- = 0V))
    Capfb[(Capacitor C in feedback)] --- Nint
    Capfb --- Voutint[Vout]
    GNDI((Ground)) --> Nplus[Non-Inverting Input]
    Nplus --> OAint>Op-Amp]
    Nint --> OAint
    OAint --> Voutint
```

> [!NOTE]
> The capacitor $C$ is in the feedback path. Charge accumulates on $C$ over time, producing an output that is the running integral of the input voltage.

---

## 4.4 Op-Amp Differentiator — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vinsig2[Vin] -- C --> Ndiff((Inverting Node V- = 0V))
    Resfb((Resistor R in feedback)) --- Ndiff
    Resfb --- Voutdiff[Vout equals minus RC dVindt]
    GNDD((Ground)) --> Nplusd[Non-Inverting Input]
    Nplusd --> OAdiff>Op-Amp]
    Ndiff --> OAdiff
    OAdiff --> Voutdiff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The capacitor $C$ is at the **input**. The current it draws is proportional to the **rate of change** of $V_{in}$, which when pushed through feedback resistor $R$ produces the derivative output.

---

## 4.5 Practical Modifications to Integrator and Differentiator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PRACINT[PRACTICAL INTEGRATOR]
        Vpi[Vin] -- R --> Npi((Inverting Node))
        Rfpi((Resistor Rf)) --- Npi
        Cfpi[(Capacitor C)] --- Npi
        Rfpi --- Vopi[Vout]
        Cfpi --- Vopi
    end
    subgraph PRACDIFF[PRACTICAL DIFFERENTIATOR]
        Vpd[Vin] -- C --> Rpd((Resistor R1)) --> Npd((Inverting Node))
        Rfpd((Resistor Rf)) --- Npd
        Cfpd[(Capacitor Cf)] --- Npd
        Rfpd --- Vopd[Vout]
        Cfpd --- Vopd
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Integrator:** $R_f$ in parallel with $C$ introduces a low-frequency pole at $f_a = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$. For $f \gg f_a$, the circuit behaves as an ideal integrator; for $f \ll f_a$, the gain is finite ($R_f/R$), preventing drift and saturation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Differentiator:** $R_1$ in series with $C$ introduces a high-frequency pole at $f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C}$. For $f \ll f_b$, the circuit behaves as an ideal differentiator; for $f \gg f_b$, the gain is limited, suppressing high-frequency noise.
