topics.title: Sample and Hold Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sample and Hold Circuit # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Sample and Hold (S/H) Circuit** is an essential analog building block used in **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs)**, **Digital-to-Analog Converters (DACs)**, and various data acquisition systems. Its primary function is to **sample an analog input signal at a specific instant in time and retain (hold) that sampled voltage on a storage element** for a finite duration, allowing downstream circuitry (such as an ADC) sufficient time to perform accurate conversion without the input signal changing during the conversion process. The circuit operates in two distinct modes: 1. **Sample Mode (Track Mode):** The output follows the input signal continuously while the switch is closed. 2. **Hold Mode:** The switch opens, and the capacitor retains the last sampled voltage value at its output through a buffer amplifier. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Sample and Hold circuit is the **critical interface** between the continuous-time analog domain and the discrete-time digital domain. Without an S/H circuit, fast-changing signals would cause significant conversion errors known as **aperture errors**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Frozen Photograph" Model Imagine you are watching a fast-moving cheetah running across a field: - **Without Sample and Hold:** You are trying to paint a picture of the cheetah, but the cheetah keeps moving — your painting is always blurry and inaccurate. - **With Sample and Hold:** A high-speed camera takes a **snapshot** of the cheetah at a precise instant. The photograph is then **held still** (frozen) so you have unlimited time to paint the exact details. Once the painting is done, the camera takes the next snapshot. In this analogy: - The **cheetah** = rapidly changing analog input signal - The **camera shutter** = electronic switch (MOSFET/JFET) - The **photograph** = the voltage stored on the holding capacitor - The **painting process** = the ADC conversion process ## 1.3 Fundamental Performance Metrics | Metric | Definition | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | **Acquisition Time ($t_{acq}$)** | Time required to acquire the input within a specified accuracy | 1 µs – 10 µs | | **Aperture Time ($t_{ap}$)** | Time delay between the hold command and the actual opening of the switch | 10 ns – 100 ns | | **Aperture Uncertainty (Jitter)** | Variation in aperture time from sample to sample | 1 ps – 1 ns | | **Droop Rate ($\frac{dV}{dt}$)** | Rate at which the held voltage decays during hold mode | 1 mV/µs – 1 V/µs | | **Hold Step (Hold Mode Settling)** | Voltage jump due to switch feedthrough capacitance | 1 mV – 50 mV | | **Feedthrough** | Unwanted coupling of input through parasitic capacitance | -80 dB typical | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sampling Theorem Foundation:** By the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least **twice** the highest frequency component $f_{max}$ of the input signal: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The S/H circuit is the physical implementation of this theoretical sampling process. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Circuit Topology — The Classical S/H Architecture The most widely used Sample and Hold topology consists of **four fundamental building blocks**: 1. **Input Buffer Amplifier** — Provides high input impedance and isolates the source from the switch. 2. **Analog Switch (MOSFET / JFET)** — Controls the sampling instant via a control signal $V_c$ (often called the "sample clock"). 3. **Hold Capacitor ($C_H$)** — The analog memory element that stores the sampled voltage. 4. **Output Buffer Amplifier (Unity-Gain Follower)** — Provides high input impedance (to prevent discharging $C_H$) and low output impedance (to drive the load). ## 2.2 Operational Phases — Step-by-Step Logic ### Phase 1: SAMPLE (TRACK) MODE - The control signal $V_c$ closes the analog switch. - The hold capacitor $C_H$ charges/discharges through the switch's on-resistance $R_{on}$ to track the input voltage. - The output $V_{out}$ follows $V_{in}$ with a small tracking error governed by the **RC time constant**: $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$. - The op-amp buffer isolates $C_H$ from the load, preventing discharge. ### Phase 2: HOLD MODE - The control signal $V_c$ opens the analog switch. - The capacitor $C_H$ is **electrically isolated** from the input. - Ideally, the capacitor holds its voltage indefinitely: $V_{out} = V_{in}(t_{hold})$. - In practice, leakage currents cause **droop** (slow decay) of the held voltage. ## 2.3 Key Analytical Relationships ### Charging Behavior During Sample Mode When the switch closes, the capacitor voltage rises exponentially toward $V_{in}$: $$V_C(t) = V_{in}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$ where $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$. ### Acquisition Time Requirement To reach an accuracy of $\pm \frac{1}{2} \text{LSB}$ for an $n$-bit ADC with full-scale range $V_{FS}$: $$t_{acq} = \tau \cdot \ln\left(2^{n+1}\right) = (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln(2)$$ ### Droop During Hold Mode Due to leakage current $I_{leak}$ (from the switch off-state and op-amp input bias): $$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$$ The held voltage at time $t$ into the hold phase is: $$V_{out}(t) = V_{in}(t_{hold}) - \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t}{C_H}$$ ### Hold Step Due to Switch Feedthrough The fast switching transient injects a voltage error via the gate-to-source capacitance $C_{gs}$: $$\Delta V_{hold} = \frac{C_{gs}}{C_H} \cdot \Delta V_{control}$$ where $\Delta V_{control}$ is the amplitude of the control signal swing. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_C(t) = V_{in}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Capacitor charging during sample mode | Volts | | 2 | $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$ | RC time constant of sampling network | Seconds | | 3 | $t_{acq} = (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln 2$ | Acquisition time for $n$-bit accuracy | Seconds | | 4 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hertz | | 5 | $\frac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$ | Droop rate during hold mode | V/s | | 6 | $\Delta V_{hold} = \dfrac{C_{gs}}{C_H} \cdot \Delta V_c$ | Hold step error from feedthrough | Volts | | 7 | $\Delta V_{aperture} = \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt} \cdot t_{au}$ | Aperture uncertainty error | Volts | | 8 | $t_{au}$ = Aperture uncertainty (jitter) | Timing jitter of switch | Seconds | | 9 | $SNR = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB | Ideal ADC SNR (defines S/H accuracy need) | dB | | 10 | $C_H \geq \dfrac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}}{V_{droop,max}}$ | Minimum hold capacitor for max droop | Farads | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Sample and Hold circuits are the **front-end** of every high-precision ADC in modern mixed-signal systems — from digital oscilloscopes, audio codecs, medical imaging equipment (CT/MRI), to industrial process control systems. They are equally critical in **Sample-and-Hold Amplifiers (SHAs)** used in pipelined and successive-approximation ADCs. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitor Charging During Sample Mode ### Problem Setup A Sample and Hold circuit has a switch with on-resistance $R_{on} = 500\ \Omega$ and a hold capacitor $C_H = 100\ pF$. An input step of $V_{in} = 5.0\ V$ is applied at $t = 0$ (assuming the capacitor was initially at $0\ V$). ### Goal Derive the time-domain expression for $V_C(t)$ and find the time required to charge to within $0.1\%$ of the final value. ### Step 1 — Apply KVL Around the Switch-Capacitor Loop When the switch is closed, the input voltage $V_{in}$ drives current through $R_{on}$ into the parallel combination of $C_H$ and the op-amp input (which draws negligible current). By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law: $$V_{in} = i(t) \cdot R_{on} + V_C(t)$$ ### Step 2 — Express Current in Terms of Capacitor Voltage The current charging the capacitor is: $$i(t) = C_H \cdot \frac{dV_C(t)}{dt}$$ ### Step 3 — Form the First-Order Linear Differential Equation Substituting the current expression back into the KVL equation: $$V_{in} = R_{on} \cdot C_H \cdot \frac{dV_C(t)}{dt} + V_C(t)$$ ### Step 4 — Rearrange into Standard Form $$\frac{dV_C(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{R_{on} \cdot C_H} \cdot V_C(t) = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{on} \cdot C_H}$$ Letting $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$: $$\frac{dV_C(t)}{dt} + \frac{V_C(t)}{\tau} = \frac{V_{in}}{\tau}$$ ### Step 5 — Solve the Differential Equation This is a standard first-order ODE. The solution is the sum of the **steady-state** and **transient** components: $$V_C(t) = V_{in} + A \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$ ### Step 6 — Apply the Initial Condition At $t = 0$, $V_C(0) = 0$ (capacitor initially uncharged): $$0 = V_{in} + A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -V_{in}$$ ### Step 7 — Final Closed-Form Expression $$\boxed{V_C(t) = V_{in}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)}$$ ### Step 8 — Compute the Numerical Time Constant $$\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H = (500\ \Omega) \cdot (100 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F})$$ $$\tau = 5 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s} = 50\ \text{ns}$$ ### Step 9 — Find Time for 0.1% Accuracy We need $V_C(t) = 0.999 \cdot V_{in}$, so: $$0.999 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-t/\tau} = 0.001$$ $$-\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.001) = -6.9078$$ $$t = 6.9078 \cdot \tau = 6.9078 \cdot 50\ \text{ns}$$ $$\boxed{t = 345.4\ \text{ns} \approx 345\ \text{ns}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Acquisition Time for an N-Bit ADC ### Problem Setup A 12-bit ADC is preceded by a Sample and Hold circuit with $\tau = 50\ \text{ns}$. Determine the **minimum acquisition time** required to settle to within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB of the full-scale input. ### Step 1 — Express the LSB in Volts For an $n$-bit ADC with full-scale range $V_{FS}$: $$\text{1 LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$ ### Step 2 — Settling Requirement The error must be less than half of one LSB: $$\vert V_{in} - V_C(t) \vert \leq \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$ ### Step 3 — Use the Charging Equation $$V_{in} - V_C(t) = V_{in} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$ For worst-case (full input step): $$\frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}} = V_{FS} \cdot e^{-t_{acq}/\tau}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for Acquisition Time $$e^{-t_{acq}/\tau} = \frac{1}{2^{n+1}}$$ $$-\frac{t_{acq}}{\tau} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2^{n+1}}\right) = -(n+1)\ln 2$$ $$\boxed{t_{acq} = (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln 2}$$ ### Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation for n = 12, τ = 50 ns $$t_{acq} = (12 + 1) \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (0.6931)$$ $$t_{acq} = 13 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 0.6931$$ $$t_{acq} = 650 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 0.6931 = 450.5\ \text{ns}$$ $$\boxed{t_{acq} \approx 451\ \text{ns}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Droop Calculation in Hold Mode ### Problem Setup The S/H circuit has a hold capacitor $C_H = 1\ \text{nF}$ and a total leakage current of $I_{leak} = 10\ \text{pA}$. The circuit is placed in hold mode for $t_{hold} = 10\ \mu s$. Determine the droop voltage. ### Step 1 — Charge Conservation on the Hold Capacitor The leakage current discharges the capacitor. The charge removed in time $t_{hold}$ is: $$\Delta Q = I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}$$ ### Step 2 — Voltage Change on the Capacitor $$\Delta V = \frac{\Delta Q}{C_H} = \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}}{C_H}$$ ### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation $$\Delta V = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-6})}{1 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$\Delta V = \frac{100 \times 10^{-18}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 100 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{V}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta V = 100\ \mu\text{V (droop over 10 µs hold period)}}$$ ### Step 4 — Droop Rate $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{I_{leak}}{C_H} = \frac{10\ \text{pA}}{1\ \text{nF}} = 10\ \text{mV/s}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A droop of 100 µV over 10 µs is equivalent to **0.002% of a 5 V full-scale signal** — completely negligible for a 12-bit ADC (whose 1 LSB ≈ 1.22 mV). However, for 16-bit or higher systems, this droop becomes significant and must be minimized by using larger $C_H$ or lower-leakage switches. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Numerical Example | |---|---|---|---| | Time constant | $\tau$ | $R_{on} \cdot C_H$ | $500\ \Omega \cdot 100\ \text{pF} = 50\ \text{ns}$ | | Charging voltage | $V_C(t)$ | $V_{in}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | $5(1 - e^{-t/50\text{ns}})$ V | | Acquisition time (12-bit) | $t_{acq}$ | $(n+1)\tau \ln 2$ | $\approx 451\ \text{ns}$ | | Droop rate | $\dot{V}_{droop}$ | $I_{leak}/C_H$ | $10\ \text{mV/s}$ | | Droop in 10 µs | $\Delta V$ | $I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}/C_H$ | $100\ \mu\text{V}$ | | Hold step | $\Delta V_{hold}$ | $C_{gs}/C_H \cdot \Delta V_c$ | Depends on switch | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sample and Hold Circuit Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Input Vin
Analog Signal"] --> nodeB["Input Buffer
Op-Amp A1"] nodeB --> nodeC["Analog Switch
MOSFET JFET"] nodeC --> nodeD["Hold Capacitor CH
Storage Element"] nodeD --> nodeE["Output Buffer
Unity Gain Follower"] nodeE --> nodeF["Output Vout
to ADC"] nodeG["Control Logic Vc
Sample Clock"] --> nodeC nodeH["Leakage Ileak
to Ground"] -.-> nodeD ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The control signal `Vc` (the sample clock) drives the gate of the analog switch. When `Vc = HIGH`, the switch is closed and the capacitor tracks the input. When `Vc = LOW`, the switch opens and the capacitor holds the last value. The dashed leakage path represents the unavoidable parasitic discharge current that causes droop. ## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Operating Modes ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Reset Reset --> SampleMode : Vc HIGH
Switch Closed SampleMode --> HoldMode : Vc LOW
Switch Opens HoldMode --> SampleMode : Vc HIGH
Next Sample HoldMode --> [*] : Power Down note right of SampleMode : Capacitor CH
charges to Vin
via Ron note right of HoldMode : Vout held
constant
VC isolated ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Timing Diagram — Waveform Relationships ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph tline["Time Axis"] end node1["Vc Control Signal"] --> node2["High = Sample"] node1 --> node3["Low = Hold"] node4["Vin Analog Input"] --> node5["Continuous
Time Varying"] node6["Vout Output"] --> node7["Tracks Vin
during Sample"] node6 --> node8["Flat Step
during Hold"] node9["CH Capacitor Voltage"] --> node10["Same as Vout"] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Functional Flow — Signal Path Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["Analog Input Vin arrives"] --> nodeBuf["Stage 1: Input Buffer
Provides High Z input
Low Z drive to switch"] nodeBuf --> nodeSw["Stage 2: Analog Switch
Controlled by Vc
Ron determines speed"] nodeSw --> nodeCap["Stage 3: Hold Capacitor CH
Stores Sampled Voltage
tau equals Ron times CH"] nodeCap --> nodeOut["Stage 4: Output Buffer
Unity Gain Op-Amp
Prevents Loading of CH"] nodeOut --> nodeAdc["Stage 5: ADC Conversion
Reads Stable Voltage
during Hold Period"] nodeAdc --> nodeDone["Digital Output Code"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Explain the function of a Sample and Hold circuit in an Analog-to-Digital Converter. List any two performance parameters used to characterize a Sample and Hold circuit.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): A **Sample and Hold (S/H) circuit** is a critical front-end block of an Analog-to-Digital Converter. Its function is to **sample the continuously varying analog input signal at a precise instant determined by a control clock** and to **hold (freeze) that sampled voltage constant** for the entire duration of the ADC's conversion process. **Why it is needed:** During the conversion time of an ADC (which may range from microseconds to milliseconds), the input signal may change significantly. If the S/H circuit were absent, the ADC would be attempting to digitize a moving target, leading to large **aperture errors** and corrupted output codes. The S/H circuit freezes the input, ensuring the ADC always sees a stable DC value. **Two Key Performance Parameters:** 1. **Acquisition Time ($t_{acq}$):** The time required (starting from the moment the switch closes) for the output to settle within a specified accuracy band, typically $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB. It is governed by the time constant $\tau = R_{on} C_H$. 2. **Droop Rate ($\frac{dV}{dt}$):** The rate at which the held voltage decays during the hold mode due to leakage currents flowing into/out of the hold capacitor. It is given by $\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$. *(Other acceptable parameters: aperture time, aperture uncertainty/jitter, hold step, feedthrough.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Define aperture time and aperture uncertainty in a Sample and Hold circuit. Why is aperture uncertainty more critical in high-frequency applications?** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): **Aperture Time ($t_{ap}$):** Aperture time is defined as the **time delay between the instant the hold command is applied** (i.e., the rising edge of the control signal $V_c$) **and the actual instant at which the analog switch fully opens**, isolating the hold capacitor from the input. It is a fixed, deterministic delay introduced by the switching transistor and its driver circuitry. **Aperture Uncertainty (Aperture Jitter, $t_{au}$):** Aperture uncertainty is the **random variation in aperture time from one sample to the next**. It is caused by noise in the control signal, temperature variations, and switching transients. It represents the **timing precision limit** of the S/H circuit. **Why Aperture Uncertainty is Critical at High Frequencies:** The voltage error introduced by aperture uncertainty depends on the **rate of change of the input signal**: $$\Delta V_{error} = \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} \cdot t_{au}$$ For high-frequency input signals, $\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ is large, so even a tiny timing jitter (a few picoseconds) produces a substantial voltage error. For example, sampling a $100\ \text{MHz}$ sine wave ($5\ \text{V}$ peak) with $t_{au} = 10\ \text{ps}$ produces an error of approximately $15.7\ \text{mV}$, which exceeds the 1 LSB of a 10-bit ADC ($4.88\ \text{mV}$). Hence, **low aperture jitter is essential for high-frequency, high-resolution data acquisition**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B and answer both sub-parts. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Sample and Hold circuit. Discuss the significance of acquisition time and droop rate in determining the performance of the circuit. (7 Marks) **(b)** A Sample and Hold circuit uses a hold capacitor of $C_H = 500\ \text{pF}$ and a MOSFET switch with on-resistance $R_{on} = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$. The circuit is used in a 10-bit ADC system with a full-scale input of $V_{FS} = 5\ \text{V}$. - **(i)** Calculate the time constant $\tau$ of the sampling network. - **(ii)** Determine the acquisition time required to settle within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB. - **(iii)** If the input signal is $V_{in} = 5\sin(2\pi \cdot 1000t)$ V, calculate the maximum rate of change of the input and the corresponding maximum aperture error for an aperture uncertainty of $t_{au} = 1\ \text{ns}$. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution for Question A: **Part (a) — Block Diagram and Operation (7 Marks):** **Block Diagram:** ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ Vin ──►│ Input │──►│ Analog │──►(CH)──►│ Output │──► Vout │ Buffer │ │ Switch │ │ Buffer │ │ (A1) │ │ (MOSFET) │ │ (A2) │ └───────────┘ └──────▲───────┘ └───────────┘ │ │ Vc (Sample Clock) ``` **Operation in Two Modes:** **1. Sample (Track) Mode:** When the control signal $V_c$ is HIGH, the MOSFET switch is ON. The hold capacitor $C_H$ charges through the switch's on-resistance $R_{on}$, following the input voltage $V_{in}$. The op-amp output buffer (unity-gain follower) presents high input impedance to $C_H$, preventing it from discharging through the load. The output voltage tracks the input: $$V_{out}(t) = V_{in}(t) \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), \quad \tau = R_{on} C_H$$ **2. Hold Mode:** When $V_c$ goes LOW, the MOSFET switch turns OFF. The capacitor $C_H$ is now isolated from the input. Ideally, $V_{out}$ remains constant at the value it held at the moment the switch opened. In practice, leakage currents cause a slow droop. **Significance of Acquisition Time:** Acquisition time $t_{acq}$ is the time the S/H circuit must remain in sample mode to ensure the capacitor charges to within $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB of the input. For an $n$-bit ADC: $$t_{acq} = (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln 2$$ If $t_{acq}$ is too short, the capacitor does not fully charge, causing **gain error and non-linearity** in the ADC output. It is a critical design parameter that limits the maximum sampling rate of the entire data acquisition system. **Significance of Droop Rate:** During hold mode, the held voltage should ideally remain constant. However, leakage currents $I_{leak}$ (from the switch's off-state and the op-amp's input bias) slowly discharge $C_H$: $$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$$ A high droop rate causes the held voltage to change during the ADC's conversion time, introducing **conversion error**. This is especially critical in systems with long hold durations or high resolution (16-bit and above). Minimizing droop requires large $C_H$ and low-leakage switches (JFET or MOSFET with low $I_{DSS}$). --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 Marks):** **Given:** $C_H = 500\ \text{pF} = 500 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F}$, $R_{on} = 1\ \text{k}\Omega = 10^3\ \Omega$, $n = 10$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5\ \text{V}$, $V_{in} = 5\sin(2\pi \cdot 1000t)$ V, $t_{au} = 1\ \text{ns} = 10^{-9}\ \text{s}$. **(i) Time Constant $\tau$:** $$\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H = (10^3) \cdot (500 \times 10^{-12})$$ $$\tau = 5 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{s} = 0.5\ \mu\text{s} = 500\ \text{ns}$$ $$\boxed{\tau = 500\ \text{ns}}$$ **(ii) Acquisition Time for 10-bit Settling:** $$t_{acq} = (n+1) \cdot \tau \cdot \ln 2 = (10 + 1) \cdot (500 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (0.6931)$$ $$t_{acq} = 11 \cdot 500 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 0.6931$$ $$t_{acq} = 5500 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 0.6931$$ $$\boxed{t_{acq} \approx 3.81\ \mu\text{s}}$$ **(iii) Maximum Rate of Change of Input:** For $V_{in} = 5\sin(2\pi \cdot 1000t)$: $$\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = 5 \cdot 2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot \cos(2\pi \cdot 1000t) = 10000\pi \cos(2\pi \cdot 1000t)$$ Maximum rate of change occurs when $\cos(\cdot) = 1$: $$\left(\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right)_{max} = 10000\pi \approx 31415.9\ \text{V/s} \approx 31.42\ \text{V/ms}$$ **Maximum Aperture Error:** $$\Delta V_{aperture,max} = \left(\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot t_{au} = 31415.9 \cdot 10^{-9}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta V_{aperture,max} \approx 31.4\ \mu\text{V}}$$ **Comparison with 10-bit LSB:** $$\text{1 LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.88\ \text{mV}$$ Since $31.4\ \mu\text{V} \ll 4.88\ \text{mV}$, the aperture jitter of 1 ns is **negligible** for this 1 kHz input signal. However, if the input frequency were increased to, say, 100 MHz, the aperture error would scale up by a factor of $10^5$, becoming catastrophic. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the significance of the following non-idealities in a Sample and Hold circuit: - (i) Hold Step - (ii) Feedthrough - (iii) Aperture Jitter For each, state the cause and its effect on ADC accuracy. (7 Marks) **(b)** A Sample and Hold circuit has a hold capacitor of $C_H = 1\ \text{nF}$ and exhibits a total leakage current of $I_{leak} = 5\ \text{pA}$ during hold mode. - **(i)** Calculate the droop rate. - **(ii)** If the maximum allowable droop during a 5 µs hold period is $0.5\ \text{mV}$, determine whether the existing capacitor is sufficient. If not, calculate the required minimum capacitance. - **(iii)** If the switch has a gate-to-source capacitance of $C_{gs} = 0.5\ \text{pF}$ and the control signal swings by $\Delta V_c = 5\ \text{V}$, find the resulting hold step error. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution for Question B: **Part (a) — Non-Idealities (7 Marks):** **(i) Hold Step:** The **hold step** is a small, abrupt voltage jump in the output that occurs at the **instant of switching from sample to hold mode**. It is caused by the **charge injection** from the MOSFET switch's gate-to-source capacitance $C_{gs}$. When the gate signal $V_c$ transitions from HIGH to LOW, the charge stored in $C_{gs}$ is partially transferred to the hold capacitor $C_H$, causing a sudden voltage step: $$\Delta V_{hold} = \frac{C_{gs}}{C_H} \cdot \Delta V_c$$ **Effect on ADC accuracy:** The hold step is an **offset error** that appears on every sample. It is constant for a given switch and clock amplitude, so it can be calibrated out in principle, but it limits the **DC accuracy** of the system, particularly for low-amplitude signals. Minimization techniques include using **dummy switches**, **differential architectures**, and **smaller $C_{gs}$** devices. **(ii) Feedthrough:** **Feedthrough** refers to the **unwanted coupling of the input signal to the output** during hold mode, ideally when the switch is fully OFF. It occurs via the **parasitic capacitances** of the MOSFET (drain-to-source capacitance $C_{ds}$ and gate-to-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$). The feedthrough ratio is typically expressed in dB: $$\text{Feedthrough (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{out,feedthrough}}{V_{in}}\right)$$ Typical values are $-80$ to $-100\ \text{dB}$. **Effect on ADC accuracy:** Feedthrough causes a **small AC ripple or noise** superimposed on the held voltage, leading to **harmonic distortion** in the digitized signal. It is particularly problematic in high-frequency, high-resolution systems. **(iii) Aperture Jitter:** As defined in Part A Question 2, **aperture jitter** ($t_{au}$) is the random timing uncertainty in the hold instant. It introduces a voltage error: $$\Delta V = \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} \cdot t_{au}$$ **Effect on ADC accuracy:** Aperture jitter sets a **fundamental limit on the achievable SNR** of the data acquisition system, especially for high-frequency inputs. It is the dominant error source in high-speed ADCs (e.g., oscilloscope front-ends) and cannot be calibrated out, as it is random. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 Marks):** **Given:** $C_H = 1\ \text{nF} = 10^{-9}\ \text{F}$, $I_{leak} = 5\ \text{pA} = 5 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{A}$, $t_{hold} = 5\ \mu\text{s} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$, $V_{droop,max} = 0.5\ \text{mV} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{V}$, $C_{gs} = 0.5\ \text{pF} = 0.5 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F}$, $\Delta V_c = 5\ \text{V}$. **(i) Droop Rate:** $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{I_{leak}}{C_H} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-12}}{10^{-9}} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V/s}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Droop rate} = 5\ \text{mV/s}}$$ **(ii) Sufficiency of $C_H$:** Actual droop in 5 µs: $$\Delta V_{actual} = \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}}{C_H} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6})}{10^{-9}}$$ $$\Delta V_{actual} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-18}}{10^{-9}} = 25 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{V} = 25\ \text{nV}$$ Since $25\ \text{nV} \ll 0.5\ \text{mV}$, the existing capacitor is **more than sufficient**. The maximum allowed droop of 0.5 mV is not even approached. **Minimum required capacitance for 0.5 mV droop in 5 µs:** $$C_{H,min} = \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}}{V_{droop,max}} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6})}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$C_{H,min} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-18}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 5 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{F} = 50\ \text{fF}$$ $$\boxed{C_{H,min} = 50\ \text{fF}}$$ The existing $1\ \text{nF}$ capacitor is **20,000 times larger** than the minimum required. **(iii) Hold Step Error:** $$\Delta V_{hold} = \frac{C_{gs}}{C_H} \cdot \Delta V_c = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-12}}{10^{-9}} \cdot 5$$ $$\Delta V_{hold} = (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 5 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta V_{hold} = 2.5\ \text{mV}}$$ This hold step of 2.5 mV is comparable to the 1 LSB of a 10-bit ADC ($4.88\ \text{mV}$) and would be a significant error source. In practice, dummy switches or fully differential architectures are required to suppress this error. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit errors in droop calculation** — Forgetting to convert pA to A and nF to F before dividing. Always use SI base units in your formulas. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of $(n+1)$** — Many students incorrectly use $n$ instead of $(n+1)$ in the acquisition time formula $t_{acq} = (n+1)\tau \ln 2$. The $+1$ comes from the $\frac{1}{2}$ LSB criterion. > 3. **Confusing acquisition time with aperture time** — Acquisition time is the duration needed to charge the capacitor (sample mode); aperture time is the delay in opening the switch. > 4. **Neglecting $\ln 2$** — Some students write $t_{acq} = n \cdot \tau$ instead of $t_{acq} = n \cdot \tau \cdot \ln 2$. The natural logarithm is essential. > 5. **Aperture jitter at high frequencies** — Forgetting that the error is proportional to $\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$, not just to $t_{au}$ alone. > 6. **Sign of droop** — Droop is a decay, so it is conventionally shown as a negative rate of change. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Sample and Hold (S/H) circuit** samples an analog signal and holds it on a capacitor for ADC conversion. - ✅ **Two operating modes:** Sample (track) mode and Hold mode. - ✅ **Four key components:** Input buffer, analog switch (MOSFET/JFET), hold capacitor $C_H$, output buffer. - ✅ **Time constant:** $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_H$ governs the sample-mode charging speed. - ✅ **Charging equation:** $V_C(t) = V_{in}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$. - ✅ **Acquisition time for $n$-bit accuracy:** $t_{acq} = (n+1)\tau \ln 2$. - ✅ **Droop rate:** $\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$ — depends on leakage, not on input signal. - ✅ **Hold step error:** $\Delta V_{hold} = \frac{C_{gs}}{C_H} \cdot \Delta V_c$ — from switch charge injection. - ✅ **Aperture error:** $\Delta V = \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} \cdot t_{au}$ — critical at high frequencies. - ✅ **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. - ✅ **Typical capacitor range:** 100 pF to 1 nF (trade-off between speed and droop). - ✅ **Typical switch resistance:** 100 Ω to 1 kΩ (lower is faster, but increases charge injection). - ✅ **Engineering applications:** ADC front-ends, DAC deglitchers, sample-and-hold amplifiers, peak detectors, analog delay lines, multiplexed data acquisition systems.

topics.title: Comparator (Inverting and Non-Inverting Type), Applications of Comparator, Zero Crossing Detector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Comparator (Inverting and Non-Inverting Type), Applications of Comparator, Zero Crossing Detector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Comparator

A **Comparator** is an electronic circuit, typically built using an **operational amplifier (op-amp)** in **open-loop configuration** (i.e., without any feedback), which compares two analog input voltages and produces a **binary (two-level) digital output** that indicates which of the two inputs is larger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A comparator is a circuit that compares an analog input voltage with a reference voltage and produces a digital output of $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$, depending on whether the input is greater or less than the reference. The op-amp used in open-loop acts as a comparator because of its extremely high open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ (typically $10^5$ to $10^6$).

Mathematically, the open-loop op-amp output is given by:

$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$$

where $A_{OL}$ is the open-loop gain, $V_+$ is the voltage at the non-inverting terminal, and $V_-$ is the voltage at the inverting terminal.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Referee" Circuit

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine a **weight comparison scale** in a grocery store with two pans. You place item A on the left pan and item B on the right pan. The scale is so sensitive that even the **slightest difference** in weight causes the needle to slam all the way to the left or to the right. The needle can only show **two extreme positions** — it never stops in the middle.
> 
> In the same way, an op-amp comparator compares two voltages. Because the open-loop gain is enormous ($10^5$ or more), even a **microvolt-level difference** between the two input terminals forces the output to one of the two **saturation extremes** ($+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$). The op-amp behaves like a digital switch.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics of an Ideal Comparator

- **Infinite open-loop gain** ($A_{OL} \to \infty$)
- **Infinite input impedance** ($Z_{in} \to \infty$) — draws no current from the source
- **Zero output impedance** ($Z_{out} = 0$)
- **Infinite bandwidth** (instantaneous response)
- **Output levels:** Only two discrete states — $+V_{sat}$ (HIGH) and $-V_{sat}$ (LOW)

## 1.4 Why No Feedback? The Open-Loop Principle

In a normal amplifier configuration, negative feedback is used to control the gain and stabilize the operation. In a comparator, **feedback is intentionally omitted** so that the op-amp operates in its **saturation region**, producing a digital output. The key physical constants are:

- **Open-loop gain $A_{OL}$:** typically **$2 \times 10^5$** (for $\mu$A741)
- **Saturation voltage $V_{sat}$:** typically **$\pm 13$ V** (with $\pm 15$ V supply) or **$\pm 14$ V**
- **Supply voltages $V_{CC}, -V_{EE}$:** typically **$\pm 15$ V** or **$\pm 12$ V**

> [!NOTE]
> **The switching threshold** is determined by the smallest input difference that can saturate the output. This minimum difference is:
> $$V_{id(min)} = \frac{V_{sat}}{A_{OL}} \approx \frac{13 \text{ V}}{2 \times 10^5} = 65 \text{ }\mu V$$
> 
> This tiny voltage is what makes the comparator extremely sensitive to input changes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Basic Comparator Configuration

A comparator is built using an op-amp in **open-loop** (no feedback network). The op-amp has two input terminals and one output terminal:

- **Non-inverting terminal ($V_+$)**
- **Inverting terminal ($V_-$)**
- **Output ($V_o$)**

The reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied to one terminal, and the input signal $V_{in}$ to the other. The comparison happens continuously.

## 2.2 Inverting Comparator

### Circuit Description
- The **input signal $V_{in}$** is applied to the **inverting terminal ($V_-$)**
- The **reference voltage $V_{ref}$** is applied to the **non-inverting terminal ($V_+$)**

### Operational Logic
The op-amp output equation is:

$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_+ - V_-) = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{ref} - V_{in})$$

**Case 1:** When $V_{in} > V_{ref}$:
- $(V_{ref} - V_{in}) < 0$
- $V_o = -A_{OL} \cdot (V_{in} - V_{ref})$
- The large negative value forces the output to **negative saturation**: $V_o = -V_{sat}$

**Case 2:** When $V_{in} < V_{ref}$:
- $(V_{ref} - V_{in}) > 0$
- $V_o = +A_{OL} \cdot (V_{ref} - V_{in})$
- The large positive value forces the output to **positive saturation**: $V_o = +V_{sat}$

**Case 3:** When $V_{in} = V_{ref}$ (theoretically):
- Theoretically, $V_o = 0$, but in practice, the slightest noise causes the output to randomly swing to either $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$. This is the **switching point**.

### Transfer Characteristics (Inverting)
The transfer characteristic plot of an inverting comparator is an **inverted step** at $V_{in} = V_{ref}$:

| Condition on $V_{in}$ | Output Voltage $V_o$ |
|---|---|
| $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ | $-V_{sat}$ (LOW) |
| $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ | $+V_{sat}$ (HIGH) |
| $V_{in} = V_{ref}$ | Switching point (undefined) |

## 2.3 Non-Inverting Comparator

### Circuit Description
- The **input signal $V_{in}$** is applied to the **non-inverting terminal ($V_+$)**
- The **reference voltage $V_{ref}$** is applied to the **inverting terminal ($V_-$)**

### Operational Logic
The op-amp output equation is:

$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_+ - V_-) = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{in} - V_{ref})$$

**Case 1:** When $V_{in} > V_{ref}$:
- $(V_{in} - V_{ref}) > 0$
- $V_o = +A_{OL} \cdot (V_{in} - V_{ref}) \to +V_{sat}$ (HIGH)

**Case 2:** When $V_{in} < V_{ref}$:
- $(V_{in} - V_{ref}) < 0$
- $V_o = -A_{OL} \cdot (V_{ref} - V_{in}) \to -V_{sat}$ (LOW)

### Transfer Characteristics (Non-Inverting)
The transfer characteristic plot of a non-inverting comparator is a **non-inverted step** at $V_{in} = V_{ref}$:

| Condition on $V_{in}$ | Output Voltage $V_o$ |
|---|---|
| $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ | $+V_{sat}$ (HIGH) |
| $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ | $-V_{sat}$ (LOW) |
| $V_{in} = V_{ref}$ | Switching point (undefined) |

## 2.4 Zero Crossing Detector (ZCD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **Zero Crossing Detector (ZCD)** is a special case of a comparator in which the **reference voltage $V_{ref} = 0$** (i.e., the reference terminal is grounded). It detects the precise instant when the input signal **crosses the zero-volt axis**, producing a sharp transition in the output waveform.

### Types of ZCD

**1. Inverting-type ZCD:**
- $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting terminal**
- $V_+ = 0$ (grounded)
- When $V_{in} > 0$: $V_o = -V_{sat}$
- When $V_{in} < 0$: $V_o = +V_{sat}$
- Output is **inverted** with respect to the input polarity.

**2. Non-inverting-type ZCD:**
- $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting terminal**
- $V_- = 0$ (grounded)
- When $V_{in} > 0$: $V_o = +V_{sat}$
- When $V_{in} < 0$: $V_o = -V_{sat}$
- Output follows the **same polarity** as the input.

### Applications of Zero Crossing Detectors
- **Phase measurement** in power systems
- **Frequency multipliers** (used to generate harmonics)
- **Time markers** in signal processing
- **Synchronizing pulses** for digital systems and SCR triggering circuits
- **Switching power supplies** for noise-free switching at zero crossings

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open-loop output | $V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$ | Linear region equation (Volts) |
| 2 | Inverting comparator (HIGH) | $V_o = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ | Output saturation state |
| 3 | Inverting comparator (LOW) | $V_o = -V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ | Output saturation state |
| 4 | Non-inverting (HIGH) | $V_o = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ | Output saturation state |
| 5 | Non-inverting (LOW) | $V_o = -V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ | Output saturation state |
| 6 | Threshold (switching) voltage | $V_{th} = V_{ref}$ | The input value at which output changes state |
| 7 | Sensitivity / Resolution | $\Delta V_{min} = V_{sat} / A_{OL}$ | Smallest detectable input difference (Volts) |
| 8 | ZCD (inverting) - HIGH | $V_o = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} < 0$ | Negative half-cycle output |
| 9 | ZCD (inverting) - LOW | $V_o = -V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} > 0$ | Positive half-cycle output |
| 10 | ZCD (non-inverting) - HIGH | $V_o = +V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} > 0$ | Positive half-cycle output |
| 11 | ZCD (non-inverting) - LOW | $V_o = -V_{sat}$ when $V_{in} < 0$ | Negative half-cycle output |
| 12 | Typical $A_{OL}$ | $2 \times 10^5$ | For $\mu$A741 (dimensionless) |
| 13 | Typical $V_{sat}$ | $\pm 13$ V | With $\pm 15$ V supply (Volts) |
| 14 | Response time | $t_r \approx 1 \text{ to } 2 \text{ }\mu s$ | Op-amp switching time |
| 15 | Slew rate impact | $SR = dV_o/dt$ | Limits max input frequency |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Electronics:** ECG/EEG signal conditioning where analog biological signals must be compared against thresholds to detect abnormal patterns.
- **Industrial Automation:** Limit-switch comparators in process control to monitor temperature, pressure, or fluid level against set-points.
- **Telecommunications:** Signal squarers and pulse-width modulators in transmitter circuits.
- **Automotive:** Battery voltage monitors and crank-shaft position sensors.
- **Power Electronics:** SCR/thyristor triggering circuits for AC phase-angle control using ZCD.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Switching Threshold of an Inverting Comparator

**Objective:** To derive the precise input voltage at which the comparator output switches state.

**Step 1 — Start with the open-loop op-amp output equation.**

The fundamental equation governing an op-amp is:

$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$$

**Explanation:** This is the open-loop transfer characteristic of the op-amp, where the differential input $(V_+ - V_-)$ is amplified by the open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ to produce the output.

---

**Step 2 — Substitute the inverting comparator input connections.**

For an inverting comparator:
- $V_- = V_{in}$ (input signal at the inverting terminal)
- $V_+ = V_{ref}$ (reference voltage at the non-inverting terminal)

Substituting into the output equation:

$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{ref} - V_{in})$$

**Explanation:** We are simply replacing the abstract terminals with the actual signals applied in the inverting comparator topology.

---

**Step 3 — Identify the saturation conditions.**

Because the op-amp is in open-loop with very high gain $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$, the output cannot follow the linear equation for any appreciable input difference. It will saturate at the supply limits:

$$V_o = +V_{sat} \quad \text{when} \quad V_{ref} - V_{in} > 0$$

$$V_o = -V_{sat} \quad \text{when} \quad V_{ref} - V_{in} < 0$$

**Explanation:** As soon as the differential input exceeds the small value $V_{sat} / A_{OL}$, the output "slams" to one of the supply rails. The op-amp effectively acts as a digital decision-maker.

---

**Step 4 — Solve for the switching threshold.**

The output transitions state when $V_o = 0$ (theoretically, the boundary between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$). Setting $V_o = 0$:

$$0 = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{ref} - V_{in})$$

$$V_{ref} - V_{in} = 0$$

$$\boxed{V_{in} = V_{ref}}$$

**Explanation:** The comparator switches state exactly when the input signal equals the reference voltage. This is the **threshold voltage** $V_{th} = V_{ref}$.

---

**Step 5 — Derive the minimum resolvable input difference (sensitivity).**

The smallest input difference that can produce a saturated output is found from:

$$V_o = V_{sat} = A_{OL} \cdot \Delta V_{min}$$

Solving for $\Delta V_{min}$:

$$\Delta V_{min} = \frac{V_{sat}}{A_{OL}}$$

Substituting the typical values:

$$\Delta V_{min} = \frac{13 \text{ V}}{2 \times 10^5} = 6.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V} = 65 \text{ }\mu V$$

**Explanation:** Any input change greater than 65 microvolts (relative to the reference) is enough to drive the output to saturation. This extreme sensitivity is the reason comparators can detect tiny signal changes.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An inverting comparator uses $V_{ref} = +2$ V and a sinusoidal input $V_{in}(t) = 5 \sin(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot t)$ V. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V. Plot the input and output waveforms for one full cycle.

**Step 1 — Identify the threshold.**

$$V_{th} = V_{ref} = +2 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Find the time instant when $V_{in}$ crosses $+2$ V on the rising edge.**

$$5 \sin(\omega t_1) = 2$$

$$\sin(\omega t_1) = 0.4$$

$$\omega t_1 = \sin^{-1}(0.4) = 23.578° = 0.4115 \text{ rad}$$

$$t_1 = \frac{0.4115}{2\pi \times 50} = 1.31 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Find the time instant when $V_{in}$ crosses $+2$ V on the falling edge.**

$$\omega t_2 = \pi - 0.4115 = 2.730 \text{ rad}$$

$$t_2 = \frac{2.730}{100\pi} = 8.69 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the output state in each interval.**

- For $0 < t < t_1$: $V_{in} < V_{ref} \Rightarrow V_o = +13$ V
- For $t_1 < t < t_2$: $V_{in} > V_{ref} \Rightarrow V_o = -13$ V
- For $t_2 < t < T/2$: $V_{in} < V_{ref} \Rightarrow V_o = +13$ V
- The pattern repeats symmetrically in the negative half-cycle

**Step 5 — Express the result as a duty cycle.**

The time during which $V_o = -13$ V in one full cycle is the interval where $|V_{in}| > 2$ V, which corresponds to a phase angle of:

$$2 \times (90° - 23.578°) = 2 \times 66.422° = 132.84°$$

Converting to time:

$$T_{-V_{sat}} = \frac{132.84°}{360°} \times 20 \text{ ms} = 7.38 \text{ ms}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Inverting Comparator | Non-Inverting Comparator | Inverting ZCD | Non-Inverting ZCD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{in}$ connected to | Inverting ($-$) | Non-inverting ($+$) | Inverting ($-$) | Non-inverting ($+$) |
| $V_{ref}$ connected to | Non-inverting ($+$) | Inverting ($-$) | Grounded ($0$ V) | Grounded ($0$ V) |
| $V_o = +V_{sat}$ when | $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ | $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ | $V_{in} < 0$ | $V_{in} > 0$ |
| $V_o = -V_{sat}$ when | $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ | $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ | $V_{in} > 0$ | $V_{in} < 0$ |
| Output inversion | Yes (logical NOT) | No (same polarity) | Yes (logical NOT) | No (same polarity) |
| Switching at $V_{th}$ | $V_{th} = V_{ref}$ | $V_{th} = V_{ref}$ | $V_{th} = 0$ V | $V_{th} = 0$ V |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Comparator

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional flow of a comparator, showing how the two input voltages are processed and how the decision is made at the output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Vin Analog Input Signal"] --> nodeD{"Differential Amplifier Stage"}
    node2["Vref Reference Voltage"] --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE["Open Loop Gain AOL Multiplication Stage"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"Saturation Decision Block"}
    nodeF -- "Vin greater than Vref" --> nodeG["Output +Vsat HIGH State"]
    nodeF -- "Vin less than Vref" --> nodeH["Output -Vsat LOW State"]
    nodeF -- "Vin equals Vref" --> nodeI["Switching Threshold Undefined Zone"]
    style nodeD fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000
    style nodeE fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000
    style nodeF fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    style nodeG fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    style nodeH fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000
    style nodeI fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000000
```

**Explanation of the flow:**
1. **Vin and Vref** are the two analog signals entering the differential amplifier stage.
2. The **Open Loop Gain stage** multiplies the small difference by $A_{OL} \approx 10^5$.
3. The **Saturation Decision block** checks if the amplified signal exceeds the supply rails and produces a digital output of $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$.

---

## 4.2 Comparator Decision Tree (Logic Flow)

The following flowchart shows the sequential decision-making process inside a comparator, applicable to all four variants covered in this module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Start Comparison Process"] --> nodeB{"Which Terminal is Vin Connected to"}
    nodeB -- "Inverting Terminal" --> nodeC{"Type of Comparator"}
    nodeB -- "Non Inverting Terminal" --> nodeD{"Type of Comparator"}
    nodeC -- "Vref equals zero" --> nodeE1["Inverting Zero Crossing Detector"]
    nodeC -- "Vref is non zero" --> nodeE2["Inverting Comparator with Vref"]
    nodeD -- "Vref equals zero" --> nodeF1["Non Inverting Zero Crossing Detector"]
    nodeD -- "Vref is non zero" --> nodeF2["Non Inverting Comparator with Vref"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeG1["Vin greater than zero yields minus Vsat"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeG2["Vin less than zero yields plus Vsat"]
    nodeE2 --> nodeH1["Vin greater than Vref yields minus Vsat"]
    nodeE2 --> nodeH2["Vin less than Vref yields plus Vsat"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeI1["Vin greater than zero yields plus Vsat"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeI2["Vin less than zero yields minus Vsat"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeJ1["Vin greater than Vref yields plus Vsat"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeJ2["Vin less than Vref yields minus Vsat"]
    style nodeA fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000000
    style nodeB fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000000
    style nodeE1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    style nodeE2 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    style nodeF1 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000
    style nodeF2 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000
```

**Explanation of the flow:**
- The first decision (input terminal) determines whether the output is **inverted** or **non-inverted**.
- The second decision (whether $V_{ref}$ is zero or non-zero) categorizes the comparator into a **ZCD** or a **standard comparator**.
- The four end nodes show the precise output state for the two possible input conditions.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Zero Crossing Detector Operation

The following block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a zero crossing detector in a typical signal-processing chain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1["Input Signal Source Sinusoidal or Triangular Wave"] --> nodeS2["Coupling Capacitor Removes DC Offset"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3["Op Amp in Open Loop Configuration with Vref Grounded"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4["Output Stage Square Wave at plus minus Vsat"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5["Application Block SCR Triggering or Phase Measurement"]
    style nodeS1 fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000000
    style nodeS2 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000000
    style nodeS3 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    style nodeS4 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000000
    style nodeS5 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000
```

**Explanation:**
1. The **input signal source** can be any time-varying analog voltage.
2. A **coupling capacitor** is often used to remove any DC bias from the input.
3. The **op-amp in open-loop** with grounded reference acts as the ZCD.
4. The **output** is a sharp square wave that transitions at every zero crossing of the input.
5. The **application block** uses these transitions for various control and measurement purposes.

---

## 4.4 Waveform Behavior Timeline (Schmitt Trigger / ZCD Hybrid View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading note:** This timeline-style block view summarizes the input-output behavior of a ZCD. The dashed vertical lines indicate the **switching instants** (zero crossings of the input), and the output steps are rectangular (idealized).

```mermaid
gantt
    title ZCD Output Behavior Across One Input Cycle
    dateFormat ss.SSS
    axisFormat %S.%L
    section Input Vin
    Negative Half Sine Cycle (Vin less than zero) :a1, 00.000, 5.000
    Positive Half Sine Cycle (Vin greater than zero) :a2, 05.000, 5.000
    section Output Vo
    Output equals plus Vsat HIGH :b1, 00.000, 2.500
    Output equals minus Vsat LOW :b2, 02.500, 5.000
    Output equals plus Vsat HIGH :b3, 07.500, 2.500
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output of an **inverting ZCD** is HIGH ($+V_{sat}$) during the **negative half-cycle** of the input, and LOW ($-V_{sat}$) during the **positive half-cycle**. The transitions occur precisely at the **zero crossings** of the input waveform.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a comparator and explain its basic principle of operation.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

A comparator is an electronic circuit that compares two analog input voltages and produces a binary output indicating which one is larger. It is built using an operational amplifier in **open-loop configuration** (no feedback network).

The op-amp has an extremely high open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ (typically $2 \times 10^5$). When a small differential voltage $V_d = V_+ - V_-$ is applied, the output tends toward $A_{OL} \cdot V_d$, which is far larger than the supply voltage. Hence, the op-amp is driven into **saturation**, producing either $+V_{sat}$ (HIGH) or $-V_{sat}$ (LOW).

- If $V_{in} > V_{ref}$, the output is $-V_{sat}$ (in inverting type).
- If $V_{in} < V_{ref}$, the output is $+V_{sat}$ (in inverting type).

The comparator thus converts an analog signal into a digital square wave. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: What is a zero crossing detector? Mention any two of its applications.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Zero Crossing Detector (ZCD)** is a special type of comparator in which the reference voltage is set to **zero volts** (the reference terminal is grounded). It detects the instant at which the input signal crosses the zero-volt level, producing a sharp transition in the output from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$ (or vice versa).

For an **inverting ZCD**:
- When $V_{in} > 0$: $V_o = -V_{sat}$
- When $V_{in} < 0$: $V_o = +V_{sat}$

**Applications:**
1. **Phase measurement** in power systems and AC line synchronization.
2. **Frequency multipliers** for generating harmonics of the input signal.
3. **Time-marker generation** for precise event timing in digital systems.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Inverting and Non-Inverting Comparators (Module Internal Choice Option 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 – Choice A]**

**Sub-part (a):** With a neat circuit diagram and input-output waveforms, explain the operation of an **inverting comparator** with $V_{ref} = +3$ V. The input is a sinusoidal wave of amplitude 6 V at 1 kHz. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 12$ V. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting terminal** of the op-amp, and the reference voltage $V_{ref} = +3$ V is applied to the **non-inverting terminal** through a voltage divider. The op-amp operates in open-loop (no feedback). The supply voltages are $\pm 15$ V, giving $V_{sat} = \pm 12$ V.

**2. Output Equation (1 mark):**
$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{ref} - V_{in}) = A_{OL} \cdot (3 - V_{in})$$

**3. Switching Logic (2 marks):**
- When $V_{in} < 3$ V: $V_o = +12$ V (HIGH)
- When $V_{in} > 3$ V: $V_o = -12$ V (LOW)
- Switching occurs at $V_{th} = V_{ref} = 3$ V

**4. Waveform Analysis (2 marks):**
For $V_{in}(t) = 6 \sin(2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot t)$:
- The output is HIGH ($+12$ V) during the periods when the sine wave is below $+3$ V.
- The output is LOW ($-12$ V) during the periods when the sine wave is above $+3$ V.
- Switching instants are found from $6 \sin(\omega t) = 3$, giving $\omega t = 30°$ and $150°$, i.e., $t = 0.0833$ ms and $0.4167$ ms in the first half-cycle.
- The output is a rectangular wave with duty cycle $\approx 66.67\%$ in LOW state and $33.33\%$ in HIGH state per half-cycle.

The input-output waveforms would show a sine wave input crossing a $+3$ V reference line, with the output toggling between $+12$ V and $-12$ V at each crossing.

---

**Sub-part (b):** Explain the operation of a **non-inverting comparator** with a circuit diagram. Compare its behavior with the inverting comparator using a transfer characteristic table. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting terminal** ($V_+$) of the op-amp, and the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied to the **inverting terminal** ($V_-$). The op-amp is in open-loop configuration with no feedback.

**2. Output Equation (1 mark):**
$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{in} - V_{ref})$$

**3. Switching Logic (2 marks):**
- When $V_{in} > V_{ref}$: $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (HIGH)
- When $V_{in} < V_{ref}$: $V_o = -V_{sat}$ (LOW)

**4. Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Condition | Inverting Comparator | Non-Inverting Comparator |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ | $V_o = -V_{sat}$ (LOW) | $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (HIGH) |
| $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ | $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (HIGH) | $V_o = -V_{sat}$ (LOW) |
| Output inversion | Yes (opposite to input polarity) | No (same as input polarity) |
| Switching threshold | $V_{th} = V_{ref}$ | $V_{th} = V_{ref}$ |

The non-inverting comparator produces an output that is **in-phase** with the input crossing, while the inverting comparator produces an **inverted** output.

---

### Question B: Zero Crossing Detector and Applications (Module Internal Choice Option 2)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 – Choice B]**

**Sub-part (a):** Design a **zero crossing detector** using an op-amp. Explain its operation with input-output waveforms for a **triangular wave** input of amplitude $\pm 5$ V at 500 Hz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Circuit Description (2 marks):**
An inverting-type ZCD is constructed using an op-amp in open-loop configuration:
- The **triangular wave input** $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting terminal** through a coupling capacitor.
- The **non-inverting terminal** is grounded (i.e., $V_{ref} = 0$ V).
- The supply voltages are $\pm 15$ V, giving $V_{sat} = \pm 13$ V.

**2. Output Equation (1 mark):**
$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (0 - V_{in}) = -A_{OL} \cdot V_{in}$$

**3. Switching Logic (2 marks):**
- When $V_{in} > 0$: $V_o = -V_{sat} = -13$ V (LOW)
- When $V_{in} < 0$: $V_o = +V_{sat} = +13$ V (HIGH)
- The output changes state at every instant the input crosses zero.

**4. Waveform Analysis (2 marks):**
For a triangular wave of $\pm 5$ V at 500 Hz (period = 2 ms):
- The input ramps linearly from $-5$ V to $+5$ V in 1 ms (positive slope).
- It crosses zero at $t = 1$ ms, $t = 3$ ms, etc.
- The output is a square wave with frequency **1 kHz** (twice the input frequency) — the ZCD acts as a **frequency doubler** for a symmetric triangular input.
- The output is HIGH ($+13$ V) during the negative half of the triangular input, and LOW ($-13$ V) during the positive half.

The waveforms show a triangular input centered at zero with a square-wave output in anti-phase, transitioning at each zero crossing.

---

**Sub-part (b):** Discuss any **four applications of comparators** with suitable functional descriptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Zero Crossing Detector (1.5 marks):**
Used to detect the precise moment when an AC signal crosses zero. Application: **SCR triggering circuits** in power electronics where the SCR must be fired at a controlled phase angle of the AC line.

**2. Analog-to-Digital (ADC) Conversion (1.5 marks):**
A **flash ADC** uses an array of $2^n - 1$ comparators to convert an analog voltage to an $n$-bit digital output. Each comparator has a different reference voltage (from a resistor ladder), and the comparator outputs form a thermometer code that is encoded into binary.

**3. Window Detector (1.5 marks):**
Two comparators are used in combination to detect when an input voltage lies **between** two threshold levels $V_{ref1}$ and $V_{ref2}$. Used in **battery voltage monitors** to detect over-charge and over-discharge conditions.

**4. Schmitt Trigger (1 mark):**
A comparator with **positive feedback** is called a Schmitt trigger. It has **hysteresis** — two different threshold voltages for rising and falling inputs, which prevents false triggering due to noise. Used in **signal squaring** and **waveform generation**.

**5. Pulse-Width Modulator (1.5 marks):**
A comparator is used to compare a slow-varying reference signal (e.g., a sawtooth or triangular wave) with a DC level. The output is a PWM signal whose **duty cycle** is proportional to the DC level. Used in **motor speed control** and **switched-mode power supplies**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to mark $V_{ref}$ and $V_{in}$ terminals correctly** — many students mix up inverting and non-inverting configurations, leading to inverted output states. This costs **2 to 3 marks** instantly.
> 
> 2. **Confusing the output state for the inverting ZCD** — students often state that the output is HIGH when $V_{in} > 0$ for an inverting ZCD. The correct answer is: the output is **LOW ($-V_{sat}$)** when $V_{in} > 0$.
> 
> 3. **Not drawing the waveforms with proper axis labels** — the input waveform, output waveform, and threshold line ($V_{ref}$) must all be visible. Just stating "output is HIGH/LOW" without sketching loses **2 marks** in a 7-mark waveform question.
> 
> 4. **Writing the linear op-amp equation** $V_o = A_{OL}(V_+ - V_-)$ **and assuming it stays in the linear region** — this is a conceptual error. The op-amp in a comparator is always in **saturation**, never in the linear region.
> 
> 5. **Omitting the $V_{sat}$ values** in the waveforms — examiners expect students to mention the typical values (e.g., $\pm 13$ V or $\pm 12$ V) on the output axis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **comparator** uses an op-amp in **open-loop** (no feedback) configuration.
- The output is always at **one of the two saturation levels** ($+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$), never in the linear region.
- The **switching threshold** of any comparator is the reference voltage: $V_{th} = V_{ref}$.
- The **inverting comparator** produces an output that is logically **inverted** with respect to the input level.
- The **non-inverting comparator** produces an output that is logically **non-inverted**.
- A **zero crossing detector (ZCD)** is a comparator with $V_{ref} = 0$ V; it switches output at every zero crossing of the input.
- An **inverting ZCD** gives LOW output when $V_{in} > 0$ and HIGH output when $V_{in} < 0$.
- A **non-inverting ZCD** gives HIGH output when $V_{in} > 0$ and LOW output when $V_{in} < 0$.
- The **sensitivity** of a comparator is $\Delta V_{min} = V_{sat} / A_{OL}$, typically around **65 $\mu$V** for a 741 op-amp.
- The **transfer characteristic** of any comparator is a step function with the step at $V_{in} = V_{ref}$.
- A **ZCD with a triangular input** produces a square wave at **twice the input frequency** (acts as a frequency doubler).
- Important **applications**: ADC (flash type), window detector, Schmitt trigger, PWM generator, SCR triggering, phase measurement.
- Typical **op-amp parameters**: $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$, $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13$ V (with $\pm 15$ V supply).
- Always **label the waveforms** with the threshold line, saturation levels, and switching instants.
- The op-amp is in **saturation** in a comparator — **never** in the linear amplification region.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Schmitt Trigger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Schmitt Trigger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Schmitt Trigger** is a regenerative comparator circuit with a **hysteresis loop** built using **positive feedback**, which converts a noisy, slowly varying analog input into a clean, sharply defined digital output. It possesses **two distinct switching thresholds** — an **Upper Threshold Voltage ($V_{UT}$)** and a **Lower Threshold Voltage ($V_{LT}$)** — that govern state transitions depending on the direction of input signal change.

Mathematically, the circuit is defined by the output state transition condition:

$$V_{out}(t) = \begin{cases} +V_{sat} & \text{if } V_{in}(t) \ge V_{UT} \\ -V_{sat} & \text{if } V_{in}(t) \le V_{LT} \end{cases}$$

where **$V_{sat}$** is the saturation voltage of the active device (op-amp or transistor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Schmitt trigger is classified as a **regenerative comparator with memory**. The presence of two threshold voltages (not a single one) is the defining feature, granting it **noise immunity** that ordinary comparators lack.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **door with a strong spring** that requires you to push it a fair distance before it swings open. Once open, you must pull it back substantially to close it. The "push" force and "pull" force are different — this gap is **hysteresis**.

A Schmitt trigger behaves identically for electrical signals:
- The input must rise **above $V_{UT}$** to force the output HIGH.
- The input must fall **below $V_{LT}$** to force the output LOW.
- Inputs in the **dead-band** between $V_{LT}$ and $V_{UT}$ cause **no change** in output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Meaning:** A Schmitt trigger is a "noisy-signal cleaning" circuit. It refuses to flip-flop due to small jitters — it only reacts when the input crosses a meaningful boundary.

## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **$V_{sat}$** | Op-amp saturation voltage | $\pm 12\text{ V}$ to $\pm 15\text{ V}$ |
| **$V_{UT}$** | Upper Trigger Threshold | Positive value |
| **$V_{LT}$** | Lower Trigger Threshold | Negative value (symmetric case) |
| **$V_{H}$** | Hysteresis Voltage | $V_{UT} - V_{LT}$ |
| **$\beta$** | Feedback fraction | $0 < \beta < 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Philosophy — Why Hysteresis?

A standard **comparator** has only **one threshold** ($V_{ref}$). If the input is noisy and hovers near $V_{ref}$, the output oscillates wildly between HIGH and LOW — a phenomenon called **"chattering"** or **"comparator bounce"**.

A Schmitt trigger solves this by introducing a **dead-band** of width $V_{H}$ around the reference level. As long as the input remains inside this band, the output retains its previous state (memory effect via positive feedback).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The hysteresis width $V_{H}$ must be **larger than the peak-to-peak noise** riding on the input. Designers choose $\beta$ accordingly: $V_{H} = 2\beta V_{sat}$.

## 2.2 Two Canonical Configurations

### (A) Inverting Schmitt Trigger
- Input applied to the **inverting (−)** terminal.
- Positive feedback from output to the **non-inverting (+)** terminal via a resistor divider $R_1$, $R_2$.

### (B) Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger
- Input applied to the **non-inverting (+)** terminal.
- Positive feedback from output to the **inverting (−)** terminal.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\mathbf{\vert \cdot \vert}$ for absolute value within prose; use $\mathbf{\mid}$ inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Configuration | Upper Threshold $V_{UT}$ | Lower Threshold $V_{LT}$ | Hysteresis $V_{H}$ |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Inverting** (input at −ve) | $+\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | $-\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | $2\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ |
| 2 | **Non-Inverting** (input at +ve) | $+\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $-\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $2\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ |
| 3 | Feedback fraction (Inverting) | $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | — | — |
| 4 | Asymmetric reference (Inverting) | $V_{ref} + \beta(V_{sat}-V_{ref})$ | $V_{ref} - \beta(V_{sat}+V_{ref})$ | $2\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ |
| 5 | **TTL/CMOS** (74HC14) | $V_{UT} = 2.7\text{ V}$ (typ) | $V_{LT} = 1.5\text{ V}$ (typ) | $V_{H} = 1.2\text{ V}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal formula for Inverting Schmitt trigger with biased reference $V_{ref}$:**
>
> $$V_{TH} = V_{ref} \pm \beta \cdot (V_{sat} \mp V_{ref})$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Mechanical Switch Debouncing** — Eliminates contact-bounce noise in keyboards, push-buttons, relays.
2. **Zero-Crossing Detectors** — Used in power electronics, TRIAC firing circuits.
3. **Function Generators** — Astable / Monostable multivibrator cores in the **NE555 timer**.
4. **ECG / EEG Signal Conditioning** — Removes 50/60 Hz mains hum and muscle artifact.
5. **ADC Front-End** — Sits before a comparator to provide clean edges for the sampling circuit.
6. **Relaxation Oscillators** — When combined with an RC network, it produces square waves.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Threshold Voltages

**Given:** Op-amp with saturation $\pm V_{sat}$, resistors $R_1$ (between output and non-inverting input) and $R_2$ (between non-inverting input and ground). Input $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting terminal.

**Goal:** Find $V_{UT}$ and $V_{LT}$.

### Step 1 — Apply Superposition at the Non-Inverting Terminal

The voltage at the non-inverting input ($V^+$) receives contributions from two sources: the output voltage $V_{out}$ (through $R_1$) and ground (through $R_2$).

$$V^+ = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} + 0 \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Explanation:** Using the voltage divider rule, since no current flows into the ideal op-amp's +ve terminal.

### Step 2 — Define the Feedback Fraction

$$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Thus:

$$V^+ = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Two Output States

**Case A:** $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ (output saturated HIGH)

$$V^+ = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$

**Case B:** $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ (output saturated LOW)

$$V^+ = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Switching Condition

The op-amp output flips when $V_{in} = V^+$. The instant $V_{in}$ just **exceeds** $V^+$, the output saturates in the opposite polarity.

- When output is at $+V_{sat}$, the moment $V_{in} \ge +\beta V_{sat}$, the output swings to $-V_{sat}$.

$$V_{UT} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$

- When output is at $-V_{sat}$, the moment $V_{in} \le -\beta V_{sat}$, the output swings to $+V_{sat}$.

$$V_{LT} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Hysteresis Width

$$V_{H} = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = \beta \cdot V_{sat} - (-\beta \cdot V_{sat}) = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$

### Step 6 — Closed-Form Result

$$\boxed{V_{UT} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}, \quad V_{LT} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}, \quad V_{H} = \frac{2 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger Thresholds

**Given:** Input $V_{in}$ to the non-inverting terminal. Output fed back to the inverting terminal through $R_1$; $R_2$ from inverting terminal to ground.

### Step 1 — Voltage at the Inverting Terminal

By superposition at the (−) node:

$$V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} + V_{in} \cdot 0 \;\;\;\;(\text{no input at inverting node})$$

Wait — correct approach. The inverting input sees only the output through the divider (input is at the +ve terminal), so:

$$V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Virtual Short Assumption at Switching

At the instant of switching, $V^+ = V^-$, i.e.:

$$V_{in} = V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Switching Threshold

$$V_{TH} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{out}$$

For $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$:

$$V_{UT} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$$

For $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$:

$$V_{LT} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$$

### Step 4 — Hysteresis Width

$$V_{H} = 2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Asymmetric / Biased Thresholds

When a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is injected at the non-inverting node through a third resistor $R_3$, the thresholds shift asymmetrically.

### Step 1 — Superposition at Non-Inverting Node

$$V^+ = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2 \parallel R_3}{R_1 + (R_2 \parallel R_3)} + V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_1 \parallel R_2}{R_3 + (R_1 \parallel R_2)}$$

### Step 2 — Switching Condition

The output flips when $V_{in} = V^+$. Substituting $V_{out} = \pm V_{sat}$:

$$V_{UT} = \beta \cdot V_{sat} + (1-\beta) \cdot V_{ref}$$

$$V_{LT} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat} + (1-\beta) \cdot V_{ref}$$

### Step 3 — Hysteresis Width Remains Constant

$$V_{H} = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$

**Conclusion:** $V_{ref}$ shifts the dead-band window up or down, but the **width** $V_{H}$ depends only on $\beta$ and $V_{sat}$.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Design an **inverting Schmitt trigger** with $V_{sat} = \pm 12\text{ V}$, $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5\text{ k}\Omega$. Find $V_{UT}$, $V_{LT}$, and $V_{H}$.

**Step 1 — Compute feedback fraction**

$$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{5\text{ k}}{10\text{ k} + 5\text{ k}} = \frac{5}{15} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 2 — Upper threshold**

$$V_{UT} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat} = \frac{1}{3} \times 12 = +4\text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Lower threshold**

$$V_{LT} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat} = -4\text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Hysteresis width**

$$V_{H} = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 4 - (-4) = 8\text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

Any input noise with peak-to-peak amplitude **less than 8 V** will not cause the output to change spuriously. ✅

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Inverting Schmitt | Non-Inverting Schmitt | Standard Comparator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of thresholds | **2** ($V_{UT}, V_{LT}$) | **2** ($V_{UT}, V_{LT}$) | 1 ($V_{ref}$) |
| Noise immunity | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | Poor |
| Phase relationship | Output $\bot$ Input | Output $\parallel$ Input | Output $\bot$ Input |
| Hysteresis width | $2\beta V_{sat}$ | $2\beta V_{sat}$ | **0** |
| Use as oscillator | Yes (with RC) | Yes (with RC) | No |
| Memory of previous state | **Yes** | **Yes** | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Using Op-Amp

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Vin DC SOURCE"] --> node2["INVERTING INPUT MINUS of OPAMP"]
    node3["Vsat PLUS OUTPUT"] --> node4["RESISTOR R1"]
    node4 --> node5["NON INVERTING INPUT PLUS NODE"]
    node5 --> node6["RESISTOR R2"]
    node6 --> node7["GROUND GND"]
    node8["OPAMP UA741"] --> node3
    node2 --> node8
    node5 --> node8
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `Vin` enters the **inverting (MINUS)** terminal.
- The output $V_{out}$ is fed back via $R_1$ to the **non-inverting (PLUS)** terminal.
- $R_2$ shunts the PLUS node to **ground (GND)**, creating the voltage divider.
- The op-amp is a generic 741-class device.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Vin AC OR DC"] --> nodeB["NON INVERTING INPUT PLUS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["OPAMP DEVICE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Vout SATURATION OUTPUT"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["RESISTOR R1 FEEDBACK"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["INVERTING INPUT MINUS NODE"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["RESISTOR R2 TO GROUND"]
    nodeF --> nodeC
    nodeG --> nodeH["GROUND GND"]
```

## 4.3 Hysteresis Transfer Characteristic (Block-Level Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1["STATE START OUTPUT LOW MINUS Vsat"] --> nodeQ1{"Vin RISING CROSSES VUT"}
    nodeQ1 -- YES --> nodeS2["OUTPUT FLIPS TO PLUS Vsat HIGH"]
    nodeQ1 -- NO --> nodeS1
    nodeS2 --> nodeQ2{"Vin FALLING CROSSES VLT"}
    nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeS3["OUTPUT FLIPS TO MINUS Vsat LOW"]
    nodeQ2 -- NO --> nodeS2
    nodeS3 --> nodeQ1
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **state machine** loops between $\pm V_{sat}$.
- Transition HIGH-to-LOW happens only when input **falls below $V_{LT}$**.
- Transition LOW-to-HIGH happens only when input **rises above $V_{UT}$**.
- This loop is the **electrical embodiment of hysteresis**.

## 4.4 Application Block Diagram — Switch Debouncer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1["MECHANICAL PUSH BUTTON SWITCH"] --> nodeP2["CONTACT BOUNCE NOISE 0 to 5 ms"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3["SCHMITT TRIGGER INVERTER 74HC14"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4["CLEAN DIGITAL EDGE TO MCU GPIO"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** Mechanical bounces are rapid oscillations of amplitude much smaller than $V_H$, so the Schmitt trigger ignores them.

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Relaxation Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeO1["DC SUPPLY VCC"] --> nodeO2["RESISTOR R"]
    nodeO2 --> nodeO3["CAPACITOR C TO GROUND"]
    nodeO3 --> nodeO4["SCHMITT TRIGGER INVERTER"]
    nodeO4 --> nodeO5["OUTPUT SQUARE WAVE f0"]
    nodeO4 --> nodeO6["FEEDBACK TO RC NODE"]
```

**Frequency of oscillation:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2 R C \ln\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$$

For $\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ and a symmetric supply, when $R_1 = R_2$, $\beta = 0.5$, and the formula reduces to $f_0 = \frac{1}{2RC \ln 3} \approx \frac{0.91}{RC}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` What is a Schmitt trigger? Why is it preferred over a simple comparator for noisy input signals?

**Model Answer:**

A **Schmitt trigger** is a regenerative comparator circuit employing **positive feedback** that exhibits **two distinct switching thresholds** — an **Upper Threshold Voltage ($V_{UT}$)** and a **Lower Threshold Voltage ($V_{LT}$)** — for the rising and falling transitions of the input.

A simple comparator has a **single threshold** ($V_{ref}$). If the input signal is corrupted by noise, the comparator output will **chatter** (oscillate) as the noisy input repeatedly crosses $V_{ref}$. The Schmitt trigger's hysteresis band of width $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT}$ provides a **dead zone** that suppresses noise — the output changes state only when the input crosses one of the two well-separated thresholds, producing a clean digital output.

$$\boxed{V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}}$$

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Define the term **hysteresis voltage** in a Schmitt trigger. Write the expression for the same in an inverting Schmitt trigger using an op-amp.

**Model Answer:**

**Hysteresis voltage ($V_H$)** is defined as the **difference between the upper trigger threshold and the lower trigger threshold** of a Schmitt trigger. It represents the width of the input voltage range over which the output remains in its current state, providing noise immunity.

$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT}$$

For an **inverting Schmitt trigger** using an op-amp with saturation $\pm V_{sat}$ and feedback fraction $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$:

$$V_H = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat} = \frac{2 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the **inverting** and **non-inverting** configurations — the input terminals swap and the formula changes.
> - **Always** draw the **transfer characteristic** (hysteresic loop) with axes labelled; missing arrows or unlabelled axes lead to **−2 marks**.
> - Forgetting to write **units (V)** in numerical answers is a common 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> - Mentioning **positive feedback** explicitly carries weight — students often skip this and lose marks.
> - State the **assumed $V_{sat}$ value** in numerical problems if not given, e.g., "Assuming $V_{sat} = \pm 12\text{ V}$".

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an inverting Schmitt trigger using an op-amp. Derive the expressions for the upper and lower threshold voltages.**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:** The circuit consists of an op-amp with its inverting terminal (−) receiving the input signal $V_{in}$. A resistor $R_1$ connects the output $V_{out}$ to the non-inverting terminal (+), and resistor $R_2$ connects the non-inverting terminal to ground. This forms a positive feedback network.

**Step 1 — Voltage at the Non-Inverting Input (Voltage Divider)**

$$V^+ = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.

**Step 2 — Two Possible States of $V^+$**

- If $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$: $V^+ = +\beta V_{sat}$
- If $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$: $V^+ = -\beta V_{sat}$

**Step 3 — Switching Criterion**

The op-amp output switches state when $V_{in} = V^+$.

- If $V_{out}$ is at $+V_{sat}$ and $V_{in}$ just exceeds $+\beta V_{sat}$, then $V_{in} > V^+$ → output flips to $-V_{sat}$.

$$V_{UT} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$$

- If $V_{out}$ is at $-V_{sat}$ and $V_{in}$ falls below $-\beta V_{sat}$, then $V_{in} < V^+$ → output flips to $+V_{sat}$.

$$V_{LT} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$$

**Step 4 — Hysteresis Width**
