2. **Operating Principle (2 marks):** Assume $C$ is initially at $-\beta V_{sat}$ and output is $+V_{sat}$. The capacitor charges through $R$ towards $+V_{sat}$. When $v_C$ reaches $+\beta V_{sat}$, the comparator flips, output becomes $-V_{sat}$, and the cycle reverses.

3. **Derivation of $T$ (2 marks):** Solving the exponential charging equation with $V_{initial} = -\beta V_{sat}$ and reaching $+\beta V_{sat}$ gives the half-period $T/2 = RC \ln[(1 + \beta)/(1 - \beta)]$, and full period $T = 2RC \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_2}\right)$.

4. **Final Frequency Expression (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2 R C \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_2}\right)}}
$$

#### Model Solution to Part B(b) — 7 marks

**Part (i) — Threshold Voltages (3 marks):**

The feedback fraction is $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \dfrac{15 \text{ k}\Omega}{15 \text{ k}\Omega + 30 \text{ k}\Omega} = \dfrac{15}{45} = \dfrac{1}{3}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{TH} &= +\beta V_{sat} = +\frac{1}{3} \times 13 = +4.333 \text{ V} \\
V_{TL} &= -\beta V_{sat} = -\frac{1}{3} \times 13 = -4.333 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The feedback divider scales the saturation voltage by the ratio $R_1/(R_1+R_2)$, which is $1/3$ in this design.

**Part (ii) — Frequency and Period (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= \frac{1}{2 R C \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_2}\right)} \\
    &= \frac{1}{2 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \times \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{2 \times 15}{30}\right)} \\
    &= \frac{1}{2 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-7} \times \ln(1 + 1)} \\
    &= \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3} \times \ln 2} \\
    &= \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6931} \\
    &= \frac{1}{1.386 \times 10^{-3}} \\
    &\approx 721.3 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Time Period:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_0} = \frac{1}{721.3} \approx 1.386 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{TH} = +4.33 \text{ V}, \quad V_{TL} = -4.33 \text{ V}, \quad f_0 \approx 721.3 \text{ Hz}, \quad T \approx 1.386 \text{ ms}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Since $R_1 < R_2$, the feedback fraction $\beta < 0.5$, the threshold voltages are **smaller in magnitude** than $V_{sat}/2$, and the resulting frequency is **higher** than the $R_1 = R_2$ case would give (which is $f_0 = 0.455/RC \approx 455$ Hz for the same $R$, $C$). The value 721 Hz is therefore consistent and physically realistic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3)**

- **Barkhausen Criterion:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ AND $\angle A\beta = 0^{\circ}$ (or $360^{\circ}$). Two simultaneous conditions, not one.
- **Wien Bridge Oscillator:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ for $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$. Required gain $A = 3$, so $R_f = 2 R_i$. Best for **low-distortion audio sine waves**. Uses both positive and negative feedback.
- **RC Phase Shift Oscillator:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C \sqrt{6}}$. Uses three RC sections (each $60^{\circ}$ shift) + inverting op-amp ($180^{\circ}$ shift). Required gain $A = 29$. Output sine wave has noticeable distortion.
- **Hartley Oscillator:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M) C}}$. Two inductors, one capacitor.
- **Colpitts Oscillator:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L (C_1 C_2)/(C_1 + C_2)}}$. One inductor, two capacitors.
- **Astable Multivibrator:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2 R C \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{2 R_1}{R_2}\right)}$. **Square wave** output. Threshold voltages are $\pm \beta V_{sat}$ where $\beta = R_1/(R_1+R_2)$, **NOT** $\pm V_{sat}$.
- **Triangular Wave Generator:** Built by cascading a **Schmitt trigger** (comparator with hysteresis) followed by an **integrator**. Frequency set by integrator time constant and Schmitt hysteresis width.
- **Function Generator ICs (e.g., ICL 8038, XR 2206):** Produce **three simultaneous outputs** — sine, square, and triangular — from a single chip using current-source charging of an external capacitor and a flip-flop-controlled discharge path.
- **Duty Cycle of Astable:** 50% only when $R_1 = R_2$. Use **diode-steering networks** for asymmetric duty cycles.
- **Practical Frequency Range:** RC oscillators dominate **1 Hz – 1 MHz**; LC oscillators dominate **above 1 MHz**; crystal oscillators for **ultra-stable references** (ppm-level stability).
- **Slew Rate Limitation:** Maximum usable frequency of any op-amp oscillator is limited by the op-amp's slew rate, $f_{max} \approx S_R / (2 \pi V_{peak})$.
- **Why positive feedback in relaxation oscillators:** Provides **hysteresis**, which is essential to create a *two-state* switching action — without it, the output would just sit at the threshold and never produce a clean square wave.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Valuation Tip:**
> When answering any 14-mark derivation question, always present the solution in **four explicit sections**: (1) Circuit / block description, (2) Identification of feedback factor or threshold levels, (3) Step-by-step algebraic derivation with the Barkhausen condition explicitly invoked, and (4) Final boxed formula with units. Examiners award **2 to 3 marks per section**; missing any one of them guarantees a partial-loss of marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable and Monostable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** AEC403 - ANALOG ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Astable and Monostable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multivibrator:** A regenerative electronic switching circuit used to implement a two-state system, characterized by two amplifier stages connected in a positive feedback loop. Multivibrators are classified into three families based on their stable states: **Astable** (no stable state), **Monostable** (one stable state), and **Bistable** (two stable states).

**Astable Multivibrator:**
An astable multivibrator is a free-running oscillator circuit that has **NO stable states**. It continuously switches between two quasi-stable states without any external triggering, producing a **square wave** (or rectangular wave) output. The circuit automatically oscillates as soon as DC power is applied, making it a self-excited oscillator.

**Monostable Multivibrator:**
A monostable multivibrator (also called a *one-shot* or *single-shot* multivibrator) has **ONE stable state and ONE quasi-stable state**. It remains in its stable state indefinitely until an external trigger pulse forces it into the quasi-stable state. After a predetermined time interval (determined by an RC network), the circuit automatically returns to its stable state, producing a single output pulse of fixed width for every input trigger.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the revised NEP 2020 syllabus, special emphasis is placed on the **IC 555 Timer** implementation of these multivibrators due to its industrial relevance, compactness, and the elimination of bulky discrete components.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### Astable Multivibrator: "The Eternal Pendulum"
Imagine a **metronome** that keeps swinging left and right *on its own* without anyone ever touching it. It never rests at any extreme — it just keeps ticking back and forth forever. The astable multivibrator behaves exactly like this: its output is perpetually oscillating between HIGH and LOW, producing a continuous train of square pulses. **It needs no external push.**

### Monostable Multivibrator: "The Self-Righting Sign"
Think of a **road sign on a spring-loaded hinge** that normally stands upright (its stable, rest position). When a gust of wind (the trigger pulse) knocks it flat, the spring automatically pulls it back upright after a few seconds (the timing period). After it returns upright, it waits for the *next* gust. **One trigger in, one pulse out.**

## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters and Constants

- **Threshold Voltage Level:** **V_CC × (2/3)** — set internally in 555 timer (1/3 V_CC comparator in monostable)
- **Trigger Voltage Level:** **V_CC × (1/3)** — used to initiate a state change
- **Output Voltage Swing:** Approximately **0 V to V_CC** (for TTL-compatible 555)
- **Standard Supply Range (NE555):** **+4.5 V to +16 V**
- **Timing Constant:** **τ = RC** — fundamental time-scaling parameter

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the fractions **1/3 V_CC** and **2/3 V_CC** — these are the *two magic numbers* that govern every 555-timer-based circuit. Nearly all derivations, threshold diagrams, and waveform sketches on KTU exams pivot around these levels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 555 Timer — Foundational Block

Both the astable and monostable configurations are built around the **IC 555 Timer**, an 8-pin hybrid analog/digital integrated circuit. Its internal architecture consists of:

- A **Voltage Divider** (three 5 kΩ resistors → derives 1/3 V_CC and 2/3 V_CC reference voltages)
- **Two Comparators** (Threshold Comparator at Pin 6 and Trigger Comparator at Pin 2)
- An **SR Flip-Flop** (set by trigger, reset by threshold)
- A **Discharge Transistor** (open-collector NPN at Pin 7)
- An **Output Driver Stage** (totem-pole at Pin 3)

## 2.2 Astable Multivibrator — Operating Logic

In the astable configuration, the 555 timer is wired so that:
- Pin 2 (Trigger) and Pin 6 (Threshold) are **tied together** and connected to the timing capacitor **C** through resistor **R_B**.
- The discharge pin (Pin 7) is connected between two resistors **R_A** and **R_B**, with R_A tied to V_CC and R_B tied to the capacitor C.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic:

1. **Initial Power-On:** Capacitor C is uncharged → V_C = 0 V < 1/3 V_CC.
2. **Trigger Comparator fires** → Sets the internal Flip-Flop → Output goes **HIGH**.
3. **Discharge transistor (Pin 7) turns OFF** → Capacitor C begins charging through the series combination of R_A and R_B toward V_CC.
4. **Charging Continues** until V_C reaches **2/3 V_CC** (the threshold level).
5. **Threshold Comparator fires** → Resets the internal Flip-Flop → Output goes **LOW**.
6. **Discharge transistor turns ON** → Capacitor C now discharges through R_B alone (Pin 7 sinks current to ground).
7. **Discharging Continues** until V_C drops back to **1/3 V_CC**.
8. **Trigger Comparator fires again** → Cycle repeats indefinitely.

> [!NOTE]
> The two resistors R_A and R_B are *asymmetrically placed* in the charge/discharge paths. R_B appears in **both** paths, while R_A appears only in the charging path. This asymmetry is the origin of the *uneven* duty cycle characteristic of the 555 astable.

## 2.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Operating Logic

In the monostable configuration:
- Pin 6 (Threshold) and Pin 7 (Discharge) are **tied together** and connected to the timing capacitor C.
- Pin 2 (Trigger) receives **external negative-going trigger pulses**.
- A single timing resistor R connects V_CC to the capacitor node.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic:

1. **Stable State (Rest):** Output is **LOW**. Discharge transistor is **ON** → Capacitor C is shorted to ground → V_C ≈ 0 V.
2. **Trigger Arrives:** A negative-going pulse drives Pin 2 below 1/3 V_CC.
3. **Trigger Comparator fires** → Sets the internal Flip-Flop → Output goes **HIGH**.
4. **Discharge transistor turns OFF** → Capacitor C begins charging through R toward V_CC.
5. **Quasi-Stable Duration:** Output remains HIGH until V_C reaches 2/3 V_CC.
6. **Threshold Comparator fires** → Resets the Flip-Flop → Output returns **LOW**.
7. **Discharge transistor turns ON** → Capacitor rapidly discharges through Pin 7.
8. **Recovery State:** Circuit returns to the stable state, awaiting the *next* trigger pulse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** The monostable's *only* stable state is LOW. It is *passive* — it does nothing until triggered. This makes it ideal for **pulse stretching, missing pulse detection, and digital timing** applications.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| Parameter | Astable (555) | Monostable (555) | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Charging Time Constant** | $\tau_c = (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$ | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | seconds |
| **Discharging Time Constant** | $\tau_d = R_B \cdot C$ | N/A (instantaneous via Pin 7) | seconds |
| **HIGH Duration (T_HIGH)** | $T_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$ | $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ | seconds |
| **LOW Duration (T_LOW)** | $T_L = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$ | N/A (depends on trigger rate) | seconds |
| **Total Time Period (T)** | $T = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$ | $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ | seconds |
| **Frequency (f)** | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$ | $f_{trigger}$ determines output rate | Hz |
| **Duty Cycle (D)** | $D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$ | N/A (single pulse concept) | % |
| **50% Duty Cycle Condition** | $R_A \ll R_B$ (practical limit) | N/A | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** The factor is **0.693** (which equals $\ln 2$), **NOT 0.5** and **NOT 1.0**. The 1.1 factor in monostable comes from $\ln 3 \approx 1.0986 \approx 1.1$, since the capacitor charges from **0 to 2/3 V_CC** (voltage ratio = 2) and the decay in the natural log uses the ratio between 1/3 V_CC and 2/3 V_CC.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Astable:** LED flashers, pulse generators, clock signals for digital logic, PWM generation, tone generation in alarms and toys.
- **Monostable:** Debouncing mechanical switches, missing pulse detectors, frequency dividers, time-delay relays, analog-to-digital conversion sample-and-hold triggering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Astable Multivibrator Time Period

### Universal RC Charging Equation
The voltage across a capacitor being charged through a resistor from a supply V_CC, starting from an initial voltage V_i, is given by:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - V_i) \cdot e^{-t / \tau}$$

where $\tau = R \cdot C$ is the time constant of the charging path.

### Step 1 — Charging Phase Derivation (T_HIGH)
During charging, the capacitor charges from $V_i = \dfrac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ to $V_f = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ through $\tau_c = (R_A + R_B) C$.

Substituting the boundary values into the charging equation:

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}\right) \cdot e^{-T_H / \tau_c}$$

The expression in parentheses simplifies: $V_{CC} - \dfrac{1}{3} V_{CC} = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{CC}$.

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-T_H / \tau_c}$$

Rearranging to isolate the exponential term:

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-T_H / \tau_c} = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$$

Dividing both sides by $\dfrac{2}{3} V_{CC}$:

$$e^{-T_H / \tau_c} = \frac{1/3}{2/3} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides (note: $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2$):

$$-T_H / \tau_c = -\ln 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_H = \tau_c \cdot \ln 2$$

Substituting the numerical value $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ and $\tau_c = (R_A + R_B) C$:

$$T_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$$

### Step 2 — Discharging Phase Derivation (T_LOW)
During discharging, the capacitor discharges from $V_i = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ to $V_f = \dfrac{1}{3} V_{CC}$ through the discharge pin 7 and resistor R_B. The discharge time constant is $\tau_d = R_B C$.

The general discharge equation is:

$$V_C(t) = V_i \cdot e^{-t / \tau_d}$$

Substituting boundary values:

$$\frac{1}{3} V_{CC} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-T_L / \tau_d}$$

Isolating the exponential:

$$e^{-T_L / \tau_d} = \frac{1/3}{2/3} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$-T_L / \tau_d = -\ln 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_L = \tau_d \cdot \ln 2$$

Substituting the numerical value and $\tau_d = R_B C$:

$$T_L = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$$

### Step 3 — Total Time Period and Frequency
The total period of one complete cycle is the sum of the HIGH and LOW durations:

$$T = T_H + T_L = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C + 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$$

Factoring out the common term $0.693 \cdot C$:

$$T = 0.693 \cdot C \cdot (R_A + R_B + R_B) = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$$

The oscillation frequency is the reciprocal of the period:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$$

Using the identity $\dfrac{1}{0.693} \approx 1.443 \approx 1.44$:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Monostable Multivibrator Pulse Width

### Setup of Initial and Final Conditions
- **Initial Voltage:** When the circuit is in the stable state, the discharge transistor is ON, so $V_i \approx 0$ V (capacitor fully discharged).
- **Final Voltage:** When the quasi-stable state ends, the capacitor has charged to $V_f = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{CC}$.

The charging time constant is $\tau = R \cdot C$ (Pin 7 is OFF, R connects V_CC to C).

### Application of the Charging Equation

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - 0) \cdot e^{-t / \tau} = V_{CC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t / \tau}\right)$$

Setting $V_C(T) = \dfrac{2}{3} V_{CC}$ at the end of the pulse width $T$:

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = V_{CC} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-T / \tau}\right)$$

Dividing both sides by $V_{CC}$:

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T / \tau}$$

Rearranging:

$$e^{-T / \tau} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-T / \tau = \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln 3$$

$$T = \tau \cdot \ln 3 = R \cdot C \cdot \ln 3$$

Using the numerical value $\ln 3 \approx 1.0986 \approx 1.1$:

$$\boxed{T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Design a 555 astable multivibrator to generate a square wave of frequency **1 kHz** with a duty cycle of approximately **75%**. Assume C = 0.1 μF.

**Step 1:** Calculate the total period.
$$T = 1/f = 1/1000 = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2:** Express the duty cycle formula and solve for the ratio R_B / R_A.

$$D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} = 0.75$$

$$R_A + R_B = 0.75 R_A + 1.5 R_B$$

$$0.25 R_A = 0.5 R_B \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_A = 2 R_B$$

**Step 3:** Use the period formula to solve for R_B.

$$T = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C = 0.693 \cdot (2R_B + 2R_B) \cdot C = 0.693 \cdot 4R_B \cdot C$$

$$1 \times 10^{-3} = 0.693 \cdot 4 \cdot R_B \cdot (0.1 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$R_B = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{0.693 \cdot 4 \cdot 10^{-7}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{2.772 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 3.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Calculate R_A.

$$R_A = 2 R_B = 2 \times 3.6 \text{ k}\Omega = 7.2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Final Answer:** R_A ≈ 7.2 kΩ, R_B ≈ 3.6 kΩ, C = 0.1 μF.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 555 Timer — Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC_NODE["VCC Pin 8 GND Pin 1"]
    DIVIDER["Resistive Divider 3 x 5k ohm"]
    REF1["Reference 1 = 2/3 VCC"]
    REF2["Reference 2 = 1/3 VCC"]
    COMPA["Comparator A Threshold Pin 6"]
    COMPB["Comparator B Trigger Pin 2"]
    FLIPFLOP["SR Flip Flop Logic Core"]
    QBAR["Q Bar Output"]
    QOUT["Q Output"]
    DISCHG["Discharge Transistor Pin 7 Open Collector NPN"]
    DRIVER["Output Driver Stage Totem Pole"]
    OUTPUT["Output Pin 3"]
    RESET["Reset Pin 4 Active Low"]

    VCC_NODE --> DIVIDER
    DIVIDER --> REF1
    DIVIDER --> REF2
    REF1 --> COMPA
    REF2 --> COMPB
    COMPA -->|R input to FF| FLIPFLOP
    COMPB -->|S input to FF| FLIPFLOP
    FLIPFLOP --> QOUT
    FLIPFLOP --> QBAR
    QOUT --> DRIVER
    DRIVER --> OUTPUT
    QBAR --> DISCHG
    RESET -->|Clear input| FLIPFLOP
```

## 4.2 Astable Multivibrator — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START(["DC Power Applied"])
    INIT["Capacitor uncharged Vc = 0 V"]
    TRIG1["Vc less than 1/3 VCC Trigger Comparator fires"]
    SETHIGH["Flip Flop SET Output goes HIGH Discharge OFF"]
    CHARGING["Capacitor charges through RA and RB toward VCC"]
    THRESH["Vc reaches 2/3 VCC Threshold Comparator fires"]
    RESETLOW["Flip Flop RESET Output goes LOW Discharge ON"]
    DISCHARGING["Capacitor discharges through RB to ground"]
    LOOPCHECK{"Vc less than 1/3 VCC again?"}
    BACKTRIG["Return to Trigger Comparator stage"]
    OUTPUTWAVE["Continuous square wave at Pin 3"]

    START --> INIT --> TRIG1 --> SETHIGH --> CHARGING --> THRESH --> RESETLOW --> DISCHARGING --> LOOPCHECK
    LOOPCHECK -->|Yes| BACKTRIG --> TRIG1
    LOOPCHECK -->|Cycle Repeats| OUTPUTWAVE
```

## 4.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    POWEROFF(["Power applied to circuit"])
    STABLE["STABLE STATE Output LOW Discharge ON Capacitor shorted Vc near 0V"]
    WAIT1["Awaiting external trigger at Pin 2"]
    EXT_TRIG["Negative trigger pulse arrives Vtrigger drops below 1/3 VCC"]
    SETQ["Trigger Comparator fires Flip Flop SET"]
    QUASI["QUASI STABLE Output HIGH Discharge OFF"]
    CHARGE_RC["Capacitor charges through R toward VCC"]
    CHECK_THRESH{"Vc reaches 2/3 VCC?"}
    RESETQ["Threshold Comparator fires Flip Flop RESET"]
    RETSTABLE["Return to STABLE STATE Output LOW Discharge ON Rapid recovery"]
    PULSEOUT["Single output pulse of width T = 1.1 RC generated"]

    POWEROFF --> STABLE --> WAIT1 --> EXT_TRIG --> SETQ --> QUASI --> CHARGE_RC --> CHECK_THRESH
    CHECK_THRESH -->|Yes| RESETQ --> RETSTABLE --> PULSEOUT
    CHECK_THRESH -->|No waiting| CHARGE_RC
    RETSTABLE --> WAIT1
```

## 4.4 Astable vs Monostable — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ASTABLE_BLOCK["ASTABLE 555 CONFIGURATION"]
        A_VCC["VCC"] --> A_RA["RA"]
        A_RA --> A_NODE["Junction Pin 7 and Pin 6 and Pin 2"]
        A_NODE --> A_RB["RB"]
        A_RB --> A_CAPNODE["Capacitor Node to Pin 6 and Pin 2"]
        A_CAPNODE --> A_CAP["C to GND"]
        A_NODE --> A_OUT["Pin 3 Output Square Wave"]
    end

    subgraph MONOSTABLE_BLOCK["MONOSTABLE 555 CONFIGURATION"]
        M_VCC["VCC"] --> M_R["R Timing Resistor"]
        M_R --> M_NODE["Junction Pin 7 and Pin 6"]
        M_NODE --> M_CAP["C to GND"]
        M_TRIG["External Trigger Pin 2 Negative Pulse"]
        M_TRIG --> M_OUT["Pin 3 Output Single Pulse Width 1.1 RC"]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between astable and monostable multivibrators. List any two applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Astable | Monostable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stable States | None (continuously oscillating) | One (LOW state) |
| External Trigger | Not required | Required |
| Output | Continuous square wave | Single pulse per trigger |
| Application 1 | Clock generator for digital systems | Switch debouncing circuits |
| Application 2 | LED flasher / Tone generator | Missing pulse detector |

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **What are the two reference voltage levels used internally in the 555 timer? Why are these specific fractions (1/3 and 2/3 of V_CC) chosen?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The two reference voltage levels are **V_CC/3 (≈ 33.3% of V_CC)** and **2V_CC/3 (≈ 66.7% of V_CC)**. They are derived by an internal resistive divider of three equal 5 kΩ resistors connected between V_CC and ground. These specific fractions are chosen because they create a **symmetric hysteresis band** centered around 50% of V_CC, ensuring equal noise margins for the comparator transitions. The mathematical factor $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ arises directly from the 2:1 ratio between the upper and lower thresholds (since $2/3 \div 1/3 = 2$), simplifying the timing equations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a 555 timer-based **astable multivibrator**. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation and duty cycle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 555 astable multivibrator is designed with R_A = 4.7 kΩ, R_B = 6.8 kΩ, and C = 0.01 μF. Calculate the **frequency of oscillation**, **time period**, and **duty cycle**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Working & Derivation

**Circuit Diagram Description:** Connect Pin 8 and Pin 4 to V_CC. Pin 1 to ground. Tie Pin 2 and Pin 6 together, and connect them to the junction of R_B and C. Connect R_A from V_CC to Pin 7, and R_B from Pin 7 to the capacitor node. Pin 3 is the output. Pin 5 is bypassed to ground via 0.01 μF.

**Working Principle:**
- When the circuit is powered, capacitor C is initially uncharged.
- The Trigger Comparator (Pin 2 < V_CC/3) sets the flip-flop, output goes HIGH, and the discharge transistor (Pin 7) turns OFF.
- Capacitor C charges through the series combination **R_A + R_B** toward V_CC.
- When V_C reaches **2V_CC/3**, the Threshold Comparator resets the flip-flop, output goes LOW, and the discharge transistor turns ON.
- Capacitor C discharges through R_B alone (Pin 7 to ground) until V_C falls back to V_CC/3.
- The cycle repeats indefinitely, generating a continuous rectangular waveform at Pin 3.

**Derivation (as shown in Section 3.1):**
- $T_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) C$
- $T_L = 0.693 \cdot R_B C$
- $T = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) C$
- $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C}$
- $D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** R_A = 4.7 kΩ = 4700 Ω, R_B = 6.8 kΩ = 6800 Ω, C = 0.01 μF = $10^{-8}$ F.

**Step 1: Time Period**

$$T = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$$

$$T = 0.693 \cdot (4700 + 2 \times 6800) \cdot 10^{-8}$$

$$T = 0.693 \cdot (4700 + 13600) \cdot 10^{-8}$$

$$T = 0.693 \cdot 18300 \cdot 10^{-8}$$

$$T = 0.693 \cdot 1.83 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$T \approx 1.268 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 126.8 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 2: Frequency**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1.268 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$f \approx 7886 \text{ Hz} \approx 7.89 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3: Duty Cycle**

$$D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$$

$$D = \frac{4700 + 6800}{4700 + 2(6800)} \times 100\%$$

$$D = \frac{11500}{18300} \times 100\%$$

$$D \approx 62.84\%$$

**Final Answer:** f ≈ 7.89 kHz, T ≈ 126.8 μs, D ≈ 62.84%.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram and waveform sketch, explain the working of a 555 timer-based **monostable multivibrator**. Derive the expression for the output pulse width. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Design a 555 monostable multivibrator to generate an output pulse of width **10 ms**. Choose C = 0.1 μF and calculate the required value of R. Also, calculate the minimum trigger pulse width specification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Working & Derivation

**Circuit Diagram Description:** Connect Pin 8 and Pin 4 to V_CC, Pin 1 to ground. Connect R from V_CC to Pin 7. Tie Pin 6 and Pin 7 together and connect them to capacitor C (whose other terminal is grounded). Pin 2 receives external negative trigger pulses. Pin 3 is the output. Pin 5 is bypassed to ground via 0.01 μF.

**Waveform Sketch Description:** The output (Pin 3) is initially LOW (stable state). When a negative trigger pulse arrives at Pin 2, the output goes HIGH for a duration T. After T, the output automatically returns to LOW and remains there until the next trigger.

**Working Principle:**
- **Stable State:** Output is LOW. Discharge transistor is ON, keeping the capacitor fully discharged (V_C ≈ 0).
- **Triggering:** A negative pulse at Pin 2 (< V_CC/3) sets the internal flip-flop, output goes HIGH, discharge transistor turns OFF.
- **Quasi-Stable State:** Capacitor charges through R toward V_CC. Output stays HIGH.
- **Termination:** When V_C reaches 2V_CC/3, the threshold comparator resets the flip-flop. Output returns LOW, discharge transistor turns ON, and the capacitor rapidly discharges.
- **Recovery:** Circuit is now back in the stable state, waiting for the next trigger.

**Derivation (as shown in Section 3.2):**
Using the universal charging equation from V_i = 0 to V_f = 2V_CC/3, with τ = RC:
- $e^{-T/RC} = 1/3$
- $T = RC \cdot \ln 3$
- $T \approx 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$

#### Model Solution (b) — Design Calculation

**Given:** Required pulse width T = 10 ms, C = 0.1 μF = $10^{-7}$ F.

**Step 1: Calculate the value of R from the pulse width equation.**

$$T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$$

$$10 \times 10^{-3} = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot 10^{-7}$$

$$R = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{10^{-2}}{1.1 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$R \approx 90909 \text{ Ω} \approx 90.9 \text{ k}\Omega$$

A standard value of **R = 91 kΩ** would be selected in practice.

**Step 2: Calculate the minimum trigger pulse width.**

The 555 timer requires that the trigger pulse at Pin 2 stay below V_CC/3 for a duration that allows the internal comparator to respond. The minimum trigger pulse width is typically specified as:

$$t_{trigger(min)} \approx 100 \text{ ns to } 1 \text{ μs}$$

(dependent on the specific 555 variant, often provided in the datasheet).

**Design Verification:**
$$T = 1.1 \times 91 \text{ k}\Omega \times 0.1 \text{ μF} = 1.1 \times 91000 \times 10^{-7} = 1.001 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} \approx 10 \text{ ms} \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** R ≈ 91 kΩ, t_trigger(min) ≈ 1 μs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Penalty (-1 to -2 marks):** Students frequently write R in kΩ but leave C in μF without consistent SI conversions. Always convert **everything to base SI units** (Ω, F) before computing, then convert the final answer back for presentation.
> 2. **Confusing 0.693 with 0.5 (-1 mark):** The factor 0.693 (= ln 2) comes from the **2:1 ratio** between threshold and trigger levels. Do not approximate it as 0.5.
> 3. **Missing the Asymmetry of R_A and R_B (-2 marks):** R_A appears only in the charging path. R_B appears in both. Drawing a circuit with R_A and R_B symmetrically placed will be marked wrong.
> 4. **Confusing Astable Formula with Monostable (-3 marks):** Astable uses 0.693 with $(R_A + 2R_B)C$. Monostable uses **1.1** with $RC$. Mixing these will lose heavy marks.
> 5. **No Waveform Sketch in Monostable (-1 mark):** Even a rough ASCII waveform showing the trigger pulse and the resulting single output pulse earns at least 1 mark of grace.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your wall before exams!)**

- The **555 timer** is the universal building block for both astable and monostable circuits.
- The **two magic reference voltages** inside the 555 are **V_CC/3** and **2V_CC/3**, generated by an internal 3-resistor divider.
- **Astable** = no stable state, free-running oscillator, needs **no** external trigger.
- **Monostable** = one stable state (LOW), produces **one output pulse per trigger**.
- **Astable Time Period:** $T = 0.693 (R_A + 2R_B) C$ → **Frequency:** $f = 1.44 / [(R_A + 2R_B) C]$.
- **Astable Duty Cycle:** $D = [(R_A + R_B) / (R_A + 2R_B)] \times 100\%$. It is **never less than 50%** in a standard 555 astable.
- **Monostable Pulse Width:** $T = 1.1 R C$. The factor is **1.1, not 1.0**.
- The capacitor in an astable charges through **R_A + R_B** and discharges through **R_B only**.
- The capacitor in a monostable charges through **R only** (Pin 7 acts as an open circuit during quasi-stable state).
- Always draw **waveform sketches** (capacitor voltage + output voltage) in the exam — they carry easy marks.
- The **discharge transistor (Pin 7)** is the key switching element that differentiates the charge and discharge paths.
- The **SR flip-flop** inside the 555 is **set by the trigger comparator** and **reset by the threshold comparator** — memorizing this helps in tracing any circuit's behavior.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Working (Using 555 and 741), Triangular and Sawtooth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Design and Working (Using 555 and 741), Triangular and Sawtooth # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Function Generator** is an electronic test instrument or circuit arrangement capable of producing multiple periodic waveforms (typically sinusoidal, square, triangular, and sawtooth) over a wide range of frequencies. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the design and working of such generators is realized using two fundamental analog building blocks: 1. **IC 555 (Timer/Crystal Oscillator)** — Operated in **Astable Multivibrator Mode** to generate a precision **Square Wave** (clock/reference waveform). 2. **IC 741 (Operational Amplifier)** — Configured as either an **Integrator** (to convert square wave → triangular wave) or a **Comparator with RC network** (to convert triangular wave → sawtooth wave, or vice versa). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Definition (KTU Standard):** > A triangular wave generator using 741 op-amp is essentially a **square wave generator cascaded with an integrator**. The 555 timer provides a stable square wave reference; the 741 op-amp integrator integrates the constant-amplitude square wave to produce a linearly rising and falling triangular waveform. A sawtooth waveform, by contrast, has asymmetric rise/fall times and is produced by introducing unequal charge/discharge paths (using diodes or asymmetric triggering). --- ## 1.2 Waveform Taxonomy | Waveform | Shape Characteristics | Generation Method | |----------|----------------------|-------------------| | **Square Wave** | Instantaneous transitions between two levels | 555 Astable | | **Triangular Wave** | Linear rise AND linear fall (symmetric) | 741 Integrator fed by square wave | | **Sawtooth Wave** | Linear rise (slow) + sharp fall (or vice versa) | 741 Integrator with asymmetric reset (diode/MOSFET) | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket and Sand" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy (Bucket Filling & Emptying):** > Imagine a **bucket** that is being filled with water at a **constant rate** (this represents a constant input current into a capacitor). The water level rises **linearly** with time — this is the **rising ramp** of a triangular/sawtooth wave. Now, when the bucket suddenly **tips over** (analogous to a transistor/op-amp reset switch closing), the water drains out almost instantaneously, creating a **sharp fall** — this is the **falling edge**. The 555 timer acts like a "person" who periodically tips the bucket at fixed time intervals, while the 741 op-amp is the "bucket-and-sand mechanism" itself, providing a perfectly linear filling rate because of its virtual ground behavior. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Supply Voltage (V_CC):** Typically **+5 V to +15 V** (for 555) and **±5 V to ±18 V** (for 741). - **Threshold Levels (555):** $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ (internally fixed by a 3-resistor divider). - **Output Swing (741):** Approximately **±(V_CC − 2 V)** under typical load. - **Standard Frequency Range:** **1 Hz to 100 kHz** (audio and instrumentation range). --- ## 1.5 Role in Modern Engineering - **Test and Measurement:** Signal source for testing amplifiers, filters, and ADCs. - **Communication Systems:** Used as sub-carrier generators in PWM/PPM modulators. - **Medical Electronics:** Function generators drive biomedical stimulators. - **Industrial Control:** Triangular waves are used in **SCR/thyristor firing circuits** and PWM motor drives. - **Educational Labs:** Standard experiment in KTU linear integrated circuits lab. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Block-Level System Architecture The complete function generator is realized as a **two-stage cascade**: $$ \boxed{\text{555 Astable} \;\longrightarrow\; V_{sq}(t) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{741 Integrator} \;\longrightarrow\; V_{tri}(t) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{741 Comparator/Reset} \;\longrightarrow\; V_{saw}(t)} $$ ### Stage 1 — 555 Timer as Astable Multivibrator In astable mode, the 555 timer has **no stable state**. It continuously flips between HIGH and LOW, generating a square wave. The two internal comparators reference the external capacitor voltage $V_C$ against $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$. **Operational Sequence (Why and How):** - **Why** does the capacitor charge through $R_A + R_B$ and discharge through $R_B$ only? Because the discharge transistor (pin 7) shorts pin 7 to ground only during the discharge phase. - **How** is the threshold switching achieved? An internal **SR flip-flop** is SET when $V_C < \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and RESET when $V_C > \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$. ### Stage 2 — 741 Op-Amp as Integrator The square wave is fed to the inverting input of the 741 (configured with capacitor $C$ in feedback and resistor $R$ at input). Since the virtual ground at the inverting input forces a **constant current** $I = \frac{V_{sq}}{R}$ through the capacitor, the output voltage is: $$ V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{sq}(t)\, dt $$ A **constant input** (square wave HIGH or LOW) produces a **linear ramp** at the output. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > **No vertical pipe symbol `|` is used inside table cells** (use `\vert` or `\mid` instead) to maintain markdown table integrity. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions | |---|-----------|---------|-------|---------------------| | 1 | 555 Charging Time ($T_{HIGH}$) | $T_H = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$ | seconds | $R_A, R_B > 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ | | 2 | 555 Discharging Time ($T_{LOW}$) | $T_L = 0.693\,R_B\,C$ | seconds | Independent of $R_A$ | | 3 | 555 Total Time Period ($T$) | $T = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$ | seconds | $T = T_H + T_L$ | | 4 | 555 Frequency ($f$) | $f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$ | Hz | $1\,\text{Hz} \le f \le 100\,\text{kHz}$ | | 5 | 555 Duty Cycle ($D$) | $D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$ | % | $D > 50\%$ always | | 6 | 741 Integrator Output | $V_{out}(t) = -\frac{V_{sq}}{RC} \cdot t$ | Volts | Valid for constant $V_{sq}$ | | 7 | Peak Triangular Amplitude | $V_{tri,pk} = \frac{V_{sq,pk}}{4fRC}$ | Volts | Must be $\le V_{sat}$ of 741 | | 8 | Sawtooth Ramp Slope | $\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{V_{sq}}{RC}$ | V/s | Constant during ramp | | 9 | Op-Amp Saturation (741) | $V_{sat} \approx \pm(V_{CC} - 2)$ | Volts | Depends on supply rail | | 10 | Internal Threshold Voltages | $V_{th1} = \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$, $V_{th2} = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ | Volts | Fixed by 3-resistor divider | --- ## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters - **PWM Modulation:** The comparator of a triangular wave with a DC level produces a PWM signal of variable duty cycle — fundamental in **DC motor speed control** and **switching power supplies (SMPS)**. - **ADC Testing:** Triangular waves are the standard test input for determining the **Integral Non-Linearity (INL)** and **Differential Non-Linearity (DNL)** of ADCs. - **Time Base Generators:** Oscilloscope sweep circuits historically use sawtooth generators. - **Function Synthesizers:** Modern DDS (Direct Digital Synthesis) chips use triangular lookup tables internally. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: 555 Astable Frequency The 555 timer operates on a **capacitor charging equation** between two threshold levels. The general RC charging equation is: $$ V_C(t) = V_{\infty} - (V_{\infty} - V_0)\,e^{-t/RC} $$ **Step 1:** During charging, the capacitor charges from $V_C(0) = \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ toward $V_{\infty} = V_{CC}$ through resistance $R_A + R_B$. $$ V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}\right)e^{-t/(R_A + R_B)C} $$ **Step 2:** The charging stops when $V_C(t) = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$. Substitute into the equation: $$ \frac{2}{3}V_{CC} = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}\,e^{-T_H/(R_A + R_B)C} $$ **Step 3:** Rearrange by isolating the exponential term: $$ \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}\,e^{-T_H/(R_A + R_B)C} = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3}V_{CC} = \frac{1}{3}V_{CC} $$ **Step 4:** Divide both sides by $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$: $$ e^{-T_H/(R_A + R_B)C} = \frac{\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}}{\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}} = \frac{1}{2} $$ **Step 5:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides: $$ -\frac{T_H}{(R_A + R_B)C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln 2 $$ **Step 6:** Solve for $T_H$: $$ T_H = (R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln 2 = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C $$ > **Logic Explanation:** The factor **0.693** is $\ln 2$, which arises directly because the voltage transitions are between $\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{2}{3}$ of $V_{CC}$ — exactly a **factor of 2 in voltage**. This is why 555 circuits are said to have "natural" 0.693 time constants. **Step 7:** Repeat for discharging phase (capacitor discharges from $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ to $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ through $R_B$ only): $$ T_L = 0.693\,R_B\,C $$ **Step 8:** Total time period: $$ T = T_H + T_L = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C + 0.693\,R_B\,C = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C $$ **Step 9:** Frequency of oscillation: $$ f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C} $$ --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: 741 Integrator Output (Triangular Wave) The 741 op-amp in integrator configuration has its **non-inverting input grounded** and the **inverting input at virtual ground** (because of negative feedback). **Step 1:** Apply KCL at the inverting node (which is at 0 V due to virtual ground): $$ i_R = i_C $$ **Step 2:** The current through input resistor $R$ is: $$ i_R = \frac{V_{sq} - 0}{R} = \frac{V_{sq}}{R} $$ **Step 3:** The current through feedback capacitor $C$ is: $$ i_C = C\frac{d(0 - V_{out})}{dt} = -C\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} $$ **Step 4:** Equating the two currents (KCL): $$ \frac{V_{sq}}{R} = -C\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} $$ **Step 5:** Rearrange: $$ \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{sq}}{RC} $$ **Step 6:** Integrate both sides with respect to time: $$ V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{sq}(t)\, dt $$ **Step 7:** For a square wave of constant amplitude $\pm V_{sq,pk}$: $$ V_{out}(t) = -\frac{V_{sq,pk}}{RC} \cdot t + K $$ **Step 8:** When the square wave switches from $+V_{sq,pk}$ to $-V_{sq,pk}$ (at half period $T/2$), the output slope reverses — producing the **falling ramp** of the triangular wave. **Step 9:** Peak-to-peak triangular amplitude: $$ V_{tri,pp} = \frac{V_{sq,pk}}{2fRC} $$ > **Logic Explanation:** The factor of **2** appears because the square wave has equal positive and negative halves, and the integrator ramps for half a period before reversing. --- ## 3.3 Design Numerical Example (Worked Out) **Problem:** Design a triangular wave generator with $f = 1\,\text{kHz}$ and peak amplitude $V_{tri,pk} = 5\,\text{V}$ using 555 + 741, with $V_{CC} = \pm 12\,\text{V}$. **Step 1:** Choose 555 astable frequency = 1 kHz (same as desired triangular frequency). **Step 2:** Choose $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ for the 555. **Step 3:** Compute $(R_A + 2R_B)$: $$ R_A + 2R_B = \frac{1.44}{fC} = \frac{1.44}{1000 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}} = 144\,\text{k}\Omega $$ **Step 4:** Assume $D = 50\%$ for symmetric square wave, so $R_A = R_B$: $$ R_B + 2R_B = 3R_B = 144\,\text{k}\Omega \;\Rightarrow\; R_B = 48\,\text{k}\Omega,\;\; R_A = 48\,\text{k}\Omega $$ (Use nearest standard: $R_A = R_B = 47\,\text{k}\Omega$.) **Step 5:** For the 741 integrator, choose $C_1 = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ and compute $R_1$: $$ R_1 = \frac{V_{sq,pk}}{4 f V_{tri,pk} C_1} = \frac{5}{4 \times 1000 \times 5 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.5\,\text{k}\Omega $$ (Use standard $R_1 = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$ with a small trim pot for fine-tuning.) **Step 6:** Verify the 741 output does not saturate: $V_{tri,pk} = 5\,\text{V} < V_{sat} \approx 10\,\text{V}$ ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Function Generator Architecture ```mermaid graph LR DC["DC Supply +12V GND -12V"] --> A1["Stage 1: 555 IC Astable"] A1 -- Pin 3 Square Wave --> A2["Stage 2: 741 IC Integrator"] A2 -- Triangular Output --> A3["Stage 3: 741 IC Comparator and Reset"] A3 -- Sawtooth Output --> OUT["Output Terminals"] A2 -- Triangular Out --> OUT A1Sub["RA, RB, C timing network
f = 1.44 / RA + 2RB C"] -.-> A1 A2Sub["R1 input, C1 feedback
Vout = -1/R1C1 integral Vsq dt"] -.-> A2 A3Sub["Diode D1 for fast reset
Asymmetric charge discharge path"] -.-> A3 ``` ## 4.2 555 Astable Internal Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TB VCC["VCC Pin 8"] --> DIVIDER["3 Resistor Voltage Divider"] DIVIDER --> N1["Node 1 = 2/3 VCC"] DIVIDER --> N2["Node 2 = 1/3 VCC"] N1 --> CMP1["Comparator 1 Threshold"] N2 --> CMP2["Comparator 2 Trigger"] EXT["External Capacitor VC at Pin 6"] --> CMP1 EXT --> CMP2 CMP1 --> FF["SR Flip Flop"] CMP2 --> FF FF --> DRV["Output Driver Pin 3"] FF --> DIS["Discharge Transistor Pin 7"] DIS --> REXT["External RA RB network"] REXT --> EXT DRV --> SQOUT["Square Wave Output"] ``` ## 4.3 741 Integrator Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR VSQ["Square Wave Input Vsq"] --> R1["Resistor R1"] R1 --> NODE["Inverting Node Vx = 0"] NODE --> CFBK["Feedback Capacitor C1"] CFBK --> VOUT["Triangular Output Vout"] GND["Ground 0V"] --> NINV["Non Inverting Input"] NODE --- NODE ``` ## 4.4 Sawtooth Generator with Diode Reset ```mermaid graph TB VSQ["Square Wave from 555"] --> R1["Resistor R1"] R1 --> NODE["Op-amp Inverting Node"] D1["Diode D1 forward biased during reset"] --> NODE NODE --> CFBK["Capacitor C1 feedback"] CFBK --> VOUT["Sawtooth Output"] SW["MOSFET or Transistor reset switch"] --> CFBK SQGATE["Square wave inverted drives gate of switch"] --> SW ``` ## 4.5 Waveform Timing Diagram (Textual Schematic) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Time Domain Waveforms" W1["Square Wave: HIGH for TH then LOW for TL"] W2["Triangular: Rises linearly during TH, falls during TL"] W3["Sawtooth: Slow rise during TH, instant fall via diode reset"] end W1 --> W2 W1 --> W3 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of IC 555 in astable multivibrator mode. Derive the expression for frequency of oscillation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Block diagram (1 mark):** Draw the three-resistor divider, two comparators, SR flip-flop, output buffer, and discharge transistor. Reference Figure 4.2 from SECTION 4. - **Operation (1 mark):** Capacitor charges through $R_A + R_B$ from $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ and discharges through $R_B$ from $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ to $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$. - **Frequency derivation (1 mark):** $$ f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C} $$ --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: Explain how a triangular wave generator is realized using op-amp 741. Draw the circuit and output waveform.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Concept (1 mark):** A square wave from 555 is fed to a 741 op-amp integrator; constant square wave amplitude produces linear ramps. - **Circuit (1 mark):** Inverting integrator — $R_1$ at input, $C_1$ in feedback, non-inverting input grounded. - **Formula and Waveform (1 mark):** $$ V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_1C_1}\int V_{sq}(t)\,dt $$ Output is a linear triangular wave with rise time = $T/2$ of the input square wave. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper] **Q: (a)** Design a triangular wave generator using 555 timer and 741 op-amp integrator to generate a triangular wave of frequency **2 kHz** and peak amplitude **4 V**. Assume $V_{CC} = \pm 12\,\text{V}$. Draw the complete circuit and explain its working. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain how a sawtooth wave generator is designed using 741 op-amp. How does it differ from a triangular wave generator? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Design Steps:** 1. **Choose 555 capacitor:** $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ (1 mark) 2. **Compute total resistance for 555 astable at 2 kHz:** (1 mark) $$ R_A + 2R_B = \frac{1.44}{fC} = \frac{1.44}{2000 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}} = 72\,\text{k}\Omega $$ 3. **Choose 50% duty cycle:** $R_A = R_B$ gives $3R_B = 72\,\text{k}\Omega$ → $R_A = R_B = 24\,\text{k}\Omega$ (use $22\,\text{k}\Omega$ standard with $2\,\text{k}\Omega$ trim pot) (1 mark) 4. **741 Integrator Design:** Choose $C_1 = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. Compute $R_1$ for $V_{tri,pk} = 4\,\text{V}$: (2 marks) $$ R_1 = \frac{V_{sq,pk}}{4f V_{tri,pk} C_1} = \frac{5}{4 \times 2000 \times 4 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.56\,\text{k}\Omega $$ Use standard $R_1 = 1.5\,\text{k}\Omega$. 5. **Circuit Description and Waveform (1 mark):** 555 output (square wave, 0–5 V) → coupling capacitor → 741 inverting integrator → triangular wave of $\pm 4\,\text{V}$ at 2 kHz. 6. **Verification:** $V_{tri,pk} = 4\,\text{V} < V_{sat} \approx 10\,\text{V}$ ✓ (1 mark) #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks 1. **Sawtooth Concept (2 marks):** Sawtooth has a slow linear rise and a sharp fall. Achieved by **asymmetric charge/discharge paths** using a **diode in parallel with the input resistor**. 2. **Circuit Diagram (2 marks):** 741 integrator with diode $D_1$ across $R_1$ such that: - During positive half cycle: $D_1$ conducts → current flows through small $R_2$ → fast charging → sharp fall. - During negative half cycle: $D_1$ is OFF → current flows through large $R_1$ → slow linear rise. 3. **Difference from Triangular Generator (2 marks):** - **Triangular:** Symmetric rise and fall times, equal slopes. - **Sawtooth:** Asymmetric — long rise, short fall (or vice versa). 4. **Applications (1 mark):** Time-base generators in CROs, PWM modulators, ADC ramp-compare converters. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional block diagram of IC 555. Describe its operation in astable mode with necessary waveforms. Derive expressions for charging time, discharging time, frequency, and duty cycle. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An astable multivibrator using 555 timer is designed with $R_A = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_B = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. Calculate: (i) charging time, (ii) discharging time, (iii) total time period, (iv) frequency of oscillation, and (v) duty cycle. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks 1. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** 8-pin IC with three-resistor divider (sets $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$), two comparators, SR flip-flop, discharge transistor, and output buffer. 2. **Astable Operation (2 marks):** No stable state; capacitor charges from $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ through $R_A + R_B$, then discharges from $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ to $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ through $R_B$ only. 3. **Waveforms (1 mark):** Capacitor voltage is a sawtooth-like exponential; output at pin 3 is a square wave. 4. **Formulas (2 marks):** $$ T_H = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C,\quad T_L = 0.693\,R_B\,C $$ $$ f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C},\quad D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}\times 100\% $$ #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $R_A = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_B = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. **(i) Charging Time:** (1.5 marks) $$ T_H = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C = 0.693 \times (10 + 5)\,\text{k}\Omega \times 0.1\,\mu\text{F} $$ $$ T_H = 0.693 \times 15 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.693 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ T_H = 1.0395 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1.0395\,\text{ms} $$ **(ii) Discharging Time:** (1.5 marks) $$ T_L = 0.693\,R_B\,C = 0.693 \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.3465\,\text{ms} $$ **(iii) Total Time Period:** (1 mark) $$ T = T_H + T_L = 1.0395 + 0.3465 = 1.386\,\text{ms} $$ **(iv) Frequency:** (1.5 marks) $$ f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1.386 \times 10^{-3}} = 721.5\,\text{Hz} $$ (Alternatively using direct formula: $f = \frac{1.44}{20 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 720\,\text{Hz}$) **(v) Duty Cycle:** (1.5 marks) $$ D = \frac{T_H}{T} \times 100 = \frac{1.0395}{1.386} \times 100 \approx 75\% $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $R_A$ and $R_B$:** $R_A$ is between $V_{CC}$ and pin 7; $R_B$ is between pin 7 and pin 6/2. Mixing them in formulas yields **wrong duty cycle but correct frequency** (this is a classic 2-mark loss). > 2. **Forgetting the 0.693 factor:** Always remember $\ln 2 = 0.693$. Some students write $T = RC$ — **zero marks**. > 3. **Ignoring 741 saturation:** A triangular wave is only valid if $V_{tri,pk} \le V_{sat}$. If your design gives $V_{tri,pk} > V_{sat}$, the op-amp clips and the wave becomes **trapezoidal** — **lose 1 mark** for failing to verify. > 4. **Sawtooth vs Triangular confusion:** Sawtooth has **asymmetric** slopes; triangular is **symmetric**. Drawing a symmetric ramp for a sawtooth question costs full marks. > 5. **Missing the coupling capacitor:** Between 555 output and 741 input, a DC blocking capacitor is needed (since 555 output is 0–V_CC unipolar, while 741 needs bipolar drive for a true integrator). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Bullets:** - **555 Astable Frequency Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):** $f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$ - **Charging Time:** $T_H = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$ — depends on BOTH $R_A$ and $R_B$. - **Discharging Time:** $T_L = 0.693\,R_B\,C$ — depends on $R_B$ ONLY. - **Duty Cycle of 555** is **always greater than 50%** (because $R_A > 0$). - **741 Integrator Output Equation:** $V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\,dt$ - **Triangular Wave** = Square wave fed to integrator → symmetric ramp. - **Sawtooth Wave** = Triangular with asymmetric rise/fall → use **diode** or **MOSFET reset**. - **Internal 555 Thresholds:** $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ (trigger) and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ (threshold). - **741 Saturation Voltage:** $\approx V_{CC} - 2\,\text{V}$ (must be greater than desired output). - **Input Resistor Selection for Integrator:** $R_1 = \frac{1}{4fC_1} \cdot \frac{V_{sq}}{V_{tri,pk}}$ - **Application Checklist:** PWM, CRO time base, function generator, ADC testing, audio tone generation. - **Design Procedure:** Choose $f$ → compute 555 timing components → compute 741 integrator components → verify $V_{tri,pk} < V_{sat}$. - **Common Exam Trap:** When $R_A$ is very small ($R_A \to 0$), duty cycle approaches 50% but can NEVER be less than 50% in standard 555 astable configuration. - **Diode Trick for 50% Exact Duty Cycle:** Place a diode across $R_B$ — charges through diode, discharges through $R_B$ alone, achieving exact 50% duty cycle (favourite KTU 2-mark question). - **Sawtooth vs Triangular:** Triangular = two equal ramps; Sawtooth = one long ramp + one instantaneous flyback. - **Favourite 3-Mark Question:** "Differentiate between triangular and sawtooth waveforms" — answer must include **symmetry of slopes** and **reset mechanism**. --- **END OF NOTES — KTU 2024 SCHEME | MODULE 3 | TOPIC: DESIGN AND WORKING USING 555 AND 741, TRIANGULAR AND SAWTOOTH**

topics.title: RC Phase Shift and Wien Bridge Oscillators (No Analysis Required), Voltage Regulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** RC Phase Shift and Wien Bridge Oscillators (No Analysis Required), Voltage Regulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Oscillators (RC Type) & Voltage Regulators

Welcome to the most scoring module of the Analog Electronics / Linear Integrated Circuits paper. This section introduces **audio-frequency (AF) oscillators** built using purely resistive-capacitive (RC) networks, and **DC voltage regulators** that provide stable, ripple-free power supply rails — a non-negotiable building block for every analog and digital system.

---

## 1.1 Classification of Oscillators (Context Setting)

An **oscillator** is a circuit that converts DC energy from the supply into AC energy at a specific frequency without any external input signal. It generates a periodic waveform (sine, square, triangular, etc.) using positive feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** RC oscillators cover the **20 Hz – 100 kHz** (audio) frequency range. Below 20 Hz, RC values become impractically large; above 100 kHz, stray capacitances dominate. Hence, **LC oscillators** take over for RF frequencies.

| Criterion | RC Oscillator | LC Oscillator | Crystal Oscillator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Range** | 20 Hz – 100 kHz | 100 kHz – GHz | Fixed (kHz – MHz) |
| **Frequency Stability** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Application** | Audio generators, function generators | RF transmitters, IF stages | Clocks, microcontrollers |
| **KTU Module** | ✅ Module 3 | Higher semester | Higher semester |

---

## 1.2 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Core Definition

The **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** is a sinusoidal oscillator that uses an **inverting amplifier** (CE transistor / op-amp) and a **three-section RC ladder network** in the feedback path. Each RC section provides a phase shift of approximately **60°**, and three sections together produce the mandatory **180° phase shift** required to satisfy the **Barkhausen criterion**. The amplifier contributes the remaining 180°, giving a total loop phase of **360° (≡ 0°)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Barkhausen Criterion (Recap):** For sustained oscillations, two conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:
> 1. The **loop gain magnitude** must equal **unity**: $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
> 2. The **total loop phase shift** must be **zero (or an integer multiple of 360°)**: $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$ where $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marching Soldiers Around a Circle"

Imagine a group of soldiers marching in a closed circular parade ground. Each soldier represents a signal, and each RC section represents a **sharp turn** the soldier must make. Three sharp turns (3 × 60° = 180°) turn the soldier halfway around. The amplifier provides another half-turn. The soldier arrives back at the starting point — **in step with the original beat**. This perfectly timed return is "positive feedback," and it sustains the marching forever.

---

## 1.3 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Core Definition

The **Wien Bridge Oscillator** is the most popular low-frequency sine-wave generator. It uses a **non-inverting amplifier** (gain ≥ 3) and a **frequency-selective Wien Bridge network** consisting of one **series RC arm** and one **parallel RC arm**. At the resonant (balancing) frequency, the network has zero net phase shift, and the loop gain is precisely **1/3**, so the amplifier must compensate by providing a gain of exactly **3**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Bridge"?** The circuit is a Wheatstone-bridge-like configuration where the four arms are two RC networks and two pure resistors. At balance, the bridge detector (the amplifier input) sees zero signal, and the network feeds back exactly the right magnitude and phase.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tightrope Walker with a Counterweight"

The Wien bridge network is like a tightrope walker whose rope is held horizontal only when weights on both ends are perfectly matched. Any drift makes the rope tilt. The non-inverting amplifier acts like a **smart counterweight system** that constantly nudges the rope back to horizontal — and that horizontal position *is* the desired oscillation frequency.

---

## 1.4 Voltage Regulator — Core Definition

A **voltage regulator** is an electronic circuit that maintains a **constant DC output voltage** despite variations in input voltage, load current, or temperature. It is the final, critical stage of any DC power supply (transformer → rectifier → filter → **regulator**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why regulators matter:** A microcontroller running at **5 V DC** tolerates only ±5% deviation. Without regulation, ripple and load transients cause resets, ADC errors, and communication glitches. Regulators guarantee that **V_OUT stays within a tight window** no matter what the source or load does.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure-Reducing Valve"

Think of a city water tap. The municipal supply (input) may fluctuate wildly — sometimes too high, sometimes low. A **pressure-reducing valve** at your house keeps the water flow constant regardless. A voltage regulator does the same for electrons: even when the upstream "pressure" (V_IN) or downstream "demand" (I_LOAD) changes, the downstream "pressure" (V_OUT) remains rock-steady.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Working Principle

The circuit consists of two blocks:

1. **Amplifier Block:** A common-emitter (CE) BJT amplifier or an inverting op-amp provides a phase shift of exactly **180°** between input and output. It must also provide sufficient gain to compensate for the attenuation of the RC network.

2. **Feedback Block:** A cascade of **three identical RC sections** (each R, each C). Each section is a high-pass filter that contributes ~60° of phase lag. The total feedback factor $\beta$ has magnitude $1/29$ and phase shift of 180°.

### 2.1.1 The Three-Section RC Ladder

```
   o─────R─────┬─────R─────┬─────R─────┐
              C            C            C
              │            │            │
   o──────────┴────────────┴────────────┘
```

The ladder is driven from the amplifier output, and the junction after the third C is fed back to the amplifier input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why exactly 3 sections?** Two RC sections can only produce a maximum phase shift of 180° (at very high frequency, asymptotically). They cannot reach a stable 180° at a finite, well-defined frequency. **Three sections** produce exactly 180° at a specific, finite frequency — this is the oscillation frequency. Four or more sections add complexity without benefit.

### 2.1.2 Operational Step-by-Step

- **Step 1 — Power ON:** Thermal noise in resistors/Cs provides a tiny random voltage containing all frequencies.
- **Step 2 — Frequency Selection:** The RC ladder only passes the frequency at which its total phase shift equals exactly 180°.
- **Step 3 — Amplification & Inversion:** The CE amplifier inverts this 180°-shifted signal by another 180°, giving a total of 360° → positive feedback.
- **Step 4 — Growth:** Since $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ initially (gain = 29 designed slightly higher), the amplitude grows.
- **Step 5 — Stabilization:** As amplitude increases, the transistor enters the non-linear region, effective gain drops, $\vert A\beta \vert \to 1$, and a steady sine wave is produced.

---

## 2.2 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Working Principle

### 2.2.1 The Wien Bridge Network

The network consists of two arms connected in parallel from the amplifier output to ground:

- **Series Arm:** $R_1$ in series with $C_1$ (also called the **lead network**)
- **Parallel Arm:** $R_2$ in parallel with $C_2$ (also called the **lag network**)

The junction between these two arms is connected to the **non-inverting (+) input** of the op-amp.

### 2.2.2 Operational Step-by-Step

- **Step 1 — Frequency-Discriminating Action:** At the **resonant frequency**, the impedance of the series arm equals the impedance of the parallel arm, and the voltage divider gives maximum output with **zero phase shift**.
- **Step 2 — Phase Compensation:** Since the network provides 0° phase shift, the amplifier must also provide 0° → hence a **non-inverting** configuration is used.
- **Step 3 — Gain Requirement:** The Wien network attenuates by exactly **1/3** at resonance. So the amplifier must have a gain of **A = 3** to satisfy $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$.
- **Step 4 — Practical Gain = 3 + ε:** In practice, gain is set slightly greater than 3 (e.g., 3.001) using a tungsten lamp, thermistor, or back-to-back Zener diodes for **automatic amplitude stabilization (AGC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Point:** A common exam question asks: *"Why is the gain set slightly greater than 3?"* The answer is that the loop gain must **start above unity** for oscillations to build up from noise; amplitude-limiting components then reduce it to exactly 1 once the desired amplitude is reached.

### 2.2.3 Why Wien Bridge Beats RC Phase Shift

| Feature | RC Phase Shift | Wien Bridge |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Amplitude Stability** | Poor | Excellent (with AGC) |
| **Distortion** | High (3–5%) | Very Low (<0.1%) |
| **Frequency Tuning** | Difficult (change 3 Rs or 3 Cs) | Easy (ganged R or C) |
| **Frequency Range** | 20 Hz – 100 kHz | 1 Hz – 1 MHz |
| **KTU Preference** | Analysis-type derivations | Conceptual + numerical |

---

## 2.3 Voltage Regulators — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Zener Diode Shunt Regulator

A Zener diode operated in its **reverse breakdown region** maintains a nearly constant voltage (V_Z) across itself. Placed in parallel with the load, it "shunts" any excess current away from the load.

- **Operating Condition:** $V_{IN} \geq V_Z$ and the Zener stays in breakdown.
- **Output Voltage:** $V_{OUT} = V_Z$

**Limitations:**
- Poor efficiency (current flows even at no load).
- No short-circuit protection.
- Limited current handling.

### 2.3.2 Transistor Series Regulator

A **pass transistor** is placed in series between input and load. A Zener reference diode provides a stable reference, and a feedback network samples the output and adjusts the transistor's base current to maintain constant V_OUT.

**Block Diagram:**

```
   V_IN ──┬──►[Pass Q1]──┬──► V_OUT ──► Load
          │              │
          │           [Sample]
          │              │
          │           [Compare with V_Z]
          │              │
          └────[Error Amp]────────┘
```

**Advantages over Zener shunt:**
- Higher efficiency.
- Better regulation.
- Higher current capability.
- Built-in short-circuit protection possible.

### 2.3.3 Three-Terminal IC Regulators

Modern designs use monolithic IC regulators with three pins: **Input, Ground, Output**.

| Series | Type | Output Polarity | Example | Output Voltage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **78xx** | Fixed Linear | Positive | 7805, 7812, 7815 | +5 V, +12 V, +15 V |
| **79xx** | Fixed Linear | Negative | 7905, 7912 | -5 V, -12 V |
| **LM317** | Adjustable | Positive | LM317T | +1.25 V to +37 V |
| **LM337** | Adjustable | Negative | LM337T | -1.25 V to -37 V |

---

## 2.4 📌 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table** — these formulas appear in **90% of KTU numericals** on this module. The `(No Analysis Required)` tag for oscillators means **derivation is optional, but formulas are compulsory.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Value/Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **RC Phase Shift Oscillator Frequency** | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}$ | Hz | When R, R, R, C, C, C are identical |
| 2 | **Minimum Gain $\vert A \vert$ (RC Phase Shift)** | $\vert A \vert_{min} = 29$ | Dimensionless | For 3 identical sections |
| 3 | **Feedback Factor (RC Phase Shift)** | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{29} \angle -180°$ | — | Magnitude at $f_o$ |
| 4 | **Wien Bridge Oscillator Frequency** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | When $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$ |
| 5 | **Feedback Factor (Wien Bridge)** | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3} \angle 0°$ | — | At resonance only |
| 6 | **Required Amplifier Gain (Wien Bridge)** | $A \geq 3$ | Dimensionless | Non-inverting |
| 7 | **Zener Shunt Regulator Output** | $V_{OUT} = V_Z - I_Z \cdot r_Z$ | Volts | $r_Z$ is Zener impedance |
| 8 | **Series Regulator Output** | $V_{OUT} = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ | Volts | Adjustable divider feedback |
| 9 | **LM317 Output Voltage** | $V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} R_2$ | Volts | $V_{REF} = 1.25$ V (typical) |
| 10 | **Line Regulation** | $\text{LR} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} \times 100\,\%$ | %/V | Should be < 0.01 %/V for ICs |
| 11 | **Load Regulation** | $\text{LR}_{L} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | $V_{NL}$ = no-load, $V_{FL}$ = full-load |
| 12 | **Ripple Rejection Ratio** | $\text{RR} = 20 \log \left(\dfrac{V_{IN,ripple}}{V_{OUT,ripple}}\right)$ | dB | Typical: 60–80 dB for 78xx |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Tip:** In Wien Bridge problems, when the question says *"identical components"*, it means $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$, allowing direct use of the simplified formula $f = 1/(2\pi RC)$. If components differ, you must use the **general formula**:
>
> $$f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Oscillator / Regulator | Real-World Use Case |
|---|---|
| **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** | Low-cost function generators, electronic sirens, tone generators, musical keyboards |
| **Wien Bridge Oscillator** | Laboratory sine-wave generators (HP/Agilent/Keysight), audio test equipment, carrier generators |
| **Zener Shunt Regulator** | Reference voltage source, simple hobby projects, low-current digital logic supplies |
| **Series Pass Regulator** | High-current analog supplies, pre-regulation stage before linear regulators |
| **7805/7812 IC Regulator** | Microcontroller boards (Arduino UNO uses 7805), sensor supplies, op-amp rails |
| **LM317 Adjustable Regulator** | Battery chargers, variable bench supplies, LED drivers, constant-current sources |

---

## 2.6 Amplitude Stabilization — Critical Concept

Oscillators built with $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ are **theoretically marginal** — any drift either kills the oscillation or causes runaway growth. Practical designs use **non-linear amplitude-limiting** to bring the loop gain back to unity.

| Method | How It Works | Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Tungsten Lamp** (incandescent bulb) | Resistance rises with temperature → gain drops as amplitude grows | Classic Wien Bridge (HP 200CD) |
| **Thermistor (NTC)** | Resistance falls as it heats up → gain drops | Compact Wien Bridge designs |
| **Back-to-Back Zener Diodes** | Conduct at amplitude threshold → clip sine to a near-pure wave | Modern low-distortion oscillators |
| **FET as Voltage-Controlled Resistor** | Drain-source resistance varies with gate bias | AGC in instrumentation |
| **Diode + Resistor Network** | Diodes conduct on peaks, adding parallel resistance | Guitar pedal oscillators |

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Guideline:** Both oscillators are tagged **"No Analysis Required"** in the syllabus. This means **lengthy derivations are not expected** in the answer paper. However, KTU frequently gives **numerical problems** that require correct application of the formula. We therefore derive the formulas carefully **once** so that the student understands the underlying physics and can confidently substitute values.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of RC Phase Shift Oscillator Frequency

### 3.1.1 Circuit Description

A common-emitter amplifier has voltage gain $A$ (negative sign indicating 180° inversion). Its output drives a ladder of three identical RC high-pass sections. The ladder output is fed back to the amplifier input.

Let each section have resistance $R$ and capacitance $C$.

### 3.1.2 Feedback Factor of One RC Section

For a single high-pass RC section driven by source $V_{in}$ and loaded by the next stage (assumed to have high input impedance), the transfer function is:

$$\beta_1(s) = \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$$

### 3.1.3 Combining Three Sections

Multiplying three identical sections:

$$\beta_{total}(s) = \left(\dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}\right)^3$$

Let $s = j\omega$ and define $x = \omega RC$:

$$\beta_{total} = \left(\dfrac{jx}{1 + jx}\right)^3 = \dfrac{(jx)^3}{(1 + jx)^3}$$

### 3.1.4 Setting Loop Phase Shift to 180°

The numerator $jx = jx$ has phase **+90°**; cubed gives phase **+270°**.
The denominator $(1 + jx)$ has phase $\arctan(x)$; cubed gives phase $3 \arctan(x)$.

So total phase of $\beta$:

$$\phi_\beta = 270° - 3 \arctan(x)$$

For the loop to have zero net phase, we need $\phi_\beta = 180°$:

$$270° - 3 \arctan(x) = 180°$$

$$3 \arctan(x) = 90°$$

$$\arctan(x) = 30°$$

$$x = \tan(30°) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

Therefore $\omega RC = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$.

### 3.1.5 Solving for Frequency

$$\omega_o = \dfrac{1}{RC\sqrt{3}}$$

Converting $\omega_o = 2\pi f_o$:

$$f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Wait — the formula table said $\sqrt{6}$!** This is the most common KTU trap. The $\sqrt{3}$ derivation assumes the ladder is **unloaded** (infinite input impedance at the next stage). With a **finite load** (transistor input resistance $h_{ie}$), the effective load changes, and the formula becomes $1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$. **KTU accepts both** depending on assumptions — read the question carefully.

### 3.1.6 Required Amplifier Gain

Substituting $x = 1/\sqrt{3}$ back into the magnitude equation:

$$\vert \beta_{total} \vert = \left(\dfrac{1/\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{1 + 1/3}}\right)^3 = \left(\dfrac{1/\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{4/3}}\right)^3$$

$$= \left(\dfrac{1/\sqrt{3}}{2/\sqrt{3}}\right)^3 = \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^3 = \dfrac{1}{8}$$

Wait — this is the **unloaded** case. For the **loaded** case (most KTU problems), the calculation yields:

$$\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{29}$$

By Barkhausen: $\vert A \vert \cdot \vert \beta \vert = 1$

$$\vert A \vert = \dfrac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 29$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Wien Bridge Oscillator Frequency

### 3.2.1 Setting Up the Transfer Function

The series arm impedance: $Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$

The parallel arm impedance: $Z_p = \dfrac{R \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}} = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$

The voltage across $Z_p$ (which is the feedback to the amplifier) is:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

### 3.2.2 Simplification

Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:

$$Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = \dfrac{j\omega RC + 1}{j\omega C}$$

$$Z_p = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

Therefore:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\dfrac{j\omega RC + 1}{j\omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

### 3.2.3 Finding the Frequency of Zero Phase

For the network phase to be zero, the imaginary part of the denominator must be zero. Working through the algebra (long but straightforward):

$$\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

For $\beta$ to be purely real (phase = 0°):

$$\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC} = 0$$

$$\omega RC = \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$$

$$(\omega RC)^2 = 1$$

$$\omega_o = \dfrac{1}{RC}$$

$$f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

### 3.2.4 Feedback Magnitude at Resonance

Substituting $\omega = \omega_o$:

$$\beta(\omega_o) = \dfrac{1}{3 + j(0)} = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

Therefore, by Barkhausen:

$$A = \dfrac{1}{\beta} = 3$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Sample KTU-Style Problems

### Problem 3.3.1 — RC Phase Shift Oscillator Design

**Q:** Design an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT to generate a 2 kHz sine wave. Choose $C = 10$ nF and find $R$. Assume the loaded formula is required.

**Solution:**

The design formula:

$$f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$$

Rearranging for R:

$$R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_o C \sqrt{6}}$$

Substituting $f_o = 2 \times 10^3$ Hz and $C = 10 \times 10^{-9}$ F:

$$R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \cdot 2 \times 10^3 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-9} \cdot \sqrt{6}}$$

$$R = \dfrac{1}{4\pi \times 10^{-5} \cdot 2.449}$$

$$R = \dfrac{1}{3.077 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$R \approx 3.25 \; k\Omega$$

The amplifier gain must be $\vert A \vert = 29$.

---

### Problem 3.3.2 — Wien Bridge Oscillator Frequency Calculation

**Q:** A Wien bridge oscillator has $R_1 = R_2 = 10 \; k\Omega$ and $C_1 = C_2 = 0.01 \; \mu F$. Calculate the frequency of oscillation.

**Solution:**

For identical components:

$$f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

$$f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10 \times 10^3 \cdot 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10^{-4}}$$

$$f_o = \dfrac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$f_o \approx 1591.5 \; Hz$$

---

### Problem 3.3.3 — LM317 Adjustable Regulator Output

**Q:** An LM317 regulator has $R_1 = 240 \; \Omega$ and $R_2 = 720 \; \Omega$. Find $V_{OUT}$. ($V_{REF} = 1.25$ V, $I_{ADJ} \approx 0$.)

**Solution:**

$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

$$V_{OUT} = 1.25 \left(1 + \dfrac{720}{240}\right)$$

$$V_{OUT} = 1.25 \times (1 + 3)$$

$$V_{OUT} = 1.25 \times 4 = 5.0 \; V$$

> [!TIP]
> **Recognize the pattern:** With $R_2 = 3 R_1$, you always get $V_{OUT} = 5$ V from LM317. This is the **standard 5 V regulator trick** used by hobbyists who want a 7805 replacement with adjustable features.

---

### Problem 3.3.4 — Voltage Regulator Load Regulation

**Q:** A regulator has $V_{NL} = 12.1$ V (no load) and $V_{FL} = 11.9$ V (full load 1 A). Calculate the load regulation.

**Solution:**

$$\text{Load Regulation} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

$$= \dfrac{12.1 - 11.9}{11.9} \times 100\%$$

$$= \dfrac{0.2}{11.9} \times 100\%$$

$$\approx 1.68\%$$

Better regulators achieve $< 0.1\%$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Key Distinctions (Exam Favorite)

| Question Asked in Exam | RC Phase Shift Answer | Wien Bridge Answer |
|---|---|---|
| Type of amplifier used | Inverting (CE BJT / op-amp) | Non-inverting (op-amp) |
| Number of RC sections | **3** (high-pass ladder) | **2** (one series, one parallel) |
| Frequency formula (identical) | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| Feedback factor | $1/29$ | $1/3$ |
| Required gain | $\geq 29$ | $\geq 3$ |
| Total loop phase | 360° (180° RC + 180° amp) | 0° (0° RC + 0° amp) |
| Output distortion | Higher (3–5%) | Lower (< 0.1% with AGC) |
| Frequency tuning | Difficult | Easy (ganged R or C) |
| Common application | Audio tone generator | Lab sine-wave generator |

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define **Barkhausen criterion** for sustained oscillations. State the two conditions.

**Model Answer:**

Barkhausen criterion specifies the conditions under which a feedback amplifier will operate as a sinusoidal oscillator without any external input signal. The two conditions are:

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity:
$$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$$

2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be zero or an integer multiple of 360°:
$$\angle A\beta = 2n\pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

When both conditions are satisfied simultaneously, the circuit produces sustained oscillations at a specific frequency determined by the components.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for stating magnitude condition, 1 mark for phase condition, 1 mark for explanation.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q:** Compare **RC phase shift oscillator** and **Wien bridge oscillator** based on (i) feedback network, (ii) frequency formula, and (iii) amplifier gain.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RC Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien Bridge Oscillator |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback Network** | Three-section RC high-pass ladder | One series RC + one parallel RC arm (lead-lag network) |
| **Frequency Formula** | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| **Amplifier Gain Required** | $\vert A \vert \geq 29$ (inverting) | $A \geq 3$ (non-inverting) |
| **Type of Amplifier** | Inverting (CE BJT / op-amp inverting) | Non-inverting op-amp |
| **Output Distortion** | Higher (3–5%) | Lower (<0.1% with AGC) |

*(3 marks for correct comparison across the three parameters.)*

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each module carries one 14-mark question with **internal choice** (OR). You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
