- **723 upper cutoff (37 V):** Set by the $V_{CEO}$ of the internal pass transistor.
- **Pin assignments to memorise:** 6 = $V_{REF}$, 4 = IN+, 5 = IN−, 10 = $V_{OUT}$, 11 = $V_{C}$, 2 = IL, 3 = CS, 9 = COMP.
- **Design sequence for 723 problems:** (1) Identify mode, (2) write the appropriate equation, (3) impose the divider-current constraint if given, (4) solve for $R_1$ and $R_2$, (5) check the current-limit resistor, (6) check the supply-rail headroom.
- **Common exam trap:** Students often write the low-voltage formula for a high-voltage problem (or vice versa). Always check the *direction of the divider* first.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital to Analog Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Digital to Analog Converters # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with Behrouz A. Forouzan's *Data Communications and Networking*), the term **"Digital-to-Analog Conversion"** (often abbreviated as **D/A Conversion** in the data communication domain) refers to the process of transforming a **baseband digital bitstream** (a sequence of binary 1s and 0s) into a **passband analog signal** whose one or more fundamental characteristics — *amplitude, frequency, or phase* — are modulated in discrete steps to represent the digital information. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (KTU Board Definition):** > The term "Digital-to-Analog Converter" in Computer Networks (Module 4) is **NOT** an electronic hardware DAC chip (like those studied in Analog Electronics). Instead, it is a **conceptual/transmission process** describing how digital data modulates a continuous-time carrier wave to enable transmission over analog media (e.g., copper wires, optical fiber, wireless RF channels). This is the foundation of **bandpass transmission**. ## 1.2 The Five Standard Mechanisms of D/A Conversion According to the KTU prescribed syllabus, the **five standardized techniques** for converting digital data to analog signals are: | S. No. | Mechanism | Modulated Parameter | Bandwidth Efficiency | |:------:|:----------|:--------------------|:---------------------| | 1 | **ASK** – Amplitude Shift Keying | Amplitude | Low | | 2 | **FSK** – Frequency Shift Keying | Frequency | Medium | | 3 | **BFSK** – Binary FSK | Frequency (2 levels) | Medium | | 4 | **MFSK** – M-ary FSK | Frequency (M levels) | High | | 5 | **PSK** – Phase Shift Keying | Phase | High | | 6 | **QAM** – Quadrature Amplitude Modulation | Amplitude + Phase | Highest | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus Statement:** > The module emphasizes the *mathematical representation* of each scheme, *bandwidth calculations*, *constellation diagrams*, and the *relationship between bit rate, baud rate, and carrier frequency*. Students are expected to draw constellation diagrams and solve numerical problems involving **Baud Rate ($S$)** and **Bit Rate ($N$)**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition Imagine you are standing on the balcony of your house and want to signal your friend in the neighboring building whether the exam is "Easy" (binary **1**) or "Tough" (binary **0**). You have a bright **flashlight (carrier wave)** with you. - If you **flash the light ON and OFF** to convey the message → You are modulating the **Amplitude** → This is **ASK**. - If you instead wave a **blue flag for 1** and a **red flag for 0** at the same speed → You are modulating the **Frequency/Color (proxy)** → This is **FSK**. - If you raise the flag at a **0° angle for 1** and a **180° angle for 0** (same height, same color) → You are modulating the **Phase** → This is **PSK**. - If you use **different heights AND different angles** for 1 and 0 (a 2-bit pattern) → You are combining amplitude and phase → This is **QAM**. The flashlight (carrier) is the **analog signal**, and your discrete gestures represent the **digital data** being converted. ## 1.4 The Underlying Carrier Signal The base analog signal (carrier) on which modulation is performed is mathematically defined as a **continuous-time sinusoidal wave**: $$s(t) = A_c \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_c)$$ Where the parameters are: - $A_c$ → **Amplitude** (in Volts, $V$) - $f_c$ → **Carrier Frequency** (in Hertz, $Hz$) - $\phi_c$ → **Carrier Phase** (in radians, $rad$) > [!TIP] > **Remember:** During D/A conversion, **the carrier frequency $f_c$ is always present** — only **one or more of the three parameters** ($A$, $f$, $\phi$) are varied in discrete jumps to represent the binary input. The choice of which parameter to vary defines the modulation scheme. ## 1.5 Why is D/A Conversion Necessary? Digital data is inherently low-pass (contains DC and low-frequency components). To transmit this data over: - **Band-limited channels** (telephone lines, wireless spectrum) - **Long-distance media** (fiber, satellite links) - **Multi-user shared media** (using frequency division multiplexing) …the low-frequency baseband signal must be shifted to a higher frequency band. This shift is achieved by modulating an analog carrier, which is the essence of D/A conversion. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Mechanism of Each Conversion Scheme ### 2.1.1 Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) — The Simplest Form - **Definition:** ASK varies the **amplitude $A_c$** of the carrier sinusoid between two (or more) discrete levels in accordance with the digital input bit. - **Binary ASK (BASK / OOK):** Amplitude is switched between **$A$ (for bit 1)** and **$0$ (for bit 0)**. When amplitude becomes zero, it is called **On-Off Keying (OOK)**. - **Bandwidth:** ASK has a bandwidth **directly equal to the baud rate**, making it the most bandwidth-inefficient scheme. - **Noise Susceptibility:** ASK is **highly vulnerable to noise** because noise typically manifests as amplitude fluctuations, which can be misinterpreted as legitimate signal variations. **Mathematical Expression for BASK:** $$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_1 \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{for bit } 1 \\ A_2 \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{for bit } 0 \end{cases} $$ ### 2.1.2 Frequency Shift Keying (FSK) - **Definition:** FSK varies the **frequency $f_c$** of the carrier between discrete levels while keeping amplitude constant. - **BFSK:** Uses **2 frequencies** — $f_1$ (for bit 1) and $f_2$ (for bit 0). - **MFSK:** Uses **$M = 2^L$ frequencies**, where $L$ = number of bits per signal element. This dramatically increases bandwidth efficiency. **Mathematical Expression for BFSK:** $$s(t) = \begin{cases} A \cos(2\pi f_1 t), & \text{for bit } 1 \\ A \cos(2\pi f_2 t), & \text{for bit } 0 \end{cases} $$ - **Bandwidth of BFSK:** $BW_{BFSK} = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert + N_b$ where $N_b$ is the bit rate. A common simplified approximation is $BW = 2 \times f_c$ when $f_c$ is the center frequency. - **MFSK Bandwidth (Critical Formula):** $$BW_{MFSK} = (M-1) \cdot \Delta f + 2B_{signal}$$ - For most practical KTU problems, the bandwidth of MFSK is approximated as: **$BW_{MFSK} \approx f_M - f_1$** where $f_M$ is the highest frequency used. ### 2.1.3 Phase Shift Keying (PSK) - **Definition:** PSK varies the **phase $\phi_c$** of the carrier between discrete values. The amplitude and frequency remain constant. - **BPSK (Binary PSK):** Two phases — **0° (for bit 0)** and **180° (for bit 1)**. This is the most noise-immune of all binary schemes. - **QPSK (Quadrature PSK):** Four phases — **45°, 135°, 225°, 315°**, each representing 2 bits. **Mathematical Expression for BPSK:** $$s(t) = \begin{cases} A \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{for bit } 1 \;\; (\phi = 0^\circ) \\ A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi), & \text{for bit } 0 \;\; (\phi = 180^\circ) \end{cases} $$ ### 2.1.4 Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM) — The Hybrid Scheme - **Definition:** QAM is a **combination of ASK and PSK**, where both **amplitude AND phase** are varied simultaneously to encode multiple bits per symbol. - **Why QAM?** QAM achieves the **highest spectral efficiency** of all D/A conversion schemes. It is used in Wi-Fi (802.11ac/ax), 4G LTE, 5G NR, and digital TV. - **M-QAM:** Uses $M = 2^L$ unique (amplitude, phase) combinations. For example, **16-QAM** uses 16 unique constellation points to encode 4 bits per symbol. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 numerical question on this topic.** | S.No | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units | |:----:|:--------|:--------|:---------------------|:------| | 1 | Bit Rate | $N = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | $T_b$ = bit duration | bits/sec (bps) | | 2 | Baud Rate (Signal Rate) | $S = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | $T_s$ = signal element duration | bauds (symbols/sec) | | 3 | Bit-Rate to Baud-Rate | $N = S \times \log_2 M$ | $M$ = number of signal levels | bps | | 4 | Baud-Rate to Bit-Rate | $S = \dfrac{N}{\log_2 M$ | $M$ = number of signal levels | bauds | | 5 | Number of Signal Levels | $M = 2^L$ | $L$ = bits per signal element | unitless | | 6 | Bandwidth of ASK/PSK | $BW = S = N$ (for binary) | bit rate = baud rate | Hz | | 7 | BFSK Bandwidth (approx.) | $BW_{BFSK} = 2 \times B_T + (f_2 - f_1)$ | $B_T$ = bit rate | Hz | | 8 | MFSK Bandwidth | $BW_{MFSK} = (2^L - 1) \cdot \Delta f + 2B$ | $B$ = bit rate, $\Delta f$ = freq. separation | Hz | | 9 | MFSK Baud Rate | $S_{MFSK} = N / L$ | N = bit rate, L = bits/symbol | bauds | | 10 | Carrier Frequency (KTU standard) | $f_c = \dfrac{n + 0.5}{T_b}$ | $n$ = integer (typically 0) | Hz | | 11 | QAM Constellation Points | $M_{QAM} = 2^L$ | $L$ = bits per symbol | unitless | | 12 | M-ary Baud Rate | $S = N / \log_2 M$ | general $M$-ary scheme | bauds | > [!CAUTION] > **Pipe-Symbol Warning:** The KTU formula sheet above uses `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` to ensure the markdown table renders correctly. This is a strict KTU 2024 scheme formatting requirement. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ASK:** Used in **optical fiber transmission** (since light intensity = amplitude) and **low-cost RFID systems**. - **FSK:** Used in **early modems (Bell 202)**, **pager systems**, and **low-power IoT telemetry** (e.g., LoRa uses a variant of FSK). - **PSK:** Used in **satellite communications (DVB-S)**, **GPS**, and **deep space probes (NASA uses BPSK for its low SNR margin)**. - **QAM:** Used in **Wi-Fi (up to 1024-QAM in 802.11ac)**, **4G LTE (up to 256-QAM)**, **5G NR (up to 1024-QAM)**, and **digital cable TV (64-QAM/256-QAM)**. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Example for KTU Viva:** > When you stream a 4K video over Wi-Fi, your router uses **256-QAM or 1024-QAM** to pack 8 or 10 bits into a single symbol, achieving the high data rates needed for video. This is precisely why D/A conversion schemes (specifically QAM) are crucial in modern networking. # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Relationship Between Bit Rate and Baud Rate This is the single most tested derivation in KTU 2024 module-4 questions. ### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Definitions Let us define: - $N$ = Bit rate (in **bits per second, bps**) - $S$ = Baud rate or signal rate (in **symbols per second, bauds**) - $L$ = Number of bits encoded per signal element - $M$ = Number of distinct signal elements (signal points in constellation) ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation **Step 1 — Relationship between $L$ and $M$:** If a signal element (symbol) can take $M$ distinct values, and each value must encode a unique combination of $L$ bits, then the total number of possible bit patterns is $2^L$. For all $M$ values to uniquely map to $2^L$ bit patterns, we must have: $$ \begin{aligned} M &= 2^L \\ \Rightarrow L &= \log_2 M \quad \text{(taking } \log_2 \text{ of both sides)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Relationship between $N$, $S$, and $L$:** The bit rate is the number of bits transmitted per second. The baud rate is the number of signal elements transmitted per second. If each signal element carries $L$ bits, then the number of bits per second is: $$ \begin{aligned} N &= S \times L \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Substituting $L = \log_2 M$:** Substituting the expression for $L$ into the bit-rate equation: $$ \begin{aligned} N &= S \times \log_2 M \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Final Form (Solving for Baud Rate):** Rearranging to solve for $S$: $$ \begin{aligned} S &= \frac{N}{\log_2 M} = \frac{N}{L} \\ \end{aligned} $$ This is the **master formula** the KTU examiner expects in your Part-B answers. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (BFSK Constellation & Bandwidth) **Problem Statement (KTU University Exam - June 2024 type):** A digital signal of bit rate $N = 2000$ bps is to be converted to an analog signal using BFSK. The carrier frequency is $f_c = 1000$ Hz. The two frequencies used are $f_1 = 800$ Hz and $f_2 = 1200$ Hz. Calculate: (a) The baud rate (b) The bandwidth required (c) The number of signal elements per second (d) The minimum carrier frequency for KTU standard carrier alignment ### Solution: **Part (a) — Baud Rate:** BFSK uses only $M = 2$ signal levels (one frequency for 1, another for 0). Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} S &= \frac{N}{\log_2 M} = \frac{2000}{\log_2 2} = \frac{2000}{1} = 2000 \text{ bauds} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) — Bandwidth:** The bandwidth of BFSK is given by the difference between the two carrier frequencies plus the bit rate: $$ \begin{aligned} BW_{BFSK} &= \vert f_2 - f_1 \vert + N \\ BW_{BFSK} &= \vert 1200 - 800 \vert + 2000 \\ BW_{BFSK} &= 400 + 2000 = 2400 \text{ Hz} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Part (c) — Signal Elements Per Second:** The number of signal elements per second is exactly the baud rate, which is **2000 signal elements/second**. **Part (d) — Minimum Carrier Frequency:** According to the KTU standard, the minimum carrier frequency is given by: $$ \begin{aligned} f_c &= \frac{0.5}{T_b} = 0.5 \times N \\ f_c &= 0.5 \times 2000 = 1000 \text{ Hz} \\ \end{aligned} $$ Since the given $f_c = 1000$ Hz equals the minimum required, the design is **valid**. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (16-QAM Constellation Analysis) **Problem Statement:** A 16-QAM system transmits at a bit rate of $N = 48{,}000$ bps. Calculate: (a) Number of bits per signal element (b) Baud rate (c) Effective bandwidth (assume ASK-equivalent, $BW = S$) ### Solution: **Part (a) — Bits per Signal Element:** 16-QAM uses $M = 16$ signal levels. So: $$ \begin{aligned} L &= \log_2 M = \log_2 16 = 4 \text{ bits per symbol} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) — Baud Rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} S &= \frac{N}{L} = \frac{48{,}000}{4} = 12{,}000 \text{ bauds} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Part (c) — Effective Bandwidth:** $$ \begin{aligned} BW_{16\text{-}QAM} &= S = 12{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 12 \text{ kHz} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison Insight (Important for KTU):** If the same $N = 48{,}000$ bps were transmitted using **BPSK**, the baud rate would be $48{,}000$ bauds, requiring **48 kHz of bandwidth**. Using 16-QAM reduces the bandwidth requirement by a factor of 4, demonstrating why QAM is preferred in bandwidth-constrained systems. ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix | Parameter | ASK (BASK) | BPSK | QPSK | 16-QAM | 16-PSK | 16-FSK | |:----------|:----------:|:----:|:----:|:------:|:------:|:------:| | No. of signal levels ($M$) | 2 | 2 | 4 | 16 | 16 | 16 | | Bits per symbol ($L$) | 1 | 1 | 2 | 4 | 4 | 4 | | Bandwidth Efficiency | Poor | Good | Better | Best | Best | Moderate | | Noise Immunity | Poor | Best | Very Good | Moderate | Good | Good | | Baud Rate for $N=48000$ | 48000 | 48000 | 24000 | 12000 | 12000 | 12000 | | Constellation Points | 2 | 2 | 4 | 16 | 16 | 16 (on freq axis) | # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Generic D/A Conversion System Architecture This diagram illustrates the **conceptual block-level architecture** of a Digital-to-Analog modulation system as it operates in the data link layer of a computer network. ```mermaid graph TD node1["Digital Data Source
Binary Bitstream"] --> node2["Serial to Parallel
Converter"] node2 --> node3["Symbol Mapper
Assigns Constellation Point"] node3 --> node4["Carrier Oscillator
Sinusoidal Wave Source"] node4 --> node5["Modulation Engine
ASK or FSK or PSK or QAM"] node3 --> node5 node5 --> node6["Bandpass Analog Signal
Output to Channel"] node6 --> node7["Transmission Medium
Twisted Pair or Fiber or RF"] node7 --> node8["Receiver Demodulator
Reverse Process"] node8 --> node9["Decision Device
Symbol to Bit Mapping"] node9 --> node10["Recovered Digital Data
Output to Destination"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** > The digital bitstream enters from the source. A serial-to-parallel converter groups bits into symbols (e.g., 2 bits for QPSK, 4 bits for 16-QAM). The symbol mapper assigns a unique (amplitude, phase) point from the constellation. The carrier oscillator generates a continuous-time sinusoid, which the modulation engine then modifies according to the assigned symbol. The result is a bandpass analog signal transmitted over the channel. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Choosing a D/A Scheme ```mermaid graph TD startA["Need Digital to Analog
Conversion"] --> startB{"Bandwidth
Critical?"} startB -- "Yes, Very Critical" --> startC{"Noise Immunity
Required?"} startB -- "No, Bandwidth
Abundant" --> startD["Use FSK Family
BFSK or MFSK"] startC -- "Yes, Maximum
Noise Immunity" --> startE["Use BPSK
Most Robust"] startC -- "Moderate" --> startF{"Acceptable
Complexity?"} startF -- "Yes" --> startG["Use M-PSK
or M-QAM"] startF -- "No, Keep Simple" --> startH["Use BFSK
Robust Simple"] startG --> startI["QAM for WiFi and 4G
PSK for Satellites"] startD --> startJ["Pagers and
Legacy Modems"] startE --> startK["GPS and
Deep Space Links"] startH --> startL["IoT Telemetry
and RFID"] ``` ## 4.3 ASCII Constellation Diagram (BPSK) For BPSK, the constellation has **2 points** diametrically opposite on the I-axis (in-phase axis): ``` Q (Quadrature) | | | | ------X------ I (In-Phase) / \ / \ / \ / \ (0,0) (1,0) bit 0 bit 1 -A +A ``` **Interpretation:** - Bit **1** is mapped to phase **0°** at coordinates $(+A, 0)$. - Bit **0** is mapped to phase **180°** at coordinates $(-A, 0)$. - The two points are maximally separated (180° apart), giving BPSK the **best noise immunity** among binary schemes. ## 4.4 ASCII Constellation Diagram (QPSK) For QPSK, the constellation has **4 points** equally spaced at 90° intervals on the unit circle: ``` Q | 01 | 11 | -1 ----- 0 ----- +1 I | 00 | 10 | ``` **Interpretation:** Each quadrant encodes a unique 2-bit pattern: - **00** → 225° (lower left) - **01** → 135° (upper left) - **10** → 315° (lower right) - **11** → 45° (upper right) Since 2 bits are transmitted per symbol, the **baud rate is half the bit rate** for QPSK. ## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — 16-QAM Constellation Structure ```mermaid graph LR nodeQ["Q axis 4 amplitude levels"] --- nodeConst["16 QAM Constellation
4 rows by 4 columns"] nodeI["I axis 4 amplitude levels"] --- nodeConst nodeConst --> nodeBit["4 bits per symbol
L equals 4"] nodeBit --> nodeEff["Bandwidth Efficiency
N by S equals 4"] ``` # 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Note:** Part A questions test direct recall and definition-level understanding. The examiner expects precise technical terminology, not vague explanations. Use keywords like "baseband," "bandpass," "carrier," "constellation," and "bandwidth efficiency" in your answers to score full marks. ### Question 1: Define Digital-to-Analog Conversion. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 type]** **Model Answer:** Digital-to-Analog conversion in the data communication domain is the process of converting a digital bitstream (sequence of binary 1s and 0s) into an analog passband signal by modulating one or more characteristics of a continuous-time sinusoidal carrier wave — namely, **amplitude**, **frequency**, or **phase** — in discrete steps. The resulting analog signal occupies a bandpass frequency spectrum suitable for transmission over band-limited media such as telephone lines, optical fibers, or wireless RF channels. The five primary techniques are ASK, FSK, PSK, QAM, and their M-ary variants. --- ### Question 2: Distinguish between Bit Rate and Baud Rate. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 type]** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Bit Rate ($N$) | Baud Rate ($S$) | |:--------|:--------------|:----------------| | **Definition** | Number of bits transmitted per second | Number of signal elements (symbols) transmitted per second | | **Unit** | bits per second (bps) | symbols per second (bauds) | | **Formula** | $N = S \times \log_2 M$ | $S = N / \log_2 M$ | | **Example** | In 16-QAM, $N = 48000$ bps | In 16-QAM, $S = 12000$ bauds | For binary schemes ($M = 2$), $N = S$ since $\log_2 2 = 1$. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Evaluation Rule:** For 14-mark questions, the examiner follows a strict split — typically **7 marks for part (a)** and **7 marks for part (b)**. Each sub-part must contain: a **diagram** (1-2 marks), a **formula with substitution** (2-3 marks), and a **final numerical/textual answer** (2-3 marks). Skipping the diagram forfeits those marks. --- ### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks): BPSK and QPSK Comparison Problem **Question:** **(a)** With the help of neat constellation diagrams, explain Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK) and Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK). Compare their bandwidth efficiency. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A digital bitstream of $N = 72{,}000$ bps is converted to analog using QPSK. Calculate the baud rate, the bandwidth required (assuming Nyquist minimum), and the minimum carrier frequency. If the same bit rate were transmitted using BPSK instead, what would be the new baud rate and bandwidth? Comment on the bandwidth saving. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part A(a): **BPSK (Binary Phase Shift Keying):** BPSK uses two distinct phase values — **0° and 180°** — to represent the binary bits 1 and 0 respectively. The amplitude and frequency of the carrier remain constant. **Constellation Diagram for BPSK:** ``` Q axis | 135° | 180° ----- 0° I axis | 225° | ``` - Bit **1** is encoded at phase **0°** (in-phase, $+A$). - Bit **0** is encoded at phase **180°** (inverted, $-A$). - The two points are **180° apart** — providing the **maximum possible noise immunity** for a binary scheme. **QPSK (Quadrature Phase Shift Keying):** QPSK uses four distinct phase values — **45°, 135°, 225°, 315°** — each encoding **2 bits** per symbol. **Constellation Diagram for QPSK:** ``` Q | 01 | 11 | -1 ----- 0 ----- +1 I | 00 | 10 | ``` - **45°** → bits **11** - **135°** → bits **01** - **225°** → bits **00** - **315°** → bits **10** **Bandwidth Comparison:** For the same bit rate $N$: - BPSK: $S = N$, so $BW = N$ Hz - QPSK: $S = N/2$, so $BW = N/2$ Hz - **QPSK requires exactly half the bandwidth of BPSK** for the same data throughput. --- #### Model Solution — Part A(b): **For QPSK transmission ($N = 72{,}000$ bps):** **Step 1 — Bits per symbol for QPSK:** QPSK uses $M = 4$ signal levels, so: $$ \begin{aligned} L &= \log_2 M = \log_2 4 = 2 \text{ bits per symbol} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Baud Rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{QPSK} &= \frac{N}{L} = \frac{72{,}000}{2} = 36{,}000 \text{ bauds} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Bandwidth (Nyquist minimum):** For QPSK, the minimum bandwidth is equal to the baud rate (signal rate): $$ \begin{aligned} BW_{QPSK} &= S_{QPSK} = 36{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 36 \text{ kHz} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Minimum Carrier Frequency:** $$ \begin{aligned} f_{c,min} &= \frac{0.5}{T_b} = 0.5 \times N = 0.5 \times 72{,}000 = 36{,}000 \text{ Hz} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **For BPSK transmission (same $N = 72{,}000$ bps):** **Step 5 — Baud Rate for BPSK:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{BPSK} &= \frac{N}{1} = 72{,}000 \text{ bauds (since } L = 1 \text{)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Bandwidth for BPSK:** $$ \begin{aligned} BW_{BPSK} &= 72{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 72 \text{ kHz} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Bandwidth Saving Comment:** QPSK achieves a **bandwidth saving of $72 - 36 = 36$ kHz (a 50% reduction)** compared to BPSK for the same bit rate. This is because QPSK encodes 2 bits per symbol, effectively halving the symbol rate and therefore the bandwidth requirement. --- ### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks): BFSK, MFSK, and QAM Numerical Problem **Question:** **(a)** Explain the working of Binary Frequency Shift Keying (BFSK) and M-ary FSK (MFSK) with suitable diagrams. Derive the expression for bandwidth of MFSK. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Consider an MFSK system with $M = 16$ signal levels, transmitting at a bit rate of $N = 64{,}000$ bps. The minimum frequency is $f_1 = 50$ kHz and the maximum frequency is $f_M = 100$ kHz. Calculate: (i) the number of bits per symbol, (ii) the baud rate, (iii) the bandwidth, (iv) the number of signal elements per second, and (v) comment on the bandwidth efficiency compared to 16-PSK. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part B(a): **BFSK (Binary Frequency Shift Keying):** In BFSK, the digital data modulates the **frequency** of the carrier while keeping amplitude constant. - Bit **1** → carrier frequency $f_1$ - Bit **0** → carrier frequency $f_2$ - Mathematical expression: $s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ for bit 1, and $s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_2 t)$ for bit 0. **MFSK (M-ary FSK):** MFSK is a generalization of BFSK using $M$ distinct frequencies. Each frequency represents a unique $L$-bit pattern, where $L = \log_2 M$. **Bandwidth Derivation of MFSK:** Let the $M$ frequencies be equally spaced with separation $\Delta f$. The first (lowest) frequency is $f_1$ and the last (highest) is $f_M$. The total spread of frequencies is: $$ \begin{aligned} f_M - f_1 &= (M - 1) \cdot \Delta f \\ \end{aligned} $$ Each frequency component has a bandwidth equal to the baud rate (which is the bit rate of the underlying binary NRZ signal, $B$). Therefore, the total bandwidth is: $$ \begin{aligned} BW_{MFSK} &= (M - 1) \cdot \Delta f + 2B \\ \end{aligned} $$ where $B$ is the bit rate, $M = 2^L$, and $\Delta f$ is the frequency separation. This is the **standard KTU formula for MFSK bandwidth**. --- #### Model Solution — Part B(b): Given: $M = 16$, $N = 64{,}000$ bps, $f_1 = 50$ kHz, $f_M = 100$ kHz. **(i) Number of bits per symbol:** $$ \begin{aligned} L &= \log_2 M = \log_2 16 = 4 \text{ bits per symbol} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Baud Rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{MFSK} &= \frac{N}{L} = \frac{64{,}000}{4} = 16{,}000 \text{ bauds} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Bandwidth:** The bandwidth of MFSK equals the spread of frequencies (from $f_1$ to $f_M$): $$ \begin{aligned} BW_{MFSK} &= f_M - f_1 = 100{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 50{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 50 \text{ kHz} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Number of signal elements per second:** The number of signal elements transmitted per second is exactly the baud rate: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Signal elements/sec} &= S = 16{,}000 \text{ elements/sec} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **(v) Comparison with 16-PSK:** For 16-PSK with the same $N = 64{,}000$ bps: - 16-PSK uses the same $L = 4$ bits/symbol, so $S_{PSK} = 16{,}000$ bauds (same as MFSK). - The bandwidth of 16-PSK is $BW_{PSK} = S = 16{,}000$ Hz = 16 kHz. - **Therefore, 16-PSK requires only 16 kHz of bandwidth, while 16-MFSK requires 50 kHz.** **Conclusion:** 16-PSK is **far more bandwidth-efficient** than 16-MFSK. However, 16-MFSK has better noise immunity because each frequency is widely separated, making it easier to distinguish symbols in a noisy channel. This is a classic **trade-off between bandwidth efficiency and noise immunity** that the KTU examiner often tests. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing "Bit Rate" with "Baud Rate" (3-mark penalty):** Students often write $N = S$ for all schemes. The KTU key demands the correct use of $N = S \log_2 M$. Always specify $M$ before applying the formula. > > 2. **Forgetting the Constellation Diagram (2-3 marks lost):** A constellation diagram is **mandatory** for any 7-mark or 14-mark question on PSK or QAM. The examiner will deduct marks if it is omitted, even if the numerical answers are correct. > > 3. **MFSK Bandwidth Formula Error:** Students often write $BW = (M \cdot \Delta f)$ instead of $BW = (M-1) \cdot \Delta f + 2B$. The "$(M-1)$" part is critical — using $M$ directly gives a wrong answer. > > 4. **Using $f_c = N$ instead of $f_c = N/2$:** The KTU standard minimum carrier frequency is $f_c = N/2$ (i.e., $f_c = 0.5/T_b$). Using $f_c = N$ is a common error. > > 5. **Ignoring Units in the Final Answer:** Always state units (Hz, kHz, bps, bauds). A correct number without units loses 0.5 marks. > > 6. **Not Drawing the Constellation:** For BPSK/QPSK/16-QAM questions, missing the diagram is an automatic 2-mark loss in the KTU scheme. --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Use this section for last-minute revision before the KTU university exam.** ### 🎯 Core Concepts to Memorize: - ☐ **D/A Conversion** = transforming digital bits into an analog passband signal by modulating a sinusoidal carrier. - ☐ **Five primary techniques:** ASK, BFSK, MFSK, BPSK, QPSK, and QAM. - ☐ **Carrier Signal:** $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_c)$ — only one or more of $(A_c, f_c, \phi_c)$ is varied. - ☐ **BPSK** modulates **phase** with 2 levels (0° and 180°), $M=2$, $L=1$. - ☐ **QPSK** modulates **phase** with 4 levels (45°, 135°, 225°, 315°), $M=4$, $L=2$. - ☐ **QAM** modulates **both amplitude and phase** simultaneously, $M=16, 64, 256$ common. - ☐ **MFSK** modulates **frequency** with $M$ levels, $L = \log_2 M$. ### 🔢 Master Formulas (Write These 5 Times Before the Exam): - ☐ $N = S \times \log_2 M$ → **The single most important formula** of this module. - ☐ $M = 2^L$ → defines the number of signal levels from bits per symbol. - ☐ $BW_{MFSK} = (M-1) \cdot \Delta f + 2B$ → MFSK bandwidth. - ☐ $BW_{ASK/BPSK} = N$ (for binary) → minimum bandwidth. - ☐ $f_{c,min} = N/2$ → KTU standard minimum carrier frequency. ### 📊 Comparison Mnemonic (Remember the Trade-offs): - ☐ **ASK:** Simplest, **worst** noise immunity. - ☐ **FSK:** Better noise immunity, more bandwidth. - ☐ **PSK:** Excellent noise immunity, bandwidth efficient. - ☐ **QAM:** **Best bandwidth efficiency**, used in Wi-Fi and 4G/5G. ### ⚡ High-Yield KTU Exam Tips: - ☐ Always draw the **constellation diagram** for any PSK/QAM question. - ☐ Always state **$M$ and $L$** before applying the bit-rate formula. - ☐ Always include **units** in the final answer. - ☐ Always mention the **bandwidth-noise trade-off** in comparison answers. - ☐ Remember: **bit rate ≥ baud rate**; equality holds only for binary schemes. ### 🎨 Quick Constellation Identification (For Drawing in Exam): - ☐ 2 points on I-axis (opposite) → **BPSK** - ☐ 4 points on axes/quadrants (90° apart) → **QPSK** - ☐ 4 points on axes only (two amplitudes) → **4-QAM** (same as QPSK) - ☐ 16 points in 4×4 grid → **16-QAM** - ☐ 16 points on a circle → **16-PSK** - ☐ $M$ vertical lines (or frequency markers) → **MFSK** ### 🧠 Key Terminology to Use in Answers (Impresses Examiners): - ☐ "**Baseband**" vs "**Passband**" transmission - ☐ "**Constellation diagram**" (not "signal diagram") - ☐ "**Bandwidth efficiency**" or "**spectral efficiency**" - ☐ "**Carrier frequency alignment**" - ☐ "**Bit error rate (BER)** vs **Symbol error rate (SER)**" > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Day Mantra:** > "Master the 5 formulas, draw every constellation, state $M$ and $L$ explicitly, and always end with bandwidth-noise trade-off commentary. Full marks guaranteed! 🎯"

topics.title: Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of "Specifications" in Computer Networks

In the context of **KTU PCERT403 – Computer Networks (Module 4)**, the topic **"Specifications"** refers to the **standardized, formally defined technical parameters, header formats, field semantics, and operational rules of Network Layer protocols** — most prominently the **Internet Protocol (IP) specifications** for **IPv4** and **IPv6**, alongside auxiliary protocols such as **ICMP**, **IGMP**, and **ARP**.

These specifications are codified in **Request For Comments (RFC)** documents maintained by the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** and define:

> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *A protocol specification is a precise, unambiguous technical document that defines the structure (syntax), behavior (semantics), and timing (synchronization) of data exchanged between communicating network entities, ensuring interoperability across heterogeneous systems.*

The primary IP specifications covered in Module 4 include:
- **IPv4 Specification** — RFC 791
- **IPv6 Specification** — RFC 8200
- **ICMP Specification** — RFC 792
- **IGMP Specification** — RFC 1112 / RFC 3376
- **ARP Specification** — RFC 826

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The Board Examiner predominantly tests the **IPv4 Header Format**, **IPv6 Header Format**, **Differences between IPv4 and IPv6**, and the role of **ICMP/IGMP/ARP** as supporting specifications. Numerical problems on header field calculations are **high-yield**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sending a **parcel through a global courier service** (say, FedEx or DHL):

| Courier Concept | Network Specification Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The parcel itself | **IP Datagram (Packet)** |
| The shipping label with addresses, tracking ID, weight, fragile sticker | **IP Header fields** (Source IP, Dest IP, TTL, Protocol, etc.) |
| The standardized label format defined by courier HQ | **RFC Specifications** (IPv4/IPv6 Standards) |
| The "Return to Sender" or "Address Not Found" slip | **ICMP Error Messages** |
| The local warehouse's internal dispatch register | **ARP Cache / IGMP Group Membership** |
| A premium express label with extra tracking features | **IPv6 Extension Headers** |

> Just as every courier worldwide must follow the **same label format** so that parcels can traverse multiple sorting hubs, every router and host on the Internet must follow the **same IP specification** so that datagrams can be forwarded globally without misinterpretation.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Logical Constants in IP Specifications

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Specification Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **IPv4 Address Size:** **32 bits** (4 bytes)
> - **IPv6 Address Size:** **128 bits** (16 bytes)
> - **IPv4 Header:** **20 to 60 bytes** (without/with options)
> - **IPv6 Header:** **Fixed 40 bytes** (extensions handled separately)
> - **Maximum IPv4 Datagram Size:** **65,535 bytes** (2¹⁶ − 1)
> - **Default MTU (Ethernet):** **1500 bytes**
> - **Time To Live (TTL) / Hop Limit:** **8-bit field**, decremented at every hop
> - **IPv6 Hop Limit default:** Typically **64** when set by hosts
> - **IPv4 Protocol Number for ICMP:** **1**
> - **IPv4 Protocol Number for IGMP:** **2**
> - **IPv4 Protocol Number for TCP:** **6**
> - **IPv4 Protocol Number for UDP:** **17**
> - **IPv6 Next Header for ICMPv6:** **58**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IPv4 Header Specification (RFC 791)

The IPv4 header is a **variable-length structure** ranging from **20 bytes (mandatory)** to **60 bytes (with options)**. Every field serves a precise specification role.

### 2.1.1 Bit-Level Breakdown of the IPv4 Header

| Bit Offset | Field Name | Size (bits) | Specification Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Version | 4 | Always `0100` (4) for IPv4 |
| 4 | IHL (Internet Header Length) | 4 | Header length in **32-bit words**; Min = 5, Max = 15 |
| 8 | Type of Service (ToS / DSCP/ECN) | 8 | QoS and Congestion Notification |
| 16 | Total Length | 16 | Entire datagram size (header + data) in bytes |
| 32 | Identification | 16 | Unique ID for fragmented datagram reassembly |
| 48 | Flags | 3 | DF (Don't Fragment), MF (More Fragments), Reserved |
| 51 | Fragment Offset | 13 | Position of fragment in original datagram (in 8-byte units) |
| 64 | Time To Live (TTL) | 8 | Hop count limit; decremented by 1 at each router |
| 72 | Protocol | 8 | Next-layer protocol identifier (TCP=6, UDP=17, ICMP=1) |
| 80 | Header Checksum | 16 | 1's complement checksum of header only |
| 96 | Source IP Address | 32 | Sender's IPv4 address |
| 128 | Destination IP Address | 32 | Receiver's IPv4 address |
| 160 | Options (if IHL > 5) | 0–320 | Optional specification features (Record Route, Timestamp, etc.) |
| Variable | Padding | Variable | Zeros to make header a multiple of 32 bits |
| Variable | Data (Payload) | Variable | Upper-layer data |

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic — Why Each Field Exists

1. **Version field (4 bits):** Allows routers to parse mixed IPv4/IPv6 traffic on dual-stack networks.
2. **IHL (4 bits):** Because options are variable-length, the receiver needs an explicit length pointer to locate the payload. Specified in **32-bit words**, so multiply by 4 to get bytes.
3. **Total Length (16 bits):** Maximum = `2¹⁶ − 1 = 65,535` bytes. This bounds the datagram size.
4. **Identification + Flags + Fragment Offset:** These three fields form the **fragmentation specification**, allowing large datagrams to be split across networks with smaller MTUs.
5. **TTL (8 bits):** Prevents infinite routing loops. When TTL reaches 0, the datagram is discarded and an **ICMP Time Exceeded** message is sent back to the source.
6. **Protocol (8 bits):** Multiplexing key that tells the destination host which transport-layer (or other) protocol should receive the payload. This is the **IP "demux key"**.
7. **Header Checksum (16 bits):** Since TTL changes at every hop, the checksum must be **recomputed at every router** — a major performance cost and a key motivation for IPv6's removal of it.
8. **Source/Destination IP (32 bits each):** The network-layer addressing specification.

## 2.2 The IPv6 Header Specification (RFC 8200)

IPv6 was designed to fix IPv4's limitations. The base header is **simplified, fixed at 40 bytes**, with optional features moved to **extension headers**.

### 2.2.1 IPv6 Base Header Layout

| Bit Offset | Field Name | Size (bits) | Specification Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Version | 4 | Always `0110` (6) for IPv6 |
| 4 | Traffic Class | 8 | Equivalent to IPv4 ToS/DSCP for QoS |
| 12 | Flow Label | 20 | Identifies a packet flow for QoS/real-time handling |
| 32 | Payload Length | 16 | Length of payload **only** (not header) |
| 48 | Next Header | 8 | Identifies type of header following (TCP, UDP, ICMPv6, or extension header) |
| 56 | Hop Limit | 8 | Equivalent to IPv4 TTL; decremented per hop |
| 64 | Source Address | 128 | Sender's IPv6 address |
| 192 | Destination Address | 128 | Receiver's IPv6 address |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Specification Change:** IPv6 does **NOT** include a Header Checksum. It also does **NOT** include fragmentation fields in the base header — fragmentation is handled via an **Fragment Extension Header** (RFC 8200 specifies that fragmentation is **not permitted** by routers; only the source can fragment).

### 2.2.2 IPv6 Extension Headers (Chained via "Next Header" Field)

1. **Hop-by-Hop Options** (Next Header = 0)
2. **Destination Options** (Next Header = 60)
3. **Routing Header** (Next Header = 43)
4. **Fragment Header** (Next Header = 44)
5. **Authentication Header (AH)** (Next Header = 51)
6. **Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)** (Next Header = 50)

## 2.3 Auxiliary Protocol Specifications

### 2.3.1 ICMP — Internet Control Message Protocol (RFC 792)

- **Type:** Network Layer supporting protocol
- **Purpose:** Error reporting and diagnostic messaging
- **Encapsulation:** Inside IP datagrams (Protocol = 1)
- **Key Messages:**
  - **Type 3:** Destination Unreachable (Codes 0–15)
  - **Type 4:** Source Quench (deprecated)
  - **Type 5:** Redirect
  - **Type 8 / 0:** Echo Request / Echo Reply (`ping` utility)
  - **Type 11:** Time Exceeded (used by `traceroute`)
  - **Type 12:** Parameter Problem

### 2.3.2 IGMP — Internet Group Management Protocol (RFC 3376)

- **Type:** Network Layer multicast group management
- **Purpose:** Hosts inform local multicast routers about group memberships
- **Encapsulation:** Protocol = 2 in IPv4
- **Message Types:**
  - **0x11:** Membership Query
  - **0x12:** IGMPv1 Membership Report
  - **0x16:** IGMPv2 Membership Report
  - **0x22:** IGMPv3 Membership Report

### 2.3.3 ARP — Address Resolution Protocol (RFC 826)

- **Purpose:** Maps **IP address → MAC (Physical) address** on a local network
- **Operates:** Between Data Link and Network layers
- **Packet Types:** ARP Request (broadcast) and ARP Reply (unicast)
- **Encapsulation:** Directly in Ethernet frame (EtherType = 0x0806)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Specifications Cheat Sheet — Memorize for KTU Board Exam:**

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Header Length (IPv4) | $L_{header} = IHL \times 4$ | Bytes |
| Total Length check | $L_{data} = L_{total} - L_{header}$ | Bytes |
| Max IPv4 Datagram | $L_{max} = 2^{16} - 1$ | **65,535 bytes** |
| Max Fragment Offset | $FO_{max} = 2^{13} - 1$ | In **8-byte units** |
| Max Fragment Payload Offset | $FO_{max} \times 8 = 65,528$ | Bytes |
| IPv4 Address Count | $2^{32}$ | **4,294,967,296** addresses |
| IPv6 Address Count | $2^{128}$ | ~$3.4 \times 10^{38}$ addresses |
| Number of IPv4 Fragments | $\lceil L_{datagram} / MTU_{payload} \rceil$ | Integer ceiling |
| Header Checksum (1's complement) | $\sum_{16\text{-bit words}} \text{word} \rightarrow \overline{\text{sum}}$ | One's complement of sum |
| Bytes per Fragment data field | $Data = MTU - Header_{size}$ | For example, $1500 - 20 = 1480$ |
| Fragment Offset position | $FO_i = \frac{\text{bytes already sent}}{8}$ | Rounded down to 8-byte boundary |

> **Engineering Utility:** These specifications enable **vendor-neutral interoperability** — Cisco, Juniper, Huawei, and Linux-based routers all parse IPv4/IPv6 headers identically because they strictly follow the same RFC-defined bit-layout. The TTL/Hop Limit specification prevents **count-to-infinity** routing failures; fragmentation fields enable **transparent traversal** of networks with heterogeneous MTUs (e.g., 1500-byte Ethernet vs. 4470-byte FDDI vs. 9000-byte Jumbo Frames).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1: IPv4 Header Length Calculation (Model KTU Problem)

**Question:** An IPv4 datagram arrives with the **IHL field value = 12** and the **Total Length field = 60,000 bytes**. Compute:
1. Header length in bytes
2. Payload (data) length in bytes
3. Number of data bytes per fragment if the MTU is 1500 bytes (assuming no options) and the header remains 20 bytes per fragment.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute Header Length**

The IPv4 specification states that IHL is the number of **32-bit words**. Therefore:

$$
L_{header} = IHL \times 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

Substituting IHL = 12:

$$
L_{header} = 12 \times 4 = 48 \text{ bytes}
$$

> **Logic:** IHL counts 4-byte words because the IPv4 header is architecturally aligned to 32-bit boundaries, simplifying hardware-based header parsing.

**Step 2: Compute Payload Length**

The specification defines Total Length as the **entire datagram (header + data)**:

$$
L_{data} = L_{total} - L_{header}
$$

$$
L_{data} = 60{,}000 - 48 = 59{,}952 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Data per Fragment under Standard 1500-byte MTU**

The maximum IP datagram that fits in a 1500-byte frame has:

$$
L_{max\text{-}per\text{-}fragment} = MTU - Header_{size} = 1500 - 20 = 1480 \text{ bytes}
$$

The 1480-byte figure is the **maximum payload** per fragment when the standard 20-byte header is used.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Header length | **48 bytes** |
| Total data | **59,952 bytes** |
| Data per fragment | **1,480 bytes** |
| Number of fragments | $\lceil 59{,}952 / 1480 \rceil = \lceil 40.508 \rceil =$ **41 fragments** |

---

## 3.2 Problem 2: Fragmentation Offset & Identification (Classic KTU Problem)

**Question:** A 4000-byte datagram (20-byte header) must traverse a network with **MTU = 1500 bytes**. Compute the **Identification**, **Flags (DF, MF)**, and **Fragment Offset** for each fragment. Show that reassembly reconstructs the original payload.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute Maximum Data per Fragment**

$$
L_{data\text{-}per\text{-}fragment} = MTU - Header_{size} = 1500 - 20 = 1480 \text{ bytes}
$$

Since 1480 is **divisible by 8** (the fragment offset unit), this is the cleanest fragment size.

**Step 2: Determine Number of Fragments**

$$
N_{fragments} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{datagram} - Header_{size}}{1480} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{3980}{1480} \right\rceil = 3
$$

Let us compute the precise payload distribution:

- **Fragment 1:** Carries first **1480 bytes** of data
- **Fragment 2:** Carries next **1480 bytes** of data
- **Fragment 3:** Carries remaining $3980 - 1480 - 1480 = 1020$ bytes of data

**Step 3: Compute Fragment Offsets (in 8-byte units)**

Fragment Offset specification: position of fragment's data start, measured in **8-byte units** from the beginning of original payload.

$$
FO_1 = \frac{0}{8} = 0
$$

$$
FO_2 = \frac{1480}{8} = 185
$$

$$
FO_3 = \frac{1480 + 1480}{8} = \frac{2960}{8} = 370
$$

**Step 4: Determine MF (More Fragments) Flags**

| Fragment | MF Flag | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Fragment 1 | **MF = 1** (More fragments follow) | Not the last fragment |
| Fragment 2 | **MF = 1** (More fragments follow) | Not the last fragment |
| Fragment 3 | **MF = 0** (Last fragment) | Final fragment of datagram |

The **DF (Don't Fragment)** flag is 0 in all (we *are* fragmenting). The **Reserved** bit is 0.

**Step 5: Reassembly Verification**

Total reassembled payload:

$$
L_{reassembled} = 1480 + 1480 + 1020 = 3980 \text{ bytes}
$$

Original payload (excluding header) was 4000 − 20 = 3980 bytes. ✓ Match confirmed.

**Step 6: Fragment Header Summary Table (Answer Key)**

| Fragment | Header (B) | Data (B) | Total (B) | Offset (8B units) | MF | Identification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 1480 | 1500 | 0 | 1 | X (same for all) |
| 2 | 20 | 1480 | 1500 | 185 | 1 | X (same for all) |
| 3 | 20 | 1020 | 1040 | 370 | 0 | X (same for all) |

> **Logic Note:** The `Identification` field is **identical for all fragments** of the same datagram — the destination uses (Source IP, Destination IP, Identification, Protocol) as the 4-tuple to recognize fragments of the same original packet.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3: Header Checksum Computation (1's Complement)

**Question:** Given the following 4 contiguous 16-bit words of an IPv4 header, compute the **Header Checksum**:

Word 1: `0x4500`, Word 2: `0x003C`, Word 3: `0x1C46`, Word 4: `0x4000` (Checksum field is `0x0000` initially).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Sum the 16-bit Words (One's Complement Addition)**

$$
S_1 = 0x4500 + 0x003C = 0x453C
$$

**Step 2: Add the Next Word (with End-Around Carry)**

$$
S_2 = 0x453C + 0x1C46 = 0x6182
$$

No carry-out (0x6182 < 0x10000), so no wrap-around needed.

**Step 3: Add the Fourth Word**

$$
S_3 = 0x6182 + 0x4000 = 0xA182
$$

No carry-out.

**Step 4: Take the One's Complement**

The Header Checksum is the **one's complement** of the sum:

$$
\text{Checksum} = \overline{0xA182} = 0x5E7D
$$

**Step 5: Verification (Receiver Side)**

Receiver sums **all five 16-bit words including the checksum**; the result must be `0xFFFF`:

$$
S_{verify} = 0x4500 + 0x003C + 0x1C46 + 0x4000 + 0x5E7D
$$

Intermediate:
$$
0x4500 + 0x003C = 0x453C
$$
$$
0x453C + 0x1C46 = 0x6182
$$
$$
0x6182 + 0x4000 = 0xA182
$$
$$
0xA182 + 0x5E7D = 0xFFFF
$$

Verified. ✓

> **Logic:** The one's complement checksum property ensures that any single-bit error in the transmitted header (with high probability) will be detected at the receiver, since the result will not equal `0xFFFF`.

---

## 3.4 Problem 4: IPv4 vs IPv6 Header Comparison Analysis

**Question:** A KTU Board question asks for a structured comparison of IPv4 and IPv6 headers along **seven specification parameters**. Build the analytical matrix:

| Specification Parameter | IPv4 (RFC 791) | IPv6 (RFC 8200) | Design Rationale for Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Header Size | Variable, 20–60 bytes | **Fixed 40 bytes** | Faster hardware/software parsing |
| Address Length | 32 bits | **128 bits** | Solve address exhaustion |
| Header Checksum | Present | **Removed** | Reduce per-hop processing cost |
| Fragmentation | Routers + source can fragment | **Source-only (using extension header)** | Simplify router forwarding |
| Options | Variable in header | **Extension headers** | Cleaner, optional processing |
| Flow QoS Support | ToS/DSCP only | **Traffic Class + Flow Label** | Better real-time / multimedia support |
| Security (IPsec) | Optional, external | **Native mandatory support** | Mandate network-layer security |
| Hop Limit / TTL | TTL (8 bits) | **Hop Limit (8 bits)** | Same value, clearer naming |

---

## 3.5 Problem 5: Protocol Number Demultiplexing

**Question:** An IPv4 datagram has `Protocol = 6`. Identify the upper-layer protocol and explain what the destination host does with the payload.

**Solution:**

Protocol = 6 corresponds to **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** as defined in the IANA Protocol Number registry. The destination host:
1. Strips the IP header.
2. Inspects the `Protocol` field.
3. Demultiplexes the payload to the TCP processing module.
4. TCP further inspects its own header (Source Port, Destination Port, Sequence Number, etc.) for connection handling.

> **Common KTU Values to Memorize:**
> - `1` → ICMP, `2` → IGMP, `6` → TCP, `17` → UDP, `41` → IPv6 encapsulation, `58` → ICMPv6, `89` → OSPF, `132` → SCTP.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IPv4 Datagram Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IP Datagram Arrival at Router] --> B[Parse Version Field - 4 bits]
    B --> C{Version = 4?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Read IHL Field]
    D --> E[Compute Header Length = IHL x 4]
    E --> F[Validate Total Length Field]
    F --> G{TTL > 0?}
    G -- No --> H[Discard & Send ICMP Time Exceeded]
    G -- Yes --> I[Decrement TTL by 1]
    I --> J[Recompute Header Checksum]
    J --> K[Inspect Destination IP]
    K --> L{Local Network?}
    L -- No --> M[Lookup Routing Table]
    M --> N[MTU > Payload?}
    N -- No --> O[Fragment Datagram]
    N -- Yes --> P[Forward to Next Hop]
    L -- Yes --> Q[ARP Lookup for MAC]
    Q --> R[Encapsulate in Ethernet Frame]
    R --> S[Transmit on Local Link]
    P --> S
    O --> S
```

## 4.2 IPv4 Header Field Map (Visual Layout)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BIT0["Bits 0-3"]
        V[Version - 4 bits]
    end
    subgraph BIT4["Bits 4-7"]
        I[IHL - 4 bits]
    end
    subgraph BIT8["Bits 8-15"]
        TOS[Type of Service - 8 bits]
    end
    subgraph BIT16["Bits 16-31"]
        TL[Total Length - 16 bits]
    end
    subgraph BIT32["Bits 32-47"]
        ID[Identification - 16 bits]
    end
    subgraph BIT48["Bits 48-50"]
        FL[Flags - 3 bits]
    end
    subgraph BIT51["Bits 51-63"]
        FO[Fragment Offset - 13 bits]
    end
    subgraph BIT64["Bits 64-71"]
        TTL[TTL - 8 bits]
    end
    subgraph BIT72["Bits 72-79"]
        PR[Protocol - 8 bits]
    end
    subgraph BIT80["Bits 80-95"]
        HC[Header Checksum - 16 bits]
    end
    subgraph BIT96["Bits 96-127"]
        SIP[Source IP - 32 bits]
    end
    subgraph BIT128["Bits 128-159"]
        DIP[Destination IP - 32 bits]
    end
    subgraph BIT160["Bits 160-191"]
        OPT[Options - 0 to 320 bits]
    end
    subgraph PAY[Payload]
        DATA[Upper Layer Data]
    end
    V --> I --> TOS --> TL --> ID --> FL --> FO --> TTL --> PR --> HC --> SIP --> DIP --> OPT --> DATA
```

## 4.3 IPv6 Header Field Map (Visual Layout)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph B0["Bits 0-3"]
        V6[Version = 6 - 4 bits]
    end
    subgraph B4["Bits 4-11"]
        TC[Traffic Class - 8 bits]
    end
    subgraph B12["Bits 12-31"]
        FL6[Flow Label - 20 bits]
    end
    subgraph B32["Bits 32-47"]
        PL[Payload Length - 16 bits]
    end
    subgraph B48["Bits 48-55"]
        NH[Next Header - 8 bits]
    end
    subgraph B56["Bits 56-63"]
        HL[Hop Limit - 8 bits]
    end
    subgraph B64["Bits 64-191"]
        SA[Source Address - 128 bits]
    end
    subgraph B192["Bits 192-319"]
        DA[Destination Address - 128 bits]
    end
    subgraph EH[Extension Headers and Payload]
        EHP[Chained Extension Headers + Upper Layer Data]
    end
    V6 --> TC --> FL6 --> PL --> NH --> HL --> SA --> DA --> EHP
```

## 4.4 IP Datagram Reassembly Process (Fragmentation Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Original IPv4 Datagram - e.g., 4000 bytes] --> Q{Check Destination Network MTU}
    Q -- MTU >= Datagram --> R[Forward Unfragmented]
    Q -- MTU < Datagram --> S[Split into Fragments]
    S --> F1[Fragment 1: Data 1480B, Offset 0, MF=1]
    S --> F2[Fragment 2: Data 1480B, Offset 185, MF=1]
    S --> F3[Fragment 3: Data 1020B, Offset 370, MF=0]
    F1 --> X[Destination Reassembly Buffer]
    F2 --> X
    F3 --> X
    X --> Y{All Fragments Received and MF=0 Seen?}
    Y -- No --> Z[Wait in Buffer with Timeout]
    Y -- Yes --> W[Reconstruct Original Datagram]
    W --> DEL[Deliver to Upper Layer]
```

## 4.5 ICMP / IGMP / ARP Operational Layering

```mermaid
graph TB
    APP[Application Layer - HTTP, FTP, DNS] --> T[Transport Layer - TCP / UDP]
    T --> N[Network Layer - IP Datagram]
    N --> ICMP[ICMP - Error Reporting & Diagnostics]
    N --> IGMP[IGMP - Multicast Group Management]
    N --> ARP[ARP - IP to MAC Resolution]
    N --> DL[Data Link Layer - Ethernet Frame]
    DL --> PHY[Physical Layer - Electrical / Optical Signals]
    ICMP -.->|Encapsulated within IP| N
    IGMP -.->|Encapsulated within IP| N
    ARP -.->|Encapsulated directly in Ethernet| DL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Protocol Specification" as used in Computer Networks. List any four IP protocol numbers and their corresponding protocols. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A **protocol specification** is a formal, precise technical document (typically an RFC published by the IETF) that defines the **syntax, semantics, and timing** of communication between network entities. For Internet protocols, the specification dictates the exact bit-layout of headers, the meaning of each field, and the behavior upon receiving/transmitting a packet.

**Four Important IP Protocol Numbers:**

| Protocol Number | Protocol Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | ICMP | Error reporting and diagnostics |
| **2** | IGMP | Multicast group membership management |
| **6** | TCP | Reliable, connection-oriented transport |
| **17** | UDP | Unreliable, connectionless transport |

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between the **Identification**, **Flags**, and **Fragment Offset** fields of the IPv4 header. State one purpose of each. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

- **Identification (16 bits):** A unique integer assigned by the source to each datagram. All fragments of the same datagram carry the **same Identification value**, allowing the destination to recognize them as belonging together for reassembly.
- **Flags (3 bits):** A 3-bit control field. Bit 0 is **Reserved** (0), Bit 1 is **DF (Don't Fragment)** — set to 1 to forbid routers from fragmenting, Bit 2 is **MF (More Fragments)** — set to 1 in all fragments except the last.
- **Fragment Offset (13 bits):** Specifies the **position of this fragment's data** within the original datagram, measured in **8-byte units** from the start of the original payload. It enables the receiver to correctly place each fragment in the reassembly buffer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**(a)** Draw the **IPv4 datagram header format** and explain the function of each field. [7 Marks]

**(b)** A 4500-byte datagram (20-byte header, no options) is to be transmitted over a network with an **MTU of 1500 bytes**. Compute the **Identification, Flags, and Fragment Offset** values for each fragment. Verify that reassembly yields the original payload. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution for (a):**

The IPv4 header (20 bytes minimum) contains the following fields in order:

1. **Version (4 bits):** Specifies IP version (= 4 for IPv4). Allows the receiving node to correctly parse the rest of the header.
2. **IHL — Internet Header Length (4 bits):** Length of the IP header in **32-bit words**; minimum value is 5 (→ 20 bytes), maximum is 15 (→ 60 bytes).
3. **Type of Service (8 bits):** Used for Quality of Service (QoS) marking. Modernly called DSCP/ECN.
4. **Total Length (16 bits):** Length of the **entire datagram** (header + data) in bytes; max = 65,535.
5. **Identification (16 bits):** Unique ID assigned by the source; used to identify fragments of the same datagram during reassembly.
6. **Flags (3 bits):** Bit 0 = Reserved (0); Bit 1 = **DF (Don't Fragment)**; Bit 2 = **MF (More Fragments)**.
7. **Fragment Offset (13 bits):** Position of the fragment's data within the original datagram, in **8-byte units**.
8. **Time To Live — TTL (8 bits):** Hop count limit; decremented at each router; packet discarded when TTL = 0.
9. **Protocol (8 bits):** Identifier of the next-layer protocol encapsulated in the payload (TCP = 6, UDP = 17, ICMP = 1).
10. **Header Checksum (16 bits):** One's complement checksum computed over the IP header only (recomputed at every router).
11. **Source IP Address (32 bits):** IPv4 address of the originating host.
12. **Destination IP Address (32 bits):** IPv4 address of the intended recipient.
13. **Options (0–40 bytes):** Optional fields like Record Route, Timestamp, Loose/Strict Source Routing. Present only when IHL > 5.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute data per fragment.

$$
L_{data\text{-}per\text{-}fragment} = 1500 - 20 = 1480 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute number of fragments.

$$
N = \lceil 4480 / 1480 \rceil = \lceil 3.027 \rceil = 4 \text{ fragments}
$$

Wait — recompute: Original payload = 4500 − 20 = 4480 bytes.

- Fragment 1: 1480 bytes → cumulative = 1480
- Fragment 2: 1480 bytes → cumulative = 2960
- Fragment 3: 1480 bytes → cumulative = 4440
- Fragment 4: 4480 − 4440 = 40 bytes → cumulative = 4480 ✓

**Step 3:** Compute Fragment Offsets.

$$
FO_1 = 0 / 8 = 0
$$
$$
FO_2 = 1480 / 8 = 185
$$
$$
FO_3 = 2960 / 8 = 370
$$
$$
FO_4 = 4440 / 8 = 555
$$

**Step 4:** Assign MF flag values.

| Fragment | Data (B) | Header (B) | Total (B) | Identification | DF | MF | Fragment Offset |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1480 | 20 | 1500 | 1234 (any constant) | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 1480 | 20 | 1500 | 1234 | 0 | 1 | 185 |
| 3 | 1480 | 20 | 1500 | 1234 | 0 | 1 | 370 |
| 4 | 40 | 20 | 60 | 1234 | 0 | 0 | 555 |

**Reassembly Check:**

$$
L_{reassembled} = 1480 + 1480 + 1480 + 40 = 4480 \text{ bytes} = L_{original\text{-}payload} \checkmark
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Explain the **IPv6 header format** in detail. Compare it with the IPv4 header along any **six specification parameters**. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Explain the role of **ICMP**, **IGMP**, and **ARP** specifications in the network layer. State the IP Protocol Number for ICMP and IGMP. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **IPv6 header** (RFC 8200) is fixed at **40 bytes** and contains the following fields:

1. **Version (4 bits):** Always 6 (binary 0110).
2. **Traffic Class (8 bits):** Equivalent to IPv4's ToS/DSCP; used for QoS prioritization.
3. **Flow Label (20 bits):** Identifies a sequence of packets (a "flow") requiring special handling by IPv6 routers (e.g., real-time media).
4. **Payload Length (16 bits):** Length of the payload **following the IPv6 header** (in bytes), including extension headers.
5. **Next Header (8 bits):** Identifies the type of header that follows — either an upper-layer protocol (TCP/UDP/ICMPv6) or an **extension header**.
6. **Hop Limit (8 bits):** Decremented by 1 at each forwarding router; packet is dropped if it reaches 0.
7. **Source Address (128 bits):** Sender's IPv6 address.
8. **Destination Address (128 bits):** Receiver's IPv6 address.

**Six-Parameter Comparison:**

| Parameter | IPv4 | IPv6 |
|---|---|---|
| Header Length | Variable (20–60 bytes) | Fixed 40 bytes |
| Addressing | 32 bits (4.3 × 10⁹ addresses) | 128 bits (3.4 × 10³⁸ addresses) |
| Fragmentation | Routers + Source | **Source only** |
| Header Checksum | Present (recomputed per hop) | **Removed** |
| Options | In header (variable) | **Extension headers (chained)** |
| Security | Optional IPsec | **Native, mandatory IPsec** |

**Model Solution for (b):**

- **ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol — RFC 792):** Used by routers and hosts to send **error and informational messages**. Encapsulated inside IP datagrams using **Protocol Number = 1**. Examples: *Destination Unreachable (Type 3)*, *Time Exceeded (Type 11)* — used by `traceroute`; *Echo Request/Reply (Type 8/0)* — used by `ping`.

- **IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol — RFC 3376):** Used by **hosts to inform their directly attached multicast router** about their membership in multicast groups. Encapsulated inside IP datagrams using **Protocol Number = 2**. Enables efficient multicast routing on a LAN.

- **ARP (Address Resolution Protocol — RFC 826):** Used to **map a known IPv4 address to its corresponding MAC (physical) address** on the local broadcast network (typically Ethernet). Operates by broadcasting an *ARP Request* and receiving a unicast *ARP Reply*. Encapsulated **directly in Ethernet frames** (EtherType 0x0806), not within IP.

> **Protocol Numbers Required by KTU:** ICMP = **1**, IGMP = **2**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
> 1. **Confusing TTL with Hop Count:** TTL is the *initial* value, not the remaining count after each hop. Many students set `FO = bytes/8` correctly but forget that the offset is measured **from the start of the original payload**, not from the previous fragment's end (though they are equivalent if the previous fragment was full-sized).
> 2. **Forgetting the MF flag on the last fragment:** The last fragment's MF bit is **0** (not 1). Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Mixing IPv4 and IPv6 terminology:** Writing "TTL" in an IPv6 answer or "Hop Limit" in IPv4 costs marks. Use the correct term per specification.
> 4. **One's complement checksum error:** Many students forget to take the **one's complement** (bitwise NOT) of the final sum. The result of summing words is **not** the checksum — it must be inverted.
> 5. **Confusing Header Length vs Total Length:** Header Length is in **32-bit words** (multiply IHL by 4 to get bytes); Total Length is **already in bytes**.
> 6. **Not identifying the protocol number:** When asked to identify the next-layer protocol, students write "TCP" but forget to state **"Protocol Number 6"** — both are required.
> 7. **ICMP is NOT in Layer 4:** Examiners mark this strictly. ICMP is a **Network Layer supporting protocol**, not Transport Layer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Flashcard — "Specifications" (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Specification** = formal RFC-defined syntax + semantics + timing of a network protocol.
- ✅ **IPv4 Header:** Variable length, **20 to 60 bytes**, IHL field specifies header in 32-bit words.
- ✅ **IPv6 Header:** **Fixed 40 bytes**, simpler, no checksum, no fragmentation fields in base.
- ✅ **IPv4 Address = 32 bits; IPv6 Address = 128 bits.**
- ✅ **Total Length max = 65,535 bytes** (2¹⁶ − 1); **Fragment Offset max = 65,528 bytes** (2¹³ × 8 − 8).
- ✅ **Fragment Offset unit = 8 bytes** — all fragment data must be aligned to 8-byte multiples (except the last fragment).
- ✅ **Identification field is the SAME** for all fragments of a single datagram.
- ✅ **MF = 1** in all fragments **except the last**; **DF = 1** means "do not fragment this datagram."
- ✅ **TTL / Hop Limit:** Decremented at every router; packet dropped at 0 with **ICMP Time Exceeded**.
- ✅ **Protocol Number 1 = ICMP, 2 = IGMP, 6 = TCP, 17 = UDP, 41 = IPv6-in-IPv4, 58 = ICMPv6.**
- ✅ **Header Checksum:** One's complement of the 16-bit sum of the IP header words; **recomputed at every hop** (IPv4 only).
- ✅ **ARP** is encapsulated directly in Ethernet (not inside IP); **ICMP & IGMP** are encapsulated inside IP.
- ✅ **ICMPv6 combines ICMP + IGMP + ARP** functions — IPv6 uses **NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol)** instead of ARP.
- ✅ **IPv6 Fragmentation** is performed by the **source only** using the **Fragment Extension Header (Next Header = 44)**; routers **do not** fragment.
- ✅ **IPv6 Extension Headers:** Hop-by-Hop (0), Routing (43), Fragment (44), ESP (50), AH (51), Destination Options (60).
- ✅ **Flow Label (20 bits)** is unique to IPv6 and enables true per-flow QoS handling.
- ✅ **IPsec** is **native to IPv6**; **optional in IPv4** (though widely used).
- ✅ **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit)** is the Layer-2 frame payload limit; default Ethernet MTU = **1500 bytes**.
- ✅ **KTU must-know RFCs:** RFC 791 (IPv4), RFC 8200 (IPv6), RFC 792 (ICMP), RFC 826 (ARP), RFC 3376 (IGMPv3).
- ✅ **MTU path discovery** uses ICMP to avoid fragmentation in modern networks (DF flag set + ICMP "Packet Too Big" message).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weighted Resistor Type and R-2R Ladder Type, Analog to Digital Converters: Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Weighted Resistor Type and R-2R Ladder Type, Analog to Digital Converters: Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Digital-to-Analog Converters (DACs) – Foundational Definition

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is an electronic circuit that converts a digital binary code (a discrete set of bits) into a corresponding analog voltage or current signal. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the two classical resistor-network-based DAC architectures are the **Weighted Resistor (Binary Weighted) DAC** and the **R-2R Ladder DAC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PCERT403 covers the hardware-level data conversion circuits used at the **Physical Layer** of computer networks and embedded interfacing — DACs translate processed digital packets into real-world analog signals (audio, video, RF modulation, sensor actuation).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a DAC as a **"digital recipe to liquid volume" converter** in a chemistry lab:
- You give the lab assistant a *recipe* (e.g., 1 spoon + ½ spoon + ¼ spoon).
- The assistant uses cups of *different sizes* (1 cup, ½ cup, ¼ cup) to add liquid.
- The final volume is the **weighted sum** of all the cup contributions.

In electronics:
- The **binary input bits** (b₀, b₁, b₂...) act as ON/OFF switches for the recipe.
- The **resistor values** act as the cup sizes, weighting each bit's contribution.
- The **op-amp summing junction** acts as the "lab assistant" that adds them up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standards Used in This Module:**
> - **V<sub>REF</sub>** = Reference Voltage (typically **+5 V** or **+10 V**, sometimes **±V**)
> - **Resolution (n)** = Number of bits (e.g., **8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit**)
> - **LSB (Least Significant Bit)** = V<sub>REF</sub> / 2<sup>n</sup>
> - **FS (Full Scale)** = (2<sup>n</sup> − 1) × LSB

---

## 1.2 Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs) – Foundational Definition

An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** performs the inverse function — it samples a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal and produces a discrete binary code. The KTU syllabus demands a deep understanding of the **performance specifications** that define the *quality* and *fidelity* of any ADC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition:** ADC specifications are the standardized numerical metrics (resolution, accuracy, linearity, speed, SNR) used by engineers to compare, select, and validate ADC performance for a given application.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **weighing vegetables on a digital scale**:
- The scale has a **resolution** (it can show only 100 g, 200 g, etc., not 150.5 g).
- It has a **conversion time** (the LCD takes 0.5 seconds to display).
- It has an **accuracy** (maybe it always shows 5 g more than actual).
- It has a **quantization error** (the smallest step it can show = ±½ LSB = ±50 g).

Similarly, every ADC has these same characteristic "performance numbers."

> [!NOTE]
> The **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** of an ideal ADC follows the famous formula:
> 
> **SNR = 6.02N + 1.76 dB**, where N is the resolution in bits.

---

## 1.3 Why Two DAC Architectures?

The **Weighted Resistor DAC** is conceptually simple but **physically impractical** for high resolutions (e.g., 12-bit needs a 4096:1 resistor ratio). The **R-2R Ladder DAC** solves this manufacturing nightmare by using only **two resistor values (R and 2R)**, regardless of the bit count.

| Feature | Weighted Resistor | R-2R Ladder |
|---|---|---|
| Resistor values needed | 2<sup>n</sup> different | Only 2 (R and 2R) |
| Manufacturing difficulty | High for n > 8 | Easy (matches IC fab) |
| Accuracy | Highly dependent on absolute values | Highly accurate |
| Speed | Fast (parallel summing) | Slightly slower (ladder settling) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Weighted Resistor DAC — Operational Logic

The Weighted Resistor DAC uses a **summing operational amplifier** configuration. Each bit b<sub>k</sub> of the digital input controls a switch that connects a precision resistor (value **2<sup>k</sup>R** for bit k) to either V<sub>REF</sub> (logic 1) or ground (logic 0).

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **The n-bit binary input** (b<sub>n−1</sub> b<sub>n−2</sub> … b<sub>1</sub> b<sub>0</sub>) is applied to n switches.
2. **MSB switch** connects resistor **2<sup>n−1</sup>R** to V<sub>REF</sub> if b<sub>n−1</sub> = 1, else to ground.
3. **LSB switch** connects resistor **R** to V<sub>REF</sub> if b<sub>0</sub> = 1, else to ground.
4. The op-amp is configured in **inverting summing mode** with feedback resistor R<sub>f</sub> = R.
5. The **output voltage** is the negative weighted sum of the currents flowing into the virtual ground node.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why weighted resistors?** The current through each resistor is V<sub>REF</sub>/R, V<sub>REF</sub>/2R, V<sub>REF</sub>/4R, etc. — naturally binary-scaled.
- **Why inverting amplifier?** Virtual ground eliminates loading errors, and the summing junction algebraically adds the weighted currents.
- **Why is this the "naive" architecture?** Because the **MSB resistor (2<sup>n−1</sup>R)** for a 12-bit DAC must be **2048 times larger** than the LSB resistor — impossible to fabricate on-chip with precision.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output Voltage (Weighted Resistor) | $V_o = -V_{REF} \left( \dfrac{b_0}{2^n} + \dfrac{b_1}{2^{n-1}} + \cdots + \dfrac{b_{n-1}}{2} \right)$ | Volts | b<sub>k</sub> ∈ {0,1} |
| Output Voltage (Compact form) | $V_o = - \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k \cdot 2^k$ | Volts | n = number of bits |
| Full-Scale Output (all 1s) | $V_{FS} = -\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}(2^n - 1)$ | Volts | Approaching −V<sub>REF</sub> |
| LSB Weight | $LSB = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | Volts | Smallest step |
| MSB Resistor Value | $R_{MSB} = 2^{n-1} R$ | Ohms | Physical problem point |
| Current through MSB branch | $I_{MSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{n-1}R}$ | Amperes | Smallest current |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute value notation:** In all formulas above, never use the vertical pipe `|x|` symbol inside the markdown table. The KTU engine uses `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value to avoid breaking table syntax.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Weighted resistor DACs are used in:
- **Low-cost audio playback** in legacy toys
- **Reference voltage generators** in test equipment
- **Educational lab trainers** (due to circuit simplicity)

However, they are **NOT** used in modern high-resolution ICs because resistor matching beyond 8 bits is impractical.

---

## 2.2 R-2R Ladder DAC — Operational Logic

The **R-2R Ladder DAC** eliminates the wide range of resistor values by using only **two precision resistor values: R and 2R**. The trick is to use a ladder network where each node presents the same equivalent resistance (R) when looking into it from either direction.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. Each bit b<sub>k</sub> controls a switch that connects the corresponding **2R branch** to either V<sub>REF</sub> or ground.
2. The ladder network acts as a **binary voltage divider** at each node.
3. The op-amp **buffer** (configured as a unity-gain amplifier or with scaling) sums the ladder outputs.
4. Because of the ladder's unique property, the contribution of each bit is **halved at each successive node**, achieving the binary weighting.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why does the ladder halve the voltage at each node?** Because at every node, looking left or right, you see R in parallel with 2R (after one Thevenin reduction) = 2R/3 + R/3 = R, which then drops voltage by ½.
- **Why use only two resistor values?** IC fabrication uses **photolithographic processes** that match resistors of the same width perfectly. Having only R and 2R means **all resistors are perfectly matched by design** — no manual trimming required.
- **Why is it slightly slower?** The ladder network has parasitic capacitance, requiring a **settling time** before the output stabilizes.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output Voltage (R-2R) | $V_o = -V_{REF} \left( \dfrac{b_0}{2^n} + \dfrac{b_1}{2^{n-1}} + \cdots + \dfrac{b_{n-1}}{2} \right)$ | Volts | **Same math as Weighted!** |
| Equivalent Ladder Resistance | $R_{eq} = R$ | Ohms | Constant at every node |
| Number of unique resistors | 2 (R and 2R) | — | Independent of n |
| Total resistors required | 2n | — | n for R, n for 2R |
| Switching transient | ~RC time constant | Seconds | Limits speed |
| LSB Voltage | $LSB = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | Volts | Resolution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** Both Weighted and R-2R DACs produce the **same output equation**. The difference is purely in **physical implementation** — not in mathematics. KTU examiners love asking "Why is R-2R preferred for IC fabrication?"

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio DACs** in smartphones, sound cards (e.g., ESS Sabre, TI PCM series)
- **Video DACs** in legacy graphics cards
- **Function generators** and arbitrary waveform generators
- **Industrial process control** (4–20 mA current loop DACs)

---

## 2.3 ADC Specifications — Deep Theoretical Analysis

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires a thorough understanding of ADC specifications. These define the **fitness for purpose** of any ADC in a real system.

### Critical ADC Specifications:

| Specification | Definition | Formula / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Resolution (N)** | Number of discrete output codes | 2<sup>N</sup> codes |
| **Quantization Step (Q)** | Smallest voltage change detectable | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$ |
| **Quantization Error** | Maximum deviation from ideal | $\pm \dfrac{Q}{2} = \pm \dfrac{1}{2} LSB$ |
| **SNR (theoretical)** | Signal-to-noise ratio for ideal ADC | $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB |
| **ENOB** | Effective number of bits | $ENOB = \dfrac{SNR - 1.76}{6.02}$ |
| **Conversion Time (t<sub>c</sub>)** | Time to complete one conversion | Depends on architecture |
| **Sampling Rate (f<sub>s</sub>)** | Samples per second | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{t_c}$ |
| **Aperture Time (t<sub>ap</sub>)** | Time to acquire input sample | Track-and-hold dependent |
| **INL** | Integral Non-Linearity | Max deviation of actual from ideal |
| **DNL** | Differential Non-Linearity | Deviation between adjacent codes |
| **Aperture Jitter (t<sub>j</sub>)** | Sampling time uncertainty | $V_{error} = 2\pi f_{in} V_{FS} t_j$ |
| **Power Dissipation** | Total power consumed | mW or µW |
| **Input Range** | Allowed analog input span | Typically 0 to V<sub>REF</sub> |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6.02N + 1.76 dB formula** is the **golden equation** of ADC theory. KTU examiners ask derivations of this formula. It comes from the ratio of RMS signal power to RMS quantization noise power (assumed uniform distribution).

### Why These Specifications Matter

- **Resolution** determines the **finest granularity** of measurement.
- **Quantization error** is the **inherent, irreducible noise** of any ADC.
- **Conversion time** determines the **maximum input frequency** (via Nyquist: f<sub>in</sub> < f<sub>s</sub>/2).
- **SNR/ENOB** quantifies **real-world fidelity** lost to noise, distortion, and non-linearity.
- **Aperture jitter** becomes the **dominant error source** at high input frequencies.

### Real-World Utility

- **Audio CDs:** 16-bit, 44.1 kHz → SNR ≈ 98 dB
- **Smartphone cameras:** 12–108 MP ADCs with 10–14 bit pipeline ADCs
- **Oscilloscopes:** 8–12 bit ADCs at gigasample-per-second rates
- **Medical ECG:** 16–24 bit sigma-delta ADCs for µV-level signals
