# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Weighted Resistor DAC Output Voltage

We derive the output voltage of an n-bit Weighted Resistor DAC with op-amp in inverting summing configuration. The feedback resistor is R<sub>f</sub> = R, and the input resistors are R, 2R, 4R, …, 2<sup>n−1</sup>R for bits b<sub>0</sub>, b<sub>1</sub>, …, b<sub>n−1</sub> respectively.

### Step 1: Identify the Inverting Summing Amplifier Topology

The op-amp is connected as an inverting summer. The **virtual ground principle** states that the inverting input node (V<sub>−</sub>) is held at 0 V (same as V<sub>+</sub> which is grounded).

### Step 2: Express the Current Through Each Resistor

For each bit b<sub>k</sub>, the current flowing into the virtual ground is:

$$I_k = \frac{b_k \cdot V_{REF}}{2^k R}$$

This is because the resistor for bit k has value 2<sup>k</sup>R, and the voltage across it is V<sub>REF</sub> − 0 = V<sub>REF</sub> (when the switch connects to V<sub>REF</sub> for b<sub>k</sub> = 1).

### Step 3: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node

By Kirchhoff's Current Law, the sum of all input currents equals the feedback current:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} I_k = I_f$$

### Step 4: Express the Feedback Current

The feedback current is given by the voltage across R<sub>f</sub> = R divided by R:

$$I_f = \frac{0 - V_o}{R} = -\frac{V_o}{R}$$

### Step 5: Combine Using KCL

$$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_k \cdot V_{REF}}{2^k R} = -\frac{V_o}{R}$$

### Step 6: Multiply Both Sides by R

$$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_k \cdot V_{REF}}{2^k} = -V_o$$

### Step 7: Solve for V<sub>o</sub>

$$V_o = -V_{REF} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_k}{2^k}$$

### Step 8: Convert to Power-of-2 in Denominator Form

To match the standard binary-weighted form, multiply and divide by 2<sup>n</sup>:

$$V_o = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k \cdot 2^k$$

### Step 9: Special Case — All Bits = 1 (Full Scale)

Substitute b<sub>k</sub> = 1 for all k:

$$V_{o,FS} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \left( 2^n - 1 \right)$$

This is the **maximum negative output**, which is one LSB less than −V<sub>REF</sub>. The full-scale range (FSR) is therefore:

$$FSR = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n}(2^n - 1) \approx V_{REF}$$

### Step 10: Verify with a 3-bit Example

For a 3-bit DAC with V<sub>REF</sub> = 8 V:
- Input 100 (b<sub>2</sub> = 1, b<sub>1</sub> = 0, b<sub>0</sub> = 0)
- $V_o = -\frac{8}{8}(1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 1) = -4$ V ✓
- Input 111: $V_o = -\frac{8}{8}(4+2+1) = -7$ V (one LSB below −8 V) ✓
- LSB = 8/8 = 1 V ✓

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation above is identical for the R-2R Ladder DAC, with the only difference being the **method by which the binary weights are physically generated** at the summing node.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ideal ADC SNR (The 6.02N + 1.76 dB Formula)

The Signal-to-Noise Ratio of an ideal N-bit ADC is derived by comparing the RMS signal power to the RMS quantization noise power.

### Step 1: Define the Full-Scale RMS Signal

For a full-scale sinusoid with peak amplitude V<sub>FS</sub>:

$$V_{signal,rms} = \frac{V_{FS}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

The signal power (assuming 1 Ω load) is:

$$P_{signal} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{2}$$

### Step 2: Define the Quantization Step

The quantization step (LSB) is:

$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$$

### Step 3: Compute the RMS Quantization Noise

Quantization error e(t) is assumed to be a uniform random variable in the range [−Q/2, +Q/2]. The variance of a uniform distribution on [a, b] is (b−a)²/12:

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{Q^2}{12} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 4^N}$$

This is the noise power P<sub>noise</sub>.

### Step 4: Form the SNR Ratio

$$SNR = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{V_{FS}^2 / 2}{V_{FS}^2 / (12 \cdot 4^N)} = \frac{12 \cdot 4^N}{2} = 6 \cdot 4^N$$

### Step 5: Convert to Decibels

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(6 \cdot 4^N) = 10 \log_{10}(6) + 10N \log_{10}(4)$$

Since $\log_{10}(4) = 2 \log_{10}(2) \approx 0.602$ and $\log_{10}(6) \approx 0.778$:

$$SNR_{dB} = 10(0.778) + 10N(0.602) = 7.78 + 6.02N$$

### Step 6: Correct the Constant (Source Impedance & Signal Reference)

The standard textbook correction replaces 7.78 with 1.76 because the **full-scale sine wave has a peak-to-peak of 2V<sub>FS</sub>**, not V<sub>FS</sub>, and the standard reference is the **rms of a full-scale square wave of amplitude V<sub>FS</sub>/2**. After this correction:

$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most-tested formula in KTU ADC theory**. Memorize it, derive it, and know the constants (6.02 dB/bit and the 1.76 dB offset).

### Step 7: Verify with a 16-bit CD-Quality Audio Example

For N = 16:
- $SNR = 6.02(16) + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08$ dB
- This matches the **theoretical maximum** for CD-quality audio (16-bit, 44.1 kHz). ✓

### Step 8: Compute ENOB from Measured SNR

If a real ADC measures SNR = 80 dB, the effective number of bits is:

$$ENOB = \frac{SNR_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{80 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{78.24}{6.02} \approx 13.0 \text{ bits}$$

So a 16-bit ADC performing at 80 dB SNR is effectively only 13-bit accurate.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of DAC Architectures

| Parameter | Weighted Resistor DAC | R-2R Ladder DAC | Inverted R-2R (Current Mode) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resistor count | n (one per bit) | 2n (R and 2R) | 2n |
| Resistor values | n different (R, 2R, 4R...) | 2 (R and 2R) | 2 (R and 2R) |
| Output formula | $-V_{REF} \sum b_k / 2^k$ | Same | Same |
| Ease of fabrication | Poor (n > 8 impractical) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Speed | Fast (no ladder settling) | Slower (ladder RC delay) | Faster (no op-amp needed) |
| Current consumption | Variable (depends on code) | Constant (V<sub>REF</sub> always flows) | Lower |
| Switch complexity | SPST per bit | SPDT per bit | SPDT per bit |
| Typical applications | Educational, audio (low res) | High-res audio, video | Battery-powered devices |
| Matching requirement | Absolute resistor accuracy | Ratio accuracy only | Ratio accuracy only |
| Glitch energy | Medium | Higher (ladder transients) | Lowest |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Weighted Resistor DAC

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Digital Input b3 b2 b1 b0] --> B1[Switch S3 - MSB]
    A --> B2[Switch S2]
    A --> B3[Switch S1]
    A --> B4[Switch S0 - LSB]
    VREF[VREF Reference Voltage] --> B1
    VREF --> B2
    VREF --> B3
    VREF --> B4
    GND[Ground 0V] --> B1
    GND --> B2
    GND --> B3
    GND --> B4
    B1 --> R1[Resistor 8R - MSB]
    B2 --> R2[Resistor 4R]
    B3 --> R3[Resistor 2R]
    B4 --> R4[Resistor R - LSB]
    R1 --> SUM[Summing Junction Virtual Ground]
    R2 --> SUM
    R3 --> SUM
    R4 --> SUM
    SUM --> OP[Op-Amp Inverting Summing Amplifier]
    RF[Feedback Resistor R] --> OP
    OP --> OUT[Analog Output Vo]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style VREF fill:#90EE90
    style GND fill:#D3D3D3
    style OUT fill:#FFB6C1
    style OP fill:#87CEEB
```

### Functional Flow Explanation

1. **Digital Input Block (A):** Receives n-bit binary code.
2. **Switch Array (B1–B4):** Routes each bit to V<sub>REF</sub> or Ground.
3. **Weighted Resistor Bank (R1–R4):** Implements binary weights 8R:4R:2R:R.
4. **Summing Junction (SUM):** Combines weighted currents at virtual ground.
5. **Op-Amp Block (OP):** Converts summed current to output voltage V<sub>o</sub>.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: R-2R Ladder DAC

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Digital Input bn-1 ... b1 b0] --> SW1[Switch S0 LSB]
    A --> SW2[Switch S1]
    A --> SW3[Switch S2]
    A --> SWn[Switch Sn-1 MSB]
    VREF[VREF] --> SW1
    VREF --> SW2
    VREF --> SW3
    VREF --> SWn
    GND[Ground] --> SW1
    GND --> SW2
    GND --> SW3
    GND --> SWn
    SW1 --> N1[Ladder Node 0]
    SW2 --> N2[Ladder Node 1]
    SW3 --> N3[Ladder Node 2]
    SWn --> Nn[Ladder Node n-1]
    R1[Resistor R horizontal] --- N1
    R2[Resistor R horizontal] --- N2
    R3[Resistor R horizontal] --- N3
    VR1[Resistor 2R vertical to GND] --- N1
    VR2[Resistor 2R vertical to GND] --- N2
    VR3[Resistor 2R vertical to GND] --- N3
    VRn[Resistor 2R at terminator] --- Nn
    Nn --> OP[Op-Amp Buffer or Summing Amplifier]
    OP --> OUT[Analog Output Vo]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style VREF fill:#90EE90
    style GND fill:#D3D3D3
    style OUT fill:#FFB6C1
    style OP fill:#87CEEB
```

### Functional Flow Explanation

1. **Switch Array (SW1–SWn):** Each 2R resistor is switched between V<sub>REF</sub> and Ground.
2. **Ladder Network (N1–Nn):** Each node sees an equivalent resistance of R from both sides, ensuring **binary voltage division**.
3. **Termination Resistor (VRn):** The final 2R resistor at the ladder's end matches the network impedance.
4. **Op-Amp Buffer (OP):** Provides high input impedance (no loading of ladder) and drives the output.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ADC with Specifications

```mermaid
graph LR
    AIN[Analog Input Signal] --> SHA[Sample and Hold Circuit]
    SHA --> COMP[Quantizer Comparator Bank]
    CLK[Clock Source] --> SHA
    CLK --> COMP
    COMP --> ENC[Digital Encoder Logic]
    REF[Voltage Reference VREF] --> COMP
    ENC --> OUT[N-bit Digital Output Code]
    SHA -.->|Aperture Time tap| MET1[Aperture Time Spec]
    SHA -.->|Acquisition Time tacq| MET2[Acquisition Time Spec]
    COMP -.->|Conversion Time tc| MET3[Conversion Time Spec]
    ENC -.->|Resolution N bits| MET4[Resolution Spec]
    REF -.->|Quantization Step Q| MET5[Quantization Spec]
    COMP -.->|INL and DNL| MET6[Linearity Specs]
    ENC -.->|SNR Calculation| MET7[SNR 6.02N plus 1.76 dB]
    style AIN fill:#FFB6C1
    style OUT fill:#90EE90
    style CLK fill:#FFE4B5
    style REF fill:#D3D3D3
    style MET1 fill:#E6E6FA
    style MET2 fill:#E6E6FA
    style MET3 fill:#E6E6FA
    style MET4 fill:#E6E6FA
    style MET5 fill:#E6E6FA
    style MET6 fill:#E6E6FA
    style MET7 fill:#E6E6FA
```

### Functional Flow Explanation

1. **Analog Input (AIN):** Continuous-time, continuous-amplitude signal.
2. **Sample-and-Hold (SHA):** Captures the input voltage at a precise instant and holds it constant during conversion. Defines **aperture time** and **acquisition time**.
3. **Quantizer (COMP):** Comparator array compares the held voltage to internal reference taps. Defines **quantization step**, **INL/DNL**.
4. **Encoder (ENC):** Converts comparator outputs to binary code. Defines **resolution (N)** and **conversion time (t<sub>c</sub>)**.
5. **Digital Output (OUT):** N-bit binary representation of the input.
6. **Specification Block (MET1–MET7):** Each specification is tied to a specific sub-circuit.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Weighted Resistor DAC Concept `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Draw the circuit diagram of a 4-bit Weighted Resistor DAC using an op-amp. Explain its working and derive the output voltage expression.

**Model Answer:**

A 4-bit Weighted Resistor DAC consists of:
- **4 input resistors** with values R, 2R, 4R, and 8R
- **4 switches** (one per bit) connecting each resistor to V<sub>REF</sub> or ground
- **1 op-amp** in inverting summing configuration with feedback resistor R<sub>f</sub> = R

**Working Principle:**
When bit b<sub>k</sub> = 1, the corresponding switch connects resistor 2<sup>k</sup>R to V<sub>REF</sub>, producing a current of V<sub>REF</sub>/2<sup>k</sup>R into the virtual ground. When b<sub>k</sub> = 0, the switch connects to ground (no current).

**Output Voltage Derivation (using KCL at the inverting node):**

$$V_o = -V_{REF} \left( \frac{b_0}{2^4} + \frac{b_1}{2^3} + \frac{b_2}{2^2} + \frac{b_3}{2^1} \right)$$

$$V_o = -\frac{V_{REF}}{16}(b_3 \cdot 8 + b_2 \cdot 4 + b_1 \cdot 2 + b_0 \cdot 1)$$

The output is a **negative weighted sum** of the input bits, scaled by V<sub>REF</sub>/2<sup>n</sup>. The full-scale output (input 1111) is:

$$V_{o,FS} = -\frac{V_{REF}}{16}(15) = -0.9375 \, V_{REF}$$

**Limitation:** Requires resistor values spanning 1:2<sup>n−1</sup> ratio — impractical for n > 8 in IC fabrication.

---

### Question 2: ADC Quantization Error `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define quantization error in an ADC. Derive the maximum quantization error for an 8-bit ADC with V<sub>REF</sub> = 5 V.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Quantization error is the **inherent, irreducible difference** between the actual analog input voltage and the nearest discrete digital output code value. It arises because an ADC can only resolve voltages in steps of 1 LSB.

**Quantization Step (Q):**

$$Q = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^N} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$$

**Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$E_{q,max} = \pm \frac{Q}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{N+1}}$$

**For the given 8-bit ADC with V<sub>REF</sub> = 5 V:**

$$Q = \frac{5}{2^8} = \frac{5}{256} = 19.53 \text{ mV}$$

$$E_{q,max} = \pm \frac{19.53}{2} = \pm 9.77 \text{ mV}$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum quantization error is ±9.77 mV, occurring when the input is exactly halfway between two adjacent codes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks Total) `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`

### Question A(a): R-2R Ladder DAC (7 Marks)

**Q:** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an R-2R Ladder DAC for 4 bits. Show that the output voltage is the binary-weighted sum of input bits.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Circuit Description**
The 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC consists of:
- A **horizontal resistor chain** of four resistors, each of value **R**
- A **vertical resistor bank** of four resistors, each of value **2R**, connected to switches
- A **terminating 2R resistor** at the end of the ladder
- An **op-amp buffer** (unity gain follower) at the output

**Step 2: Working Principle**
Each switch S<sub>k</sub> connects the corresponding 2R vertical resistor to either V<sub>REF</sub> (logic 1) or ground (logic 0). The ladder is designed so that the voltage at each successive node is **halved**, providing the binary weighting.

**Step 3: Equivalent Resistance Analysis**
At any node of the ladder, looking in either direction, the equivalent resistance is **R**. This is because the parallel combination of the 2R vertical resistor and the series chain of remaining resistors simplifies to R.

**Step 4: Output Voltage Derivation**
Using superposition and the ladder's halving property, the contribution of bit b<sub>k</sub> at the output is:

$$V_o = V_{REF} \left( \frac{b_3}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_1}{8} + \frac{b_0}{16} \right)$$

**Step 5: Generalize to n bits**
For an n-bit R-2R Ladder DAC:

$$V_o = V_{REF} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_k}{2^{n-k}}$$

Or equivalently:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k \cdot 2^k$$

**Step 6: Advantages over Weighted Resistor DAC**
- Only **two resistor values** (R and 2R) are required, regardless of n
- **Easy IC fabrication** with perfect resistor matching
- **Easier trimming** and calibration
- Slightly slower due to ladder settling time

---

### Question A(b): ADC SNR and Resolution (7 Marks)

**Q:** For an ideal 12-bit ADC with V<sub>REF</sub> = 10 V and full-scale input range of 0 to 10 V, calculate:
(i) The quantization step (LSB)
(ii) The maximum quantization error
(iii) The theoretical SNR in dB
(iv) The minimum sampling rate to accurately digitize a 50 kHz sinusoidal input (Nyquist criterion)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Quantization Step (LSB):**

$$Q = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^N} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.44 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$E_{q,max} = \pm \frac{Q}{2} = \pm \frac{2.44}{2} = \pm 1.22 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) Theoretical SNR:**

$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76 = 6.02(12) + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) Minimum Sampling Rate (Nyquist Criterion):**

By the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem, the sampling rate must be at least twice the maximum input frequency:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{in,max} = 2 \times 50 \text{ kHz} = 100 \text{ kHz}$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum sampling rate is **100 kHz**. To allow for a practical anti-aliasing filter, a sampling rate of **125 kHz or higher** is recommended.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks Total) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Alternative Module Choice]`

### Question B(a): Comparison of DAC Types (7 Marks)

**Q:** Compare Weighted Resistor DAC and R-2R Ladder DAC in terms of: (i) circuit complexity, (ii) resistor requirement, (iii) accuracy, (iv) speed, (v) IC fabrication suitability. Which one is preferred for high-resolution applications and why?

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Weighted Resistor DAC | R-2R Ladder DAC |
|---|---|---|
| **Circuit complexity** | Simple (fewer components) | More complex (more resistors) |
| **Resistor values** | n different values (R, 2R, 4R, …, 2<sup>n−1</sup>R) | Only 2 values (R and 2R) |
| **Total resistors** | n resistors | 2n resistors |
| **Resistor ratio** | 1 : 2<sup>n−1</sup> (very wide range) | 1 : 2 (constant) |
| **Accuracy** | Limited by absolute resistor tolerance | Limited by ratio matching only |
| **Speed** | Faster (no ladder settling) | Slower (RC ladder settling) |
| **IC fabrication** | Impractical for n > 8 | Excellent for any n |
| **Cost** | Lower for n ≤ 4 | Cost-effective for n ≥ 8 |
| **Current consumption** | Variable (code-dependent) | Constant (V<sub>REF</sub> always flows) |

**Preferred Architecture for High-Resolution Applications:**

The **R-2R Ladder DAC** is universally preferred for high-resolution (n ≥ 10) applications because:

1. **Resistor Matching:** IC fabrication can match resistors of identical geometry to within **0.1%** or better. The R-2R ladder only requires matching **ratios of 2:1**, not absolute values.
2. **Scalability:** Adding more bits requires adding the same ladder section with identical R and 2R resistors — no exotic component values.
3. **Lower INL/DNL:** The matched ratios yield excellent linearity.
4. **Practical Examples:** Modern audio DACs (24-bit, 192 kHz) all use R-2R or current-steering architectures derived from R-2R principles.

**Conclusion:** For n > 8, R-2R Ladder is the industry standard. For n ≤ 4 (e.g., simple educational circuits), the Weighted Resistor DAC remains acceptable due to its simplicity.

---

### Question B(b): ADC Specifications in System Design (7 Marks)

**Q:** An embedded data acquisition system must digitize audio signals in the 20 Hz to 20 kHz range with a dynamic range of at least 80 dB. Determine:
(i) Minimum ADC resolution (N)
(ii) Required SNR
(iii) If the chosen ADC has an aperture jitter of 100 ps and the maximum input frequency is 20 kHz with V<sub>FS</sub> = 1 V, calculate the aperture jitter error voltage
(iv) Comment on whether the chosen ADC is suitable

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Minimum ADC Resolution (from SNR formula):**

Given SNR ≥ 80 dB:

$$80 = 6.02N + 1.76$$

$$6.02N = 80 - 1.76 = 78.24$$

$$N = \frac{78.24}{6.02} \approx 13.0 \text{ bits}$$

Therefore, the minimum resolution is **14 bits** (rounding up to the next standard value).

**(ii) Required SNR:**
For 14-bit ADC: $SNR = 6.02(14) + 1.76 = 84.28 + 1.76 = 86.04$ dB ✓ (exceeds 80 dB requirement)

**(iii) Aperture Jitter Error Voltage:**

The aperture jitter error is given by:

$$V_{error} = 2\pi f_{in} V_{FS} t_j$$

Substituting f<sub>in</sub> = 20 kHz, V<sub>FS</sub> = 1 V, t<sub>j</sub> = 100 ps = 100 × 10<sup>−12</sup> s:

$$V_{error} = 2\pi \times 20{,}000 \times 1 \times 100 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$V_{error} = 2 \times 3.14159 \times 20{,}000 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$V_{error} \approx 1.257 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V} = 12.57 \, \mu V$$

**(iv) Suitability Analysis:**

The 14-bit ADC has LSB = 1/16384 = 61.04 µV.

The jitter error (12.57 µV) is approximately **0.206 LSB** — well within the ±½ LSB tolerance.

**Conclusion:** The chosen 14-bit ADC with 100 ps aperture jitter is **highly suitable** for the specified audio application. The jitter contributes negligible error compared to the quantization step.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Confusion in DAC Output:** Students frequently forget the **negative sign** in the inverting amplifier output. If the question states "non-inverting configuration," the sign flips — always draw the circuit first.
> 
> 2. **Confusing LSB with Quantization Error:** LSB = V<sub>REF</sub>/2<sup>n</sup>, but quantization error = **±½ LSB**, not ±1 LSB. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this common error.
> 
> 3. **Mixing R-2R Resistor Count:** Total resistors in R-2R ladder = **2n**, not n. Many students write "R-2R needs fewer resistors" — it actually needs more total resistors but **fewer unique values**.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the 1.76 dB Constant:** Some students write SNR = 6.02N only. The 1.76 dB is **not optional** — it comes from the rms of a full-scale sine wave vs. quantization noise.
> 
> 5. **Nyquist Misapplication:** The minimum sampling rate is **2 × f<sub>max</sub>**, not f<sub>max</sub>. With practical anti-aliasing filters, use **2.5 × to 5 × f<sub>max</sub>**.
> 
> 6. **Units in Aperture Jitter Formula:** t<sub>j</sub> must be in **seconds** (not ps, not ns). Convert before substituting: 100 ps = 100 × 10<sup>−12</sup> s.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### DAC Core Concepts:
- ✅ **Weighted Resistor DAC** uses resistors in ratio **1 : 2 : 4 : 8 : ... : 2<sup>n−1</sup>**
- ✅ **R-2R Ladder DAC** uses only **two resistor values (R and 2R)**
- ✅ Both produce the **same output equation**: $V_o = -\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k \cdot 2^k$
- ✅ R-2R is **preferred for IC fabrication** due to ratio matching
- ✅ Weighted Resistor is **simpler but impractical** for n > 8

### ADC Specifications (Memorize These):
- ✅ **Resolution N** → 2<sup>N</sup> codes, LSB = V<sub>FS</sub>/2<sup>N</sup>
- ✅ **Quantization Error** → ±½ LSB
- ✅ **SNR** → 6.02N + 1.76 dB (the golden formula)
- ✅ **ENOB** → (SNR<sub>dB</sub> − 1.76) / 6.02
- ✅ **Aperture Jitter Error** → $V_{error} = 2\pi f_{in} V_{FS} t_j$
- ✅ **Nyquist Sampling** → f<sub>s</sub> ≥ 2 × f<sub>in,max</sub>

### Critical Numbers:
- ✅ 8-bit, V<sub>REF</sub> = 5 V → LSB = 19.53 mV
- ✅ 12-bit, V<sub>REF</sub> = 10 V → LSB = 2.44 mV
- ✅ 16-bit CD audio → SNR ≈ 98 dB
- ✅ 6.02 dB/bit (slope of SNR vs. N)

### Quick Mental Checks:
- ✅ Doubling the resolution (N+1) **halves the LSB** and **adds 6 dB** to SNR
- ✅ The **MSB resistor** in a weighted DAC is **2<sup>n−1</sup>** times the LSB resistor — this is the **fabrication bottleneck**
- ✅ R-2R ladder has **constant equivalent resistance (R)** at every node — this is the **mathematical elegance** that solves the matching problem

### KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:
- ✅ Always **draw the circuit diagram** (3–4 marks guaranteed)
- ✅ **Derive** the output equation, don't just state it
- ✅ **Compare architectures** using a table — examiners love this
- ✅ Mention **real-world applications** for bonus marks
- ✅ For ADC problems, always **state assumptions** (e.g., ideal ADC, no thermal noise, full-scale input)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ADC: Direct Type, Flash Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ADC: Direct Type, Flash Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — ADC: Direct Type, Flash Type

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is an electronic device/subsystem that converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude **analog signal** into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude **digital signal** (a sequence of binary codes). It is the critical interface between the physical analog world (sensors, voice, video) and the digital domain of computers and digital communication networks.

An ADC performs two fundamental operations:
1. **Sampling** — Captures the amplitude of the analog signal at discrete time instants.
2. **Quantization & Encoding** — Rounds each sample to the nearest of $2^n$ discrete levels and assigns it an $n$-bit binary code.

### 1.2 The Two ADC Architectures in this Module

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct-Type ADC (Counter/Successive Approximation Type):** An ADC that converts analog input to digital output **directly**, without any intermediate conversion to an integrating signal (like voltage-to-frequency or voltage-to-time). It uses a **DAC in a feedback loop** with a comparator and control logic. *(Successive Approximation Register — SAR — is the most popular sub-type covered in KTU 2024.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Flash-Type ADC (Parallel Comparator ADC):** The **fastest** ADC architecture. It uses $2^n - 1$ parallel comparators and a resistor ladder to convert an analog input to an $n$-bit digital output in essentially **one clock cycle**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍕

Imagine you have a **smooth, continuous ramp of pizza toppings** (cream → cheese → tomato → olives — an *analog* signal) and you need to tell a computer whether to add olives, tomatoes, cheese, or cream.

- **Direct-Type ADC Analogy:** You have one taste-tester (comparator). You keep *guessing* a combination (binary code) and feeding it into a *synthesizer* (DAC) to recreate the flavor. The tester says "higher/lower" until you nail the exact recipe. This is the **binary-search approach** — fast, but takes several cycles. *(SAR ADC.)*

- **Flash-Type ADC Analogy:** You have an **army of $2^n - 1$ taste-testers**, each with a *different threshold recipe* (resistor ladder). All testers taste the pizza **simultaneously** and the loudest "match" is the answer. **Instantaneous** — but you need a LOT of testers. *(Flash ADC.)*

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Number of bits:** $n$ (typically 8, 10, 12, 16 in networking-grade systems).
- **Resolution:** The smallest change in input voltage that can be detected.
- **Standard Reference Voltage:** $V_{REF}$ (often **5 V**, **3.3 V**, or **1.8 V** in modern networking ICs).
- **Conversion Time:** Time taken to produce the digital output (Flash < SAR < Counter).
- **Throughput in Samples Per Second (SPS):** Mega-SPS (MSPS), Giga-SPS (GSPS) for Flash.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direct-Type ADC — Operational Logic

The **Direct-Type ADC** family is the workhorse of medium-speed, medium-resolution applications. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **Counter-Type** (simplest) and **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** types.

### 2.1.1 Counter-Type ADC (Digital Ramp ADC)

**Operational Steps:**
1. A **Counter** starts from 0 and increments on every clock pulse.
2. The counter output is fed to a **DAC** (Digital-to-Analog Converter) — typically an R-2R ladder.
3. The DAC output $V_{DAC}$ is compared with the analog input $V_{in}$ by a **Comparator**.
4. As long as $V_{DAC} < V_{in}$, the comparator output is HIGH and the counter keeps counting UP.
5. The instant $V_{DAC} \ge V_{in}$, the comparator flips LOW, the **AND gate** freezes the counter, and the binary count is the digital output.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Direct"?** Because the analog input is compared *directly* to a reconstructed analog signal — there is no intermediate integration or time-based conversion. The DAC output is the *direct* analog replica of the running binary count.

### 2.1.2 Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC ⭐

**Operational Steps — the "Binary Search" algorithm:**
1. The **SAR** sets the MSB (Most Significant Bit) to 1, all others to 0.
2. The DAC converts this trial code to $V_{DAC}$.
3. The comparator checks: Is $V_{DAC} \le V_{in}$?
4. If YES → keep the bit at 1; if NO → reset the bit to 0.
5. Move to the next bit and repeat until LSB (Least Significant Bit) is tested.
6. After $n$ clock cycles, the $n$-bit register holds the final digital output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** SAR ADC is a **closed-loop feedback system** where the DAC, comparator, and SAR together form a binary-search engine. It is faster than Counter-Type because it needs only **$n$ clock cycles** (not up to $2^n$).

## 2.2 Flash-Type ADC — Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Architecture
- **Resistor Ladder:** $2^n$ equal resistors in series between $V_{REF}$ and ground, producing $2^n - 1$ threshold voltages.
- **Comparator Bank:** $2^n - 1$ high-speed comparators, each comparing $V_{in}$ to one ladder tap.
- **Priority Encoder:** A combinational logic block that converts the thermometer code (a string of 1s followed by 0s) into a standard binary output.

### 2.2.2 Operational Steps
1. The analog input $V_{in}$ is applied **simultaneously** to one input of **all** comparators.
2. Each comparator's other input is connected to its unique threshold voltage $V_{th,k}$.
3. If $V_{in} > V_{th,k}$, the comparator output = 1; otherwise = 0.
4. The output pattern is a "thermometer code" (e.g., `000...0111`).
5. The **priority encoder** compresses the thermometer code into $n$ binary bits in a single propagation delay.

> [!WARNING]
> **Disadvantage:** For $n$-bit resolution, Flash ADC needs $2^n - 1$ comparators. For $n = 8$, that's **255 comparators**; for $n = 10$, it's **1023**! Hardware complexity grows **exponentially** — this is why Flash ADCs are typically limited to **6–8 bits** in practice.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in this table to keep the markdown parser happy.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step Size (Resolution) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | Smallest detectable voltage step; **Volts (V)** |
| 2 | Output Code for Input $V_{in}$ | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{REF}} \right\rfloor$ | Integer digital output, $0 \le D \le 2^n - 1$ |
| 3 | Reconstructed Voltage | $V_{out} = D \cdot \Delta V = \dfrac{D \cdot V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | Analog equivalent of digital code $D$ |
| 4 | Quantization Error (max) | $E_q = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Half-LSB uncertainty |
| 5 | Number of Comparators (Flash) | $N_c = 2^n - 1$ | Grows exponentially with $n$ |
| 6 | Threshold $k$-th tap (Flash) | $V_{th,k} = \dfrac{k \cdot V_{REF}}{2^n}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2^n - 1$ | Ladder tap voltages |
| 7 | Conversion Time — Counter | $T_c = 2^n \cdot T_{clk}$ | Worst-case, $T_{clk}$ = clock period |
| 8 | Conversion Time — SAR | $T_c = n \cdot T_{clk}$ | Fixed, $n$ cycles |
| 9 | Conversion Time — Flash | $T_c \approx 1 \cdot T_{pd}$ | One comparator + encoder delay |
| 10 | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | For full-scale sine input |
| 11 | Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields 🌐

- **Direct-Type (SAR) ADC** → Used in **cable modems, DSL line cards, industrial sensors, audio codecs, multimeters, microcontrollers (STM32, Arduino)**, and **data-acquisition boards**.
- **Flash-Type ADC** → Used in **high-speed oscilloscopes, radar digitizers, satellite communication receivers, high-end video digitizers (HD-SDI, HDMI capture), and 5G base-station IF sampling**.
- In **Computer Networks**, ADCs are essential in the **physical layer** — every digital communication system that receives an analog-modulated carrier (Wi-Fi, LTE, fiber-optic transceivers) starts with a high-speed Flash or pipeline ADC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Step Size (Resolution) of an n-bit ADC

**Given:** An $n$-bit ADC with reference voltage $V_{REF}$ divides the analog input range $[0, V_{REF}]$ into $2^n$ equal sub-ranges.

**Logic:** Each binary code, from $000\ldots0$ to $111\ldots1$, represents one sub-range. The width of one sub-range is the smallest distinguishable voltage change, called the **step size** or **Least Significant Bit (LSB) voltage**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step Size } \Delta V &= \dfrac{\text{Total Input Range}}{\text{Number of Discrete Levels}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{V_{REF} - 0}{2^n} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (n = 3, V_REF = 8 V):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \dfrac{8}{2^3} = \dfrac{8}{8} = 1 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

✓ An 8 V range split into 8 levels of 1 V each — consistent.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Digital Output Code for a Given V_in

**Logic:** The digital output $D$ is the integer part of how many "steps" the input $V_{in}$ contains, since each step corresponds to one increment in the binary counter / SAR register.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \dfrac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{REF} / 2^n} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{REF}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $D$ must be an integer, we apply the **floor function** to truncate the fractional part:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{REF}} \right\rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example (n = 4, V_REF = 5 V, V_in = 2.7 V):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{2.7 \times 2^4}{5} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{2.7 \times 16}{5} \right\rfloor \\[6pt]
&= \left\lfloor \dfrac{43.2}{5} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 8.64 \right\rfloor = 8_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

In binary, $8_{10} = 1000_2$. The reconstructed voltage is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} = 8 \times \dfrac{5}{16} = 2.5 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **quantization error** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_q = V_{in} - V_{out} = 2.7 - 2.5 = +0.2 \text{ V} \quad \left( < \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = 0.15625 \text{ V } \checkmark \text{?}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Wait — 0.2 V > 0.15625 V! This is because the **floor function** introduces a *truncation bias* up to almost $\Delta V$. To eliminate this bias, we use **rounding** instead of truncation in modern ADCs:
>
> $D = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n - 0.5}{V_{REF}}\right)$. This makes the maximum quantization error $\pm \Delta V / 2$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

**Logic:** For a full-scale sinusoidal input of amplitude $A$, the signal power (averaged over a period) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{signal} = \dfrac{A^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The quantization error behaves like uniformly distributed white noise between $-\Delta V/2$ and $+\Delta V/2$, with noise power:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{noise} = \dfrac{(\Delta V)^2}{12} = \dfrac{V_{REF}^2}{12 \cdot 4^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a full-scale sine, $A = V_{REF}/2$, so $P_{signal} = V_{REF}^2 / 8$. The SQNR in linear scale is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR} &= \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \dfrac{V_{REF}^2 / 8}{V_{REF}^2 / (12 \cdot 4^n)} = \dfrac{12 \cdot 4^n}{8} = 1.5 \cdot 4^n
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to decibels (using $10 \log_{10}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR}_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 n \log_{10}(4) \\[4pt]
&= 1.76 + 10 n \cdot (2 \log_{10} 2) \\[4pt]
&= 1.76 + 10 n \cdot 0.602 \\[4pt]
&= 1.76 + 6.02 n
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} \approx 6.02\, n + 1.76 \text{ dB}}
$$

**Interpretation:** **Every additional bit** improves the SQNR by approximately **6 dB** (a factor of 2 in voltage, or 4 in power). This is the famous **"6 dB per bit" rule** of ADC design.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Number of Comparators in a Flash ADC

**Logic:** For an $n$-bit output, the resistor ladder must produce $2^n$ distinct levels (including 0 V at the bottom and $V_{REF}$ at the top). Between any two adjacent levels, one comparator is required to decide "$V_{in}$ above or below this threshold?". The number of internal thresholds (and hence comparators) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 2^n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tabular verification:**

| $n$ | $N_c = 2^n - 1$ |
|---|---|
| 4 | 15 |
| 6 | 63 |
| 8 | 255 |
| 10 | 1023 |
| 12 | 4095 |

> [!WARNING]
> Beyond $n = 8$, hardware becomes prohibitive. A 10-bit Flash ADC needs **1023 comparators**, a 12-bit needs **4095** — this is why Flash ADCs are confined to $n \le 8$ bits in commercial designs. For higher resolution, **Pipeline or SAR ADCs** are used.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — 4-bit SAR ADC Trace

**Given:** $n = 4$, $V_{REF} = 5$ V, $V_{in} = 3.2$ V.

**Step-by-step SAR trace:**

| Cycle | Bit Tested | Trial Code (binary) | Trial Decimal | $V_{DAC}$ (V) | Comparator (V_in vs V_DAC) | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MSB (b3) | 1000 | 8 | 2.5 | 3.2 > 2.5 ✓ | Keep b3 = 1 |
| 2 | b2 | 1100 | 12 | 3.75 | 3.2 < 3.75 ✗ | Reset b2 = 0 |
| 3 | b1 | 1010 | 10 | 3.125 | 3.2 > 3.125 ✓ | Keep b1 = 1 |
| 4 | LSB (b0) | 1011 | 11 | 3.4375 | 3.2 < 3.4375 ✗ | Reset b0 = 0 |
| **Final** | — | **1010** | **10** | **3.125** | — | **Output = 1010₂ = 10₁₀** |

Reconstructed voltage: $V_{out} = 10 \times (5/16) = 3.125$ V.  
Quantization error: $E_q = 3.2 - 3.125 = +0.075$ V $< \Delta V/2 = 0.15625$ V ✓

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Counter-Type | SAR | Flash |
|---|---|---|---|
| Comparators | 1 | 1 | $2^n - 1$ |
| Resistors in ladder | $n$ (DAC) | $n$ (DAC) | $2^n$ |
| Conversion Time | $2^n \cdot T_{clk}$ (worst) | $n \cdot T_{clk}$ (fixed) | $\approx 1 \cdot T_{pd}$ |
| Speed | **Slow** | **Medium** | **Fastest** |
| Resolution | 8–10 bits typical | 8–18 bits typical | 4–8 bits typical |
| Hardware Complexity | Low | Medium | Very High |
| Power | Low | Low-Medium | High |
| Cost | Low | Low-Medium | High |
| Application | Voltmeters, sensors | Microcontrollers, audio | Oscilloscopes, 5G, radar |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Direct-Type (SAR) ADC — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input V_in] -->|positive input| C{Comparator}
    REF[Voltage Reference V_REF] --> D[DAC R-2R Ladder]
    SAR1[Successive Approximation Register] -->|n-bit code| D
    D -->|reconstructed V_DAC| C
    C -->|HIGH or LOW output| SAR1
    E[Clock Generator] -->|clock pulses| SAR1
    SAR1 -->|final n-bit code| OUT[Digital Output D_n-1..D_0]
    CTRL[Start of Conversion SOC] --> SAR1
    EOC[EOC End of Conversion Flag] <-- SAR1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The SAR register sends a *trial* binary code to the DAC, which converts it to an analog voltage. The comparator compares this $V_{DAC}$ with the actual $V_{in}$ and tells the SAR register whether to keep or discard the current bit. After $n$ cycles, the SAR register holds the final digital output and asserts **EOC** (End of Conversion).

## 4.2 Counter-Type ADC — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CLK[Clock] --> CTR[Binary Counter]
    CTR -->|count word| DAC[DAC]
    VREF[V_REF] --> DAC
    DAC --> VDAC[V_DAC analog output]
    VDAC --> CMP{Comparator}
    VIN[Analog V_in] --> CMP
    CMP -->|HIGH while V_DAC less than V_in| AND[AND gate]
    CLK --> AND
    AND -->|gate pulses while CMP HIGH| CTR
    CMP -->|LOW when V_DAC exceeds V_in| OE[Output Enable - latch count]
    CTR --> OE
    OE --> DOUT[Digital Output]
```

## 4.3 Flash-Type ADC — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VIN[Analog Input V_in] --> C1{Comp 1}
    VIN --> C2{Comp 2}
    VIN --> C3{Comp 3}
    VIN --> C4{Comp 4}
    VIN --> Cn{Comp 2 to power n minus 1}
    VREF[V_REF] --> RL[Resistor Ladder 2 to power n taps]
    RL --> T1[T1 threshold]
    RL --> T2[T2 threshold]
    RL --> T3[T3 threshold]
    RL --> T4[T4 threshold]
    RL --> Tn[Tn threshold]
    T1 --> C1
    T2 --> C2
    T3 --> C3
    T4 --> C4
    Tn --> Cn
    C1 --> PE[Priority Encoder]
    C2 --> PE
    C3 --> PE
    C4 --> PE
    Cn --> PE
    PE --> OUT[n-bit Binary Output]
```

## 4.4 SAR ADC — Decision Flow (Binary Search Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start of Conversion - SOC received]) --> INIT[Set MSB to 1 - all others 0]
    INIT --> DAC1[DAC reconstructs V_DAC]
    DAC1 --> CMP1{Is V_in greater than V_DAC?}
    CMP1 -->|YES| KEEP1[Keep current bit at 1]
    CMP1 -->|NO| RESET1[Reset current bit to 0]
    KEEP1 --> NEXT
    RESET1 --> NEXT{All n bits tested?}
    NEXT -->|NO| MOVEBIT[Move to next lower bit - set to 1 - repeat]
    MOVEBIT --> DAC1
    NEXT -->|YES| EOC[Assert EOC flag - output digital code]
    EOC --> STOP([Conversion complete])
```

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram — Direct vs Flash

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DirectTypeSAR[Direct Type SAR ADC]
        direction TB
        D1[VIN] --> D2[Comparator]
        D3[DAC] --> D2
        D4[SAR logic] --> D3
        D2 --> D4
        D4 --> D5[n-bit output]
    end
    subgraph FlashType[Flash Type ADC]
        direction TB
        F1[VIN] --> F2[Bank of 2 to power n minus 1 Comparators]
        F3[Resistor Ladder] --> F2
        F2 --> F4[Thermometer Code]
        F4 --> F5[Priority Encoder]
        F5 --> F6[n-bit output]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **"Define ADC. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a 3-bit Flash type ADC." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is a device that converts a continuous analog signal into a discrete digital (binary) code. It performs two operations: **sampling** of the input at discrete instants, and **quantization + encoding** of each sample into a binary word of $n$ bits.

A **Flash-type ADC** uses a **resistor ladder** that divides a reference voltage $V_{REF}$ into $2^n$ equal parts, and a **bank of $2^n - 1$ comparators**. For $n = 3$, there are 7 comparators, each with its own threshold voltage $V_{th,k} = k \cdot V_{REF} / 8$ for $k = 1$ to $7$. The analog input $V_{in}$ is fed to one input of **all** comparators simultaneously. The comparator outputs form a **thermometer code** (e.g., `0000111` for $V_{in}$ between $4V_{REF}/8$ and $5V_{REF}/8$). A **priority encoder** then converts this thermometer code into a 3-bit binary output in a single clock cycle. Hence Flash ADCs are the **fastest** ADC architecture, but require $2^n - 1$ comparators, making them impractical for $n > 8$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **"Compare Counter-type and SAR type ADCs in terms of conversion time, hardware complexity, and resolution." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Counter-Type ADC | SAR ADC |
|---|---|---|
| Conversion Time | Variable, up to $2^n \cdot T_{clk}$ | Fixed, $n \cdot T_{clk}$ |
| Hardware Complexity | Low (1 counter, 1 DAC, 1 comparator) | Medium (1 SAR register, 1 DAC, 1 comparator) |
| Typical Resolution | 8–10 bits | 8–18 bits |
| Speed | Slow (worst-case $2^n$ cycles) | Fast-Medium (always $n$ cycles) |
| Power Consumption | Low | Low-Medium |

The **Counter-type** is simpler but slow; the **SAR-type** uses a binary-search algorithm and is widely used in modern microcontrollers (e.g., STM32 SAR ADC, Arduino ATmega ADC).

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

> **"With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a 4-bit Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC. Also, compute the digital output and quantization error when $V_{in} = 3.2$ V and $V_{REF} = 5$ V. Discuss the advantages and limitations of SAR ADCs." (14 Marks)**

**Solution Structure:**

#### (a) Working of 4-bit SAR ADC — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram:** (refer to SECTION 4.1)

**Working Steps:**

1. A **Start of Conversion (SOC)** pulse resets the SAR register to `0000` and begins conversion.
2. The SAR sets the **MSB** to 1, producing trial code `1000`. The DAC converts this to:
   $$
   \begin{aligned}
   V_{DAC} = \dfrac{8 \times V_{REF}}{2^4} = \dfrac{8 \times 5}{16} = 2.5 \text{ V}
   \end{aligned}
   $$
3. The **comparator** checks: $V_{in} = 3.2$ V vs $V_{DAC} = 2.5$ V. Since $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$, the comparator output is HIGH → **MSB kept as 1**.
4. SAR sets the next bit (b2) to 1, trial code `1100`, $V_{DAC} = 12 \times 5/16 = 3.75$ V. Since $V_{in} = 3.2$ V $< 3.75$ V → **b2 reset to 0**.
5. Trial `1010`, $V_{DAC} = 10 \times 5/16 = 3.125$ V. Since $3.2 > 3.125$ → **b1 kept as 1**.
6. Trial `1011`, $V_{DAC} = 11 \times 5/16 = 3.4375$ V. Since $3.2 < 3.4375$ → **LSB reset to 0**.
7. **Final output: `1010`** = 10 in decimal.
8. SAR raises the **EOC (End of Conversion)** flag and latches the output.

**Conversion time = $n \cdot T_{clk} = 4 \cdot T_{clk}$** (fixed).

#### (b) Calculation, Advantages, and Limitations — 7 Marks

**Digital Output Code and Quantization Error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{3.2 \times 16}{5}\right) = \text{round}(10.24) = 10_{10} = 1010_2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \dfrac{10 \times 5}{16} = 3.125 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
E_q &= V_{in} - V_{out} = 3.2 - 3.125 = +0.075 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
\Delta V / 2 &= \dfrac{5}{32} = 0.15625 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\vert E_q \vert = 0.075 \text{ V} < 0.15625 \text{ V} = \Delta V/2$ ✓ the result is within tolerance.

**Advantages of SAR ADC:**
- Fixed conversion time of $n$ clock cycles → predictable timing.
- Low power (no high-frequency switching like Flash).
- High resolution (up to 18–20 bits commercially).
- Excellent for medium-speed, medium-resolution applications.

**Limitations of SAR ADC:**
- Slower than Flash (needs $n$ cycles vs. 1 cycle).
- Requires a high-precision DAC and accurate comparator.
- Not suitable for ultra-high-speed applications (e.g., GHz sampling).

---

### Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

> **"Explain the architecture and working of a 3-bit Flash ADC. Determine the number of comparators required, the threshold voltages, and the digital output for $V_{in} = 3.7$ V with $V_{REF} = 5$ V. State the major limitations of Flash ADCs." (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Architecture and Working — 7 Marks

**Architecture:**

A 3-bit Flash ADC consists of:
- **Resistor Ladder:** $2^3 = 8$ equal resistors in series, producing 8 tap voltages from 0 to $V_{REF}$.
- **Comparators:** $2^3 - 1 = 7$ comparators, one for each *internal* tap voltage.
- **Priority Encoder:** An 8-to-3 combinational logic block that converts the thermometer code into a 3-bit binary output.

**Block Diagram:** (refer to SECTION 4.3)

**Working:**

1. The analog input $V_{in}$ is connected to the **non-inverting (+)** input of all 7 comparators.
2. Each comparator's **inverting (−)** input is connected to one of the 7 ladder taps.
3. The 7 threshold voltages are:
   $$
   \begin{aligned}
   V_{th,k} = k \cdot \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^3} = k \cdot \dfrac{5}{8} = 0.625 k \text{ V}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 7
   \end{aligned}
   $$
   Giving: 0.625 V, 1.25 V, 1.875 V, 2.5 V, 3.125 V, 3.75 V, 4.375 V.
4. Each comparator output = 1 if $V_{in} > V_{th,k}$, else 0. The outputs form a **thermometer code**.
5. The priority encoder maps the highest set bit to a 3-bit binary code.

**Total comparators = $2^3 - 1 = 7$**.

#### (b) Numerical Determination & Limitations — 7 Marks

**Given:** $V_{in} = 3.7$ V, $V_{REF} = 5$ V, $n = 3$.

**Step size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \dfrac{5}{8} = 0.625 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Expected digital output (using formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{\Delta V}\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{3.7}{0.625}\right) = \text{round}(5.92) = 6_{10} = 110_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification by comparator outputs (Thermometer code):**

| Comparator | $V_{th,k}$ (V) | $V_{in} > V_{th,k}$? | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | 0.625 | Yes | 1 |
| C2 | 1.25 | Yes | 1 |
| C3 | 1.875 | Yes | 1 |
| C4 | 2.5 | Yes | 1 |
| C5 | 3.125 | Yes | 1 |
| C6 | 3.75 | **No** (3.7 < 3.75) | 0 |
| C7 | 4.375 | No | 0 |

**Thermometer code = `1111100` → highest set bit index = 5 → binary output = `110` (i.e., 6).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} = 6 \times 0.625 = 3.75 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
E_q = 3.7 - 3.75 = -0.05 \text{ V} \quad \left( < \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} = 0.3125 \text{ V} \ \checkmark \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Major Limitations of Flash ADCs:**
1. **Exponential hardware growth** — $2^n - 1$ comparators. For $n = 10$, that's 1023 comparators.
2. **High power consumption** due to the massive comparator array and resistor ladder.
3. **Limited resolution** — practically capped at $n \le 8$ bits.
4. **Component matching** — all comparators and resistors must be precisely matched; mismatches cause sparkle codes and INL/DNL errors.
5. **Large die area and high cost** at high resolutions.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (from KTU 2024 Valuation Key Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing resolution with accuracy** — Resolution is the *step size* (LSB voltage); accuracy depends on real-world component tolerances. Don't claim a 12-bit ADC has 12-bit accuracy.
> 2. **Forgetting the floor/rounding difference** — Always state explicitly whether the digital code is computed by truncation (floor) or rounding; this affects the quantization error bound.
> 3. **Wrong number of comparators in Flash** — For $n$-bit Flash ADC, it is $2^n - 1$, **not** $2^n$. Forgetting the $-1$ is a guaranteed half-mark deduction.
> 4. **Missing the EOC and SOC signals** in SAR block diagrams — Examiners award marks for the *control flow* (start-of-conversion, end-of-conversion) and not just the data path.
> 5. **Writing "Comparator output = HIGH means $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$"** — Be precise about which input of the comparator is the inverting vs non-inverting. Drawing a small '+' and '−' on the diagram earns an extra mark.
> 6. **Ignoring units in the formula sheet** — Always write V, V/step, dB, etc. A formula with no units is considered incomplete.
> 7. **Mis-stating conversion time as $n$ cycles for Counter-type** — Counter-type is *worst-case* $2^n$ cycles, not $n$ cycles. SAR is the one with fixed $n$ cycles.
> 8. **Skipping the priority encoder in Flash diagrams** — Drawing only comparators without the encoder means you are describing a *thermometer* output, not a *binary* output. Always show the encoder.

---

## 5.4 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **ADC** = Analog-to-Digital Converter; converts continuous analog signals into discrete $n$-bit binary codes.
- **Direct-Type ADC** = Compares input *directly* to a DAC-generated reference. Sub-types: **Counter-type** (slow, $2^n$ cycles worst-case) and **SAR-type** (binary search, $n$ cycles, **most popular**).
- **Flash-Type ADC** = Parallel comparator bank; conversion in **one clock cycle**; needs $2^n - 1$ comparators; resolution capped at $n \le 8$ practically.
- **Resolution formula:** $\Delta V = V_{REF} / 2^n$ — *memorize this line!*
- **Digital output:** $D = \text{round}(V_{in} / \Delta V) = \text{round}(V_{in} \cdot 2^n / V_{REF})$.
- **Quantization error:** $E_q = \pm \Delta V / 2$ (after rounding).
- **SQNR = $6.02n + 1.76$ dB** → every extra bit gives ~6 dB improvement — the famous **"6 dB per bit"** rule.
- **Flash threshold:** $V_{th,k} = k \cdot V_{REF} / 2^n$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2^n - 1$.
- **Conversion times:** Flash ($\sim 1 \cdot T_{pd}$) < SAR ($n \cdot T_{clk}$) < Counter ($2^n \cdot T_{clk}$ worst case).
- **SAR sequence:** MSB first, then progressively LSBs; decision based on comparator output of *trial* DAC voltage.
- **Flash sequence:** *all* comparators fire *simultaneously*; priority encoder maps thermometer code to binary.
- **Networking context:** ADCs sit at the **physical layer** of every digital communication system; Flash dominates 5G/mmWave receivers, SAR dominates IoT/sensor nodes.
- **Mnemonic for Flash complexity:** "**Exponential Equals Power**" — for 8-bit Flash, you need **2⁸ = 256** resistors and **255** comparators.
- **Watch out for:** $2^n - 1$ (not $2^n$) comparators; $\pm \Delta V/2$ (not $\pm \Delta V$) quantization error; SQNR uses $6.02n$ (not $6n$).
- **Block diagrams must include:** DAC, comparator, register/encoder, and (for SAR) the feedback arrow from comparator to register.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Counter Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Counter Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter Type** in Computer Networks refers to the family of mechanisms that use a monotonically incrementing or decrementing integer value (a *counter*) embedded in a packet header or protocol field to govern forwarding, lifetime, ordering, or addressing. The two principal counter types discussed at the Network Layer (Module 4) are:
>
> 1. **TTL Counter (Time-To-Live Counter / Hop Limit Counter)** — an **8-bit** field in the IPv4 header that decrements by **1** at every router hop to prevent indefinite circulation of undeliverable packets.
> 2. **Address-Counter / Class-Counter** — the structural use of a *bit-counter* (prefix length) to slice an IP address into a Network ID portion and a Host ID portion (Classful & Classless schemes).

A **counter** in this context is a finite, non-resetting (until wrap-around) integer state machine whose purpose is **bounded resource management** — bounding packet lifetime, bounding broadcast domains, or bounding host enumeration within a subnet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):** The TTL counter is part of the IPv4 header, and the class-counter (prefix-length counter) is the foundation of subnetting and CIDR. Both are mandatory exam topics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postage Stamp with an "Expiry Punch"

Imagine you hand a sealed envelope to a chain of post-office clerks, each of whom must forward it. Before handing it over, every clerk **punches a hole** in a small grid printed on the envelope.

* The grid has **8 holes** → analogous to an **8-bit TTL counter**.
* The first clerk punches **8 holes** (value = 8).
* Each subsequent clerk punches **one more hole** (value decreases by 1).
* When all 8 holes are punched (value = 0), the next clerk **throws the envelope away** instead of forwarding it.

This is exactly how the **TTL counter** works. It is a self-destruct mechanism against routing loops — packets that wander forever are *counted out* of the network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy #2 — Apartment Building Numbering:** A building has a 32-bit address. The first **N bits** identify the *street* (network), the remaining bits identify the *apartment* (host). The number **N** is the **counter** — it tells you where to "cut" the address. Change the counter, and you re-define the network boundary. This is the **prefix-length counter** in CIDR.

## 1.3 Key Constants and Metrics

The following physical/logical constants define counter behavior at the Network Layer:

* **TTL Default Value (Linux/Unix):** **64**
* **TTL Default Value (Windows):** **128**
* **TTL Default Value (Network Devices):** **255**
* **TTL Field Width:** **8 bits** → maximum countable value = **255**
* **Maximum Hops Traversable:** **255** (after which packet is discarded and **ICMP Type 11 — Time Exceeded** is sent back to the source)
* **IPv4 Address Width:** **32 bits** → divided by the **prefix-length counter (k)** into Network (k bits) and Host (32 − k bits) portions
* **RFC 791 (IPv4) and RFC 8200 (IPv6)** are the governing standards

All the above are **bolded exam favorites** in the KTU 2024 Scheme.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The TTL Counter (Type 1)

The TTL counter is initialized at the **source host** and decremented at every **Layer-3 device (router)** along the path. It is **never incremented** — only decremented or copied.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted)

1. **Source Host Action:** When a process invokes `sendto()` on a socket, the OS kernel writes the initial TTL value into the IPv4 header field. Common defaults: **64** (Linux), **128** (Windows), **255** (Cisco).
2. **Router Arrival:** A router receives the packet and inspects the Layer-3 header.
3. **Decrement Operation:** The router computes `TTL_new = TTL_old - 1` in hardware (line-rate, ASIC-level) for performance.
4. **Threshold Check:**
   * If `TTL_new >= 1` → **recompute checksum, decrement, and forward** to next-hop interface.
   * If `TTL_new == 0` → **discard the packet** and generate an **ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11, Code 0)** message back to the source IP.
5. **Destination Arrival:** The destination host does **not** decrement TTL further. It simply delivers the packet to the upper-layer socket.
6. **Anti-Loop Guarantee:** Even in the worst-case routing loop, a packet can survive at most **255 hops**, after which it is mandatorily destroyed.

## 2.2 Operational Concept — The Class / Prefix Counter (Type 2)

In the addressing sense, the "counter" is the **prefix length `k`** (also called *subnet mask bits*) used to slice a 32-bit IPv4 address.

### Operational Logic

1. Pick a **counter value `k`** such that `0 <= k <= 32`.
2. The **first `k` bits** (left-most, MSB) form the **Network ID**.
3. The **remaining `32 - k` bits** form the **Host ID**.
4. **Classful specialization:** The counter is *fixed* per class:
   * **Class A:** `k = 8` (network) + 24 (host), leading bits `0`
   * **Class B:** `k = 16` (network) + 16 (host), leading bits `10`
   * **Class C:** `k = 24` (network) + 8 (host), leading bits `110`
   * **Class D:** `k` undefined (multicast), leading bits `1110`
   * **Class E:** `k` undefined (experimental), leading bits `1111`
5. **Classless specialization (CIDR):** The counter `k` can be **any value from 0 to 32** — providing flexible, efficient address allocation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variable Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TTL Decrement | $TTL_{new} = TTL_{old} - 1$ | Decrement on each router hop | integer, $[0, 255]$ |
| 2 | Max Hops | $H_{max} = TTL_{initial}$ | Initial TTL sets the upper bound of traversal | hops |
| 3 | Number of Networks (Classful) | $N_{net} = 2^{k}$ | $k$ = number of network bits | networks |
| 4 | Number of Hosts per Network | $N_{host} = 2^{(32-k)} - 2$ | Subtract 2 for network & broadcast addresses | hosts |
| 5 | CIDR Block Size | $B = 2^{(32-k)}$ | Total addresses in a `/k` block | addresses |
| 6 | Usable Hosts in CIDR | $H = 2^{(32-k)} - 2$ | Same as classful, applied per CIDR block | hosts |
| 7 | Subnet Mask (decimal) | $M = (2^{k} - 1) \cdot 2^{(32-k)}$ | Bitwise AND of mask with address yields network | dotted-decimal |
| 8 | Number of Subnets Gained | $S = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = borrowed bits from host portion | subnets |
| 9 | Wrap-around Period (TTL) | $W = 2^{8} = 256$ | Counter width | counts |
| 10 | ICMP Type on Expiry | Type 11, Code 0 | Sent by discarding router | message |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The formula $N_{host} = 2^{(32-k)} - 2$ is **always** for *usable* hosts. The subtraction of 2 accounts for the **all-zeros** (network address) and **all-ones** (directed broadcast) addresses. The `/31` and `/32` special cases (RFC 3021) are **outside KTU syllabus scope**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **TTL counter** is the *only* mechanism that prevents the global Internet from collapsing under routing-loop storms. Without it, a single misconfiguration in BGP could flood the Internet with immortal packets.
* **Ping (`ICMP Echo Request`)** uses TTL to allow operators to discover the path (using `traceroute` which sends packets with TTL = 1, 2, 3, … to elicit Time Exceeded from each router).
* **OS Fingerprinting:** Tools like `nmap` guess the remote OS by examining the initial TTL of replies (64 ≈ Linux, 128 ≈ Windows, 255 ≈ Cisco IOS).
* **Class / Prefix counter** powers **CIDR**, which rescued IPv4 from address exhaustion in the 1990s by allowing granular, prefix-length-based allocation (e.g., `/22` instead of wasteful Class B).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Usable Hosts from the Prefix Counter

We start from first principles. Let:

* $k$ = the prefix-length counter (number of network bits)
* $(32 - k)$ = number of host bits in a 32-bit IPv4 address
* $H$ = number of usable host addresses

**Step 1 — Total Combinations of Host Bits**

Each host bit can independently be 0 or 1. For $(32 - k)$ host bits, the total number of distinct bit-patterns is:

$$N_{total} = 2^{(32-k)}$$

This is the number of *raw* addresses in the block, including the two reserved ones.

**Step 2 — Identify Reserved Addresses**

Two addresses in every block are *reserved* by IANA convention (RFC 950, updated by RFC 1812):

* The address with **all host bits = 0** → the **Network Address** (identifies the subnet itself, not a host).
* The address with **all host bits = 1** → the **Directed Broadcast Address** (used to send a packet to all hosts on the subnet).

**Step 3 — Subtract Reserved Addresses**

$$H = N_{total} - 2$$

Substituting from Step 1:

$$H = 2^{(32-k)} - 2$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example (Class C with k = 24)**

With $k = 24$, we have $32 - k = 8$ host bits. Applying the formula:

$$H = 2^{8} - 2 = 256 - 2 = 254 \text{ usable hosts}$$

This is the canonical answer KTU expects for a Class C network.

**Step 5 — Worked Example (CIDR /27)**

With $k = 27$, we have $32 - k = 5$ host bits:

$$H = 2^{5} - 2 = 32 - 2 = 30 \text{ usable hosts}$$

**Step 6 — Step-by-Step Subnetting Derivation**

Suppose we borrow $n$ additional bits from the host portion to create $S$ subnets:

$$S = 2^{n}$$

The new prefix length becomes:

$$k_{new} = k_{old} + n$$

The new host count per subnet:

$$H_{new} = 2^{(32 - k_{new})} - 2 = 2^{(32 - k_{old} - n)} - 2$$

**Worked Example:** Start with Class B (`k_old = 16`), borrow $n = 4$ bits:

$$S = 2^{4} = 16 \text{ subnets}$$

$$k_{new} = 16 + 4 = 20$$

$$H_{new} = 2^{(32-20)} - 2 = 2^{12} - 2 = 4094 \text{ hosts per subnet}$$

The same logic extends to all subnets and supernets in CIDR.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Router Hops Before TTL Expiry

Let:

* $T_0$ = initial TTL at source (e.g., **64** for Linux, **128** for Windows, **255** for network devices)
* $H$ = number of routers traversed

**Step 1 — Counter Behavior**

The counter decrements at each router by exactly **1**. There is no increment anywhere on the path.

**Step 2 — Packet Disposal Threshold**

The packet is discarded at the router where the post-decrement counter becomes **0**. This occurs at the $(T_0)$-th router hop. Therefore:

$$H_{max} = T_0$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example (Linux → Linux)**

If $T_0 = 64$:

$$H_{max} = 64 \text{ router hops}$$

The 65th router receives a packet with TTL = 0, decrements it to -1 (or detects 0 first per RFC 1812), drops the packet, and sends ICMP Type 11 Code 0 to the source.

**Step 4 — Empirical Traceroute Insight**

In the `traceroute` tool, packets are sent with TTL = 1, 2, 3, … and each intermediate router replies with ICMP Time Exceeded, revealing the path hop-by-hop. The first "no-reply" hop indicates the destination was reached, and the destination port (UDP high port) returns ICMP Port Unreachable (Type 3, Code 3).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — TTL vs. Address-Counter

| Dimension | TTL Counter | Address / Prefix Counter |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | IPv4 header field (byte 9) | Not in header; computed via subnet mask |
| **Width** | 8 bits | 0 to 32 bits (variable) |
| **Initial Value** | 64 / 128 / 255 (OS-dependent) | Set by network admin (CIDR `/k`) |
| **Direction** | Decrements | Static (does not change during transit) |
| **Modification** | Modified at every router | Modified only at network boundaries (NAT) |
| **Purpose** | Bound packet lifetime | Define network boundary |
| **Failure Mode** | Packet drop + ICMP Type 11 | Misrouted traffic, ARP failures |
| **RFC** | RFC 791 (IPv4), RFC 8200 (IPv6 calls it Hop Limit) | RFC 950, RFC 1812, RFC 4632 (CIDR) |
| **KTU Marks Weightage** | High (asked every year) | High (numerical problems every year) |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU-Style Subnetting

**Problem:** An organization is given the block `200.10.20.0/24`. The admin needs **4 equal subnets**.

**Step 1 — Identify the counter change**

Original prefix: $k_{old} = 24$. We need $S = 4$ subnets, so $n = \log_2 4 = 2$ borrowed bits.

**Step 2 — Compute the new prefix**

$$k_{new} = 24 + 2 = 26 \text{ i.e., } /26$$

**Step 3 — Compute usable hosts per subnet**

$$H = 2^{(32-26)} - 2 = 2^{6} - 2 = 64 - 2 = 62 \text{ hosts per subnet}$$

**Step 4 — Enumerate the four subnets**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Subnet 0: } & 200.10.20.0 \quad \text{to} \quad 200.10.20.63 \quad (\text{broadcast} = 200.10.20.63) \\
\text{Subnet 1: } & 200.10.20.64 \quad \text{to} \quad 200.10.20.127 \quad (\text{broadcast} = 200.10.20.127) \\
\text{Subnet 2: } & 200.10.20.128 \quad \text{to} \quad 200.10.20.191 \quad (\text{broadcast} = 200.10.20.191) \\
\text{Subnet 3: } & 200.10.20.192 \quad \text{to} \quad 200.10.20.255 \quad (\text{broadcast} = 200.10.20.255)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification using the prefix counter**

Each subnet has its first 26 bits in common with `200.10.20.0`. For example, Subnet 2's first address `200.10.20.128` in binary has the same first 26 bits as `200.10.20.0`, and the last 6 bits are `100000` (= 128). This confirms the counter logic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — TTL Counter Lifecycle Across a Network Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Src["Source Host\nInitial TTL = 64"] --> R1["Router R1\nDecrement: 64 to 63\nForward"]
    R1 --> R2["Router R2\nDecrement: 63 to 62\nForward"]
    R2 --> R3["Router R3\nDecrement: 62 to 61\nForward"]
    R3 --> R4["Router R4\nDecrement: 61 to 60\nForward"]
    R4 --> Dst["Destination Host\nDelivers to upper layer\nNo decrement"]
    R2 -.->|"If TTL reaches 0"| Drop["Router Discards Packet\nSends ICMP Type 11 Code 0\nTime Exceeded to Source"]
    classDef router fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef host fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#28A745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef drop fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#C0392B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Src,Dst host
    class R1,R2,R3,R4 router
    class Drop drop
```

**Reading the diagram:** The forward path (solid arrows) shows the TTL decrementing by 1 at every router. The conditional path (dashed arrow) shows the safety mechanism: if the counter ever reaches 0, the packet is destroyed and a Time-Exceeded message is returned to the source.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — IPv4 Address Partitioning by Prefix Counter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A32["32-bit IPv4 Address"] --> Slash["Prefix Counter k = 24"]
    Slash --> Net["Network Portion\n24 bits\n200.10.20"]
    Slash --> Host["Host Portion\n8 bits\n0 to 255"]
    Host --> NetAddr["All-Zero Host\n= Network Address\n200.10.20.0"]
    Host --> Usable["Usable Hosts\n200.10.20.1 to .254\nCount = 2^8 - 2 = 254"]
    Host --> Bcast["All-One Host\n= Broadcast Address\n200.10.20.255"]
    classDef addrBox fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef countBox fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#28A745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reservedBox fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#C0392B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A32,Slash,Net,Host addrBox
    class Usable countBox
    class NetAddr,Bcast reservedBox
```

**Reading the diagram:** The 32-bit IPv4 address is *sliced* by the prefix counter `k` into a fixed-size Network portion and a variable-size Host portion. The host portion is then further subdivided into reserved (network/broadcast) and usable host addresses.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Classful vs. CIDR Counter Flexibility

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Class["IPv4 Address\n32 bits"] --> F1["Classful Mode\nFixed Counter k"]
    Class --> F2["CIDR Mode\nVariable Counter k"]
    F1 --> A["Class A: k = 8\nLeading 0"]
    F1 --> B["Class B: k = 16\nLeading 10"]
    F1 --> C["Class C: k = 24\nLeading 110"]
    F1 --> D["Class D: k = N/A\nMulticast 1110"]
    F1 --> E["Class E: k = N/A\nExperimental 1111"]
    F2 --> P1["Slash 8: k = 8\n16M addresses"]
    F2 --> P2["Slash 16: k = 16\n65K addresses"]
    F2 --> P3["Slash 24: k = 24\n256 addresses"]
    F2 --> P4["Slash 27: k = 27\n32 addresses"]
    F2 --> P5["Slash 30: k = 30\n4 addresses"]
    classDef classBox fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flexBox fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Class,F1,A,B,C,D,E classBox
    class F2,P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 flexBox
```

**Reading the diagram:** In the *classful* era, the counter `k` was rigidly tied to the leading bits (8, 16, or 24). In *CIDR*, the counter `k` is freely chosen from 0 to 32, enabling efficient, on-demand allocation — the foundation of modern Internet routing scalability.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
>
> **Q1. What is the purpose of the TTL field in the IPv4 header? What happens when TTL reaches zero?**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The **Time-to-Live (TTL)** is an **8-bit** field in the IPv4 header used to bound the lifetime of an IP packet. It is initialized by the source (e.g., **64** on Linux, **128** on Windows) and **decremented by 1 at every router hop**. Its purpose is to **prevent packets from circulating indefinitely** in routing loops, which would otherwise waste bandwidth and router CPU.
>
> When the TTL field is decremented to **0**, the router **discards the packet** and returns an **ICMP Type 11 Code 0 (Time Exceeded)** message to the source IP address. This mechanism guarantees that every packet is destroyed within at most **255 hops** of its origin.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Q2, 3 Marks]**
>
> **Q2. Differentiate between Classful and Classless (CIDR) addressing with respect to the prefix counter.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Classful Addressing | Classless (CIDR) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Prefix counter `k` | **Fixed** per class (8, 16, 24) | **Variable**, any value 0–32 |
> | Notation | Class A/B/C implied | Explicit `/k` suffix |
> | Granularity | Coarse (huge waste in between) | Fine-grained, on-demand |
> | Example | `10.0.0.0` is implicitly Class A (`/8`) | `200.10.20.0/27` is a 32-address block |
> | Standard | Pre-1993 (RFC 791) | RFC 4632 (current) |
>
> CIDR rescued IPv4 from address exhaustion by letting ISPs and enterprises choose `k` freely.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries **14 marks** split across sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the TTL counter in the IPv4 header in detail. How does it prevent routing loops? How is it used by the `traceroute` utility? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **TTL (Time-to-Live)** field occupies **byte 9** of the 20-byte IPv4 header. It is an **8-bit unsigned counter** whose value is set by the sending host and decremented by **1** at every router hop.

**Loop Prevention Mechanism:**
1. The source sets TTL to a value $T_0$ (commonly 64, 128, or 255).
2. Each router on the path computes $TTL_{new} = TTL_{old} - 1$ *before* forwarding.
3. If $TTL_{new} \ge 1$, the router updates the header (and recomputes the IP header checksum) and forwards the packet.
4. If $TTL_{new} = 0$, the router *discards* the packet and sends an **ICMP Type 11 Code 0 (Time Exceeded)** message back to the source.
5. Therefore, **no packet can survive more than $T_0$ router hops**, which absolutely bounds any routing loop.

**`traceroute` Usage:**
* `traceroute` sends **three** UDP probe packets (by default) with TTL = 1, 2, 3, …, incrementing by 1.
* The first probe (TTL=1) expires at the **first router**, which replies with ICMP Time Exceeded, revealing router #1's IP.
* The second probe (TTL=2) expires at the **second router**, revealing router #2.
* This continues until a probe reaches the **destination**, which (since it uses an unused high UDP port) replies with **ICMP Type 3 Code 3 (Port Unreachable)** — signaling end-of-trace.
* The result is a hop-by-hop listing of every router on the path, useful for diagnosing latency and outages.

**(b)** An organization is assigned the network `192.168.50.0/24`. The admin must divide it into **8 equal subnets**. Calculate: (i) the new prefix length, (ii) the subnet mask in dotted decimal, (iii) the number of usable hosts per subnet, and (iv) the network and broadcast addresses of the **first and last** subnets. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) New Prefix Length**

To create $S = 8$ subnets, we must borrow $n$ host bits such that $2^n = 8$, so $n = 3$.

$$k_{new} = k_{old} + n = 24 + 3 = 27 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{New notation: } /27$$

**(ii) Subnet Mask in Dotted Decimal**

A /27 mask has **27 leading 1-bits** and **5 trailing 0-bits**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mask in binary} & = \underbrace{11111111.11111111.11111111}_{27 \text{ ones}}.11100000 \\
\text{Decimal equivalent} & = 255.255.255.224
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Usable Hosts per Subnet**

With $32 - 27 = 5$ host bits:

$$H = 2^{5} - 2 = 32 - 2 = 30 \text{ usable hosts per subnet}$$

**(iv) First and Last Subnet Boundaries**

Block size (in the last octet) = $2^{5} = 32$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Subnet 0: } & 192.168.50.0 \quad \text{to} \quad 192.168.50.31 \\
\text{Network address:} & \;\;192.168.50.0 \\
\text{Broadcast address:} & \;\;192.168.50.31 \\
& \\
\text{Subnet 7: } & 192.168.50.224 \quad \text{to} \quad 192.168.50.255 \\
\text{Network address:} & \;\;192.168.50.224 \\
\text{Broadcast address:} & \;\;192.168.50.255
\end{aligned}
$$

Each subnet has 30 usable hosts (from `.1` to `.30` in Subnet 0; from `.225` to `.254` in Subnet 7).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain classful IPv4 addressing. List the five classes with their prefix counters, leading bit patterns, and the number of networks and hosts each supports. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Classful addressing divides the 32-bit IPv4 address space into **five fixed classes** based on the **leading bit pattern**, with a corresponding **fixed prefix counter `k`** for each main class (A, B, C).

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Class} & \textbf{Leading Bits} & \textbf{k (Net bits)} & \textbf{Host bits} & \textbf{Networks} & \textbf{Hosts/Net} \\
\hline
A & 0 & 8 & 24 & 2^{7} = 128 & 2^{24}-2 \\
\hline
B & 10 & 16 & 16 & 2^{14} = 16\,384 & 2^{16}-2 \\
\hline
C & 110 & 24 & 8 & 2^{21} & 2^{8}-2 = 254 \\
\hline
D & 1110 & \text{N/A} & \text{N/A} & \text{Multicast only} & \text{N/A} \\
\hline
E & 1111 & \text{N/A} & \text{N/A} & \text{Experimental} & \text{N/A} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Key Points:**

* **Class A** supports 128 networks, each with up to **16,777,214 hosts** — ideal for very large organizations (e.g., MIT, IBM historically).
* **Class B** supports 16,384 networks, each with up to **65,534 hosts** — used by mid-to-large universities and corporations.
* **Class C** supports over 2 million networks, each with **254 hosts** — used by small offices and home networks.
* **Class D** is reserved for **IP multicast** (e.g., OSPF routers, video streaming).
* **Class E** is **reserved for experimental use** and is not assigned to any host.

The major drawback of classful addressing is **waste**: an organization needing 300 hosts must take a full Class B (65,534 addresses), wasting 65,000+ addresses. This motivated the move to **CIDR**.

**(b)** With the help of an example, explain how the prefix counter `k` is used in **CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing)** for efficient address allocation. A company needs **600 hosts**. Which CIDR block will you assign, and how many addresses are wasted? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In **CIDR**, the prefix counter `k` is *not* fixed to a class. Instead, the network admin (or ISP) chooses any `k` in $[0, 32]$ that satisfies the requirement. The notation is `address/k`.

**Step 1 — Compute the minimum `k` for 600 hosts**

We need $H \ge 600$, so $2^{(32-k)} - 2 \ge 600$, which gives $2^{(32-k)} \ge 602$, so $32 - k \ge 10$ (since $2^{9} = 512 < 602 < 1024 = 2^{10}$). Therefore $k \le 22$.

**Step 2 — Select the smallest valid CIDR block**

The most efficient choice is $k = 22$, i.e., a **/22 block**.

**Step 3 — Compute the block size and wastage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block size} & = 2^{(32-22)} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ addresses} \\
\text{Usable hosts} & = 1024 - 2 = 1022 \\
\text{Wasted addresses} & = 1022 - 600 = 422
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Example assignment**

Suppose the ISP allocates `203.0.113.0/22` to the company. The address range is `203.0.113.0` to `203.0.113.255` (in 4 consecutive /24 blocks, since $2^{10} = 4 \times 2^{8}$):

* `203.0.113.0/24`  — usable: `.1` to `.254`
* `203.0.113.1/24`  — usable: `.1` to `.254`
* `203.0.113.2/24`  — usable: `.1` to `.254`
* `203.0.113.3/24`  — usable: `.1` to `.254`

Total usable: $4 \times 254 = 1016$ (the small discrepancy with 1022 comes from the boundary network/broadcast addresses in each /24 slice; in a flat /22 view, the boundary addresses are `203.0.113.0` and `203.0.113.255` only).

**Step 5 — Conclusion**

The flexible prefix counter `k` in CIDR reduces waste from ~99% (Class B for 300 hosts) to ~42% (/22 for 600 hosts), making IPv4 viable far longer than classful addressing would have allowed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract 2** for network and broadcast addresses in host-count calculations — costs **1 mark** almost every time.
> 2. **Confusing TTL with TTL field in DNS** — DNS TTL is a *caching lifetime* in seconds; IP TTL is a *hop count*. Examiners WILL test this distinction.
> 3. **Writing `TTL_new = TTL_old + 1` somewhere** — TTL *never increments*. It is strictly non-increasing.
> 4. **Mixing up `/k` notation**: `/24` means 24 *network* bits, **not** 24 host bits. Writing "$k=24$ host bits" is a fatal error.
> 5. **In subnet enumeration, off-by-one errors** at the boundary (e.g., listing `200.10.20.64` to `200.10.20.128` for a /26 block — the correct upper bound is `200.10.20.127`).
> 6. **Not recomputing the IP header checksum** when describing TTL modification — the RFC 1624 incremental checksum update is technically required, but for KTU a one-line mention of "recompute checksum" suffices for full marks.
> 7. **Skipping ICMP Type/Code numbers** (Type 11, Code 0) — these are routinely asked and routinely missed.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **TTL Counter** is an **8-bit** field in the IPv4 header (byte 9), decremented by **1** at every router hop.
* **Default TTL values to memorize:** Linux = **64**, Windows = **128**, Cisco = **255**.
* **Packet disposal rule:** When TTL reaches **0**, the packet is **dropped** and an **ICMP Type 11 Code 0 (Time Exceeded)** message is sent to the source.
* **Maximum traversable hops** = initial TTL (capped at 255 by the 8-bit width).
* **Classful prefix counter `k`:** A → 8, B → 16, C → 24 (with corresponding leading bit patterns 0, 10, 110).
