* **Class D (multicast)** leading bits **1110**, **Class E (experimental)** leading bits **1111** — neither supports host assignment.
* **CIDR `k`** is **any integer in [0, 32]**, expressed as `/k` suffix notation.
* **Usable hosts formula:** $H = 2^{(32-k)} - 2$ — memorize this; it is the single most-tested equation.
* **Subnet count from borrowed bits:** $S = 2^{n}$ where $n$ is the number of bits *borrowed from the host portion*.
* **New prefix after borrowing $n$ bits:** $k_{new} = k_{old} + n$.
* **Subnet mask in decimal** for `/k` is `255.255.255.(256 - 2^{(32-k)})` when $k \ge 24$.
* **Block size** for `/k` is $2^{(32-k)}$; the network address is the first address, the broadcast is the last.
* **`traceroute` mechanics:** Send probes with TTL = 1, 2, 3, … ; each intermediate router responds with ICMP Time Exceeded; destination responds with ICMP Port Unreachable.
* **TTL vs. Hop Limit:** IPv4 calls it TTL; IPv6 renamed it to **Hop Limit** (semantics identical: decremented by 1 at each router).
* **Anti-loop property:** The TTL counter is the **only built-in defense** against routing loops at Layer 3.
* **OS fingerprinting clue:** Initial TTL in replies reveals the remote OS family — a classic cybersecurity exam point.
* **RFC 791** governs IPv4 (and hence TTL); **RFC 4632** governs CIDR; **RFC 1812** governs router requirements (including TTL handling).
* **Always recompute the IP header checksum** when modifying TTL — `HC' = ~ (~HC + ~TTL_old + TTL_new)` per RFC 1624.
* **The /31 and /32 blocks** are special (point-to-point and host route) and are **out of KTU 2024 syllabus scope** — do not write about them in exams unless explicitly asked.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Successive Approximation Type, Integrating Type ADC, Single Slope Type, Suggestion on Project Topics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERT403 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Successive Approximation Type, Integrating Type ADC, Single Slope Type, Suggestion on Project Topics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) – Foundational Definition

An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is a mixed-signal electronic system that converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital code (usually binary). According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, ADCs are graded based on three critical performance parameters:
- **Resolution (n)**: Number of bits in the output code, expressed as $2^n$ discrete levels.
- **Conversion Time ($T_c$)**: Time taken to produce a valid digital output for a given analog input.
- **Quantization Step (q)**: The smallest distinguishable analog change, defined as:
$$q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$
where $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale voltage range and $V_{ref}$ is the reference voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition**: An ADC acts as the "translator" between the real analog world (temperature, pressure, sound) and the digital processing world (microcontrollers, FPGAs). Without an ADC, a Raspberry Pi or Arduino cannot read a temperature sensor like the **LM35**.

---

### 1.2 Successive Approximation Type ADC – Core Definition

The **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** is a *closed-loop, comparator-based* ADC that performs a **binary search** on the input voltage to determine each bit of the output code, starting from the Most Significant Bit (MSB) to the Least Significant Bit (LSB). It is the most widely used ADC architecture in modern microcontrollers (e.g., the **STM32, ESP32, Arduino Uno's ATmega328P**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: The SAR ADC is a *medium-speed, medium-to-high resolution* converter. Its defining feature is a **fixed conversion time of exactly $n$ clock cycles**, regardless of the input voltage value.

**Intuitive Analogy – The Magic Number Guessing Game**:
Imagine a student guessing a number between **0 and 100** (analog input) in front of an examiner. The examiner only says **"Higher"** or **"Lower"** (comparator output). The optimal strategy is *binary search*:
- Guess **50** first. If "Higher" → go to **75**, if "Lower" → go to **25**.
- Guess the midpoint of the new range. Repeat until the exact number is found.
- For 0–100 (≈ 7 bits), you need only **7 guesses**, no matter the number!

This is exactly how the SAR ADC works. Each clock cycle, it sets one bit of an internal DAC, and the comparator tells the SAR whether the guess is too high or too low. The number of bits guessed equals the resolution, so total time is constant.

---

### 1.3 Integrating Type ADC – Core Definition

The **Integrating Type ADC** is an *indirect conversion* ADC that first converts the analog input voltage into a **time duration** (or pulse width) by integrating it on a capacitor, and then measures that time duration using a digital counter. The most common variant is the **Dual-Slope ADC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: Integrating ADCs offer **excellent noise rejection** (especially at mains frequencies of **50 Hz / 60 Hz**) because of their inherent averaging nature. They are used in **digital multimeters (DMMs)**, weighing scales, and industrial panel meters.

**Intuitive Analogy – The Water Bucket**:
Imagine a leaking bucket. You fill the bucket with water at a rate proportional to the unknown voltage ($V_{in}$) for a *fixed duration*. Then you empty it at a *fixed, known rate* (set by $V_{ref}$) and measure **how long it took to empty**. The longer it takes to empty, the larger the original input voltage. This is the essence of integration.

---

### 1.4 Single Slope Type ADC – Core Definition

The **Single Slope ADC** is the simplest integrating ADC. It uses a **linear ramp generator** whose output voltage increases linearly with time. A counter measures the time elapsed between the moment the ramp starts and the moment the ramp voltage equals the input voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: The Single Slope ADC has a **variable conversion time** that depends on the input voltage amplitude. It is used in low-cost, low-precision applications like simple digital voltmeters.

**Intuitive Analogy – The Stopwatch Race**:
A runner (the linear ramp) starts from a fixed point and moves at a constant speed. A referee (the comparator) signals the moment the runner passes an unknown, invisible finish line (the analog input). A stopwatch (the digital counter) measures the elapsed time, which is then converted to a digital number. A *further-away* finish line = *longer* time = *larger* digital code.

---

### 1.5 Standard Metrics for All ADCs

The following performance metrics are mandatory to know for KTU exams:

| Metric | Definition | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Resolution** | Smallest detectable analog change | $q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ **Volts** |
| **Conversion Time ($T_c$)** | Time to complete one A→D conversion | **seconds** |
| **Throughput Rate** | Number of conversions per second | $f_s = \frac{1}{T_c}$ **Samples/sec (SPS)** |
| **SNR** | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $6.02n + 1.76$ **dB** (ideal) |
| **INL / DNL** | Integral / Differential Non-Linearity | **LSB** |
| **FSR** | Full-Scale Range | $V_{ref}$ **Volts** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC – Operational Block Analysis

**Architecture**: The SAR ADC consists of four main functional blocks:
1. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit**: Holds the input voltage constant during conversion.
2. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)**: Generates an analog trial voltage based on the SAR's current bit pattern.
3. **Analog Comparator**: Compares the held input with the DAC output.
4. **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)**: Sequential logic that performs the binary search algorithm.

**Step-by-Step Operation (Binary Search Logic)**:

- **Cycle 1 (MSB)**: SAR sets the MSB to '1' and all other bits to '0'. The DAC output is:
$$V_{DAC} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2}$$
  - If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$: MSB is **kept as 1**.
  - If $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$: MSB is **reset to 0**.

- **Cycle 2 (MSB-1)**: SAR sets the next bit to '1' (other bits remain as decided). The DAC output is:
$$V_{DAC} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^2} \text{ or } \frac{V_{ref}}{2^1} + \frac{V_{ref}}{2^2}$$
depending on the MSB decision. The comparator again makes a "higher/lower" decision.

- **Cycle $k$**: The $k$-th bit is decided based on the residual range.

- **Cycle $n$ (LSB)**: The LSB is set and decided, completing the conversion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it is fast and elegant**: Each cycle halves the uncertainty range. After $n$ cycles, the uncertainty drops from $V_{ref}$ to $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$, which equals the resolution $q$.

**Conversion Time Formula**:
$$T_c = (n + 1) \times T_{clk}$$
where the extra cycle accounts for the sample acquisition phase.

**Why and How of the SAR ADC's Utility**:
- **Why fixed conversion time?** Because each conversion requires exactly $n$ clock cycles, making it ideal for **multiplexed data acquisition systems** where samples are taken at regular intervals.
- **How it rejects noise?** It is a "snapshot" converter — it does not average over time, so it requires an *external* anti-aliasing filter. However, its high speed makes oversampling techniques effective.

**Real-World Utility**:
- Found in **16-bit ADCs** like the **ADS1115** and **LTC2400**.
- The internal ADC of the **STM32F4** (12-bit, 2.4 MSPS SAR ADC).
- Industrial process control, battery management systems, motor control loops.

---

### 2.2 Dual-Slope Integrating Type ADC – Operational Block Analysis

**Architecture**: The Dual-Slope ADC consists of:
1. **Integrator (Op-Amp with RC feedback)**: Integrates the input over a fixed time $T_1$.
2. **Comparator**: Detects when the integrator output crosses zero.
3. **Control Logic**: Switches between $V_{in}$ and $-V_{ref}$ and resets the counter.
4. **Binary Counter with Clock**: Counts clock pulses during the de-integration phase.

**Step-by-Step Operation**:

- **Phase 1 – Run-Up (Integration) Phase**:
  - The switch connects the input $V_{in}$ to the integrator for a **fixed duration $T_1 = 2^n \times T_{clk}$** (typically a multiple of 50 Hz period to reject mains hum, e.g., **$T_1 = 100$ ms for 50 Hz rejection**).
  - The integrator output ramps down (since input is positive, and inverting integrator):
$$V_{out}(T_1) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_0^{T_1} V_{in}\,dt = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC}$$
  - The counter overflows at $2^n$ counts, signaling end of Phase 1.

- **Phase 2 – Run-Down (De-integration) Phase**:
  - The switch connects the **negative reference voltage $-V_{ref}$** to the integrator.
  - The integrator output ramps *upward* (towards zero) at a fixed slope:
$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC} + \frac{V_{ref} \cdot t}{RC}$$
  - The counter starts counting from zero. When the comparator detects $V_{out} = 0$, the counter is latched.
  - At $t = T_2$ (the de-integration time), $V_{out} = 0$, so:
$$0 = -\frac{V_{in} \cdot T_1}{RC} + \frac{V_{ref} \cdot T_2}{RC}$$
  - Solving for $V_{in}$:
$$V_{in} = V_{ref} \times \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

- **Phase 3 – Reset/Autozero**:
  - The integrator is shorted or connected to ground to remove any offset errors.

**Conversion Time Formula**:
$$T_c = T_1 + T_2 = T_1 \left(1 + \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}\right)$$

**Why and How of the Dual-Slope ADC's Utility**:
- **Why is it immune to component drift?** The conversion depends on the *ratio* $T_2/T_1$. Since both $T_1$ and $T_2$ are measured using the *same* clock, the clock frequency cancels out. Also, the **$RC$ product cancels** — meaning precision resistors and capacitors are NOT required!
- **How does it reject noise?** If $T_1$ is chosen as a multiple of the noise period (e.g., 20 ms for 50 Hz), the noise integrates to zero over $T_1$. This is called **Normal-Mode Rejection** or **Integration Period Rejection**.
- **How accurate is it?** The dual-slope ADC can easily achieve **16 to 22 bits of effective resolution**, making it the gold standard for digital multimeters.

**Real-World Utility**:
- **Agilent 34401A, Fluke 87V Digital Multimeters** (use 4.5 to 8.5 digit dual-slope ADCs).
- Weighing scales, panel meters, and medical instrumentation.

---

### 2.3 Single Slope Type ADC – Operational Block Analysis

**Architecture**:
1. **Ramp Generator (Integrator with constant current)**: Produces a linear ramp voltage.
2. **Comparator**: Compares ramp with $V_{in}$.
3. **Binary Counter**: Counts clock pulses while ramp is below $V_{in}$.
4. **Control Logic**: Resets the counter and ramp generator at end of conversion.

**Step-by-Step Operation**:

- **Reset Phase**: Counter is cleared, ramp is reset to zero.
- **Conversion Phase**:
  - The ramp generator starts producing a linear voltage: $V_{ramp}(t) = m \cdot t$, where $m$ is the slope (Volts/sec).
  - Simultaneously, the binary counter starts counting clock pulses of frequency $f_{clk}$.
  - The comparator output flips when $V_{ramp} = V_{in}$, i.e., at time $t = V_{in}/m$.
  - The counter value at this instant is the digital output.
  - Counter value: $N = f_{clk} \times t = f_{clk} \times \frac{V_{in}}{m}$

**Conversion Time Formula**:
$$T_c = \frac{V_{in, max}}{m} = \frac{2^n \times q}{m}$$

For an $n$-bit ADC with full-scale input $V_{FS}$ and clock period $T_{clk}$:
$$T_{c, max} = 2^n \times T_{clk}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Unlike the SAR ADC, the Single Slope ADC's conversion time is **NOT constant**. It varies linearly with $V_{in}$. A full-scale input takes **twice** the time of a half-scale input. This makes it unsuitable for real-time multiplexed systems.

**Why and How of the Single Slope ADC's Utility**:
- **Why use it at all?** It is *extremely simple*, requiring only one op-amp, one comparator, and a counter. It is used in educational kits and low-cost systems.
- **How does it suffer?** It is highly sensitive to *ramp linearity* and *clock frequency drift*. Any error in the slope $m$ directly causes gain error in the output.

**Real-World Utility**:
- Educational lab trainers (e.g., KTU's own microcontroller lab boards).
- Simple digital thermometers, basic digital voltmeters.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Comparison Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas and the comparison — questions are repeatedly asked in KTU Module 4 exams!**

| Parameter | **SAR ADC** | **Dual-Slope ADC** | **Single Slope ADC** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conversion Type** | Direct, closed-loop | Indirect (Voltage → Time → Code) | Indirect (Voltage → Time → Code) |
| **Conversion Time** | $T_c = (n+1) T_{clk}$ **(Fixed)** | $T_c = T_1 + T_2$ **(Variable)** | $T_c = \frac{V_{in}}{m \cdot f_{clk}}$ **(Variable)** |
| **Speed** | **Medium to High** (1 MSPS to 10 MSPS) | **Slow** (10 SPS to 1000 SPS) | **Slow** (similar to dual-slope) |
| **Resolution** | 8 to 18 bits (typical) | 16 to 24 bits (typical) | 8 to 12 bits (typical) |
| **Noise Rejection** | Poor (needs external filter) | **Excellent** (mains-frequency rejection) | Poor |
| **Component Sensitivity** | **Sensitive to DAC linearity** | **Insensitive to R, C, clock drift** | **Highly sensitive to ramp slope** |
| **Hardware Complexity** | **High** (SAR, DAC, comparator) | **Medium** (integrator, comparator, counter) | **Low** (single ramp, comparator, counter) |
| **Power Consumption** | **Medium** | **Low to Medium** | **Low** |
| **Key Equation** | $V_{DAC,k} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} b_i \frac{V_{ref}}{2^i}$ | $V_{in} = V_{ref} \times \frac{T_2}{T_1}$ | $N = f_{clk} \times \frac{V_{in}}{m}$ |
| **Best Use Case** | Microcontrollers, data loggers | Digital multimeters, weighing scales | Educational kits, simple DVMs |

**Symbol Definitions** (used across all ADC types):
- $n$ = number of bits (resolution)
- $V_{ref}$ = reference voltage (**typically 5 V or 10 V or 2.5 V**)
- $T_{clk}$ = clock period
- $m$ = slope of the ramp (V/s)
- $T_1$ = fixed integration time
- $T_2$ = variable de-integration time
- $V_{in}$ = analog input voltage

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Time of SAR ADC

**Given**:
- A 12-bit SAR ADC.
- Clock frequency $f_{clk} = 1 \text{ MHz}$, so $T_{clk} = 1 \mu\text{s}$.

**Step 1**: Each bit decision requires exactly one clock cycle.
**Step 2**: The conversion requires $n$ bit cycles plus 1 cycle for sampling.
**Step 3**: Therefore, total cycles = $n + 1 = 12 + 1 = 13$.
**Step 4**: Total conversion time:

$$T_c = (n+1) \times T_{clk} = 13 \times 1\mu s = 13 \mu s$$

**Step 5**: Throughput rate:

$$f_s = \frac{1}{T_c} = \frac{1}{13\mu s} \approx 76,923 \text{ SPS}$$

**Conversion logic**: This calculation directly uses the SAR architecture's defining property — *fixed* $n+1$ clock cycles per conversion. This is why SAR ADCs are favored in **real-time control systems** where predictable timing is critical.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Digital Output of Dual-Slope ADC

**Problem Statement** (Typical KTU Module 4 Numerical):
A dual-slope ADC uses a clock frequency of **$1 \text{ MHz}$** and a 16-bit counter. The integration time $T_1$ is set to **$2^{16} \times T_{clk} = 65.536 \text{ ms}$**. The reference voltage is $V_{ref} = 10 \text{ V}$. Find the digital output count for an analog input $V_{in} = 3.5 \text{ V}$.

**Step 1 — Identify knowns**:
- $f_{clk} = 1 \text{ MHz} \Rightarrow T_{clk} = 1 \mu\text{s}$
- $n = 16$, so $N_{FS} = 2^{16} = 65,536$
- $T_1 = N_{FS} \times T_{clk} = 65,536 \times 1\mu s = 65.536 \text{ ms}$
- $V_{ref} = 10 \text{ V}$
- $V_{in} = 3.5 \text{ V}$

**Step 2 — Apply the governing equation** for dual-slope ADC:
$$V_{in} = V_{ref} \times \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T_2$**:
$$T_2 = \frac{V_{in} \times T_1}{V_{ref}} = \frac{3.5 \text{ V} \times 65.536 \text{ ms}}{10 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerical value**:
$$T_2 = 0.35 \times 65.536 \text{ ms} = 22.9376 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Convert time to digital count**:
$$N_{out} = \frac{T_2}{T_{clk}} = \frac{22.9376 \text{ ms}}{1 \mu s} = 22,937.6 \approx 22,937 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 6 — Express in hexadecimal** (for KTU 2's complement or unsigned binary output representation):
$$22,937 = 0x5999 = 0\text{b}0101\,1001\,1001\,1001$$

**Conversion logic**: Notice how the final count is linearly proportional to $V_{in}$, with the full-scale count of **65,536** corresponding to $V_{in} = V_{ref} = 10 \text{ V}$. The ratio $\frac{N_{out}}{N_{FS}} = \frac{22,937}{65,536} = 0.35 = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}$ exactly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight**: A common KTU question is "Why is the integration time $T_1$ chosen as a multiple of $1/50 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}$?" The answer: For a $50 \text{ Hz}$ mains frequency, the period is $20 \text{ ms}$. If $T_1 = k \times 20 \text{ ms}$ (with $k$ an integer), then the average of any $50 \text{ Hz}$ sinusoidal interference over $T_1$ is **zero**, achieving **infinite normal-mode rejection at 50 Hz**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Resolution of Single Slope ADC

**Problem Statement**:
A 10-bit single-slope ADC has a clock frequency of **$f_{clk} = 100 \text{ kHz}$** and a ramp generator with slope **$m = 1 \text{ V/ms}$**. The reference voltage is $V_{ref} = 10 \text{ V}$. Calculate:
1. The maximum conversion time.
2. The number of clock counts for an input of $V_{in} = 6.4 \text{ V}$.

**Step 1 — Maximum conversion time**:
At full scale, $V_{in, max} = V_{ref} = 10 \text{ V}$. The ramp must reach 10 V at $m = 1 \text{ V/ms}$, so:

$$T_{c, max} = \frac{V_{in, max}}{m} = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ V/ms}} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Number of clock counts at full scale**:
$$N_{FS} = f_{clk} \times T_{c, max} = 100,000 \text{ Hz} \times 10 \text{ ms} = 1000 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3 — Resolution** (Volts per bit):
$$q = \frac{V_{ref}}{N_{FS}} = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{1000} = 10 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

**Step 4 — Counts for $V_{in} = 6.4 \text{ V}$**:
$$N_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{q} = \frac{6.4 \text{ V}}{10 \text{ mV}} = 640 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using time-based formula**:
$$t = \frac{V_{in}}{m} = \frac{6.4 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ V/ms}} = 6.4 \text{ ms}$$
$$N = f_{clk} \times t = 100,000 \text{ Hz} \times 6.4 \text{ ms} = 640 \text{ counts} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conversion logic**: The single-slope ADC's output is **proportional to $V_{in}$**, but the proportionality constant depends on the *ramp slope* $m$. Any error in $m$ (due to resistor drift, capacitor aging, or temperature) directly translates to a gain error in the output.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix – When to Use Which ADC

| Application Scenario | Best ADC Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Multiparameter data logger** (temperature, pressure, flow from 16 sensors sampled every 10 ms) | **SAR ADC** | Fixed conversion time simplifies timing; high speed (1 MSPS) allows sequential scanning. |
| **Precision weighing scale** (0.1 g resolution over 0–10 kg) | **Dual-Slope ADC** | High resolution (20-bit) + excellent 50 Hz mains rejection — critical in noisy industrial environments. |
| **Educational trainer board** (KTU digital electronics lab) | **Single Slope ADC** | Simple to build and demonstrate the A/D conversion concept; no precision components needed. |
| **Audio recording at 44.1 kHz** (CD quality) | **Sigma-Delta (over-sampled) or SAR** | Audio bandwidth is 20 kHz, requiring a sampling rate > 40 kHz — too fast for integrating ADCs. |
| **Battery voltage monitor** (e.g., BMS for EVs) | **SAR ADC** | Low power, high speed, 12-bit resolution is sufficient. |
| **Digital Multimeter** (Fluke 87V) | **Dual-Slope** | 4.5+ digit resolution (23-bit equivalent) and excellent noise rejection. |
| **Microcontroller internal ADC** (STM32, Arduino) | **SAR ADC** | Most modern MCUs (Atmel, STM32, ESP32) use SAR architecture for their internal ADCs. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Sigma-Delta ADCs are not in the syllabus here**: Sigma-Delta ADCs are *over-sampling converters* and are a separate KTU topic in advanced modules. The three ADCs discussed in Module 4 — SAR, Integrating, and Single Slope — are the *foundational architectures* every KTU ECE student must master.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Successive Approximation (SAR) ADC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vin["Analog Input Vin"] --> SH["Sample and Hold Circuit"]
    SH --> CMP1["Comparator Positive Input"]
    SAR["Successive Approximation Register SAR"] --> DAC["Internal DAC R-2R Ladder"]
    DAC --> CMP2["Comparator Negative Input"]
    CMP1 --> CMPNODE{"Analog Comparator"}
    CMP2 --> CMPNODE
    CMPNODE --> CTRL["Comparator Output Higher or Lower Decision"]
    CTRL --> SAR
    CLK["External Clock fclk"] --> SAR
    SAR --> OUT["Digital Output B0 to B n-1"]
```

**Diagram Interpretation**:
- The **SAR** drives the **DAC** with a trial code.
- The **Comparator** decides if the trial is higher or lower than $V_{in}$.
- The decision is fed back to the **SAR** to determine the next bit.
- After $n$ cycles, the output register contains the digital code.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram of Dual-Slope Integrating ADC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Start Conversion"] --> S1["Switch S1 to Vin Position"]
    S1 --> INT1["Integrator Ramping Up Phase"]
    INT1 --> CNT1["Counter Counts to 2n Overflow"]
    CNT1 --> S2["Switch S1 to -Vref Position"]
    S2 --> INT2["Integrator Ramping Down Phase"]
    INT2 --> CMP0{"Comparator Detects Zero Crossing"}
    CMP0 -->|Yes| LATCH["Latch Counter Value Nout"]
    CMP0 -->|No| CNT2["Increment Counter"]
    CNT2 --> INT2
    LATCH --> RESET["Autozero and Reset Integrator"]
    RESET --> END["End of Conversion Cycle"]
```

**Diagram Interpretation**:
- **Phase 1 (Run-Up)**: Counter overflows at $2^n$ counts → switches input to $-V_{ref}$.
- **Phase 2 (Run-Down)**: Counter counts until integrator crosses zero.
- The count value $N_{out}$ is the digital output.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram of Single Slope ADC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RESET["Reset Pulse from Controller"] --> RG["Ramp Generator Linear Integrator with Constant Current"]
    RG --> CMP_P["Comparator Inverting Input"]
    VIN["Analog Input Vin"] --> CMP_N["Comparator Non Inverting Input"]
    CMP_P --> CMPNODE{"Voltage Comparator"}
    CMP_N --> CMPNODE
    CMPNODE --> GATE["Enable Gate to Counter"]
    CLK["Clock fclk"] --> CTR["Binary Counter n-bit"]
    GATE --> CTR
    CTR --> FLAG["Conversion Done Flag"]
    CTR --> OUT["Digital Output Nout"]
    RG --> SAWTOOTH["Sawtooth Waveform Reference"]
```

**Diagram Interpretation**:
- The **Ramp Generator** produces a sawtooth wave.
- The **Comparator** opens the **Gate** when $V_{ramp} < V_{in}$.
- The **Counter** counts clock pulses; the count value when the gate closes is the digital output.

---

### 4.4 Comparative Flowchart – ADC Architecture Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Need High Speed above 1 MSPS and Fixed Conversion Time"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| SAR["Use SAR ADC"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Need High Resolution above 16 bits and Excellent Noise Rejection"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| DS["Use Dual Slope Integrating ADC"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Need Lowest Cost and Lowest Component Count for Education"}
    Q3 -->|Yes| SS["Use Single Slope ADC"]
    Q3 -->|No| SD["Consider Sigma Delta or Pipelined ADC"]
```

**Diagram Interpretation**:
- This flowchart helps KTU students *choose the right ADC* for their project or design problem.
- The decision tree maps directly to the three architectures covered in Module 4.

---

### 4.5 Timing Diagram – SAR ADC Bit-by-Bit Decision

```mermaid
gantt
    title SAR ADC Conversion Timing for 4-bit ADC Vin equals 11 decimal
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Bit Decisions
    Cycle 1 MSB Test 12 Vref to 2 :a1, 0, 1
    Cycle 2 Bit 2 Test :a2, 1, 1
    Cycle 3 Bit 3 Test :a3, 2, 1
    Cycle 4 LSB Test :a4, 3, 1
    section DAC Output
    DAC 0 to 8 V :crit, 0, 1
    DAC 8 to 12 V :crit, 1, 1
    DAC 10 to 11 V :crit, 2, 1
    DAC 10.5 to 11 V :crit, 3, 1
```

**Diagram Interpretation**:
- For a 4-bit SAR ADC with $V_{ref} = 16 \text{ V}$ and $V_{in} = 11 \text{ V}$:
  - **Cycle 1**: Test $V_{DAC} = 8 \text{ V}$. $V_{in} > 8$, so MSB = 1.
  - **Cycle 2**: Test $V_{DAC} = 8 + 4 = 12 \text{ V}$. $V_{in} < 12$, so Bit 2 = 0.
  - **Cycle 3**: Test $V_{DAC} = 8 + 0 + 2 = 10 \text{ V}$. $V_{in} > 10$, so Bit 3 = 1.
  - **Cycle 4**: Test $V_{DAC} = 8 + 0 + 2 + 0.5 = 10.5 \text{ V}$. $V_{in} > 10.5$, so LSB = 1.
  - **Final code**: `1011` (binary) = **11** (decimal) ✓

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three advantages of Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC over Dual Slope ADC.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
1. **Fixed Conversion Time**: SAR ADC has a constant conversion time of $(n+1) \times T_{clk}$ independent of the input voltage, whereas dual-slope ADC has a variable conversion time proportional to $V_{in}$. This makes SAR ADCs suitable for **multiplexed data acquisition** systems. *(1 mark)*
2. **Higher Speed**: SAR ADCs can achieve conversion rates of 1 MSPS to 10 MSPS, which is 1000× faster than dual-slope ADCs (which operate at 10 to 1000 SPS). *(1 mark)*
3. **Lower Power Consumption per Conversion**: Since the conversion finishes in microseconds (not milliseconds), the *average* power consumption is lower for battery-powered applications. *(1 mark)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain why the conversion time of a Single Slope ADC varies with the analog input voltage.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
In a Single Slope ADC, the conversion time is the time taken by the **linear ramp** to rise from **0 V to $V_{in}$**. Since the ramp has a **fixed slope $m$ (V/s)**, the time to reach a given $V_{in}$ is given by:
$$t = \frac{V_{in}}{m}$$
Therefore, a **larger** input voltage requires a **longer** ramp time, which means the counter must count for a **longer** duration, resulting in a **variable conversion time proportional to $V_{in}$**. This is the primary disadvantage compared to SAR ADCs, which have fixed conversion time. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – With Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Do NOT confuse** the conversion time formulas between SAR and Integrating ADCs. Many students incorrectly write $T_c = n \cdot T_{clk}$ for SAR — the correct formula includes the **+1 for sample acquisition**, so $T_c = (n+1) \cdot T_{clk}$.
> - **Always state units** in numerical answers (e.g., **ms, µs, counts, V**). Loss of units = loss of 0.5 to 1 mark per sub-question.
> - **Draw waveforms** wherever possible (especially ramp waveform for single slope and integrator output for dual slope). KTU examiners allot **1 to 2 marks specifically for diagrams**.
> - **Double slope ≠ Dual slope**. The term is "Dual-Slope". Don't lose a mark for terminology.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – SAR and Dual Slope ADC Combined**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper 2, Modified]**

**(a) Explain the working of a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Block Diagram** (1 mark):
The SAR ADC consists of:
1. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)**: Holds $V_{in}$ during conversion.
2. **SAR Logic Block**: Performs binary search algorithm.
3. **Internal DAC**: Converts the SAR's trial code to analog voltage.
4. **Comparator**: Compares $V_{in}$ with $V_{DAC}$.

**Working Principle** (3 marks):
The SAR performs a **binary search** on the input voltage. Starting from the MSB, it sets each bit to '1' and checks if $V_{DAC} > V_{in}$ or $V_{DAC} < V_{in}$:
- If $V_{DAC} > V_{in}$: reset the bit to '0'.
- If $V_{DAC} < V_{in}$: keep the bit as '1'.
After $n$ cycles, all $n$ bits are determined, giving the final digital output.

**Conversion Time** (1 mark):
$$T_c = (n+1) \times T_{clk} \text{ (fixed)}$$

**Advantages** (1 mark):
- Fixed conversion time — good for multiplexed systems.
- High speed (1–10 MSPS).
- Moderate hardware complexity.

**Limitations** (1 mark):
- Requires a high-precision internal DAC.
- Speed limited by DAC settling time and comparator propagation delay.
- Sensitive to noise (needs external anti-aliasing filter).

**(b) A 12-bit dual-slope ADC uses a clock frequency of 4 MHz. The reference voltage is $V_{ref} = 10 \text{ V}$. The integration time is set to $T_1 = 2^{12} \times T_{clk}$. Find the digital output count for an analog input of $V_{in} = 3.7 \text{ V}$. Also calculate the maximum conversion time. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1**: Compute $T_{clk}$:
$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{4 \text{ MHz}} = 0.25 \mu s$$

**Step 2**: Compute $T_1$:
$$T_1 = 2^{12} \times 0.25 \mu s = 4096 \times 0.25 \mu s = 1024 \mu s = 1.024 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3**: Apply the dual-slope equation to find $T_2$:
$$V_{in} = V_{ref} \times \frac{T_2}{T_1} \Rightarrow T_2 = \frac{V_{in} \times T_1}{V_{ref}}$$
$$T_2 = \frac{3.7 \text{ V} \times 1.024 \text{ ms}}{10 \text{ V}} = 0.3789 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4**: Convert $T_2$ to digital count:
$$N_{out} = \frac{T_2}{T_{clk}} = \frac{0.3789 \text{ ms}}{0.25 \mu s} = 1515.5 \approx 1515 \text{ counts}$$
$$N_{out} = 1515_{10} = 0x05EB = 0\text{b}0000\,0101\,1110\,1011$$

**Step 5**: Compute maximum conversion time (at $V_{in} = V_{ref} = 10 \text{ V}$):
$$T_{2, max} = \frac{V_{ref} \times T_1}{V_{ref}} = T_1 = 1.024 \text{ ms}$$
$$T_{c, max} = T_1 + T_{2, max} = 1.024 + 1.024 = 2.048 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6**: Resolution (smallest detectable voltage):
$$q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{12}} = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{4096} = 2.44 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

**Final Answers**:
- $N_{out} = 1515$ counts
- $T_{c, max} = 2.048 \text{ ms}$
- $q = 2.44 \text{ mV/LSB}$

*(Each step carries ~1 mark; diagram carries 1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Single Slope ADC and Project Suggestion**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper 1, Modified]**

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a single slope ADC. Discuss its merits and demerits. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Block Diagram** (1 mark):
The Single Slope ADC consists of:
1. **Ramp Generator** (using a constant-current source charging a capacitor).
2. **Comparator**.
3. **Binary Counter** with clock input.
4. **Control Logic** for reset and start.

**Working Principle** (3 marks):
A **linear ramp voltage** is generated starting from 0 V. This ramp is fed to one input of the comparator; the unknown $V_{in}$ is fed to the other input. A **counter** counts clock pulses starting when the ramp begins. When the ramp voltage **exceeds $V_{in}$**, the comparator triggers, and the counter is latched. The final count is the digital representation of $V_{in}$. The counter is then reset, and a new ramp begins.

**Mathematical Relation** (1 mark):
$$N = f_{clk} \times \frac{V_{in}}{m}$$
where $m$ is the ramp slope in V/s and $f_{clk}$ is the clock frequency.

**Merits** (1 mark):
- Extremely simple hardware (one op-amp, one comparator, one counter).
- Low cost, suitable for educational demonstrations.
- Easy to understand the A/D conversion concept.

**Demerits** (1 mark):
- Conversion time is **variable** (proportional to $V_{in}$).
- Output is highly **sensitive to ramp slope** $m$ — any drift in $R$ or $C$ causes gain error.
- Poor noise rejection compared to dual-slope.
- Resolution is limited to 8–12 bits typically.

**(b) A single slope ADC uses a 10-bit counter and a clock frequency of 1 MHz. The ramp generator has a slope of 1 V/ms. Calculate: (i) the maximum conversion time, (ii) the digital output for an input of 4.32 V, and (iii) the resolution in mV. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1**: Identify given values:
- $n = 10$, $N_{FS} = 2^{10} = 1024$
- $f_{clk} = 1 \text{ MHz} \Rightarrow T_{clk} = 1 \mu s$
- Slope $m = 1 \text{ V/ms} = 1000 \text{ V/s}$

**Step 2**: Compute maximum conversion time (at $V_{in} = V_{ref}$):
First, find $V_{ref}$ corresponding to full-scale count. The full-scale input equals:
$$V_{FS} = m \times T_{c, max} = m \times N_{FS} \times T_{clk}$$
$$V_{FS} = 1000 \text{ V/s} \times 1024 \times 1 \mu s = 1000 \times 1024 \times 10^{-6} = 1.024 \text{ V}$$
$$T_{c, max} = N_{FS} \times T_{clk} = 1024 \times 1 \mu s = 1024 \mu s = 1.024 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3**: Digital output for $V_{in} = 4.32 \text{ V}$:
$$N_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{m \times T_{clk}} = \frac{4.32 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ V/ms} \times 1 \mu s}$$
$$N_{out} = \frac{4.32}{1000 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{4.32}{0.001} = 4320 \text{ counts}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert**: If $N_{out}$ exceeds 1024, the counter will **overflow** and the ADC will saturate. The student must verify the input range. Here, $N_{out} = 4320 > 1024$, meaning the ADC is **over-ranged**. The output will clip to **1023** (all 1s).

Correcting the analysis using the actual full-scale voltage:
$$V_{FS} = 1.024 \text{ V}, \text{ so } V_{in} = 4.32 \text{ V is out of range.}$$
The output would saturate at $N_{max} = 1023$ counts.

If we instead assume the design's reference voltage is $V_{ref} = 5 \text{ V}$ (a common design choice), then:
$$q = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{1024} = 4.88 \text{ mV/LSB}$$
$$N_{out} = \frac{4.32 \text{ V}}{4.88 \text{ mV}} = 885 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 4**: Resolution in mV:
$$q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{1024} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

**Final Answers**:
- $T_{c, max} = 1.024 \text{ ms}$
- $N_{out} = 885$ counts (assuming $V_{ref} = 5 \text{ V}$)
- $q = 4.88 \text{ mV/LSB}$

*(Each step carries ~1 mark; 1 mark for the saturation warning if applicable)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist** — read this 5 minutes before the exam.

#### **Successive Approximation (SAR) ADC**
- ✅ Architecture: **S/H + DAC + Comparator + SAR** (closed-loop).
- ✅ Algorithm: **Binary search**, MSB to LSB, one bit per clock cycle.
- ✅ Conversion time: $T_c = (n+1) \times T_{clk}$ — **FIXED**, independent of $V_{in}$.
- ✅ Resolution: 8 to 18 bits typical; 1 MSPS to 10 MSPS speed.
- ✅ Used in: **Microcontrollers (STM32, ESP32, Arduino), data loggers, motor control**.
- ✅ Key Limitation: Requires **precision internal DAC**; speed limited by DAC settling.

#### **Dual-Slope Integrating ADC**
- ✅ Architecture: **Integrator + Comparator + Counter** (two-phase conversion).
- ✅ Two Phases: **Run-up (integrate $V_{in}$ for fixed $T_1$) → Run-down (de-integrate $-V_{ref}$ for variable $T_2$)**.
- ✅ Key Equation: $V_{in} = V_{ref} \times \frac{T_2}{T_1}$ — $R, C$ and clock frequency **cancel out**.
- ✅ Conversion time: $T_c = T_1 + T_2$ — **variable** (longer for higher $V_{in}$).
- ✅ Resolution: 16 to 24 bits; **excellent 50 Hz / 60 Hz noise rejection**.
- ✅ Used in: **Digital multimeters (Fluke, Agilent), weighing scales, panel meters**.
- ✅ Choose $T_1 = 20 \text{ ms}, 100 \text{ ms}$ for **50 Hz mains rejection**.

#### **Single Slope ADC**
- ✅ Architecture: **Ramp Generator + Comparator + Counter** (one-phase).
- ✅ Operation: Counter starts when ramp begins; latched when ramp $\geq V_{in}$.
- ✅ Key Equation: $N = f_{clk} \times \frac{V_{in}}{m}$.
- ✅ Conversion time: **VARIABLE** (proportional to $V_{in}$).
- ✅ Resolution: 8 to 12 bits; **poor noise rejection**; **sensitive to ramp slope**.
- ✅ Used in: **Educational lab kits, low-cost digital voltmeters**.

#### **Comparison Triad (Memorize This Table)**

| Property | SAR | Dual Slope | Single Slope |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conversion Time | Fixed | Variable | Variable |
| Speed | High | Low | Low |
| Resolution | Medium | High | Low |
| Noise Rejection | Poor | Excellent | Poor |
| Hardware | Complex | Medium | Simple |

#### **Common Numerical Pitfalls**
- ❌ Writing $T_c = n \times T_{clk}$ for SAR → ✅ Correct is $T_c = (n+1) \times T_{clk}$.
- ❌ Forgetting units in answers (ms, µs, counts, V).
- ❌ Confusing **dual slope** with **single slope** in diagrams.
- ❌ Using $V_{in}/V_{ref}$ ratio in dual slope but forgetting to multiply by $2^n$ for final count.
- ❌ Not mentioning the **+1 sample cycle** in SAR timing analysis.

#### **Project Topic Selection Tips**
- ✅ Choose a project that **demonstrates all three ADC types** for maximum syllabus coverage.
- ✅ Combine **hardware + firmware + cloud/IoT** for best marks.
- ✅ Use **open-source tools**: Arduino IDE, STM32CubeIDE, Proteus simulation.
- ✅ Always include a **comparison table** in the project report showing your design vs. a commercial ADC.
- ✅ **Avoid plagiarism** — KTU uses Turnitin for project report verification.
